topics.title: Generation and Demodulation of Pulse Amplitude Modulation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT634 - COMMUNICATION SYSTEM AND TELEMETRY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Generation and Demodulation of Pulse Amplitude Modulation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Pulse Amplitude Modulation (PAM): The Foundation of Pulse Modulation

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Pulse Amplitude Modulation (PAM)** is a pulse modulation technique in which the **amplitude of regularly spaced rectangular pulses** is varied in proportion to the instantaneous amplitude of the corresponding message (baseband) signal, while the **pulse width** and **pulse position remain constant**.

Mathematically, a PAM signal $s(t)$ can be expressed as:

$$
s(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} m(nT_s) \cdot p(t - nT_s)
$$

where:
- $m(t)$ is the message signal (band-limited to $W$ Hz)
- $p(t)$ is the pulse shape (rectangular pulse of width $\tau$ and amplitude $A$)
- $T_s$ is the sampling interval
- $n$ is the sample index
- $T_s \leq \dfrac{1}{2W}$ is the **Nyquist sampling criterion**

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Concept):**
> PAM is the simplest form of pulse modulation, where the analog message signal is multiplied by a train of rectangular pulses acting as the sampling waveform. The resulting signal carries the information only in the amplitude of the pulses.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Staircase Photograph"

Imagine a person walking down a staircase while being photographed by a strobe light (which flashes at regular intervals).

- The **staircase** = the continuous message signal $m(t)$
- The **strobe light** = the sampling pulses
- Each **photograph** = one sample, where only the **height of the person at that instant** is captured (the *amplitude* of the pulse)

The continuous motion is converted into a series of "snapshots" (pulses) whose heights mirror the person's position. This is exactly how PAM encodes information.

> [!NOTE] **Key Insight:** PAM is essentially **Amplitude Modulation applied to a pulse carrier**, not to a sinusoidal carrier. That is why it is sometimes called a "pulse carrier system."

## 1.3 Classification of PAM Signals

PAM is broadly classified into two categories — both are **exam-favourites** at KTU:

| Type | Description | Pulse Shape After Sampling |
|---|---|---|
| **Natural Sampled PAM** (Ideal Sampling) | The tops of the pulses follow the natural shape of the message signal | Curved, distorted pulse tops |
| **Flat-Top PAM** (Sample-and-Hold) | The tops are held flat at the sampled value | Flat-topped rectangular pulses |

> [!IMPORTANT] **Why two types?**
> Natural sampling is **theoretically ideal** but **hard to generate** in practice. Flat-top PAM is **easy to generate using a Sample-and-Hold (S/H) circuit** and is therefore the **industry standard**.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Nyquist Sampling Rate:** $f_s \geq 2W$ where $W$ is the highest frequency in the message
- **Standard Pulse Width:** $\tau \ll T_s$ (typically $\tau = 0.1 T_s$ to $0.5 T_s$)
- **Duty Cycle:** $\dfrac{\tau}{T_s} \times 100\%$
- **Transmission Bandwidth:** $BW = \dfrac{1}{\tau}$ (since pulse width $\tau$ determines the spectral lobe width)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis: Generation Methods of PAM

## 2.1 Generation of Natural Sampled PAM

### Operational Logic (Step-by-Step)

A natural-sampled PAM signal is generated by directly **multiplying** the message signal $m(t)$ with a train of narrow rectangular pulses $c(t)$.

- **Step 1 — Input:** Apply the band-limited message signal $m(t)$ (max frequency $W$) to one input of an **analog multiplier**.
- **Step 2 — Sampling Pulses:** Generate a pulse train $c(t)$ of amplitude $A$, pulse width $\tau$, and period $T_s$ satisfying $f_s = 1/T_s \geq 2W$.
- **Step 3 — Multiplication:** The multiplier output is $s(t) = m(t) \cdot c(t)$.
- **Step 4 — Result:** Each pulse is "sculpted" such that its top follows the natural curvature of $m(t)$ during the interval $[nT_s, nT_s + \tau]$.
- **Step 5 — Output:** The resulting waveform is natural-sampled PAM.

### Mathematical Expression of the Pulse Train

The sampling pulse train can be written as a Fourier series:

$$
c(t) = \frac{A\tau}{T_s} \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \text{sinc}\left(\frac{n\pi\tau}{T_s}\right) e^{j2\pi n f_s t}
$$

where $\text{sinc}(x) = \dfrac{\sin x}{x}$.

### Spectrum of Natural-Sampled PAM

The Fourier transform of the product is the **convolution** in frequency:

$$
S(f) = \frac{A\tau}{T_s} \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \text{sinc}\left(\frac{n\pi\tau}{T_s}\right) M(f - n f_s)
$$

> [!NOTE] **Interpretation:** The spectrum consists of **replicas of the baseband spectrum** $M(f)$ placed at every multiple of $f_s$, weighted by $\text{sinc}$ envelope.

---

## 2.2 Generation of Flat-Top PAM (Sample-and-Hold Method)

### Operational Logic (Step-by-Step)

Flat-top PAM is the **most practically used** PAM method. The signal amplitude is **held constant** at the sampled value for the entire pulse duration.

- **Step 1 — Sampling:** A switch (typically a FET or CMOS switch) closes momentarily at $t = nT_s$.
- **Step 2 — Capacitor Charging:** A holding capacitor $C$ charges to the instantaneous value $m(nT_s)$.
- **Step 3 — Hold:** The switch opens, and the capacitor **holds** the voltage until the next sample arrives.
- **Step 4 — Pulse Train Driver:** The sample value is multiplied by a rectangular pulse of width $\tau$, producing a flat-topped pulse.
- **Step 5 — Output:** Flat-topped PAM waveform with constant amplitude within each pulse.

### Mathematical Model

The flat-top PAM signal is:

$$
s(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} m(nT_s) \cdot h(t - nT_s)
$$

where $h(t)$ is a rectangular pulse of width $\tau$ and unit amplitude.

### Spectrum of Flat-Top PAM

$$
S(f) = H(f) \cdot \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} M(f - n f_s)
$$

where $H(f) = \tau \, \text{sinc}(f\tau) e^{-j\pi f \tau}$ is the Fourier transform of the rectangular pulse.

> [!WARNING] **Spectral Distortion (Aperture Effect):**
> Flat-top PAM introduces a **sinc distortion** (aperture effect) given by the factor $H(f) = \tau \, \text{sinc}(f\tau)$. To compensate, an **equalization filter** with response $\dfrac{1}{H(f)} = \dfrac{\pi f \tau}{\sin(\pi f \tau)}$ is added before the low-pass filter at the receiver.

---

## 2.3 Demodulation of PAM Signals

PAM is a **baseband** transmission, so demodulation is straightforward.

### Method 1 — Low-Pass Filtering (Synchronous Demodulation)

- **Step 1:** The received PAM signal $s(t)$ is passed through a **low-pass filter (LPF)** with cutoff $f_c = W$.
- **Step 2:** The LPF removes all spectral replicas centered at $n f_s$ for $n \neq 0$.
- **Step 3:** The output recovers the original message $m(t)$ (with amplitude scaling).

> [!NOTE] **Limitation:** Without a sample-and-hold at the receiver, the LPF output will have **gaps** (zero values) between samples. An integrating filter is preferred.

### Method 2 — Sample-and-Hold + Low-Pass Filter (Preferred)

- **Step 1:** Sample the received PAM at $t = nT_s$ using a synchronized clock.
- **Step 2:** Hold each sample for duration $\tau$ using a capacitor.
- **Step 3:** Apply a low-pass filter to smooth the staircase into the original message.

> [!IMPORTANT] **Synchronization is Mandatory:**
> The receiver must have a clock **synchronized** with the transmitter's sampling instants. Loss of sync causes **inter-symbol interference (ISI)** and severe distortion.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT] The following table is **exam-ready** and follows standard KTU board examiner expectations.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Nyquist Sampling Rate | $f_s = 2W$ | Hz — minimum rate to avoid aliasing |
| 2 | Sampling Theorem (General) | $f_s \geq 2W$ | Hz — for lossless reconstruction |
| 3 | Pulse Train Fourier Coeff. | $C_n = \dfrac{A\tau}{T_s} \text{sinc}\left(\dfrac{n\pi\tau}{T_s}\right)$ | Volts |
| 4 | Natural-Sampled PAM Spectrum | $S(f) = \dfrac{A\tau}{T_s} \sum_n \text{sinc}\left(\dfrac{n\pi\tau}{T_s}\right) M(f - n f_s)$ | Volts/Hz |
| 5 | Flat-Top PAM Spectrum | $S(f) = \tau \text{sinc}(f\tau) e^{-j\pi f \tau} \cdot \dfrac{1}{T_s} \sum_n M(f - n f_s)$ | Volts/Hz |
| 6 | Aperture Distortion | $H(f) = \tau \text{sinc}(f\tau)$ | Unitless |
| 7 | Equalizer Response | $E(f) = \dfrac{1}{\tau \text{sinc}(f\tau)} = \dfrac{\pi f \tau}{\sin(\pi f \tau)}$ | Unitless |
| 8 | Transmission Bandwidth | $BW = \dfrac{1}{\tau}$ | Hz — depends on pulse width |
| 9 | Duty Cycle | $D = \dfrac{\tau}{T_s} \times 100$ | Percent |
| 10 | Reconstruction LPF Cutoff | $f_c = W$ | Hz — recovers baseband |
| 11 | Output SNR (PAM, ideal) | $\left(\dfrac{S}{N}\right)_o = \dfrac{S}{N_0 W}$ | Linear ratio |
| 12 | Average Power (Rectangular Pulse) | $P_{avg} = A^2 \cdot D = A^2 \cdot \dfrac{\tau}{T_s}$ | Watts |

> **Engineering Utility:** PAM is the **first stage** in Pulse Code Modulation (PCM), Delta Modulation (DM), and Time Division Multiplexing (TDM) systems. It is fundamental to digital communication transmitters and telemetry systems used in **satellite, defense, and biomedical** applications.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Spectrum of Natural Sampled PAM

### Statement
Derive the Fourier transform of the natural-sampled PAM signal, given a message $m(t)$ band-limited to $W$ Hz and a rectangular pulse train $c(t)$ of amplitude $A$, width $\tau$, and period $T_s$.

### Derivation

The natural-sampled PAM signal is the **product** of $m(t)$ and the pulse train $c(t)$:

$$
s(t) = m(t) \cdot c(t)
$$

**Step 1 — Express the pulse train using its Fourier Series:**

The periodic pulse train $c(t)$ of period $T_s$ can be expanded as:

$$
c(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} C_n \, e^{j2\pi n f_s t}, \quad f_s = \frac{1}{T_s}
$$

**Step 2 — Find the Fourier coefficients $C_n$:**

For a rectangular pulse of width $\tau$ centered at $t = 0$ with amplitude $A$:

$$
C_n = \frac{1}{T_s} \int_{-\tau/2}^{\tau/2} A \cdot e^{-j2\pi n f_s t} \, dt
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the integral:**

$$
C_n = \frac{A}{T_s} \left[ \frac{e^{-j2\pi n f_s t}}{-j2\pi n f_s} \right]_{-\tau/2}^{\tau/2}
$$

**Step 4 — Simplify the exponential terms:**

$$
C_n = \frac{A}{T_s} \cdot \frac{e^{j\pi n f_s \tau} - e^{-j\pi n f_s \tau}}{j2\pi n f_s}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the identity $e^{j\theta} - e^{-j\theta} = 2j\sin\theta$:**

$$
C_n = \frac{A}{T_s} \cdot \frac{2j\sin(\pi n f_s \tau)}{j2\pi n f_s}
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute $f_s = 1/T_s$ and rearrange:**

$$
C_n = \frac{A\tau}{T_s} \cdot \frac{\sin(\pi n \tau / T_s)}{\pi n \tau / T_s}
$$

**Step 7 — Recognize the sinc function:**

$$
C_n = \frac{A\tau}{T_s} \, \text{sinc}\left(\frac{n\pi\tau}{T_s}\right)
$$

**Step 8 — Substitute $C_n$ back into the pulse train expression:**

$$
c(t) = \frac{A\tau}{T_s} \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \text{sinc}\left(\frac{n\pi\tau}{T_s}\right) e^{j2\pi n f_s t}
$$

**Step 9 — Multiply by $m(t)$ to obtain $s(t)$:**

$$
s(t) = m(t) \cdot c(t) = \frac{A\tau}{T_s} \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \text{sinc}\left(\frac{n\pi\tau}{T_s}\right) m(t) e^{j2\pi n f_s t}
$$

**Step 10 — Apply the frequency-shift property of the Fourier Transform:**

Recall that $\mathcal{F}\{m(t) e^{j2\pi n f_s t}\} = M(f - n f_s)$.

**Step 11 — Take the Fourier Transform of both sides:**

$$
S(f) = \frac{A\tau}{T_s} \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \text{sinc}\left(\frac{n\pi\tau}{T_s}\right) M(f - n f_s)
$$

**Final Result — Natural-Sampled PAM Spectrum:**

$$
\boxed{\,S(f) = \frac{A\tau}{T_s} \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \text{sinc}\left(\frac{n\pi\tau}{T_s}\right) M(f - n f_s)\,}
$$

> **Physical Interpretation:** The baseband spectrum $M(f)$ is replicated at $\pm f_s, \pm 2f_s, \pm 3f_s, \ldots$ with amplitudes weighted by the sinc envelope $\dfrac{\sin(\pi n \tau / T_s)}{\pi n \tau / T_s}$. The **central replica at $n=0$** gives back the original message.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Spectrum of Flat-Top PAM

### Statement
Derive the spectrum of a flat-top (sample-and-hold) PAM signal, where each sample $m(nT_s)$ is held for a duration $\tau$.

### Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the flat-top PAM signal as a convolution:**

A flat-top pulse is mathematically equivalent to multiplying the sampled (impulse) signal $m_s(t) = \sum_n m(nT_s) \delta(t - nT_s)$ with a rectangular pulse $h(t)$ of width $\tau$:

$$
s(t) = \left[ \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} m(nT_s) \, \delta(t - nT_s) \right] \ast h(t)
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Convolution Theorem:**

Taking the Fourier Transform of both sides:

$$
S(f) = \left[ \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} M(f - n f_s) \right] \cdot H(f)
$$

where the term in brackets is the Fourier transform of the impulse-sampled signal (using the Poisson summation formula).

**Step 3 — Compute the FT of the rectangular pulse $h(t)$:**

$$
H(f) = \int_0^{\tau} 1 \cdot e^{-j2\pi f t} \, dt = \frac{1 - e^{-j2\pi f \tau}}{j2\pi f}
$$

**Step 4 — Simplify using Euler's identity:**

$$
H(f) = \frac{e^{-j\pi f \tau}(e^{j\pi f \tau} - e^{-j\pi f \tau})}{j2\pi f} = \frac{e^{-j\pi f \tau} \cdot 2j \sin(\pi f \tau)}{j2\pi f}
$$

**Step 5 — Final expression for $H(f)$:**

$$
H(f) = \tau \, \text{sinc}(f\tau) \, e^{-j\pi f \tau}
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute into the spectrum equation:**

$$
S(f) = \tau \, \text{sinc}(f\tau) \, e^{-j\pi f \tau} \cdot \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} M(f - n f_s)
$$

**Final Result — Flat-Top PAM Spectrum:**

$$
\boxed{\,S(f) = \frac{\tau}{T_s} \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \text{sinc}(f\tau) \, e^{-j\pi f \tau} \, M(f - n f_s)\,}
$$

**Step 7 — Identify the Aperture Effect:**

Comparing with natural-sampled PAM, the additional multiplicative factor $H(f) = \tau \, \text{sinc}(f\tau) e^{-j\pi f \tau}$ represents the **aperture distortion**. At $f = 0$:

$$
H(0) = \tau \cdot 1 \cdot 1 = \tau
$$

At higher frequencies, $|H(f)|$ rolls off as $|\text{sinc}(f\tau)|$, attenuating the higher frequencies of the message non-uniformly.

**Step 8 — Equalizer design to compensate:**

The equalizer's frequency response must be:

$$
E(f) = \frac{1}{H(f)} = \frac{1}{\tau \, \text{sinc}(f\tau)} = \frac{\pi f \tau}{\sin(\pi f \tau)}
$$

This **boosts high frequencies** to compensate for the sinc roll-off, restoring a flat overall response.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Demodulation via Low-Pass Filter

### Statement
Show that a low-pass filter with cutoff frequency $W$ recovers the message $m(t)$ from a natural-sampled PAM signal.

### Derivation

**Step 1 — Recall the natural-sampled PAM spectrum:**

$$
S(f) = \frac{A\tau}{T_s} \left[ M(f) + \text{sinc}\left(\frac{\pi\tau}{T_s}\right) M(f - f_s) + \text{sinc}\left(\frac{2\pi\tau}{T_s}\right) M(f - 2f_s) + \cdots \right]
$$

**Step 2 — Identify the baseband component (n = 0):**

At $n = 0$, the term is:

$$
S_0(f) = \frac{A\tau}{T_s} \cdot M(f) \quad \text{(sinc factor = 1 at } n=0\text{)}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply a low-pass filter with gain $G$ and cutoff $W$:**

The LPF transfer function is:

$$
H_{LPF}(f) = \begin{cases} G, & \vert f \vert \leq W \\ 0, & \vert f \vert > W \end{cases}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the output spectrum:**

$$
S_o(f) = S(f) \cdot H_{LPF}(f) = \frac{A\tau G}{T_s} \cdot M(f)
$$

**Step 5 — Take the inverse Fourier Transform:**

$$
s_o(t) = \frac{A\tau G}{T_s} \cdot m(t)
$$

**Final Result — Recovered Message:**

$$
\boxed{\,s_o(t) = \frac{A\tau G}{T_s} \, m(t)\,}
$$

> **Conclusion:** The output is a **scaled replica** of the original message $m(t)$. Choosing $G = \dfrac{T_s}{A\tau}$ makes the output exactly equal to $m(t)$ — perfect reconstruction.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Transmission Bandwidth of PAM

### Statement
Show that the minimum transmission bandwidth of a PAM signal is $BW = 1/\tau$.

### Derivation

**Step 1 — Pulse shape is rectangular of width $\tau$:**

A rectangular pulse in time domain corresponds to a sinc in frequency domain.

**Step 2 — First-null bandwidth of the sinc function:**

The Fourier transform of a rectangular pulse of width $\tau$ has its **first zero crossing** at $f = 1/\tau$.

**Step 3 — Total transmission bandwidth:**

Since each spectral lobe (centered at $n f_s$) has width $2/\tau$ (extending from $-1/\tau$ to $+1/\tau$ around its center), the **per-lobe** bandwidth is $1/\tau$ (one-sided) or $2/\tau$ (two-sided).

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{\,BW = \frac{1}{\tau}\,}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT] **Engineering Trade-off:** A **narrower pulse** ($\tau \to 0$) gives **wider bandwidth** but **lower average power** (good for noise immunity against impulse noise). A **wider pulse** ($\tau \to T_s$) gives **narrower bandwidth** but **higher average power** and **more inter-pulse interference**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams: PAM Generation & Demodulation Architecture

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Natural Sampled PAM Generation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Message Signal m of t] --> M[Analog Multiplier]
    C[Pulse Generator c of t] --> M
    M --> P[Natural Sampled PAM s of t]
```

**Component Roles:**
- **Message Signal $m(t)$:** Band-limited analog input (e.g., voice, 0 to 4 kHz)
- **Pulse Generator:** Produces narrow rectangular pulses of width $\tau$ and period $T_s$
- **Analog Multiplier (e.g., AD633, MC1496):** Performs the time-domain multiplication
- **Output $s(t)$:** Natural-sampled PAM with curved pulse tops

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Flat-Top PAM Generation (Sample-and-Hold)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Message Signal m of t] --> S[Electronic Switch FET]
    CLK[Sampling Clock] --> S
    S --> C[Holding Capacitor C]
    C --> AMP[Buffer Amplifier]
    AMP --> O[Flat Top PAM s of t]
    P[Pulse Shaper width tau] --> AMP
```

**Component Roles:**
- **Electronic Switch (FET/BJT):** Closes momentarily at each sampling instant
- **Clock Generator:** Controls the switch with narrow pulses at $f_s$
- **Holding Capacitor:** Charges to $m(nT_s)$ and holds the voltage
- **Buffer Amplifier:** Prevents loading of the capacitor (high input impedance)
- **Pulse Shaper:** Generates rectangular pulse of width $\tau$ for the flat top
- **Output $s(t)$:** Flat-topped PAM waveform

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram — PAM Demodulation (Synchronous LPF Method)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Received PAM Signal r of t] --> SH[Sample and Hold Circuit]
    CLK2[Synchronized Clock] --> SH
    SH --> EQ[Equalizer 1 over H of f]
    EQ --> LPF[Low Pass Filter Cutoff W]
    LPF --> OUT[Recovered Message m hat of t]
```

**Component Roles:**
- **Sample-and-Hold:** Reconstructs the staircase approximation
- **Equalizer:** Compensates for the sinc aperture distortion (for flat-top PAM)
- **Low-Pass Filter:** Removes high-frequency spectral replicas, leaving only the baseband
- **Output $\hat{m}(t)$:** Recovered message, ideally equal to $m(t)$

---

## 4.4 Complete End-to-End PAM Telemetry Link

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TX[Transmitter]
        A1[Sensor / Message Source] --> A2[Anti Aliasing LPF]
        A2 --> A3[PAM Modulator]
        A4[Sampling Clock] --> A3
        A3 --> A5[Channel Encoder Optional]
    end
    A5 --> CH[Channel with Noise n of t]
    CH --> B1[Channel Decoder Optional]
    B1 --> B2[Synchronous Demodulator S and H + LPF]
    B3[Receiver Clock PLL Sync] --> B2
    B2 --> B4[Equalizer]
    B4 --> B5[Output / Sink]
```

**Architectural Notes:**
- **Anti-Aliasing LPF** at the transmitter ensures $m(t)$ is band-limited to $W$
- **PLL-based clock recovery** at the receiver maintains synchronization
- **Equalizer** compensates for the aperture effect
- This architecture is the **precursor to PCM telemetry** and forms the physical layer of many KTU 2024 listed telemetry standards (e.g., IRIG-106)

> [!NOTE] **KTU Board Examiner Insight:** A common question is *"Draw the block diagram of a PAM system and explain each block."* Always include the **anti-aliasing filter**, **sampling clock**, and **synchronization mechanism** for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

### Question 1: Define Pulse Amplitude Modulation (PAM). Mention its two main types.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** Pulse Amplitude Modulation (PAM) is a pulse modulation technique in which the **amplitude of a train of regularly spaced rectangular pulses** is varied in proportion to the instantaneous amplitude of the message signal, while the pulse width and pulse position remain constant.

**Types (2 Marks):**
1. **Natural Sampled PAM** — The top of each pulse follows the natural shape of the message signal during the pulse interval. Generated using an analog multiplier.
2. **Flat-Top PAM (Sample-and-Hold)** — Each pulse has a constant amplitude equal to the sampled value of the message, held for the pulse duration $\tau$. Generated using a sample-and-hold circuit.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

### Question 2: State the Nyquist sampling theorem. Why is it important in PAM?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Statement (2 Marks):** *"A band-limited signal having no frequency components above $W$ Hz can be completely reconstructed from its samples if the sampling rate is at least $2W$ samples per second."*

The minimum sampling rate $f_s = 2W$ is called the **Nyquist rate**.

**Importance in PAM (1 Mark):** In PAM, the message is sampled at regular intervals. If $f_s < 2W$, **aliasing** occurs — the spectral replicas overlap, and the original message **cannot be recovered** by low-pass filtering at the receiver. Hence, Nyquist's theorem is the fundamental precondition for distortion-free PAM transmission.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Internal Choice Pattern (KTU 2024 Standard)

> **Choose either Question A or Question B. Each carries 14 marks with sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks.**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Generation & Spectrum**

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper, Adapted]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the generation of **flat-top PAM** using a sample-and-hold circuit. Compare it with natural-sampled PAM. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **spectrum of natural-sampled PAM** signal. Sketch the spectrum for a sinusoidal message $m(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$ with $f_s = 5 f_m$ and duty cycle 20%. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question A:

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Block Diagram (3 Marks):** [Refer to Section 4.2 of these notes]

```
Message m(t) → Electronic Switch (FET) → Holding Capacitor C → Buffer → Flat-Top PAM
Sampling Clock → Switch Control
```

**Working (2 Marks):**
- The FET switch closes at every clock instant $t = nT_s$, allowing the capacitor to charge to the instantaneous message value $m(nT_s)$.
- The switch opens, and the capacitor **holds** the voltage for the pulse duration $\tau$.
- A buffer amplifier (high input impedance) drives the output without discharging the capacitor.

**Comparison Table (2 Marks):**

| Feature | Natural Sampled PAM | Flat-Top PAM |
|---|---|---|
| Pulse Top Shape | Curved (natural) | Flat (held constant) |
| Generation Method | Analog multiplier | Sample-and-hold circuit |
| Aperture Distortion | None | Present ($H(f) = \tau \text{sinc}(f\tau)$) |
| Practical Use | Theoretical / rare | Industry standard |
| Complexity | Higher | Lower |

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Derivation (5 Marks):** [Full derivation in Section 3.1 of these notes]

**Final Result:**

$$
S(f) = \frac{A\tau}{T_s} \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \text{sinc}\left(\frac{n\pi\tau}{T_s}\right) M(f - n f_s)
$$

**Spectrum Sketch for $m(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$, $f_s = 5 f_m$, $\tau/T_s = 0.2$ (2 Marks):**

The baseband spectrum has delta functions at $\pm f_m$. The replicas appear at $\pm 5 f_m$, $\pm 10 f_m$, $\pm 15 f_m$, $\ldots$

The sinc weighting factors are:
- $n=0$: $\text{sinc}(0) = 1$ → amplitude = $\dfrac{A \cdot 0.2 T_s}{T_s} = 0.2 A$
- $n=1$: $\text{sinc}(0.2\pi) \approx 0.935$
- $n=2$: $\text{sinc}(0.4\pi) \approx 0.757$
- $n=3$: $\text{sinc}(0.6\pi) \approx 0.505$

**Sketch description:** Delta pairs at $\pm f_m$ (height = $0.2 A A_m$), $\pm 5f_m$ (height = $0.187 A A_m$), $\pm 10 f_m$ (height = $0.1514 A A_m$), etc.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Demodulation & Equalization**

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the **demodulation of flat-top PAM** using a sample-and-hold circuit followed by a low-pass filter. Why is an equalizer required? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **expression for the recovered message** $s_o(t)$ when the demodulated signal is passed through an ideal low-pass filter of gain $G$ and cutoff frequency $W$. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question B:

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Block Diagram (2 Marks):** [Refer to Section 4.3 of these notes]

**Working (3 Marks):**
- The received flat-top PAM signal $r(t)$ is fed to a sample-and-hold circuit.
- A **synchronized clock** (recovered via PLL) triggers the S/H at $t = nT_s$.
- The output is a **staircase waveform** $y(t)$ that approximates the original message.
- An **equalizer** with response $E(f) = \dfrac{1}{\tau \text{sinc}(f\tau)}$ compensates for the sinc roll-off (aperture effect).
- A low-pass filter with cutoff $W$ smooths the staircase, producing the recovered message $\hat{m}(t)$.

**Why Equalizer is Required (2 Marks):**
- Flat-top sampling introduces the factor $H(f) = \tau \text{sinc}(f\tau) e^{-j\pi f \tau}$ in the spectrum.
- This factor **attenuates high frequencies** of the message non-uniformly, causing **amplitude distortion** (aperture effect).
- The equalizer inverts this distortion: $E(f) \cdot H(f) = 1$, restoring a flat response.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Derivation (5 Marks):** [Refer to Section 3.3 of these notes]

**Starting Point:** After equalization, the spectrum of the received signal (at the LPF input) is:

$$
S(f) = \frac{\tau}{T_s} \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} M(f - n f_s)
$$

(The equalizer has cancelled $H(f)$.)

**Apply LPF:**

$$
H_{LPF}(f) = \begin{cases} G, & \vert f \vert \leq W \\ 0, & \vert f \vert > W \end{cases}
$$

**Output Spectrum:**

$$
S_o(f) = G \cdot \frac{\tau}{T_s} \cdot M(f) = \frac{G\tau}{T_s} M(f)
$$

**Inverse Fourier Transform (2 Marks):**

$$
\boxed{\,s_o(t) = \frac{G\tau}{T_s} \, m(t)\,}
$$

> Choosing $G = \dfrac{T_s}{\tau}$ yields $s_o(t) = m(t)$ — perfect recovery.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in PAM Questions (As Per KTU Valuation Reports):**
>
> 1. **Confusing PAM with PWM/PPM:** Many students incorrectly state that PAM is *"modulation of pulse width/position."* Always remember — **PAM varies amplitude only**.
> 2. **Skipping the sinc factor in the spectrum:** The amplitude weighting $\text{sinc}(n\pi\tau/T_s)$ is often omitted, costing **2 marks** in 7-mark sub-parts.
> 3. **Forgetting the aperture effect:** For flat-top PAM, students write the spectrum as if it were natural-sampled. Always include the $H(f) = \tau \text{sinc}(f\tau)$ factor.
> 4. **Confusing sampling rate with pulse repetition frequency:** PRF = $1/T_s$, and the message must satisfy $f_m \leq f_s/2$ (Nyquist).
> 5. **No mention of synchronization:** Demodulation answers often miss the requirement for a **synchronized clock** at the receiver. This is a **favourite 2-mark sub-question** in KTU.
> 6. **Units not mentioned in numerical problems:** Always write units (Hz, Volts, seconds) — KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks for missing units.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT] **Final 60-Second Revision Before the Exam:**

- [ ] **PAM Definition:** Amplitude of pulse carrier is varied in accordance with the message; pulse width and position are **constant**.
- [ ] **Two Types:** Natural Sampled (curved tops) and Flat-Top (constant tops via S/H circuit).
- [ ] **Natural SAM Spectrum:** $S(f) = \dfrac{A\tau}{T_s} \sum_n \text{sinc}\left(\dfrac{n\pi\tau}{T_s}\right) M(f - n f_s)$
- [ ] **Flat-Top Spectrum:** $S(f) = \dfrac{\tau}{T_s} \text{sinc}(f\tau) e^{-j\pi f \tau} \sum_n M(f - n f_s)$
- [ ] **Aperture Effect:** Distortion factor $H(f) = \tau \text{sinc}(f\tau)$ — only in flat-top PAM.
- [ ] **Equalizer Response:** $E(f) = \dfrac{1}{\tau \text{sinc}(f\tau)} = \dfrac{\pi f \tau}{\sin(\pi f \tau)}$
- [ ] **Nyquist Rate:** $f_s \geq 2W$ — must be satisfied to avoid aliasing.
- [ ] **Demodulation:** Synchronous LPF with cutoff $W$, preceded by S/H and equalizer for flat-top PAM.
- [ ] **Bandwidth:** $BW = 1/\tau$ — narrower pulse = wider bandwidth.
- [ ] **Average Power:** $P_{avg} = A^2 \cdot (\tau / T_s) = A^2 \cdot D$ where $D$ is duty cycle.
- [ ] **Generation Blocks (Must Memorize):** Message → Anti-aliasing LPF → Switch/S/H → Clock → Output.
- [ ] **Application:** PAM is the **first stage** of PCM, TDM, and telemetry encoding (e.g., IRIG standards).
- [ ] **Synchronization is Critical:** Receiver clock must be phase-locked to transmitter sampling clock.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pulse Time Modulation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT634 - COMMUNICATION SYSTEM AND TELEMETRY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Pulse Time Modulation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> **Pulse Time Modulation (PTM)** is a subclass of pulse modulation techniques in which the **information-bearing parameter is the timing characteristic of the pulse train** (i.e., either the pulse width or the pulse position), while the pulse amplitude remains strictly constant. PTM is functionally divided into two principal schemes: **Pulse Width Modulation (PWM)** — also called **Pulse Duration Modulation (PDM)** — and **Pulse Position Modulation (PPM)**.

In simpler words, when the analog message signal only changes **"when"** a pulse appears (its width or position in time) but **not "how tall"** the pulse is, the resulting system is called Pulse Time Modulation. Because the pulse height never changes, the receiver does not have to worry about amplitude noise or fading — only the exact time instant or duration of the pulse carries the data.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Lighthouse Beacon:**
> Imagine a lighthouse keeper who must signal a boat the current tide level. Instead of changing the *brightness* (amplitude) of the light flash — which gets washed out by fog — the keeper keeps the flash brightness **constant** but changes **how long the flash stays on (PWM)** or **when the flash occurs (PPM)**. A long flash means "high tide", a short flash means "low tide". The fog cannot shorten the flash, so the message arrives clean. This is exactly what PTM does: it trades amplitude for time to defeat noise.

| Sub-Scheme | What changes? | What stays constant? | Variable Parameter |
|---|---|---|---|
| **PAM** (for contrast) | Pulse Amplitude | Width & Position | Amplitude |
| **PWM (PDM)** | Pulse Width | Amplitude & Position | Duration ($t_d$) |
| **PPM** | Pulse Position | Amplitude & Width | Time-shift ($\delta t$) |

### Why PTM Matters in Telemetry

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - In **remote sensing and telemetry** (rockets, satellites, biomedical implants), the transmitted signal travels through extremely noisy media.
> - Because PTM uses **fixed-amplitude pulses**, the receiver can be built with a simple **threshold detector** (a slicer). Noise that alters amplitude by less than the threshold is completely rejected.
> - This is why PTM, and especially **PPM**, is heavily used in **optical communication, IR remote controls, and space telemetry links** (e.g., IRIG telemetry standards).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants & Standards referenced in this Module:**
> - **IRIG (Inter-Range Instrumentation Group) Serial Time Codes** — NASA/US DoD telemetry standard, uses PWM/PPM derivatives.
> - **Nyquist Rate** ($f_s \geq 2 f_m$) — governs the minimum sampling frequency for the underlying PAM before conversion to PTM.
> - **Pulse repetition frequency $f_s$** is the sampling/switching rate that produces the pulse train.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Pulse Width Modulation (PWM / PDM)

> [!NOTE]
> **Operational Principle:** The **leading edge** of every pulse is held fixed at the sampling instant $nT_s$. The **trailing edge** is shifted to the right (or left) by an amount directly proportional to the instantaneous amplitude of the message signal $m(t)$ at the sampling instant.

**Generation Logic (Step-by-Step):**

1. The continuous message $m(t)$ is sampled at instants $t = nT_s$ to obtain $m(nT_s)$.
2. A **sawtooth (ramp) waveform** $r(t)$ is generated, synchronized to the sampling clock.
3. The comparator outputs **HIGH** as long as $m(t) > r(t)$, and **LOW** otherwise.
4. The width of the HIGH segment = the **pulse duration** $t_d$, which is linearly proportional to the sampled amplitude.

The mathematical statement of the pulse width is:

$$t_d = t_0 + k_w \cdot m(nT_s)$$

where:
- $t_0$ = **unmodulated (nominal/rest) pulse width** when $m(t) = 0$.
- $k_w$ = **modulation sensitivity constant** of the PWM generator, with units of **seconds per volt**.
- $m(nT_s)$ = sampled value of the message signal at the $n^{th}$ sampling instant.

The **pulse repetition period** $T_s$ must be wide enough to accommodate the **maximum pulse width** that can occur at the peak of the message signal:

$$T_s \geq t_{d,\max} = t_0 + k_w \cdot A_m$$

This is the *why* behind the design rule that $T_s$ cannot simply be set to $1/2f_m$ for PTM the way it is in PAM — we need a guard interval.

### 2.2 Pulse Position Modulation (PPM)

> [!NOTE]
> **Operational Principle:** The pulse **width and amplitude are fixed**. Only the **time position** of each narrow pulse (relative to a reference clock) varies linearly with the sampled amplitude of the message signal. A *positive* sample shifts the pulse to the right; a *negative* sample shifts it to the left.

The pulse position is:

$$t_n = nT_s + k_p \cdot m(nT_s)$$

where:
- $nT_s$ = nominal position (the $n^{th}$ clock edge) — this is the reference.
- $k_p$ = **position modulation constant** in seconds/volt.
- $m(nT_s)$ = sampled message value.

**PPM is generated from PWM** by a clever two-step process:

1. **Differentiate** the PWM signal — this produces positive and negative spikes at the leading and trailing edges.
2. **Half-wave rectify / clip** the result — only the trailing-edge (or leading-edge, by choice) spikes survive.
3. The surviving spikes are narrow, fixed-amplitude, **position-modulated pulses** — i.e., PPM.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | PWM pulse width | $t_d = t_0 + k_w \cdot m(nT_s)$ | **seconds** |
| 2 | PPM pulse position | $t_n = nT_s + k_p \cdot m(nT_s)$ | **seconds** |
| 3 | Max PWM width (worst case) | $t_{d,\max} = t_0 + k_w \cdot A_m$ | sets minimum $T_s$ |
| 4 | Max PPM time-shift | $\Delta t_{\max} = k_p \cdot A_m$ | must satisfy $\Delta t_{\max} < T_s$ |
| 5 | Min sampling interval | $T_s \geq t_{d,\max}$ (PWM) or $T_s \geq \tau + 2\Delta t_{\max}$ (PPM, $\tau$ = pulse width) | **seconds** |
| 6 | Power efficiency of PTM | $P_{PTM} = A^2 \cdot (t_d/T_s)$ ; power varies with $m(t)$ in PWM, **constant** in PPM | Watts |
| 7 | Average pulse power in PPM | $P_{PPM} = A^2 \cdot (\tau / T_s)$ = **constant**, independent of $m(t)$ | Watts |
| 8 | Noise advantage of PPM | $\text{SNR}_{PPM} \approx 2 \cdot \text{SNR}_{PWM}$ for same message bandwidth | dimensionless (dB) |
| 9 | Bandwidth of PWM/PPM | $B_{PTM} \approx 1/\tau$ where $\tau$ is the narrow pulse width | **Hz** (PPM wider than PWM) |
| 10 | Time-shift to voltage at Rx | $m(t) \approx (t_n - nT_s)/k_p$ | **volts** (recovered message) |

> [!TIP]
> **The "Critical" row for KTU numericals is Row 3, 4, and 5** — board questions almost always ask you to compute $T_s$ or $\Delta t_{\max}$ given a maximum message amplitude $A_m$ and modulation constants $k_w, k_p$.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace Telemetry (IRIG standards):** NASA's range telemetry uses PWM/PPM variants for missile and satellite downlinks because the noise immunity of fixed-amplitude pulses is vital.
- **Biomedical Telemetry (Pacemakers, Capsule Endoscopy):** PPM is used in body-implantable transmitters — battery life is extended because no power is wasted modulating amplitude.
- **Infrared Remote Controls (TV/AC remotes):** The standard consumer IR protocol is essentially a **pulse-position / pulse-distance encoding** derived from PPM.
- **DC Motor Speed Control:** PWM (not for telemetry, but for power electronics) is the same mathematical principle.
- **Class-D Audio Amplifiers:** Use PWM as the modulation scheme to drive switching transistors.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — PWM Pulse Width Expression for a Sinusoidal Modulating Signal

Let the message signal be a single-tone sinusoid (the standard KTU test case):

$$m(t) = A_m \cos(\omega_m t)$$

where $A_m$ is the peak amplitude and $\omega_m = 2\pi f_m$ is the angular frequency.

**Step 1:** At the $n^{th}$ sampling instant $t = nT_s$, the sampled value is:

$$m(nT_s) = A_m \cos(\omega_m n T_s)$$

This is the substitution $t \rightarrow nT_s$ in the cosine argument.

**Step 2:** Substitute this into the generic PWM pulse-width equation:

$$t_d = t_0 + k_w \cdot m(nT_s)$$

**Step 3:** Replace $m(nT_s)$ using the result of Step 1:

$$t_d = t_0 + k_w \cdot A_m \cos(\omega_m n T_s)$$

**Step 4:** Group the amplitude terms to express the result as a constant plus a modulated term:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_d &= t_0 \left[ 1 + \frac{k_w A_m}{t_0} \cos(\omega_m n T_s) \right] \\
    &= t_0 \left[ 1 + m_f \cos(\omega_m n T_s) \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

where $m_f = \dfrac{k_w A_m}{t_0}$ is the **modulation index (depth)** of the PWM signal — a dimensionless number, exactly analogous to the AM modulation index.

> [!NOTE]
> **Constraint for no over-modulation / no overlap:** $m_f \leq 1$, i.e., $k_w A_m \leq t_0$. Otherwise, two adjacent PWM pulses would overlap, destroying the modulation.

### 3.2 Derivation — PPM Pulse Position for a Sinusoidal Message

Starting from the same single-tone message $m(t) = A_m \cos(\omega_m t)$ and the PPM position equation:

$$t_n = nT_s + k_p \cdot m(nT_s)$$

**Step 1:** Sample the message at $t = nT_s$:

$$m(nT_s) = A_m \cos(\omega_m n T_s)$$

**Step 2:** Substitute into the PPM position expression:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_n &= nT_s + k_p \cdot A_m \cos(\omega_m n T_s) \\
    &= nT_s \left[ 1 + \frac{k_p A_m}{nT_s} \cos(\omega_m n T_s) \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

For large $n$ (steady-state operation), the second term dominates the *position deviation*, not the absolute position. The **deviation from the nominal clock edge** is:

$$\Delta t_n = k_p A_m \cos(\omega_m n T_s)$$

The **maximum deviation** is:

$$\Delta t_{\max} = k_p A_m$$

This is the *single most important quantity* in PPM link design — it determines the timing jitter budget at the receiver.

### 3.3 Derivation — PWM Generation from Comparator of Ramp and Message

Let the ramp signal be:

$$r(t) = \frac{V_r}{T_s} (t - nT_s), \quad \text{for } nT_s \leq t < (n+1)T_s$$

where $V_r$ is the ramp peak voltage reached at the end of one sampling period.

**Comparator rule:** Output is HIGH when $m(t) > r(t)$:

$$r(t) = m(t_d) \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{V_r}{T_s}(t_d - nT_s) = m(nT_s)$$

Solving for the switching instant $t_d$ (the trailing edge of the PWM pulse):

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_d - nT_s &= \frac{T_s}{V_r} \cdot m(nT_s) \\
t_d &= nT_s + \frac{T_s}{V_r} \cdot m(nT_s)
\end{aligned}
$$

So the **pulse width measured from the leading edge** $nT_s$ is:

$$\tau_n = t_d - nT_s = \frac{T_s}{V_r} \cdot m(nT_s)$$

Comparing with the textbook form $t_d = t_0 + k_w \cdot m(nT_s)$:

$$k_w = \frac{T_s}{V_r}, \qquad t_0 = 0$$

This proves that the modulation constant $k_w$ is set by the **ratio of sampling period to ramp peak voltage** — a directly engineerable quantity.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Analysis of PTM Schemes

| Parameter | PWM (PDM) | PPM | KTU Exam Hook |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Variable parameter** | Pulse width $t_d$ | Pulse position $t_n$ | Always ask: "what changes?" |
| **Constant parameters** | Amplitude, position | Amplitude, width | 2 out of 3 are fixed |
| **Information-bearing quantity at Rx** | Duration $\rightarrow$ Voltage | Time-shift $\rightarrow$ Voltage | Demodulation logic |
| **Average power** | Depends on $m(t)$ — *non-constant* | **Constant** (independent of $m(t)$) | Most repeated Q |
| **Bandwidth requirement** | Moderate (depends on max $t_d$) | **High** (narrow pulses) | KTU often asks "which is wider?" |
| **Noise immunity** | Better than PAM, less than PPM | **Best among PTM** | 2 marks, direct |
| **Synchronization needed at Rx** | **No** (absolute width carries info) | **Yes** (must know reference clock) | Critical: PPM needs sync |
| **Generation complexity** | Simple (1 comparator) | Complex (PWM + differentiator + clipper) | Often asked as 3-mark Q |
| **Signal-dependent power wastage** | Yes (idle power when $m=0$ → width $t_0$) | **No** (all pulses identical) | Power-efficiency point |
| **Application example** | Class-D amplifiers, motor drives | IR remotes, IRIG telemetry, space links | Real-world hook |

### 3.5 Worked Numerical (KTU 2024 Typical)

**Problem:** A message signal $m(t) = 4 \cos(200\pi t)$ volts is used to generate a PWM signal. The unmodulated pulse width is $t_0 = 0.1$ ms and the modulation constant is $k_w = 25$ µs/V. Compute the **maximum** and **minimum** pulse widths and the **modulation index**.

**Solution:**

Peak amplitude: $A_m = 4$ V, Modulation constant: $k_w = 25 \times 10^{-6}$ s/V.

Maximum pulse width occurs when $\cos(\omega_m nT_s) = +1$:

$$t_{d,\max} = t_0 + k_w A_m = 0.1 \times 10^{-3} + (25 \times 10^{-6})(4)$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{d,\max} &= 100 \times 10^{-6} + 100 \times 10^{-6} \\
           &= 200 \; \mu s = 0.2 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

Minimum pulse width occurs when $\cos(\omega_m nT_s) = -1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{d,\min} &= t_0 - k_w A_m \\
           &= 100 \times 10^{-6} - 100 \times 10^{-6} \\
           &= 0 \text{ s (boundary case)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Modulation index:

$$m_f = \frac{k_w A_m}{t_0} = \frac{100 \; \mu s}{100 \; \mu s} = 1$$

> [!NOTE]
> Since $m_f = 1$, this is **100% modulation** — the most efficient PWM with no idle power. The minimum pulse width is at the theoretical limit of zero.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 PWM Generation Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Message Signal m of t] --> nodeB[Sampler Switch at f s]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Sampled Values m at nT s]
    nodeR[Ramp Generator Sawtooth] --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD[Voltage Comparator]
    nodeD --> nodeE[PWM Output Waveform]
    nodeF[Clock Oscillator f s] --> nodeR
    nodeF --> nodeB
```

**How to read the flow:** The **clock oscillator** drives both the **sampler** (which slices the analog message) and the **ramp generator** (which produces the linear sawtooth). The **comparator** continuously compares the ramp against the held sample, emitting HIGH while ramp < sample. The trailing edge of the HIGH level is therefore the PWM output.

### 4.2 PPM Generation Block Diagram (Derived from PWM)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Message Signal m of t] --> nodeB[PWM Generator]
    nodeB --> nodeC[PWM Waveform]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Differentiator C d dt]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Positive and Negative Spikes]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Half Wave Clipper at Zero]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Final PPM Output]
```

**How to read the flow:** A PWM signal is differentiated to get positive and negative spikes at the leading and trailing edges. The half-wave clipper removes one polarity (e.g., the leading-edge spike), leaving only the trailing-edge spikes. Since the trailing edge was already position-modulated by the message, the surviving narrow pulses **are the PPM signal**.

### 4.3 PWM Demodulation Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Received PWM] --> nodeB[Leading Edge Sync]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Pulse Width to Amplitude Converter]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Variable Amplitude Pulses]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Low Pass Filter LPF]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Recovered Message m hat of t]
    nodeB --> nodeC
```

**How to read the flow:** The leading edge of every incoming PWM pulse triggers a constant-slope ramp. The ramp is sampled at the trailing edge — the longer the pulse, the higher the ramp value. The series of variable-amplitude samples is then smoothed by an LPF to recover the original message.

### 4.4 PPM Demodulation Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Received PPM] --> nodeB[Reference Clock Recovery PLL]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Local Reference at nT s]
    nodeA --> nodeD[Time to Voltage Converter]
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE[Time Displacement Voltage]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Low Pass Filter]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Recovered Message m hat of t]
```

**How to read the flow:** A **Phase-Locked Loop (PLL)** reconstructs the transmitter's reference clock from the average pulse arrival rate. The time difference between each received PPM pulse and its corresponding clock edge is then converted to a voltage, and an LPF recovers the message.

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual Reading of PWM and PPM Waveforms (textual representation):**
> - A PWM train looks like: `|‾‾‾‾‾‾|‾‾‾|‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾|‾‾‾‾‾‾|` — pulse **heights** all equal, pulse **widths** varying.
> - A PPM train looks like: `    |     |       |          |     |` — pulse **widths** all equal, pulse **positions** shifted left/right of the dashed reference grid.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
Define Pulse Time Modulation (PTM). List its two main types and state the parameter that varies in each.

> **Model Answer:**
> Pulse Time Modulation (PTM) is a pulse modulation scheme in which the **timing** of the pulses (rather than their amplitude) is varied in accordance with the instantaneous amplitude of the message signal. The two main types are: (i) **Pulse Width Modulation (PWM/PDM)** — the **width** of the pulse is varied, and (ii) **Pulse Position Modulation (PPM)** — the **position** of the pulse within the sampling period is varied. In both cases, the pulse **amplitude remains constant**.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
State **two advantages** and **one disadvantage** of Pulse Position Modulation over Pulse Amplitude Modulation.

> **Model Answer:**
> **Advantages:** (1) **Constant pulse amplitude** — PPM is immune to amplitude noise and threshold effects, giving a noise advantage of approximately **3 dB** over PAM. (2) **Constant transmitted power** — power efficiency is high, important in battery-operated telemetry transmitters. **Disadvantage:** PPM requires a **synchronization clock** at the receiver (it is *not* self-synchronizing), making the receiver more complex.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** With the help of a clear block diagram, explain the **generation of a PWM signal** from a message signal $m(t)$. Derive the expression for the instantaneous pulse width in terms of the message amplitude and the modulation constant. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A message signal $m(t) = 5 \cos(1000 \pi t)$ V is applied to a PWM generator with nominal (unmodulated) pulse width $t_0 = 0.4$ ms and modulation constant $k_w = 40$ µs/V. Determine: **(i)** the **modulation index** $m_f$, **(ii)** the **maximum** and **minimum** pulse widths, and **(iii)** the **minimum sampling period** $T_{s,\min}$ that avoids pulse overlap. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Refer to the **PWM Generation Block Diagram** in Section 4.1. The message $m(t)$ is sampled at rate $f_s = 1/T_s$ to produce $m(nT_s)$. A **ramp generator** (sawtooth) is also driven by the same clock. Both signals feed a **voltage comparator**, which outputs HIGH whenever the ramp amplitude is less than the held sample.

Derivation (re-stated for the answer sheet):

Let the ramp be $r(t) = \dfrac{V_r}{T_s}(t - nT_s)$. At the comparator switching instant $t = nT_s + \tau_n$ (where $\tau_n$ is the pulse width), we have $r(t) = m(nT_s)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{V_r}{T_s} \tau_n &= m(nT_s) \\
\tau_n &= \frac{T_s}{V_r} \cdot m(nT_s) = k_w \cdot m(nT_s)
\end{aligned}
$$

Adding the resting width $t_0$, the total pulse width is:

$$\boxed{\,t_d = t_0 + k_w \cdot m(nT_s)\,}$$

**(b)** Given: $A_m = 5$ V, $\omega_m = 1000\pi$ rad/s, $t_0 = 0.4$ ms, $k_w = 40$ µs/V.

**(i) Modulation index:**

$$m_f = \frac{k_w A_m}{t_0} = \frac{(40 \times 10^{-6})(5)}{0.4 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{200 \; \mu s}{400 \; \mu s} = 0.5$$

**(ii) Maximum pulse width** (at $m(t) = +A_m = +5$ V):

$$t_{d,\max} = 0.4 \; ms + (40 \; \mu s/V)(5 \; V) = 0.4 \; ms + 0.2 \; ms = 0.6 \; ms$$

**Minimum pulse width** (at $m(t) = -A_m = -5$ V):

$$t_{d,\min} = 0.4 \; ms - 0.2 \; ms = 0.2 \; ms$$

**(iii) Minimum sampling period** to prevent overlap:

$$T_{s,\min} = t_{d,\max} = 0.6 \; ms$$

Corresponding **minimum sampling frequency**: $f_{s,\min} = 1/T_{s,\min} \approx 1.667$ kHz.

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain with a block diagram how a **PPM signal is generated from a PWM signal**. Why is PPM said to have *constant transmitted power*? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **PWM and PPM** under the following heads: (i) variable parameter, (ii) bandwidth requirement, (iii) noise immunity, (iv) synchronization requirement at the receiver. State **one application** of each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** The **PPM generation block diagram** (Section 4.2) has three cascaded stages:

1. **PWM Generator** — produces a PWM waveform with variable-width pulses.
2. **Differentiator** (a high-pass filter or ideal $C \cdot d/dt$ block) — produces a **positive spike at the leading edge** and a **negative spike at the trailing edge** of every PWM pulse.
3. **Half-wave Clipper (or rectifier)** — removes one polarity of spike (e.g., the positive leading-edge spike). The **remaining negative spikes** at the trailing edges are narrow, constant-width, constant-amplitude pulses, and because the trailing edges were already shifted by the message, the surviving pulses are **position-modulated** — i.e., the desired PPM signal.

**Why constant transmitted power:** In PPM, **every pulse has the same amplitude $A$ and the same narrow width $\tau$**, regardless of the message signal. The **average power** is therefore:

$$P_{PPM} = \frac{A^2 \cdot \tau}{T_s} = \text{constant, independent of } m(t)$$

In PWM, by contrast, the pulse width varies with $m(t)$, so the average power changes with the message — wasting power during low-signal intervals.

**(b)** Comparison Table:

| Head | PWM (PDM) | PPM |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Variable parameter | Pulse **width** $t_d$ | Pulse **position** $t_n$ |
| (ii) Bandwidth | Moderate (depends on max $t_d$) | **Higher** (narrow constant-width pulses → large $\sim 1/\tau$) |
| (iii) Noise immunity | Better than PAM, less than PPM | **Best** among PTM (~3 dB better than PWM) |
| (iv) Synchronization at Rx | **Not required** (absolute width) | **Required** (PLL needed to recover reference clock) |

**Applications:**
- **PWM** → Class-D audio amplifiers, DC motor speed control, switching power supplies.
- **PPM** → IR remote controls, optical-fiber telemetry, IRIG space telemetry, body-implantable biomedical transmitters.

---

### 5.3 Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Warning — Where students lose marks on PTM questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the "+t₀" in PWM** — many students write $t_d = k_w m(t)$ and lose 2 marks. The unmodulated width $t_0$ is **mandatory**; it represents the resting pulse width at $m(t)=0$.
> 2. **Confusing PPM with PWM generation** — PPM is **derived from PWM** via differentiation and clipping, not generated independently. If asked "explain PPM generation", the examiner expects the **two-stage PWM → Differentiator → Clipper** description.
> 3. **Wrong sign convention for modulation index** — for sinusoidal $m(t) = A_m \cos(\omega_m t)$, the modulation index is $m_f = (k_w A_m)/t_0$. **Absolute value**, no negative sign. Many students write $t_0 - k_w A_m$ for $t_{d,\max}$ — that is the *minimum* width, not the maximum.
> 4. **Ignoring the $T_s$ vs $t_{d,\max}$ constraint** — every numerical must verify that the chosen $T_s$ accommodates the *worst-case* (maximum) pulse width, else pulses overlap and the modulation is corrupted.
> 5. **Calling PPM "self-synchronizing"** — it is **NOT**. PPM absolutely requires a clock reference at the receiver (PLL). This is a classic 1-mark trap question in KTU.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Pulse Time Modulation**

- ⭐ PTM encodes information in the **timing** of pulses, not the amplitude.
- ⭐ Two flavors: **PWM (width varies)** and **PPM (position varies)**. Amplitude is **always constant** in PTM.
- ⭐ PWM: $t_d = t_0 + k_w \cdot m(nT_s)$ ; Modulation index $m_f = (k_w A_m)/t_0 \leq 1$.
- ⭐ PPM: $t_n = nT_s + k_p \cdot m(nT_s)$ ; Max deviation $\Delta t_{\max} = k_p A_m$.
- ⭐ PPM is **generated from PWM** by differentiation + half-wave clipping.
- ⭐ PWM can be **demodulated without a reference clock** (use a slope-ramp-and-sample circuit).
- ⭐ PPM **requires clock recovery** (PLL) at the receiver — it is *not* self-synchronizing.
- ⭐ PPM has **constant transmitted power** (independent of $m(t)$) → high power efficiency.
- ⭐ PPM has **~3 dB noise advantage** over PWM and is preferred in noisy telemetry.
- ⭐ **Bandwidth of PPM > PWM > PAM** because PPM uses the narrowest pulses for the same sampling rate.
- ⭐ **Design rule:** $T_s \geq t_{d,\max}$ (PWM) and $T_s \geq \tau + 2\Delta t_{\max}$ (PPM) to prevent pulse overlap.
- ⭐ **Real-world PTM uses:** IR remotes, space telemetry (IRIG), biomedical implants, Class-D amplifiers, motor drives.
- ⭐ The comparator-based **PWM generator** equation is $\tau_n = (T_s/V_r) \cdot m(nT_s)$ — the modulation constant $k_w$ is engineerable via the ratio $T_s/V_r$.
- ⭐ For a sinusoidal message $m(t) = A_m \cos(\omega_m t)$: $t_{d,\max} = t_0 + k_w A_m$, $t_{d,\min} = t_0 - k_w A_m$ (must be $\geq 0$).
- ⭐ Remember the **comparative table** (Section 3.4) — KTU loves asking direct comparison questions worth 7 marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pulse Width Modulation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT634 - COMMUNICATION SYSTEM AND TELEMETRY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Pulse Width Modulation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Pulse Width Modulation (PWM)**, also termed *Pulse Duration Modulation (PDM)* or *Pulse Length Modulation (PLM)*, is a fundamental analog pulse modulation technique in which a periodic train of pulses is transmitted with a **constant amplitude (A)** and a **constant period (T_s)**, but with a **variable pulse width (τ)** that is directly proportional to the instantaneous amplitude of the continuous message signal **m(t)** sampled at the leading edge (or trailing edge, or center) of each pulse repetition interval.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim):**
> *"In Pulse Width Modulation, the samples of the message signal are used to vary the width of the individual pulses in a uniform pulse train."*

Mathematically, the instantaneous pulse width of the *k*-th pulse is expressed as:

$$\tau(t_k) = \tau_0 + k_m \cdot m(t_k)$$

where:
- $\tau_0$ is the **unmodulated (carrier) pulse width**, often chosen as $T_s/2$
- $k_m$ is the **modulator sensitivity** (seconds/volt)
- $m(t_k)$ is the sampled value of the message signal at the *k*-th sampling instant

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Water Tap Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Builder:** Imagine you are signalling to a friend across a river using a torchlight. You decide to **flash the torch at regular intervals** (this is your *pulse repetition period T_s*). To convey "how loud" you want them to hear your message, you **hold the torch ON for a longer duration** when the message is strong, and only briefly when the message is weak. The brightness (amplitude) of the torch never changes, only the **duration** of each flash changes. That is exactly what PWM does — it encodes the **amplitude information of m(t) into the time-domain width of a pulse**.

A more engineering-oriented analogy is a **dimmer switch** controlling an LED:
- The frequency of switching is fixed (constant $T_s$)
- The duty cycle (pulse width) is varied to control brightness (message amplitude)
- The peak current through the LED never changes (constant amplitude A)

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| Carrier pulse amplitude | $A$ | **Volts (V)** |
| Sampling period | $T_s$ | **Seconds (s)** |
| Sampling frequency | $f_s = 1/T_s$ | **Hertz (Hz)** — must obey $f_s \geq 2 f_m$ |
| Unmodulated pulse width | $\tau_0$ | typically $T_s/2$ |
| Modulation index | $\mu_m$ | dimensionless, **$0 \leq \mu_m \leq 1$** |
| Modulator sensitivity | $k_m$ | **seconds/volt** |

## 1.4 Classification of PWM Variants

KTU 2024 syllabus requires familiarity with **three** edge-modulation schemes based on which edge of the pulse is held fixed:

1. **Leading-Edge Modulation (LEM-PWM):** The leading edge is fixed at $t = kT_s$; the trailing edge is shifted. Width: $\tau(t_k) = \tau_0 + k_m m(t_k)$.
2. **Trailing-Edge Modulation (TEM-PWM):** The trailing edge is fixed at $t = (k+1)T_s$; the leading edge is advanced.
3. **Double-Edge Modulation (DEM-PWM) / Symmetric PWM:** Both edges are modulated symmetrically about $t = (k+0.5)T_s$. This is the most commonly analyzed variant in textbooks.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — The "How" and "Why"

The PWM system functions in two conceptual stages:

**Stage 1 — Sampling with Pulse-Width Encoding:**
- The continuous-time message signal $m(t)$ is sampled at instants $t = kT_s$.
- The sample $m(t_k)$ is "stretched" into a rectangular pulse of width $\tau(t_k) \propto m(t_k)$.
- Mathematically: $\tau(t_k) = \tau_0 + k_m m(t_k)$, ensuring $\tau(t_k) \geq 0$ for all $k$.

**Stage 2 — Demodulation at the Receiver:**
- The received PWM signal is passed through a **low-pass filter** (LPF) whose cutoff is set to $f_m$ (message bandwidth).
- The LPF computes the **time-averaged value** of the pulse train over each period. Since the average is proportional to $\tau(t_k)$, and $\tau(t_k) \propto m(t_k)$, the output recovers $m(t)$.

> [!TIP]
> **The "Why" Insight:** PWM is **inherently more immune to channel noise than PAM** because the information is encoded in *time* (pulse width) rather than in *amplitude*. A noise spike that slightly perturbs the pulse amplitude does not destroy the time-width information, provided the noise does not shift the pulse edges by more than $\Delta \tau$.

## 2.2 Modulation Index

The **modulation index** $\mu_m$ is defined as the ratio of the maximum deviation of the pulse width to the unmodulated width:

$$\mu_m = \frac{\Delta \tau_{max}}{\tau_0} = \frac{k_m \cdot \max \vert m(t) \vert}{\tau_0}$$

For a sinusoidal message $m(t) = V_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$:

$$\tau(t_k) = \tau_0 + k_m V_m \cos(2\pi f_m kT_s) = \tau_0 \, \left[1 + \mu_m \cos(2\pi f_m kT_s)\right]$$

> [!WARNING]
> To prevent **overmodulation** (pulse width becoming negative or exceeding the period), the constraint $\mu_m \leq 1$ must be strictly enforced. KTU examiners frequently test this boundary condition.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | KTU Frequency |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :---: |
| 1 | $\tau(t_k) = \tau_0 + k_m m(t_k)$ | Instantaneous pulse width | ★★★★★ |
| 2 | $\mu_m = k_m \cdot \max \vert m(t) \vert \,/\, \tau_0$ | Modulation index | ★★★★★ |
| 3 | $\text{Duty Cycle} = \tau_0 / T_s$ | Carrier duty ratio (unmodulated) | ★★★★ |
| 4 | $B_{PWM} \approx 1/\tau_0$ | Approx. transmission bandwidth | ★★★★ |
| 5 | $P_{sig} = (A^2 k_m^2 \overline{m^2(t)}) / T_s^2$ | Signal power at demodulator output | ★★★★★ |
| 6 | $\left(\dfrac{S}{N}\right)_{PWM} = \dfrac{k_m^2 P_m \tau_0^2}{2 T_s^2 N_0 B}$ | Output SNR for PWM system | ★★★★★ |
| 7 | $\left(\dfrac{S}{N}\right)_{PWM} = \dfrac{\mu_m^2 P_m}{2 N_0 B}$ | Output SNR in terms of $\mu_m$ | ★★★★ |
| 8 | $P_{noise} = (A T_s/\tau_0)^2 N_0 B / 2$ | Output noise power | ★★★★ |
| 9 | $s_{PWM}(t) = (A \tau_0 / T_s) + (2A k_m / T_s) m(t) - \text{harmonics}$ | Natural-sampled PWM time series | ★★★★★ |
| 10 | $T_s \leq 1/(2 f_m)$ | Nyquist sampling constraint | ★★★ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Notation Note:** In the table above, $P_m = \overline{m^2(t)}$ denotes the average power of the message signal, $N_0$ is the **single-sided** noise power spectral density (watts/Hz), and $B$ is the low-pass filter bandwidth set to the message bandwidth $f_m$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

PWM is the workhorse of modern telemetry and embedded control:

- **DC Motor Speed Control:** Industrial servomotors and drone ESCs (Electronic Speed Controllers) use PWM to regulate average voltage.
- **Class-D Audio Amplifiers:** High-efficiency switching amplifiers use PWM at 250 kHz–2 MHz.
- **Telemetry Remote Sensing:** Satellite downlink channels where amplitude fidelity is compromised by fading use PWM for robust time-encoded data.
- **Switched-Mode Power Supplies (SMPS):** Voltage regulation in laptops, phone chargers, and EV powertrains.
- **Biomedical Instrumentation:** Fetal heart-rate monitors and neural stimulators.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Time-Domain Expression of Natural-Sampled PWM

We construct a single rectangular pulse centered at $t = 0$ of width $\tau$ and amplitude $A$:

$$p_\tau(t) = \begin{cases} A & \text{for } -\tau/2 \leq t \leq \tau/2 \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

The PWM signal is the periodic train of such pulses, with the *k*-th pulse having width $\tau(t_k)$:

$$s_{PWM}(t) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} p_{\tau(t_k)}\!\left(t - kT_s\right)$$

**Step-by-Step Fourier Expansion:**

Using the complex exponential Fourier series, the coefficient of the $n$-th harmonic is:

$$D_n = \frac{1}{T_s} \int_{-T_s/2}^{T_s/2} s_{PWM}(t) \, e^{-j 2\pi n t / T_s} \, dt$$

Since the pulse at $t = kT_s$ contributes only over $[kT_s - \tau(t_k)/2, \; kT_s + \tau(t_k)/2]$:

$$D_n = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} A \int_{kT_s - \tau(t_k)/2}^{kT_s + \tau(t_k)/2} e^{-j 2\pi n t / T_s} \, dt$$

Evaluating the integral:

$$D_n = \frac{A}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} e^{-j 2\pi n k T_s / T_s} \cdot \left[ \frac{e^{-j 2\pi n t / T_s}}{-j 2\pi n / T_s} \right]_{kT_s - \tau(t_k)/2}^{kT_s + \tau(t_k)/2}$$

$$D_n = \frac{A}{T_s} \cdot \frac{T_s}{j 2\pi n} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} e^{-j 2\pi n k} \left[ e^{-j \pi n \tau(t_k)/T_s} - e^{j \pi n \tau(t_k)/T_s} \right]$$

$$D_n = \frac{A}{j 2\pi n} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} (-2j) \sin\!\left(\frac{\pi n \tau(t_k)}{T_s}\right)$$

**Key Sub-step — Expand the sine for small modulation index:**

For $\tau(t_k) = \tau_0 + k_m m(t_k)$ and small $k_m m(t_k) / \tau_0$:

$$\sin\!\left(\frac{\pi n \tau(t_k)}{T_s}\right) = \sin\!\left(\frac{\pi n \tau_0}{T_s} + \frac{\pi n k_m m(t_k)}{T_s}\right)$$

$$\sin(A + B) = \sin A \cos B + \cos A \sin B$$

For $B \ll 1$: $\cos B \approx 1$, $\sin B \approx B$, giving:

$$\sin\!\left(\frac{\pi n \tau(t_k)}{T_s}\right) \approx \sin\!\left(\frac{\pi n \tau_0}{T_s}\right) + \frac{\pi n k_m m(t_k)}{T_s} \cos\!\left(\frac{\pi n \tau_0}{T_s}\right)$$

**Final Reconstruction:**

$$D_n = \frac{A}{\pi n} \left[ 2 \sin\!\left(\frac{\pi n \tau_0}{T_s}\right) + \frac{2 \pi n k_m}{T_s} \cos\!\left(\frac{\pi n \tau_0}{T_s}\right) m(t_k) \right]$$

The time-domain reconstruction via $s_{PWM}(t) = \sum_n D_n e^{j 2\pi n t / T_s}$ yields the canonical KTU result:

$$\boxed{s_{PWM}(t) = \frac{A \tau_0}{T_s} + \frac{2A k_m}{T_s} \, m(t) - \frac{2A \tau_0}{T_s} \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{\sin(\pi n \tau_0 / T_s)}{\pi n \tau_0 / T_s} \cdot \text{harmonic terms}}$$

The **first term** is a DC offset, the **second term** is the desired message component, and the **summation** represents unwanted harmonic distortion centered around the sampling frequency $f_s$ and its multiples.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Output Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)

Assume the received PWM signal is corrupted by additive white Gaussian noise (AWGN) $n_w(t)$ with PSD $N_0 / 2$. At the demodulator, the noisy pulse train passes through an LPF of bandwidth $B = f_m$.

**Step 1 — Signal Power:**

The recovered baseband signal is $m_o(t) = (2A k_m / T_s) \cdot m(t)$. Hence:

$$P_{sig} = \overline{m_o^2(t)} = \left(\frac{2A k_m}{T_s}\right)^2 \overline{m^2(t)} = \frac{4A^2 k_m^2 P_m}{T_s^2}$$

For a sinusoidal message $m(t) = V_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$: $P_m = V_m^2 / 2$, so:

$$P_{sig} = \frac{2 A^2 k_m^2 V_m^2}{T_s^2}$$

**Step 2 — Noise Power:**

The LPF output noise is the integral of the noise PSD over $[-B, B]$:

$$P_n = \int_{-B}^{B} \frac{N_0}{2} \, df = N_0 B$$

**Step 3 — SNR Assembly:**

$$\left(\frac{S}{N}\right)_{PWM} = \frac{P_{sig}}{P_n} = \frac{2A^2 k_m^2 V_m^2}{T_s^2 N_0 B}$$

**Step 4 — Express in terms of $\mu_m$:**

For sinusoidal modulation: $\mu_m = k_m V_m / \tau_0 \Rightarrow k_m V_m = \mu_m \tau_0$. Substituting:

$$\left(\frac{S}{N}\right)_{PWM} = \frac{2 A^2 \mu_m^2 \tau_0^2}{T_s^2 N_0 B}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** For $\tau_0 = T_s/2$ and $\mu_m = 1$, the PWM system achieves an SNR **6 dB better** than an equivalent PAM system under matched conditions — a classic KTU exam "compare and contrast" question.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Comparison with PAM and PPM

For the same message power $P_m$, channel noise $N_0$, and bandwidth $B$:

$$\left(\frac{S}{N}\right)_{PAM} = \frac{P_m}{N_0 B}, \quad \left(\frac{S}{N}\right)_{PPM} = \frac{P_m \tau_0^2}{2 N_0 B \, \Delta t^2}$$

where $\Delta t$ is the maximum pulse-position deviation. Since $\Delta t \ll T_s$:

$$\left(\frac{S}{N}\right)_{PPM} \gg \left(\frac{S}{N}\right)_{PWM} \gg \left(\frac{S}{N}\right)_{PAM}$$

**Verdict:** PPM > PWM > PAM in terms of noise immunity. PWM sits in the **middle ground** — better than PAM, simpler than PPM to demodulate, which is why it dominates telemetry applications.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — PWM Generation (Comparator Method)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Message Signal m of t] --> B[Sampling Circuit]
    C[Clock Generator f sub s] --> B
    B --> D[Sample and Hold S H]
    D --> E[Comparator Non Inverting]
    F[Sawtooth Carrier] --> E
    E --> G[PWM Output Pulse Train]
    G --> H[Channel with AWGN]
    H --> I[Low Pass Filter LPF]
    I --> J[Reconstructed m hat of t]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — PWM Demodulation via Low-Pass Filtering

```mermaid
graph TD
    K[Received PWM plus Noise] --> L[DC Blocker Capacitor]
    L --> M[Op-Amp Integrator]
    M --> N[Active Low Pass Filter]
    N --> O[Output Buffer Amplifier]
    O --> P[Recovered Message Signal]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Comparison of PWM Variants

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph LEDGE [Leading Edge Modulation]
        LE1[Fixed Leading Edge at kTs] --> LE2[Variable Trailing Edge]
    end
    subgraph TEDGE [Trailing Edge Modulation]
        TE1[Variable Leading Edge] --> TE2[Fixed Trailing Edge at k plus 1 Ts]
    end
    subgraph DEDGE [Double Edge Symmetric]
        DE1[Leading Edge Modulated] --> DE2[Trailing Edge Modulated]
    end
    MSG[Message m of t] --> LEDGE
    MSG --> TEDGE
    MSG --> DEDGE
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Signal-Waveform Schematic (Conceptual)

```mermaid
graph TD
    W1[Waveform 1: Message m of t - Slow Sinusoid] --> X1
    W2[Waveform 2: Unmodulated Carrier Pulse Train tau sub 0 constant] --> X1
    W3[Waveform 3: Comparator Output Reference] --> X1
    W4[Waveform 4: PWM Output tau varies with m of t] --> X1
    X1[Graph: Time on X axis, Amplitude on Y axis showing all 4 waveforms stacked]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Visualization Tip:** In the actual exam, KTU expects students to **hand-draw** waveforms showing (a) message $m(t)$, (b) sawtooth carrier, (c) comparator output (PWM). A clear, labelled drawing with amplitudes, time-periods $T_s$, and pulse widths $\tau_0$, $\tau_{min}$, $\tau_{max}$ marked earns full marks.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: Define Pulse Width Modulation. State any two advantages of PWM over PAM.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
PWM is a pulse modulation technique in which the **width of each pulse** in a periodic train is varied in proportion to the instantaneous amplitude of the message signal, while the amplitude and period of the pulses remain constant.

**Two advantages over PAM (1.5 marks each):**
1. **Better noise immunity:** Information is encoded in time-domain pulse width, which is less susceptible to amplitude noise.
2. **Simpler demodulation:** Only a low-pass filter is required at the receiver (PAM requires synchronized sampling and gating).
3. **Constant transmitter power:** Easier to design efficient power amplifiers since the pulse amplitude never varies.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: With the help of a block diagram, explain the generation of PWM using a comparator.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The PWM generator consists of:
1. A **sawtooth (or ramp) carrier generator** of period $T_s$ — provides the reference waveform.
2. A **comparator** with two inputs:
   - Non-inverting: message signal $m(t)$
   - Inverting: sawtooth carrier
3. The comparator output is **HIGH** when $m(t) > $ sawtooth, and **LOW** otherwise, producing a pulse train whose width is proportional to $m(t)$.

**(1 mark for diagram, 1 mark for working, 1 mark for mathematical relationship $\tau(t_k) = \tau_0 + k_m m(t_k)$).**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Q: Derive the expression for the time-domain representation of a natural-sampled PWM signal. Compare its bandwidth and noise performance with PAM and PPM.**

#### **Part (a) — Derivation of PWM Time-Domain Expression [7 Marks]**

Consider a periodic pulse train $c(t)$ with each pulse of unit amplitude and width $\tau(t_k) = \tau_0 + k_m m(t_k)$ centred at $t = kT_s$:

$$c(t) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} p\!\left(t - kT_s; \tau(t_k)\right)$$

The PWM signal is $s_{PWM}(t) = A \cdot c(t)$.

Using complex exponential Fourier series and exploiting the fact that the time average of $c(t)$ over one period equals $\tau(t_k)/T_s$, we obtain:

$$s_{PWM}(t) = \frac{A \tau_0}{T_s} + \frac{A k_m}{T_s} m(t) + \text{harmonic distortion terms centred at } n f_s$$

**Detailed derivation steps (as in Section 3.1):**
1. Define single-pulse $p(t; \tau)$ and construct $s_{PWM}(t)$ as a sum. **[1 mark]**
2. Compute Fourier coefficient $D_n$ via integration. **[2 marks]**
3. Apply small-modulation expansion $\sin(A+B) \approx \sin A + B \cos A$. **[2 marks]**
4. Reconstruct $s_{PWM}(t)$ identifying the DC term, message term, and harmonics. **[2 marks]**

#### **Part (b) — Comparison Table with PAM and PPM [7 Marks]**

| Parameter | PAM | PWM | PPM |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Variable parameter | Amplitude | Width | Position |
| Bandwidth | $\sim 1/\tau_0$ | $\sim 1/\tau_0$ | $\sim 1/\tau_0$ |
| Noise immunity (SNR) | Lowest | Middle | Highest |
| Demodulation complexity | High (sync needed) | Low (LPF only) | Highest |
| Transmitter power | Variable | Constant | Constant |
| Information in | Amplitude | Time (width) | Time (position) |

**Justification (2 marks):** PPM encodes information in the *position* of a narrow pulse, which is the most noise-immune of the three since narrow pulses are minimally perturbed by amplitude noise.

**Concluding statement (1 mark):** The choice between PAM, PWM, and PPM in telemetry is a trade-off between **noise immunity (PPM > PWM > PAM)** and **demodulation simplicity (PAM > PWM > PPM)**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Q: Explain the different types of PWM with neat waveform diagrams. Derive the output signal-to-noise ratio of a PWM system and show that it is greater than that of PAM.**

#### **Part (a) — Types of PWM and Waveform Analysis [7 Marks]**

**Type 1: Leading-Edge Modulation (1.5 marks)**
- Leading edge fixed at $kT_s$; trailing edge moves.
- Pulse width: $\tau(t_k) = \tau_0 + k_m m(t_k)$.
- **Waveform:** Pulse starts at $kT_s$, ends at $kT_s + \tau_0 + k_m m(t_k)$.

**Type 2: Trailing-Edge Modulation (1.5 marks)**
- Trailing edge fixed at $(k+1)T_s$; leading edge moves.
- Pulse width: $\tau(t_k) = \tau_0 + k_m m(t_k)$, with leading edge at $(k+1)T_s - \tau_0 - k_m m(t_k)$.

**Type 3: Double-Edge (Symmetric) Modulation (2 marks)**
- Both edges modulated symmetrically about $t = (k + 0.5) T_s$.
- Pulse width: $\tau(t_k) = \tau_0 + k_m m(t_k)$.
- **Centred waveform:** Edges at $(k+0.5)T_s \pm (\tau_0 + k_m m(t_k))/2$.

**Waveform Sketch (2 marks):** Draw a sinusoidal $m(t)$ with three pulse trains beneath it — fixed leading edges, fixed trailing edges, and symmetric double-edge — clearly indicating $\tau_{min}$, $\tau_{max}$, and $T_s$.

#### **Part (b) — SNR Derivation and Comparison with PAM [7 Marks]**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** As derived in Section 3.2:

$$\left(\frac{S}{N}\right)_{PWM} = \frac{2A^2 k_m^2 V_m^2}{T_s^2 N_0 B}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** For PAM with the same message amplitude, the SNR is:

$$\left(\frac{S}{N}\right)_{PAM} = \frac{A^2 V_m^2 / 2}{N_0 B} = \frac{A^2 V_m^2}{2 N_0 B}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Compute the ratio:

$$\frac{(S/N)_{PWM}}{(S/N)_{PAM}} = \frac{2A^2 k_m^2 V_m^2 / (T_s^2 N_0 B)}{A^2 V_m^2 / (2 N_0 B)} = \frac{4 k_m^2}{T_s^2}$$

For typical $k_m = T_s/4$: this ratio equals $4 \cdot (T_s^2/16) / T_s^2 = 1/4$ ... actually for $k_m = T_s / (2V_m)$ (full modulation) and $\tau_0 = T_s/2$, the ratio simplifies to **0.5 (i.e., -3 dB) for amplitude-noise limited PAM, but +6 dB advantage for PWM when noise is edge-jitter dominant** — the exact factor depends on noise model.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Conclude that **PWM has superior noise immunity compared to PAM** because PWM encodes information in time (pulse width), which is not directly corrupted by additive amplitude noise, whereas PAM's amplitude information is directly corrupted.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Common Mark Loss Zones):**
> 1. **Confusing the demodulation method:** Students often write "PWM is demodulated using a synchronous detector" — this is **WRONG**. PWM uses a simple LPF/integrator. Synchronous detection is for PAM/PPM.
> 2. **Missing the DC blocking capacitor step:** Failing to mention that the DC term $A\tau_0/T_s$ must be removed by a coupling capacitor or high-pass filter at the receiver costs 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Drawing waveforms without amplitudes:** Always label $\tau_0$, $\tau_{min}$, $\tau_{max}$, and $T_s$ on the time axis. Unlabelled diagrams get **zero** waveform marks.
> 4. **Forgetting the overmodulation constraint $\mu_m \leq 1$:** Examiners explicitly test this. State it as a footnote.
> 5. **Mixing up PWM with PPM:** PWM varies *width*; PPM varies *position*. One mark deduction for each confusion.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Read 5 minutes before the exam):**

- **PWM encodes message amplitude in pulse width;** amplitude and period of the pulse train are constant.
- **Pulse width equation:** $\tau(t_k) = \tau_0 + k_m m(t_k)$.
- **Modulation index:** $\mu_m = k_m \max \vert m(t) \vert / \tau_0$, must satisfy $\mu_m \leq 1$ to avoid overmodulation.
- **Three types:** Leading-edge, Trailing-edge, and Double-edge (symmetric) modulation.
- **Generation:** Comparator (message vs. sawtooth carrier) is the most common method.
- **Demodulation:** Low-pass filter only — no synchronization needed.
- **Time-domain signal:** $s_{PWM}(t) = (A\tau_0/T_s) + (2A k_m / T_s) m(t) + \text{harmonics at } n f_s$.
- **DC term** $A\tau_0/T_s$ must be blocked at the receiver.
- **Bandwidth:** $B_{PWM} \approx 1/\tau_0$, dominated by the narrowest pulse width.
- **SNR formula:** $(S/N)_{PWM} = k_m^2 P_m \tau_0^2 / (2 T_s^2 N_0 B)$.
- **Noise immunity ranking:** PPM > PWM > PAM (with same message power and bandwidth).
- **PWM transmits constant power** regardless of message — a major advantage for efficient transmitter design.
- **Sampling theorem constraint:** $T_s \leq 1/(2 f_m)$ must be satisfied.
- **Key applications:** DC motor control, Class-D amplifiers, SMPS, telemetry, biomedical stimulators.
- **Historical note:** PWM was first patented by British engineer **A. H. Reeves** in 1937 (the same inventor of PCM).
- **Examiner's favourite trick question:** *"Why is PWM not used in long-distance telephony?"* — Answer: Because telephone channels have limited bandwidth and the harmonics of PWM spread across multiples of $f_s$, wasting spectrum. PCM is preferred.

> **End of Module 1 — Pulse Width Modulation Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principles of Pulse Code Modulation - Delta Modulation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT634 - COMMUNICATION SYSTEM AND TELEMETRY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Principles of Pulse Code Modulation - Delta Modulation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition — Pulse Code Modulation (PCM)

Pulse Code Modulation (PCM) is the process by which an **analog message signal is sampled, quantized, and encoded into a sequence of binary digital pulses** for transmission over a digital communication channel. The three sequential operations are:

1. **Sampling** — Converting the continuous-time signal $x(t)$ into a discrete-time signal $x(nT_s)$.
2. **Quantization** — Mapping each sample amplitude to one of a finite set of discrete reconstruction levels $q_k$.
3. **Encoding** — Representing each quantized level as an $n$-bit binary word (codeword).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme):** Under Module 1, students are expected to understand the *principles* of PCM and the *limitations* that motivated the development of Differential PCM (DPCM) and Delta Modulation (DM). DM is treated as the **1-bit / simplest DPCM** variant.

### 1.2 Formal Definition — Delta Modulation (DM)
