# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – June 2023 Model]**
**Define Amplitude Shift Keying (ASK). State one advantage and one disadvantage.**

**Model Answer:**
ASK is a digital modulation technique in which the amplitude of the high-frequency carrier is varied in proportion to the digital baseband signal, while the frequency and phase are held constant. The most common form is OOK (On-Off Keying), where a binary '1' is represented by the presence of the carrier and a binary '0' is represented by its absence.
- **Advantage:** Simple to generate and demodulate (envelope detector works).
- **Disadvantage:** Highly susceptible to amplitude noise; bandwidth and power-inefficient compared to PSK.

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model]**
**Compare BPSK and BFSK in terms of bandwidth and probability of error.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | BPSK | BFSK |
|---|---|---|
| Bandwidth | $2R_b$ | $\vert f_1 - f_2 \vert + 2R_b$ (larger) |
| Probability of Error | $Q\!\left(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0}\right)$ | $Q\!\left(\sqrt{E_b/N_0}\right)$ |

BPSK has a lower probability of error (better noise performance) for the same $E_b/N_0$ and requires less bandwidth than BFSK.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks – Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**

**(a) Derive the mathematical expression for a Binary Phase Shift Keying (BPSK) signal. Draw the block diagram of a BPSK transmitter and explain its working. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

*Derivation:*
Let the binary data be $b(t) \in \{0, 1\}$ with bit duration $T_b$. Convert to a bipolar signal $m(t) = 2b(t) - 1 \in \{-1, +1\}$. The BPSK signal is:
$$s(t) = m(t) \cdot A\cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

This yields:
$$s(t) = \begin{cases} +A\cos(2\pi f_c t), & b = 1 \;\;(m = +1) \\ -A\cos(2\pi f_c t), & b = 0 \;\;(m = -1) \end{cases}$$

Equivalently:
$$s(t) = \begin{cases} A\cos(2\pi f_c t), & b = 1 \\ A\cos(2\pi f_c t + \pi), & b = 0 \end{cases}$$

The phase is 0° for bit 1 and 180° for bit 0, with constant amplitude $A$ and constant frequency $f_c$.

*Block Diagram:*
Refer to the BPSK Transmitter diagram in Section 4.3.
- **Binary input** $b(t)$ feeds a **level converter** that maps 0 → -1 V and 1 → +1 V.
- The bipolar signal $m(t)$ is multiplied with the carrier $A\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ in a **balanced modulator**.
- The output $s(t)$ has constant envelope and phase-flipping property.

*Working:* Whenever the bit changes from 0 to 1 or vice versa, the balanced modulator output phase flips by 180°. The carrier is never suppressed (no zero amplitude), so all transmitted power carries information.

---

**(b) For a BPSK system operating over an AWGN channel with $E_b/N_0 = 9$ (linear), calculate the probability of bit error. Use the Q-function approximation if needed. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** State the BPSK probability of error formula:
$$P_e = Q\!\left( \sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}} \right)$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $E_b/N_0 = 9$:
$$\sqrt{\frac{2 \times 9}{1}} = \sqrt{18} = 4.243$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate the Q-function at $x = 4.243$:
Using tables: $Q(4.243) \approx 1.1 \times 10^{-5}$.

**Step 4:** Express in dB for clarity:
$10 \log_{10}(9) = 9.54$ dB.

**Step 5:** Conclude:
$$\boxed{P_e \approx 1.1 \times 10^{-5}}$$

For 1 million bits transmitted, only about 11 bits will be received in error.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a) Explain the generation and detection of Binary Frequency Shift Keying (BFSK). With a neat block diagram, describe the non-coherent detection method. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

*Generation:*
BFSK can be generated by switching the output of an oscillator between two frequencies $f_1$ and $f_2$ depending on the input bit.
- Bit '1' → oscillator generates $f_1 = f_c + \Delta f$.
- Bit '0' → oscillator generates $f_2 = f_c - \Delta f$.
- The output is a continuous-phase (or discontinuous-phase, depending on implementation) signal with constant amplitude $A$.

Alternative method: Use two separate oscillators at $f_1$ and $f_2$ and select between them using a digital switch controlled by the data.

*Non-Coherent Detection (Envelope Detection):*
Refer to the BFSK Receiver diagram in Section 4.2.
1. The received signal $r(t)$ is fed in **parallel** to two bandpass filters centered at $f_1$ and $f_2$, each with bandwidth $2/T_b$.
2. The output of each BPF is passed through an **envelope detector** (diode + RC lowpass filter).
3. The two envelope values are **sampled** at the end of each bit period $T_b$.
4. A **comparator** decides: if envelope at $f_1$ > envelope at $f_2$, output bit '1'; else output bit '0'.
5. **No local oscillator** is needed → simpler and cheaper than coherent detection, at the cost of slightly higher error probability.

---

**(b) Compare the probability of error expressions for BPSK and BFSK under coherent detection. Show that BPSK outperforms BFSK by 3 dB. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Write the two $P_e$ expressions.
$$P_{e, \text{BPSK}} = Q\!\left( \sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}} \right)$$
$$P_{e, \text{BFSK}} = Q\!\left( \sqrt{\frac{E_b}{N_0}} \right)$$

**Step 2:** For a given $P_e$, find the required $E_b/N_0$ for each scheme.
From BPSK: $E_b/N_0 = (Q^{-1}(P_e))^2 / 2$
From BFSK: $E_b/N_0 = (Q^{-1}(P_e))^2$

**Step 3:** Take the ratio.
$$\frac{(E_b/N_0)_{\text{BFSK}}}{(E_b/N_0)_{\text{BPSK}}} = \frac{(Q^{-1}(P_e))^2}{(Q^{-1}(P_e))^2 / 2} = 2$$

**Step 4:** Convert the ratio to dB.
$$10 \log_{10}(2) \approx 3 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 5:** Conclude.
BPSK requires only half the transmitted energy per bit compared to BFSK for the same error probability, which corresponds to a **3 dB power advantage**. This is because BPSK uses antipodal signaling (signals are negatives of each other), while BFSK uses orthogonal signaling.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting to square the Q-function argument:** Many students write $P_e = Q(E_b/N_0)$ instead of $P_e = Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$. The square root is **mandatory**.
> 2. **Mixing up the $P_e$ formulas between coherent and non-coherent FSK.** Non-coherent FSK uses $P_e = \frac{1}{2} e^{-E_b/2N_0}$, not the Q-function.
> 3. **Not converting unipolar to bipolar** before PSK modulation in the block diagram. The level converter block must be explicitly drawn.
> 4. **Confusing $E_b$ with $S$ (signal power).** $E_b$ has units of Joules, $S$ has units of Watts. They are related by $E_b = S \cdot T_b$.
> 5. **In block diagrams, forgetting the integrator** in the correlation receiver. Without the integrator, the receiver is just a multiplier, not a coherent detector.
> 6. **Wrong bandwidth formula for FSK:** It is NOT $2R_b$ in general; it depends on the frequency separation $\vert f_1 - f_2 \vert$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for PSK, ASK, FSK:**

- **ASK / OOK** modulates the **amplitude**; bandwidth ≈ $2R_b$; $P_e$ is poor; simple envelope detection possible.
- **BFSK** modulates the **frequency**; bandwidth is **larger** ($\vert f_1-f_2 \vert + 2R_b$); orthogonal signaling; non-coherent detection is simple.
- **BPSK** modulates the **phase**; bandwidth = $2R_b$ (same as ASK); **antipodal signaling**; best $P_e$; requires coherent detection.
- **Carrier frequency $f_c$ is constant** in ASK and PSK; only FSK varies it.
- **BPSK phase shift** is exactly **180°** ($\pi$ radians). The two BPSK signals are mirror images about the time axis.
- **BFSK signals are orthogonal** if $\int_0^{T_b} s_1(t) s_2(t) \, dt = 0$ (satisfied when the frequency separation is a multiple of $1/T_b$).
- **Average power of any sinusoidal BPSK** signal: $P_s = A^2/2$.
- **Bit energy formula:** $E_b = A^2 T_b / 2$ (for constant-envelope PSK/FSK).
- **Q-function is monotonic decreasing:** larger argument → smaller $P_e$.
- **3 dB advantage of BPSK over BFSK** is a frequently asked derivation question.
- **BPSK constellation:** two points on the I-axis at $+A$ and $-A$.
- **BFSK constellation:** two orthogonal vectors of length $A$ along I and Q axes.
- **ASK constellation:** one point at $+A$, one at origin (0,0).
- **Application mapping to remember:**
    - ASK → IR remotes, RFID, optical comms.
    - FSK → Pagers, Caller-ID, Bluetooth (GFSK).
    - PSK → Satellites, deep-space telemetry, Wi-Fi.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Waveguides
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT634 - COMMUNICATION SYSTEM AND TELEMETRY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Waveguides

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

A **waveguide** is a hollow, single-conductor metallic transmission line, typically with a rectangular or circular cross-section, used to guide and propagate electromagnetic (EM) energy from one point to another at ultra-high frequencies (UHF) and microwave frequencies (typically above **3 GHz**).

Unlike conventional two-wire transmission lines or coaxial cables, a waveguide does not use a return conductor. Instead, the EM energy is confined within the hollow metallic boundary through the mechanism of repeated total internal reflection of the electromagnetic waves from the perfectly conducting walls, combined with boundary condition enforcement.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A waveguide is a structure that guides waves, such as electromagnetic waves or sound waves, with minimal loss of energy by restricting the transmission of energy to one direction. They are the principal means of transmission of signals at microwave frequencies.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The Water Pipe vs. The Open River

Think of a transmission line (co-axial cable) as a **small garden hose carrying water**. It works perfectly for low-pressure, low-flow situations. As we increase the pressure (analogous to increasing the frequency of the signal), the friction losses (analogous to skin effect and dielectric losses) become so severe that the hose bursts or loses too much energy.

A **waveguide**, in contrast, behaves like a **large, smooth, rectangular metal pipe** (think of a giant storm drain or a tunnel). It is deliberately made very large compared to the wavelength. The EM waves bounce off the smooth metallic walls (like light bouncing inside a mirror-lined hallway) and propagate forward. Since there is no inner conductor and no dielectric loss (it is usually air-filled or vacuum), it can carry enormous amounts of microwave power with almost no loss.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Students:** Waveguides are used only when the operating wavelength becomes comparable to or smaller than the physical dimensions of practical transmission lines. At low frequencies (kHz, MHz), they would be impractically large.

## 1.3 Why Waveguides? Limitations of Transmission Lines

At microwave frequencies, conventional transmission lines suffer from several drawbacks:

1. **Skin Effect Losses:** At high frequencies, current flows only in a thin outer layer of the conductor ($\delta \approx \sqrt{2 / (\omega \mu \sigma)}$), drastically increasing resistive ($I^2R$) losses.
2. **Dielectric Losses:** The insulating material between the conductors causes significant power dissipation at high frequencies.
3. **Radiation Losses:** Two-wire lines behave like antennas at microwave frequencies, radiating energy into space instead of guiding it.
4. **Proximity Effect:** At high frequencies, the fields are tightly confined near the conductors, leading to unpredictable coupling.

The **waveguide elegantly solves** all these problems:
- It has only one conductor (the outer wall), so no inner dielectric is required.
- Radiation is impossible because the EM field is completely enclosed.
- Conduction losses are very low because the wall surface area available for current flow is huge.
- Power handling capacity is very high (useful in RADAR and satellite transmitters).

## 1.4 Classification of Waveguides

| Type | Typical Use | Key Feature |
|------|------------|-------------|
| **Rectangular Waveguide** | Most common, used in RADAR, microwave links | Cross-section is a rectangle (a × b) |
| **Circular Waveguide** | Rotary joints, feeds for parabolic dishes | Cross-section is a circle (radius r) |
| **Ridged Waveguide** | Wideband applications, lower cut-off | Has ridges that lower the cut-off frequency |
| **Flexible Waveguide** | Interconnections where rigid guides are impractical | Made of convoluted or braided metal |

## 1.5 Mode of Propagation: TEM, TE, and TM

A fundamental concept is that within a waveguide, the EM wave cannot have all six field components ($E_x, E_y, E_z, H_x, H_y, H_z$) independent as in free space, because of the conductor boundary conditions.

- **TEM (Transverse Electro-Magnetic):** Both $E$ and $H$ are entirely transverse to the direction of propagation. $E_z = 0$ and $H_z = 0$. This mode **cannot exist** in a single-conductor hollow waveguide. It requires two separate conductors.
- **TE (Transverse Electric):** Electric field is entirely transverse ($E_z = 0$), but the magnetic field has a longitudinal component ($H_z \neq 0$). Denoted as **TE$_{mn}$** (or H-modes).
- **TM (Transverse Magnetic):** Magnetic field is entirely transverse ($H_z = 0$), but the electric field has a longitudinal component ($E_z \neq 0$). Denoted as **TM$_{mn}$** (or E-modes).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For a Rectangular Waveguide:** The subscripts $m$ and $n$ in TE$_{mn}$ / TM$_{mn}$ represent the number of half-wave variations of the field along the **broader dimension 'a'** and the **narrower dimension 'b'**, respectively. The **dominant mode** of a rectangular waveguide is **TE$_{10}$** because it has the lowest cut-off frequency.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physical Mechanism of Propagation

When an EM wave is launched inside a hollow rectangular metallic tube, it does not simply shoot straight down the axis. Instead, it **zig-zags between the conducting walls**, undergoing successive reflections. 

If the wave is incident on a wall at an angle $\theta$ greater than the critical angle (which depends on frequency), it undergoes **total internal reflection** and is guided forward. If $\theta$ is below this critical angle, the wave escapes laterally and is rapidly attenuated.

This is the geometric-optical picture. The rigorous wave-theory picture uses the solution of Maxwell's equations subject to the boundary condition that the tangential electric field must vanish on the perfect conductor walls.

## 2.2 Maxwell's Equations as the Foundation

Inside the waveguide, the wave equation is derived from:

$$\nabla^2 \vec{E} = \mu \varepsilon \frac{\partial^2 \vec{E}}{\partial t^2}$$

$$\nabla^2 \vec{H} = \mu \varepsilon \frac{\partial^2 \vec{H}}{\partial t^2}$$

Using the separation of variables method, with assumed travelling wave solution $e^{j(\omega t - \beta z)}$, the resulting dispersion relation (the master equation for waveguides) is:

$$\omega^2 \mu \varepsilon = \beta_z^2 + \beta_c^2$$

where:
- $\beta_z$ is the **longitudinal phase constant** (along the axis of propagation).
- $\beta_c$ is the **transverse phase constant** (determined by the cross-sectional geometry).
- $\mu \varepsilon$ gives the free-space phase constant: $\beta_0 = \omega \sqrt{\mu \varepsilon} = \dfrac{2\pi}{\lambda}$.

## 2.3 Cut-off Frequency and Cut-off Wavelength

The single most important concept in waveguides. From the dispersion relation, for a real $\beta_z$ (propagating wave), we need:

$$\beta_z = \sqrt{\omega^2 \mu \varepsilon - \beta_c^2}$$

If $\omega^2 \mu \varepsilon < \beta_c^2$, then $\beta_z$ becomes imaginary (the wave is evanescent, i.e., it decays exponentially). The boundary between propagation and non-propagation defines the **cut-off frequency $f_c$**:

$$f_c = \frac{\beta_c}{2\pi \sqrt{\mu \varepsilon}} = \frac{\beta_c}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{c}{\omega} \cdot \omega \quad \text{(simplified)}$$

Equivalently, the **cut-off wavelength** is:

$$\lambda_c = \frac{2\pi}{\beta_c}$$

The propagation conditions are:
- $\omega > \omega_c$ (or $f > f_c$ or $\lambda < \lambda_c$) → **Propagation occurs.**
- $\omega < \omega_c$ (or $f < f_c$ or $\lambda > \lambda_c$) → **No propagation; wave is attenuated.**

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Parameter | Formula | Units / Notes |
|-----------|---------|---------------|
| Free-space wavelength | $\lambda = c / f$ | meters |
| Cut-off frequency (rect. WG) | $f_c = \dfrac{c}{2} \sqrt{(m/a)^2 + (n/b)^2}$ | Hz |
| Cut-off wavelength (rect. WG) | $\lambda_c = \dfrac{2}{\sqrt{(m/a)^2 + (n/b)^2}}$ | meters |
| Cut-off frequency (circular WG) | $f_c = \dfrac{p'_{mn} \cdot c}{2\pi r}$ | Hz (p'$_{mn}$ is root of Bessel derivative) |
| Guide wavelength | $\lambda_g = \dfrac{\lambda}{\sqrt{1 - (\lambda/\lambda_c)^2}}$ | meters |
| Phase velocity | $v_p = \dfrac{c}{\sqrt{1 - (\lambda/\lambda_c)^2}} = \dfrac{c}{\sqrt{1 - (f_c/f)^2}}$ | m/s, always $> c$ |
| Group velocity | $v_g = c \cdot \sqrt{1 - (\lambda/\lambda_c)^2} = c \cdot \sqrt{1 - (f_c/f)^2}$ | m/s, always $< c$ |
| Relation $v_p \cdot v_g$ | $v_p \cdot v_g = c^2$ | Fundamental relation |
| Wave impedance (TE mode) | $Z_{TE} = \dfrac{\eta}{\sqrt{1 - (f_c/f)^2}}$ | ohms |
| Wave impedance (TM mode) | $Z_{TM} = \eta \cdot \sqrt{1 - (f_c/f)^2}$ | ohms |
| Intrinsic impedance of free space | $\eta = 120\pi \approx 377$ | ohms |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL KTU Exam Tip:** The relationship $v_p \cdot v_g = c^2$ is the most frequently tested identity. Although $v_p > c$ (theoretically), information/signals actually travel at $v_g$, so relativity is not violated. The product is always $c^2$.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility

The waveguide is the **workhorse of microwave engineering**. Its applications include:

1. **Radar Systems:** Feeding the parabolic dish antenna with high-power microwave pulses.
2. **Satellite Communication:** Connecting the high-power transmitter to the antenna feed horn.
3. **Microwave Ovens:** The magnetron output is routed to the cooking cavity via a waveguide.
4. **Particle Accelerators:** Guiding RF energy to accelerate charged particles.
5. **Medical Diathermy:** Delivering concentrated microwave energy for tissue heating.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield:** The **dominant mode** for a rectangular waveguide is always **TE$_{10}$**, where $m=1, n=0$. This is because $a > b$ by convention, and the cut-off frequency for TE$_{10}$ is $f_c = c / (2a)$, which is the lowest among all modes. Operating in the dominant mode ensures single-mode propagation and minimizes dispersion.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Cut-off Frequency for Rectangular Waveguide (TE$_{mn}$ Mode)

### Step 1: Set up the wave equation inside the guide

For a TE mode, we have $E_z = 0$, and the longitudinal magnetic field $H_z$ satisfies the scalar Helmholtz wave equation:

$$\nabla^2 H_z = \mu \varepsilon \frac{\partial^2 H_z}{\partial t^2}$$

### Step 2: Apply separation of variables

Assume a solution of the form:

$$H_z(x, y, z, t) = H_0(x, y) e^{j(\omega t - \beta_z z)}$$

Substituting into the wave equation gives:

$$\frac{\partial^2 H_0}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 H_0}{\partial y^2} + \beta_c^2 H_0 = 0$$

where $\beta_c^2 = \omega^2 \mu \varepsilon - \beta_z^2$.

### Step 3: Solve the transverse equation

Assume $H_0(x, y) = X(x) \cdot Y(y)$. Substituting and dividing by $XY$:

$$\frac{1}{X} \frac{d^2 X}{dx^2} + \frac{1}{Y} \frac{d^2 Y}{dy^2} + \beta_c^2 = 0$$

By separation, let each term equal a constant:

$$\frac{d^2 X}{dx^2} + k_x^2 X = 0, \quad \frac{d^2 Y}{dy^2} + k_y^2 Y = 0$$

with $k_x^2 + k_y^2 = \beta_c^2$.

### Step 4: Apply boundary conditions

On the perfectly conducting walls at $x = 0, a$ and $y = 0, b$, the normal derivative of $H_z$ must vanish (Neumann condition):

$$\frac{\partial H_z}{\partial x} = 0 \text{ at } x=0, a; \quad \frac{\partial H_z}{\partial y} = 0 \text{ at } y=0, b$$

The solution that satisfies these conditions is:

$$H_0(x, y) = H_0 \cos\left(\frac{m\pi x}{a}\right) \cos\left(\frac{n\pi y}{b}\right)$$

where $m$ and $n$ are non-negative integers. Comparing with the assumed form, we get:

$$k_x = \frac{m\pi}{a}, \quad k_y = \frac{n\pi}{b}$$

### Step 5: Form the cut-off phase constant

$$\beta_c = \sqrt{k_x^2 + k_y^2} = \pi \sqrt{\left(\frac{m}{a}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{n}{b}\right)^2}$$

### Step 6: Convert to cut-off frequency

Using $f_c = \dfrac{\beta_c}{2\pi \sqrt{\mu \varepsilon}}$ and $c = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\mu \varepsilon}}$:

$$f_c = \frac{c}{2} \sqrt{\left(\frac{m}{a}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{n}{b}\right)^2}$$

And the cut-off wavelength:

$$\lambda_c = \frac{c}{f_c} = \frac{2}{\sqrt{\left(\frac{m}{a}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{n}{b}\right)^2}}$$

This is the **master formula for rectangular waveguides** and is the basis of nearly all KTU exam numericals.

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification of Dominant Mode:** For TE$_{10}$, $m=1, n=0$, so $f_c = c / (2a)$. This is the lowest $f_c$ since $a > b$ conventionally. This single-mode regime extends from $c/2a$ to $c/a$ (the next mode TE$_{20}$ cut-off), giving a **usable bandwidth of approximately 1:2** for single-mode operation.

## 3.2 Derivation: Guide Wavelength, Phase and Group Velocity

Starting from the dispersion relation:

$$\beta_z^2 = \omega^2 \mu \varepsilon - \beta_c^2$$

Dividing both sides by $\omega^2 \mu \varepsilon$:

$$\frac{\beta_z^2}{\omega^2 \mu \varepsilon} = 1 - \frac{\beta_c^2}{\omega^2 \mu \varepsilon} = 1 - \left(\frac{f_c}{f}\right)^2$$

### Step 3.2.1: Guide Wavelength

The guide wavelength is the distance between two successive equiphase planes along the axis:

$$\lambda_g = \frac{2\pi}{\beta_z} = \frac{2\pi / (\omega \sqrt{\mu\varepsilon})}{\sqrt{1 - (f_c/f)^2}} = \frac{\lambda}{\sqrt{1 - (f_c/f)^2}}$$

### Step 3.2.2: Phase Velocity

Phase velocity is the speed of a constant-phase point on the wave:

$$v_p = \frac{\omega}{\beta_z} = \frac{c}{\sqrt{1 - (f_c/f)^2}}$$

### Step 3.2.3: Group Velocity

Group velocity is the speed of energy/information propagation. Differentiating $\omega$ with respect to $\beta_z$:

$$v_g = \frac{d\omega}{d\beta_z} = c \cdot \sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{f_c}{f}\right)^2}$$

### Step 3.2.4: The Fundamental Identity

Multiplying $v_p$ and $v_g$:

$$v_p \cdot v_g = \frac{c^2}{1 - (f_c/f)^2} \cdot \left[1 - (f_c/f)^2\right] = c^2$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This identity $v_p \cdot v_g = c^2$ is the most elegant result of waveguide theory.** It says the geometric mean of phase and group velocities equals the speed of light. It is a guaranteed question in KTU exams.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Style)

**Problem:** A rectangular air-filled waveguide has dimensions $a = 2.286$ cm, $b = 1.016$ cm. Find the cut-off frequency, guide wavelength, phase velocity, and group velocity for the dominant mode at an operating frequency of 10 GHz.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Dominant mode is TE$_{10}$, so $m=1, n=0$.

$$f_c = \frac{c}{2a} = \frac{3 \times 10^{10}}{2 \times 2.286} = 6.557 \text{ GHz}$$

**Step 2:** Operating frequency $f = 10$ GHz, so $f_c / f = 0.6557$.

**Step 3:** Free-space wavelength:

$$\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^{10}}{10 \times 10^9} = 3 \text{ cm}$$

**Step 4:** Guide wavelength:

$$\lambda_g = \frac{\lambda}{\sqrt{1 - (f_c/f)^2}} = \frac{3}{\sqrt{1 - 0.4299}} = \frac{3}{\sqrt{0.5701}} = \frac{3}{0.7551} = 3.973 \text{ cm}$$

**Step 5:** Phase velocity:

$$v_p = \frac{c}{\sqrt{1 - (f_c/f)^2}} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{0.7551} = 3.973 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 6:** Group velocity:

$$v_g = c \cdot \sqrt{1 - (f_c/f)^2} = 3 \times 10^8 \times 0.7551 = 2.265 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$$

**Verification:** $v_p \cdot v_g = 3.973 \times 10^8 \times 2.265 \times 10^8 = 9.0 \times 10^{16} = c^2$. ✓

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Waveguide Communication Link

The diagram below shows the role of the waveguide inside a complete microwave communication chain — from source to destination. This is the block-level functional view a KTU examiner expects when asking "explain the waveguide as part of a system."

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src[Microwave Source]:::srcNode --> isol[Isolator]:::passNode
    isol --> freq[Frequency Counter]:::passNode
    freq --> att[Variable Attenuator]:::passNode
    att --> dir[Directional Coupler]:::passNode
    dir --> wg1[Waveguide Section TE10]:::wgNode
    wg1 --> bend[E Plane or H Plane Bend]:::passNode
    bend --> wg2[Waveguide Twist Section]:::passNode
    wg2 --> flange[Flange Joint Connector]:::passNode
    flange --> load[Matched Load or Antenna Feed]:::endNode

    classDef srcNode fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef passNode fill:#E0E0E0,stroke:#555,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef wgNode fill:#1E90FF,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef endNode fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Flowchart: Decision Logic for Mode Propagation

This flowchart captures the rule students must apply in every numerical: "Will the wave propagate or be attenuated?"

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Operating Frequency f and Cutoff Frequency fc]:::ioNode
    cond1{f greater than fc}:::decNode
    branch1[Wave PROPAGATES inside waveguide]:::okNode
    cond2{f equals fc}:::decNode
    branch2[Wave is at THRESHOLD, no net propagation]:::warnNode
    cond3{f less than fc}:::decNode
    branch3[Wave is EVANESCENT decays exponentially]:::errNode
    next1[Calculate lambda g, v p, v g]:::calcNode
    next2[Boundary case check for design]:::calcNode
    next3[Signal blocked, no transmission]:::calcNode
    endA[Answer in propagating regime]:::endNode
    endB[Critical design boundary]:::endNode
    endC[Stopband region]:::endNode

    start --> cond1
    start --> cond2
    start --> cond3
    cond1 --> branch1
    cond1 --> next1
    next1 --> endA
    cond2 --> branch2
    cond2 --> next2
    next2 --> endB
    cond3 --> branch3
    cond3 --> next3
    next3 --> endC

    classDef ioNode fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef decNode fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef okNode fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef warnNode fill:#FFFFE0,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef errNode fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef calcNode fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#483D8B,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef endNode fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#008B8B,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Diagram: TE$_{10}$ Mode Field Pattern

Since we cannot physically draw E-field arrows, we use a Mermaid block to represent the field configuration of the dominant mode in the cross-section of a rectangular waveguide.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph crossSec[Cross Section of Rectangular Waveguide Inner Dimensions a by b]
        direction LR
        top[TOP WALL y equals b]:::wallNode
        bot[BOTTOM WALL y equals 0]:::wallNode
        left[LEFT WALL x equals 0]:::wallNode
        right[RIGHT WALL x equals a]:::wallNode
        eField[Electric Field E peaks in middle of broad wall vertical direction]:::fieldNode
        hField[Magnetic Field H forms closed loops horizontal near walls]:::fieldNode
    end

    classDef wallNode fill:#A9A9A9,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef fieldNode fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#A0522D,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** In the TE$_{10}$ dominant mode, the electric field $E_y$ has **one half-sinusoidal variation** along the broad dimension $a$ and is **uniform** along the narrow dimension $b$. The magnetic field forms closed loops in the cross-section. This is why the subscript is "10" — one variation in $a$, zero in $b$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**Define a waveguide. Why is it preferred over a two-wire transmission line at microwave frequencies?**

**Model Answer:**

A waveguide is a single-conductor hollow metallic tube, usually rectangular or circular in cross-section, used to transmit electromagnetic energy at microwave frequencies.

It is preferred over two-wire transmission lines at microwave frequencies because:

1. **Lower Losses:** Skin effect and dielectric losses in two-wire lines become severe at microwave frequencies, whereas waveguides have minimal losses due to large conducting surface area and air-filled interior.
2. **No Radiation Losses:** Two-wire lines radiate energy like antennas at high frequencies, while waveguides completely shield the field.
3. **Higher Power Handling:** Waveguides can handle kilowatts of power, while coaxial cables can only handle a few hundred watts at these frequencies.
4. **Mechanical Simplicity:** Single conductor construction is robust and simple.
5. **TEM mode limitation:** Two-wire lines support TEM mode, which has no cut-off, but is not possible in single-conductor hollow waveguides — the TE/TM modes are inherently suitable for microwave propagation.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often say "waveguides have no losses" — this is wrong. Waveguides have *much lower* losses, not zero. Always use comparative language.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**What is meant by dominant mode in a rectangular waveguide? Why is TE$_{10}$ mode preferred?**

**Model Answer:**

The **dominant mode** of a waveguide is the mode that has the **lowest cut-off frequency** and therefore propagates over the widest possible frequency range.

For a rectangular waveguide with $a > b$, the **TE$_{10}$ mode** is the dominant mode because:

1. Its cut-off frequency is $f_c = c / (2a)$, which is the lowest possible value among all TE$_{mn}$ and TM$_{mn}$ modes.
2. The next higher mode is **TE$_{20}$** with $f_c = c / a$. Thus, single-mode operation is possible in the frequency band $c/(2a) < f < c/a$, giving a usable bandwidth of approximately **1:2 (one octave)**.
3. The TE$_{10}$ mode provides the **largest operating bandwidth** for single-mode transmission.
4. It has a **simple, well-defined field structure** that is easy to excite and couple.
5. It offers **minimum attenuation** and **maximum power handling** compared to higher-order modes.

---

## Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the cut-off frequency of a TE$_{mn}$ mode in a rectangular waveguide of inner dimensions $a \times b$. Hence, calculate the cut-off frequency for the dominant mode of a waveguide with $a = 4$ cm and $b = 2$ cm. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For an air-filled rectangular waveguide of dimensions $a = 4$ cm, $b = 2$ cm, operating at 6 GHz in the dominant mode, calculate:
   (i) Cut-off frequency and cut-off wavelength
   (ii) Guide wavelength
   (iii) Phase velocity and group velocity
   (iv) Wave impedance for TE mode **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Cut-off Frequency Derivation (7 Marks)**

**Step 1:** For a TE mode, the longitudinal component of magnetic field $H_z$ satisfies the scalar Helmholtz equation inside the waveguide:

$$\nabla^2 H_z = \mu \varepsilon \frac{\partial^2 H_z}{\partial t^2}$$

**Step 2:** Assume a travelling wave solution of the form $H_z(x, y, z, t) = H_0(x, y) e^{j(\omega t - \beta_z z)}$.

**Step 3:** Substituting and simplifying yields the transverse equation:

$$\frac{\partial^2 H_0}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 H_0}{\partial y^2} + \beta_c^2 H_0 = 0$$

where $\beta_c^2 = \omega^2 \mu \varepsilon - \beta_z^2$.

**Step 4:** Using separation of variables $H_0(x, y) = X(x) Y(y)$:

$$\frac{1}{X} \frac{d^2 X}{dx^2} = -k_x^2, \quad \frac{1}{Y} \frac{d^2 Y}{dy^2} = -k_y^2$$

with $k_x^2 + k_y^2 = \beta_c^2$.

**Step 5:** Solutions are of the form $\cos(k_x x) \cos(k_y y)$. Applying the **Neumann boundary condition** $\partial H_z / \partial n = 0$ on the conducting walls at $x = 0, a$ and $y = 0, b$ gives:

$$k_x = \frac{m\pi}{a}, \quad k_y = \frac{n\pi}{b}$$

**Step 6:** Therefore:

$$\beta_c = \pi \sqrt{\left(\frac{m}{a}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{n}{b}\right)^2}$$

**Step 7:** Using $f_c = \beta_c / (2\pi \sqrt{\mu \varepsilon}) = \beta_c c / (2\pi)$:

$$\boxed{f_c = \frac{c}{2} \sqrt{\left(\frac{m}{a}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{n}{b}\right)^2}}$$

**Step 8:** Calculation for $a = 4$ cm, $b = 2$ cm, dominant TE$_{10}$ ($m=1, n=0$):

$$f_c = \frac{3 \times 10^{10}}{2 \times 4} = 3.75 \text{ GHz}$$

---

**Part (b) — Calculations at $f = 6$ GHz (7 Marks)**

**Step 1: Cut-off frequency and wavelength** (already computed)

$$f_c = 3.75 \text{ GHz}, \quad \lambda_c = \frac{c}{f_c} = \frac{3 \times 10^{10}}{3.75 \times 10^9} = 8 \text{ cm}$$

**Step 2: Free-space wavelength**

$$\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^{10}}{6 \times 10^9} = 5 \text{ cm}$$

**Step 3: Guide wavelength**

$$\lambda_g = \frac{\lambda}{\sqrt{1 - (\lambda/\lambda_c)^2}} = \frac{5}{\sqrt{1 - (5/8)^2}} = \frac{5}{\sqrt{1 - 0.3906}} = \frac{5}{\sqrt{0.6094}} = \frac{5}{0.7807} = 6.405 \text{ cm}$$

**Step 4: Phase velocity**

$$v_p = \frac{c}{\sqrt{1 - (f_c/f)^2}} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{\sqrt{1 - (3.75/6)^2}} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{\sqrt{1 - 0.3906}} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{0.7807} = 3.843 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 5: Group velocity**

$$v_g = c \cdot \sqrt{1 - (f_c/f)^2} = 3 \times 10^8 \times 0.7807 = 2.342 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 6: Wave impedance for TE mode**

$$Z_{TE} = \frac{\eta}{\sqrt{1 - (f_c/f)^2}} = \frac{377}{0.7807} = 483 \text{ }\Omega$$

**Verification:** $v_p \cdot v_g = 3.843 \times 10^8 \times 2.342 \times 10^8 = 9.0 \times 10^{16} = c^2$ ✓

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the different modes of propagation (TEM, TE, TM) in a waveguide. Why does the TEM mode not exist in a hollow rectangular waveguide? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An air-filled circular waveguide has a radius of 2 cm. Determine:
   (i) The lowest cut-off frequency
   (ii) The next two higher cut-off frequencies
   (iii) The dominant mode and its operating frequency range
   
   [Given: The first few roots of the Bessel function derivative $J'_1(x)$ are $p'_{11} = 1.841$, $p'_{21} = 5.331$, $p'_{01} = 3.832$. The first few roots of $J_n(x)$ are $p_{01} = 2.405$, $p_{11} = 3.832$.] **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Modes of Propagation (7 Marks)**

**Step 1:** In any transmission line, EM waves can propagate in different field configurations called **modes**. They are classified based on the orientation of the E and H fields relative to the direction of propagation.

**Step 2:** **TEM (Transverse Electro-Magnetic) Mode:** Both E and H are perpendicular to the direction of propagation. Mathematically, $E_z = 0$ and $H_z = 0$. This mode requires **two separate conductors** to support a potential difference and a return current. Example: Two-wire line, coaxial cable.

**Step 3:** **TE (Transverse Electric) Mode:** Electric field is entirely transverse ($E_z = 0$), but the magnetic field has a longitudinal component ($H_z \neq 0$). This mode is supported by **single-conductor hollow waveguides** and is also called the **H-mode**. Denoted TE$_{mn}$ for rectangular or TE$_{nm}$ for circular.

**Step 4:** **TM (Transverse Magnetic) Mode:** Magnetic field is entirely transverse ($H_z = 0$), but the electric field has a longitudinal component ($E_z \neq 0$). Also called the **E-mode**.

**Step 5:** **Why TEM does not exist in a hollow single-conductor waveguide:**
   - TEM mode requires $H_z = 0$. From Maxwell's equation $\nabla \times \vec{H} = \vec{J} + \partial \vec{D}/\partial t$, if $H_z = 0$ and the only conductor is the wall, there is **no return path** for displacement current to close the loop.
   - Mathematically, if $H_z = 0$ in a single-conductor region, then the divergence of $\vec{H}$ is automatically satisfied, but the curl equation for $\vec{E}$ requires that $\nabla \times \vec{E} = 0$ in the static limit — which combined with $\nabla \cdot \vec{E} = 0$ yields only a trivial solution.
   - Therefore, **a single conductor cannot sustain TEM mode**; it must be a multi-conductor structure.

---

**Part (b) — Circular Waveguide Calculations (7 Marks)**

**Step 1:** For a circular waveguide of radius $r$, the cut-off frequencies are:

$$f_c = \frac{p'_{mn} \cdot c}{2\pi r} \quad \text{(for TE}_{mn} \text{ modes)}$$

$$f_c = \frac{p_{mn} \cdot c}{2\pi r} \quad \text{(for TM}_{mn} \text{ modes)}$$

**Step 2:** With $r = 2$ cm $= 0.02$ m and $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s:

$$\frac{c}{2\pi r} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2\pi \times 0.02} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{0.1257} = 2.387 \text{ GHz}$$

**Step 3:** **Lowest cut-off frequency** — for TE$_{11}$, $p'_{11} = 1.841$:

$$f_{c,TE_{11}} = 1.841 \times 2.387 = 4.395 \text{ GHz}$$

This is the **dominant mode** of the circular waveguide.

**Step 4:** **Next two higher cut-off frequencies:**

For TM$_{01}$, $p_{01} = 2.405$:

$$f_{c,TM_{01}} = 2.405 \times 2.387 = 5.741 \text{ GHz}$$

For TE$_{21}$, $p'_{21} = 5.331$:

$$f_{c,TE_{21}} = 5.331 \times 2.387 = 12.725 \text{ GHz}$$

**Step 5:** **Dominant Mode and Operating Range:**

The dominant mode of the circular waveguide is **TE$_{11}$** with $f_c = 4.395$ GHz. The next higher mode is **TM$_{01}$** at 5.741 GHz. Hence the single-mode operating band is:

$$4.395 \text{ GHz} < f < 5.741 \text{ GHz}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mistakes on Waveguide Problems:**
> 1. **Confusing cut-off wavelength with free-space wavelength:** $\lambda_c$ depends only on dimensions, while $\lambda$ depends on operating frequency.
> 2. **Forgetting the dominant mode changes for circular waveguide:** For rectangular, dominant is TE$_{10}$; for circular, dominant is TE$_{11}$.
> 3. **Mixing up $v_p > c$ with information traveling faster than light:** $v_p$ is the speed of constant phase, not energy. Energy travels at $v_g < c$.
> 4. **Wrong mode subscripts:** Always remember $m$ refers to the broader dimension $a$ in rectangular waveguides.
> 5. **Unit errors:** $a$ and $b$ must be in **meters** when used in the $f_c$ formula with $c$ in m/s. Don't mix cm and GHz.
> 6. **Skipping the $v_p \cdot v_g = c^2$ verification step:** Examiners give bonus marks for this sanity check.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- **Waveguide:** Hollow single-conductor metallic tube used for EM wave transmission at microwave frequencies (above 3 GHz).
- **Why used:** Low loss, high power handling, no radiation loss, simple construction at high frequencies.
- **Types:** Rectangular (most common), Circular, Ridged, Flexible.
- **TEM mode** → requires two conductors; **does not exist** in hollow waveguides.
- **TE mode** → $E_z = 0$, $H_z \neq 0$, supported by hollow waveguides.
- **TM mode** → $H_z = 0$, $E_z \neq 0$, supported by hollow waveguides.
- **Rectangular WG dominant mode:** **TE$_{10}$** with $f_c = c/(2a)$.
- **Circular WG dominant mode:** **TE$_{11}$** with $f_c = 1.841 c / (2\pi r)$.
- **Cut-off frequency formula (Rectangular):** $f_c = \dfrac{c}{2} \sqrt{(m/a)^2 + (n/b)^2}$.
- **Propagation condition:** $f > f_c$ (or $\lambda < \lambda_c$).
- **Guide wavelength:** $\lambda_g = \dfrac{\lambda}{\sqrt{1 - (f_c/f)^2}}$ — **always greater than** $\lambda$.
- **Phase velocity:** $v_p = \dfrac{c}{\sqrt{1 - (f_c/f)^2}}$ — **always greater than** $c$.
- **Group velocity:** $v_g = c \sqrt{1 - (f_c/f)^2}$ — **always less than** $c$.
- **Magic identity:** $v_p \cdot v_g = c^2$ — must appear in every KTU numerical solution.
- **TE wave impedance:** $Z_{TE} = \dfrac{\eta}{\sqrt{1 - (f_c/f)^2}}$ — greater than $377$ Ω.
- **TM wave impedance:** $Z_{TM} = \eta \sqrt{1 - (f_c/f)^2}$ — less than $377$ Ω.
- **Intrinsic impedance of free space:** $\eta = 120\pi \approx 377$ Ω.
- **Single-mode bandwidth of rectangular WG:** approximately 1:2 (one octave) between $c/(2a)$ and $c/a$.
- **Excitation & detection:** Use probes, loops, or aperture coupling — never direct galvanic contact.
- **Wall current continuity:** Waveguide joints use **choke flanges** to maintain electrical continuity without DC contact.
- **Key limitation:** Waveguides are bulky and expensive; below 3 GHz, dimensions become impractically large.
- **Comparison with coaxial cable:** Coax supports TEM and has no cut-off; waveguide requires $f > f_c$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Waveguides
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT634 - COMMUNICATION SYSTEM AND TELEMETRY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Types of Waveguides**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **waveguide** is a hollow, single-conductor metallic transmission line used to guide electromagnetic (EM) energy from one point to another at microwave frequencies (typically above **1 GHz**). Unlike two-conductor transmission lines (coaxial cables, parallel-wire lines), a waveguide confines the EM wave within a closed conductive boundary, forcing the wave to propagate along a preferred longitudinal direction via successive reflections from the inner conducting walls.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A waveguide is essentially a "single-conductor" guiding structure. It cannot support a TEM (Transverse Electro-Magnetic) mode because a TEM mode requires *two* separate conductors to support a transverse electric field and a return magnetic field simultaneously. Only **TE (Transverse Electric)** and **TM (Transverse Magnetic)** modes can exist inside a hollow metallic waveguide.

## 1.2 Why Waveguides? (Motivation)

At low frequencies (VHF/UHF), open-wire lines and coaxial cables work well. As the frequency increases toward the microwave and millimeter-wave range, the following problems arise in conventional transmission lines:

- **Skin Effect Losses:** At high frequencies, current is forced to the outer surface of the conductor, drastically increasing resistive ($I^2R$) losses.
- **Radiation Losses:** Two-conductor lines radiate more at higher frequencies, acting as inefficient antennas.
- **Dielectric Losses:** The insulating material between conductors absorbs more energy.
- **Mechanical Tolerances:** A coaxial cable's dimensions become impractically small for microwave wavelengths.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Note:** Waveguides solve these problems by replacing the central conductor with a highly polished, hollow metallic tube (usually **brass, copper, or aluminum** with an inner surface of high conductivity, sometimes silver-plated). Since there is no central conductor to support a return current, the waveguide operates fundamentally differently from a transmission line.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Whispering Gallery and the Pipe"

Think of waveguides in three everyday analogies:

| Analogy | Concept Illustrated |
|---|---|
| **Whispering in a curved tunnel** | The wall reflections keep the sound energy concentrated, just as waveguide walls confine EM waves. |
| **Water flowing in a wide pipe** | Below a certain pipe size (the cutoff dimension), water cannot flow efficiently — equivalent to the **cutoff frequency** of a waveguide. |
| **Marble inside a long hollow tube** | The marble "bounces" between walls as it travels — analogous to a wave zig-zagging down a guide via **successive reflections**. |

If the inner dimensions of the waveguide are made smaller than roughly half a wavelength of the signal, the wave simply **cannot propagate** and is attenuated exponentially. This is the famous **cutoff phenomenon**, unique to waveguides.

## 1.4 Broad Classification of Waveguides

Waveguides are classified based on **geometry, material, and application**:

1. **Rigid Hollow-Pipe Waveguides**
    - **Rectangular Waveguide** — most common, standard at microwave frequencies
    - **Circular Waveguide** — used in rotary joints, feeds for parabolic antennas
    - **Ridged Waveguide** — single-ridged and double-ridged variants for wider bandwidths

2. **Flexible Waveguides**
    - Made from a spiral-wound, interlocked metallic strip (often silver-plated brass)
    - Used where rigid sections must be joined across a moving or flexible part

3. **Dielectric Waveguides**
    - Solid dielectric rod (typically plastic or glass)
    - Used at optical frequencies (fiber optics is the most famous example)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Note:** When asked to "list the types of waveguides," examiners expect the broad family listing above — *not* only the rigid metal types. Always mention at least the **rectangular, circular, ridged, flexible, and dielectric** varieties.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of Waveguide Propagation

Waveguide propagation can be understood through three complementary viewpoints. KTU examiners frequently ask students to *explain* these in 3-mark questions.

### (a) Ray / Bounce Theory (Geometric Optics View)
- The wave enters the guide at a slight angle and reflects between the top and bottom walls.
- Each reflection generates a new wavefront that reinforces the next.
- Propagation is sustained only if the **angle of incidence** is such that the wavefronts add **in-phase** (constructive interference).

### (b) Wave Equation View (Field Theory)
- Solving Maxwell's equations inside a hollow pipe with conducting boundary conditions (tangential **E = 0**, normal **∂B/∂n = 0**) yields discrete allowed field configurations — called **modes**.
- Each mode has its own **cutoff frequency** $f_c$.
- The dominant mode is the one with the **lowest cutoff frequency**.

### (c) Transverse Resonance View
- The transverse standing-wave pattern must be resonant across the guide's cross-section.
- This quantization gives rise to the half-wavelength fit condition across the broad dimension.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Transverse Electric / Magnetic" modes?**
> A TE mode has its electric field entirely in the plane perpendicular to propagation (no $E_z$). A TM mode has its magnetic field entirely transverse (no $H_z$). One of the two longitudinal field components is always present — unlike TEM — which is why Maxwell's equations can satisfy the boundary conditions.

## 2.2 The Rectangular Waveguide (Primary KTU Focus)

A rectangular waveguide is characterized by its inner broad-wall dimension **$a$** and inner narrow-wall dimension **$b$**, with $a > b$. The wave is assumed to propagate along the **$+z$** direction.

### Step 1 — Mode Naming Convention
- **TE$_{mn}$ modes:** $E_z = 0$, $H_z \neq 0$. Indices $m$ and $n$ count the half-wave variations of the field along $a$ and $b$ respectively. At least one of $m,n$ must be non-zero.
- **TM$_{mn}$ modes:** $H_z = 0$, $E_z \neq 0$. Both $m$ and $n$ must be non-zero (no TM$_{00}$ mode exists).

### Step 2 — Cutoff Frequencies
The cutoff frequency is the *minimum* frequency at which a particular mode can propagate.

$$f_{c,mn} \;=\; \frac{1}{2\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon}}\sqrt{\left(\frac{m}{a}\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{n}{b}\right)^{2}}$$

For an air-filled guide ($v = c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s, $\mu_r = 1$, $\varepsilon_r = 1$):

$$f_{c,mn} \;=\; \frac{c}{2}\sqrt{\left(\frac{m}{a}\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{n}{b}\right)^{2}}$$

The corresponding cutoff wavelength is:

$$\lambda_{c,mn} \;=\; \frac{2}{\sqrt{\left(\frac{m}{a}\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{n}{b}\right)^{2}}}$$

### Step 3 — Dominant Mode
Because $a > b$, the smallest value of $\left(m/a\right)^2 + \left(n/b\right)^2$ is achieved at $m=1, n=0$. Thus:

- **Dominant Mode of Rectangular Waveguide: TE$_{10}$**
- $f_{c,10} = \dfrac{c}{2a}$
- $\lambda_{c,10} = 2a$

### Step 4 — Guide Wavelength
Unlike a free-space wavelength, the **guide wavelength** $\lambda_g$ is stretched along the propagation axis:

$$\lambda_{g} \;=\; \frac{\lambda_{0}}{\sqrt{1-\left(\dfrac{f_{c}}{f}\right)^{2}}}$$

where $\lambda_0$ is the free-space wavelength corresponding to the operating frequency $f$.

### Step 5 — Phase and Group Velocities

$$v_{p} \;=\; \frac{c}{\sqrt{1-\left(\dfrac{f_{c}}{f}\right)^{2}}} \qquad \qquad v_{g} \;=\; c\sqrt{1-\left(\dfrac{f_{c}}{f}\right)^{2}}$$

**Fundamental relation** (always true inside any waveguide):

$$v_{p} \cdot v_{g} \;=\; c^{2}$$

### Step 6 — Wave Impedance
The wave impedance depends on the mode:

$$Z_{TE} \;=\; \frac{\eta}{\sqrt{1-\left(\dfrac{f_{c}}{f}\right)^{2}}} \qquad \qquad Z_{TM} \;=\; \eta\,\sqrt{1-\left(\dfrac{f_{c}}{f}\right)^{2}}$$

where $\eta = 120\pi \approx 377\,\Omega$ for an air-filled guide.

## 2.3 Circular Waveguide

A circular waveguide of inner radius $r_0$ supports TE$_{mn}$ and TM$_{mn}$ modes. The boundary conditions lead to **Bessel function** solutions.

$$f_{c,mn}^{\,TE} \;=\; \frac{c\,x'_{mn}}{2\pi r_{0}} \qquad \qquad f_{c,mn}^{\,TM} \;=\; \frac{c\,x_{mn}}{2\pi r_{0}}$$

- $x'_{mn}$ = non-vanishing roots of $J'_m(x)$ (derivative of Bessel function)
- $x_{mn}$ = non-vanishing roots of $J_m(x)$ (Bessel function itself)

> [!NOTE]
> **Dominant mode of circular waveguide: TE$_{11}$**, with $x'_{11} \approx 1.841$, giving $f_c \approx 0.293\,c/r_0$.

## 2.4 Ridged Waveguide

A **ridged waveguide** has one or two metallic ridges (protrusions) along the broad walls, reducing the effective broad-wall dimension in the center.

- **Advantage:** Significantly lowers the cutoff frequency of the dominant mode → wider usable bandwidth (typically 3:1 ratio instead of 1.5:1 for a standard rectangular guide).
- **Disadvantage:** Lower power-handling capacity and higher attenuation per unit length.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use this table as your last-minute revision sheet. Memorize the formula for $f_c$, $\lambda_g$, $v_p$, $v_g$, and $Z_{TE}/Z_{TM}$ — they are the heart of nearly every KTU numerical.**

| Quantity | Formula | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|
| Cutoff frequency (rectangular, mode $mn$) | $f_{c,mn} = \dfrac{c}{2}\sqrt{\left(\dfrac{m}{a}\right)^{2}+\left(\dfrac{n}{b}\right)^{2}}$ | Hz |
| Cutoff wavelength (rectangular) | $\lambda_{c,mn} = \dfrac{2}{\sqrt{\left(\dfrac{m}{a}\right)^{2}+\left(\dfrac{n}{b}\right)^{2}}}$ | meters |
| Dominant-mode cutoff (rectangular) | $f_{c,10} = c/(2a)$ | Hz |
| Guide wavelength | $\lambda_{g} = \dfrac{\lambda_{0}}{\sqrt{1-\left(f_{c}/f\right)^{2}}}$ | meters |
| Phase velocity | $v_{p} = c \big/ \sqrt{1-\left(f_{c}/f\right)^{2}}$ | m/s |
| Group velocity | $v_{g} = c\,\sqrt{1-\left(f_{c}/f\right)^{2}}$ | m/s |
| Product $v_p v_g$ | $c^{2}$ | fundamental identity |
| TE-mode impedance | $Z_{TE} = \eta \big/ \sqrt{1-\left(f_{c}/f\right)^{2}}$ | ohms |
| TM-mode impedance | $Z_{TM} = \eta\,\sqrt{1-\left(f_{c}/f\right)^{2}}$ | ohms |
| Intrinsic impedance of free space | $\eta = 120\pi \approx 377$ | ohms |
| Circular TE$_{mn}$ cutoff | $f_{c} = c\,x'_{mn} \big/ (2\pi r_0)$ | Bessel root $x'_{11} \approx 1.841$ |
| Circular TM$_{mn}$ cutoff | $f_{c} = c\,x_{mn} \big/ (2\pi r_0)$ | Bessel root $x_{01} \approx 2.405$ |
| Power transmitted (TE$_{10}$, approx) | $P \approx \dfrac{E_{0}^{2}\,a\,b}{4\,\eta}\sqrt{1-\left(f_{c}/f\right)^{2}}$ | watts |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application |
|---|---|
| **Radar Systems** | Rectangular waveguides carry high-power microwave pulses (S-band to X-band) from the magnetron to the antenna. |
| **Satellite Communication** | Circular waveguides act as rotary joints in tracking antennas. |
| **Microwave Test Benches** | Standard waveguide sizes (WR-90, WR-62, WR-42) are used as precision measurement lines. |
| **Medical Linear Accelerators** | Waveguides accelerate electron beams in cancer-treatment machines. |
| **Optical Fiber Networks** | Dielectric waveguides transmit data over thousands of kilometers at near-light-speed. |
| **Particle Accelerators (CERN)** | High-power ridged / circular waveguides drive RF cavities. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Numerical Standard:** Most 14-mark Part B problems require either (i) calculating $f_c$, $\lambda_g$, $v_p$, $v_g$ for a given rectangular waveguide, or (ii) identifying the dominant mode of a circular waveguide. The derivations below give you the *exact* algebra expected in the model solution script.

## 3.1 Derivation A — Cutoff Frequency of a Rectangular Waveguide (TE$_{mn}$)

**Starting Point:** Maxwell's curl equations in source-free, lossless dielectric (with $\sigma = 0$):

$$\nabla \times \vec{E} = -j\omega\mu\vec{H} \qquad\qquad \nabla \times \vec{H} = j\omega\varepsilon\vec{E}$$

**Step 1 — Assume a propagating wave along $z$ with propagation constant $\gamma = \alpha + j\beta$:**

$$\vec{E}(x,y,z) = \vec{E}_{0}(x,y)\,e^{-\gamma z} \qquad\qquad \vec{H}(x,y,z) = \vec{H}_{0}(x,y)\,e^{-\gamma z}$$

**Step 2 — For TE mode, $E_z = 0$. The longitudinal magnetic field $H_z$ must satisfy the 2-D Helmholtz equation:**

$$\nabla_{T}^{2}H_{z}(x,y) + k_{c}^{2}\,H_{z}(x,y) = 0$$

where $k_c^2 = \omega^{2}\mu\varepsilon + \gamma^{2}$ is the cutoff wavenumber squared.

**Step 3 — Solve by separation of variables.** Let $H_z(x,y) = X(x)\,Y(y)$. Substitution yields:

$$\frac{1}{X}\frac{d^{2}X}{dx^{2}} + \frac{1}{Y}\frac{d^{2}Y}{dy^{2}} = -k_{c}^{2}$$

Each side must equal a constant. Let:

$$\frac{d^{2}X}{dx^{2}} + k_{x}^{2}X = 0 \qquad\qquad \frac{d^{2}Y}{dy^{2}} + k_{y}^{2}Y = 0$$

with the **separation condition** $k_x^2 + k_y^2 = k_c^2$.

**Step 4 — Apply the boundary conditions** on the conducting walls ($x = 0, a$ and $y = 0, b$): the tangential component of $\vec{E}$ must vanish. For TE mode this gives $X(0) = X(a) = 0$ and $Y(0) = Y(b) = 0$.

**Step 5 — Trigonometric solution satisfying these boundary conditions:**

$$X(x) = A_{m}\sin\!\left(\frac{m\pi x}{a}\right) \qquad\qquad Y(y) = B_{n}\sin\!\left(\frac{n\pi y}{b}\right)$$

This requires $k_x = m\pi/a$ and $k_y = n\pi/b$, with $m, n = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$ (not both zero for TE).

**Step 6 — Assemble the cutoff wavenumber:**

$$k_{c}^{2} = k_{x}^{2} + k_{y}^{2} \;=\; \left(\frac{m\pi}{a}\right)^{2} + \left(\frac{n\pi}{b}\right)^{2}$$

**Step 7 — Convert to cutoff angular frequency** using $k_c^2 = \omega_c^{2}\mu\varepsilon$:

$$\omega_{c,mn} \;=\; \frac{\pi}{\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon}}\sqrt{\left(\frac{m}{a}\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{n}{b}\right)^{2}}$$

**Step 8 — Divide by $2\pi$ to obtain cutoff frequency** (in Hz):

$$f_{c,mn} \;=\; \frac{1}{2\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon}}\sqrt{\left(\frac{m}{a}\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{n}{b}\right)^{2}}$$

**Step 9 — For an air-filled guide** $\left(\mu_r = \varepsilon_r = 1,\;\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon} = 1/c\right)$:

$$f_{c,mn} \;=\; \frac{c}{2}\sqrt{\left(\frac{m}{a}\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{n}{b}\right)^{2}}$$

**Step 10 — Cutoff wavelength** (since $c = f_c \lambda_c$):

$$\lambda_{c,mn} \;=\; \frac{2}{\sqrt{\left(\frac{m}{a}\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{n}{b}\right)^{2}}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation B — Guide Wavelength, Phase & Group Velocity

**Step 1 — Starting from the dispersion relation inside the guide:**

$$\gamma^{2} = k_{c}^{2} - \omega^{2}\mu\varepsilon$$

For a *propagating* mode, $\gamma = j\beta$ (purely imaginary), so $\beta^{2} = \omega^{2}\mu\varepsilon - k_{c}^{2}$.

**Step 2 — Substitute $\omega_c = k_c/\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon}$:**

$$\beta^{2} = \omega^{2}\mu\varepsilon\!\left(1 - \frac{\omega_{c}^{2}}{\omega^{2}}\right) \;=\; \omega^{2}\mu\varepsilon\!\left(1 - \left(\frac{f_{c}}{f}\right)^{2}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Define guide wavelength** from $\beta = 2\pi/\lambda_g$ and $\omega\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon} = 2\pi/\lambda_0$:

$$\frac{1}{\lambda_{g}} \;=\; \frac{1}{\lambda_{0}}\sqrt{1-\left(\frac{f_{c}}{f}\right)^{2}} \;\Longrightarrow\; \lambda_{g} = \frac{\lambda_{0}}{\sqrt{1-\left(\dfrac{f_{c}}{f}\right)^{2}}}$$

**Step 4 — Phase velocity** is the speed of constant-phase fronts: $v_p = \omega/\beta$:

$$v_{p} \;=\; \frac{\omega}{\omega\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon}\sqrt{1-\left(\dfrac{f_{c}}{f}\right)^{2}}} \;=\; \frac{c}{\sqrt{1-\left(\dfrac{f_{c}}{f}\right)^{2}}}$$

**Step 5 — Group velocity** is the speed of energy / signal envelope. Using $v_g = d\omega/d\beta$:

$$v_{g} \;=\; \frac{d\omega}{d\beta} \;=\; \frac{\beta}{\omega\mu\varepsilon} \;=\; \frac{c^{2}}{v_{p}} \;=\; c\sqrt{1-\left(\frac{f_{c}}{f}\right)^{2}}$$

**Step 6 — Verify the fundamental identity:**

$$v_{p}\,v_{g} \;=\; \frac{c}{\sqrt{1-(f_{c}/f)^{2}}}\,\cdot\,c\sqrt{1-\left(\frac{f_{c}}{f}\right)^{2}} \;=\; c^{2} \quad\checkmark$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical — KTU-Style

**Problem:** A standard **WR-90** rectangular air-filled waveguide has $a = 2.286$ cm, $b = 1.016$ cm. The operating frequency is **$f = 10$ GHz**.

Find (i) the cutoff frequency of the dominant mode, (ii) the guide wavelength, (iii) the phase and group velocities, and (iv) the wave impedance for TE mode.

**Solution:**

**Step (i) — Dominant mode cutoff (TE$_{10}$):**

$$f_{c,10} = \frac{c}{2a} = \frac{3\times10^{8}}{2\times0.02286} = 6.557\,\text{GHz}$$

> Since $f = 10$ GHz $> f_c = 6.557$ GHz, the TE$_{10}$ mode **propagates**.

**Step (ii) — Guide wavelength:**

$$\lambda_{0} = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3\times10^{8}}{10^{10}} = 0.03\,\text{m} = 3\,\text{cm}$$

$$\lambda_{g} = \frac{0.03}{\sqrt{1-(6.557/10)^{2}}} = \frac{0.03}{\sqrt{1-0.4302}} = \frac{0.03}{0.7551} \approx 3.973\,\text{cm}$$

**Step (iii) — Phase and group velocities:**

$$v_{p} = \frac{3\times10^{8}}{0.7551} \approx 3.973\times10^{8}\,\text{m/s}$$

$$v_{g} = 3\times10^{8}\times 0.7551 \approx 2.265\times10^{8}\,\text{m/s}$$

**Sanity check:** $v_p \cdot v_g \approx 3.973\times 2.265 \times 10^{16} \approx 9.0\times10^{16} = c^{2} \;\checkmark$

**Step (iv) — TE mode wave impedance:**

$$Z_{TE} = \frac{377}{0.7551} \approx 499.3\,\Omega$$

> This is the answer students frequently write wrongly. **The TE impedance in a waveguide is *greater* than the free-space impedance**, while the TM impedance is *less*. Mark this distinction.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Mode Cutoff Frequencies for WR-90

| Mode | $f_c$ (GHz) | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **TE$_{10}$ (dominant)** | **6.557** | Lowest cutoff; the *only* mode in the single-mode band |
| TE$_{20}$ | 13.114 | Next higher TE mode |
| TE$_{01}$ | 14.745 | (since $b = 1.016$ cm is half of $a$) |
| TE$_{11}$ / TM$_{11}$ | 16.156 | First *degenerate* pair — same $f_c$ |
| TE$_{30}$ | 19.671 | Higher-order |

> [!NOTE]
> **Single-mode band:** From $f_c(\text{TE}_{10}) = 6.557$ GHz up to $f_c(\text{TE}_{20}) = 13.114$ GHz. Operating frequency 10 GHz lies inside this band — *perfect* for clean TE$_{10}$ propagation.

## 3.5 Comparative Matrix — Waveguide Types at a Glance

| Parameter | Rectangular | Circular | Ridged | Dielectric |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cross-section | $a \times b$ rectangle | radius $r_0$ | $a \times b$ with central ridges | solid / hollow dielectric rod |
| Dominant mode | TE$_{10}$ | TE$_{11}$ | TE$_{10}$ (ridge) | hybrid HE$_{11}$ |
| Manufacturing | Easy, rigid | Easy, but mode is degenerate | Difficult, low power | Easy, used at optics |
| Bandwidth ratio | ~1.5 : 1 | ~1.15 : 1 | up to 3 : 1 | very wide |
| Power handling | High | High | Low | very low (dielectric loss) |
| Typical use | Radars, test benches | Rotary joints, feeds | Wideband EW systems | Fiber optics, mm-wave ICs |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture — Waveguide Classification Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[WAVEGUIDES]:::root --> B[RIGID HOLLOW METAL]
    A --> C[FLEXIBLE METAL]
    A --> D[DIELECTRIC]

    B --> B1[Rectangular]
    B --> B2[Circular]
    B --> B3[Ridged]
    B3 --> B3a[Single Ridge]
    B3 --> B3b[Double Ridge]

    C --> C1[Spiral Wound Strip]
    C --> C2[Interlocked Braid]

    D --> D1[Plastic Rod]
    D --> D2[Glass Fiber]
    D --> D3[Optical Fiber]

    B1 --> E1[TE10 Dominant]
    B2 --> E2[TE11 Dominant]
    B3a --> E3[Lower Cutoff Wider Bandwidth]

    classDef root fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The root block **WAVEGUIDES** branches into three engineering families: *Rigid Hollow Metal*, *Flexible Metal*, and *Dielectric*.
- Each rigid family further breaks down by geometry; the dominant mode is annotated as a leaf node.

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Rectangular Waveguide Internal Field Setup (TE$_{10}$)

```mermaid
graph LR
    SRC[Source 10 GHz]:::src --> FLG[Flange Transition]:::blk
    FLG --> WG[BROAD WALL a 2.286 cm]:::wg
    FLG --> NW[NARROW WALL b 1.016 cm]:::wg
    WG --> BOUNCE[Internal Wave Bounces Zig Zag]:::field
    NW --> BOUNCE
    BOUNCE --> EFIELD[E field along y vertical]:::field
    BOUNCE --> HFIELD[H field in xz plane transverse + longitudinal]:::field
    EFIELD --> LOAD[Matched Horn Load]:::blk
    HFIELD --> LOAD
    BOUNCE --> CUTOFF{Cutoff Check fc 6.557 GHz}:::logic
    CUTOFF -- Above fc --> PROP[PROPAGATES]:::ok
    CUTOFF -- Below fc --> EVAN[EVANESCENT decays exponentially]:::err

    classDef src fill:#0f766e,stroke:#0f766e,color:#ffffff
    classDef blk fill:#334155,stroke:#334155,color:#ffffff
    classDef wg fill:#facc15,stroke:#a16207,color:#000000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef field fill:#f97316,stroke:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff
    classDef logic fill:#a78bfa,stroke:#5b21b6,color:#ffffff
    classDef ok fill:#16a34a,stroke:#15803d,color:#ffffff
    classDef err fill:#dc2626,stroke:#991b1b,color:#ffffff
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- A 10 GHz source feeds the guide through a flange.
- The wave reflects internally between the broad wall **$a$** and narrow wall **$b$**, generating a characteristic zig-zag path.
- The cutoff-check block confirms propagation since $f > f_c$.
- An *evanescent* condition would occur only if the operating frequency fell below 6.557 GHz.

## 4.3 Functional Block Diagram — Signal Journey Inside a Rectangular Waveguide

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Input Flange] --> B[Mode Launcher Excitation]:::st
    B --> C[TE10 Mode Established]:::st
    C --> D[Cutoff Filter Behavior]:::st
    D --> E[Dispersion in Waveguide lambda g]:::st
    E --> F[Group Velocity Energy Transport]:::st
    F --> G[Output Flange to Load]:::st
    G --> H[Matched Termination]:::st

    classDef st fill:#0ea5e9,stroke:#0369a1,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px
```

**Reading the diagram:** Each block represents a *functional stage* the signal passes through, from coupling at the input flange to matched termination at the load.
