<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a waveguide. Why is a TEM mode not possible inside a hollow metallic waveguide?**

**Model Answer:**
A waveguide is a hollow, single-conductor metallic structure used to guide electromagnetic energy at microwave frequencies. A TEM (Transverse Electro-Magnetic) mode requires the simultaneous existence of a transverse electric field and a transverse magnetic field, both entirely in the plane perpendicular to propagation. Such a field configuration demands *two* separate conductors to support the potential difference and the return current. Inside a single-conductor hollow pipe, Maxwell's equations together with the boundary condition that the tangential electric field on a perfect conductor must be zero, force at least one of the longitudinal field components ($E_z$ or $H_z$) to be non-zero. Hence, only TE and TM modes can exist; a pure TEM mode is impossible.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often answer "TEM is not possible because there is only one conductor" without invoking the boundary condition. This loses 1 mark. Always mention that the **tangential E-field on the conductor wall must vanish**, which is the mathematical reason.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is meant by cutoff wavelength in a waveguide? State its value for the dominant mode of a rectangular waveguide.**

**Model Answer:**
The cutoff wavelength $\lambda_c$ is the *maximum* free-space wavelength (corresponding to the *minimum* cutoff frequency $f_c$) at which a given waveguide mode can propagate. Waves of frequency below cutoff do not propagate; their amplitude decays exponentially along the guide (evanescent wave).
For the **dominant TE$_{10}$ mode** of a rectangular waveguide, $\lambda_{c,10} = 2a$, where $a$ is the broad-wall dimension.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part B questions carry 14 marks each and offer internal choice (a student answers either Q-A or Q-B). Each sub-part is typically 7 marks. Below, both choices are fully worked.

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Derive the expression for the cutoff frequency of a rectangular waveguide for TE$_{mn}$ modes. A standard WR-90 air-filled rectangular waveguide has dimensions $a = 2.286$ cm and $b = 1.016$ cm. Find the cutoff frequencies of the dominant and the next two higher-order modes. State whether the guide will support single-mode operation at 8 GHz.**

**Solution Outline:**

**(a) Derivation of cutoff frequency — 7 marks**

1. Begin with the wave equation for $H_z$ in a source-free region inside the guide.
2. Apply separation of variables and the perfect-conductor boundary conditions: tangential **E** = 0 on the four walls $x=0,a$ and $y=0,b$.
3. Show that the only solutions are sinusoidal:

$$H_{z}(x,y) = H_{0}\cos\!\left(\frac{m\pi x}{a}\right)\cos\!\left(\frac{n\pi y}{b}\right)$$

4. Compute the cutoff wavenumber $k_c^2 = (m\pi/a)^2 + (n\pi/b)^2$.
5. Convert to cutoff frequency using $\omega_c = k_c/\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon}$ and $f_c = \omega_c/2\pi$:

$$f_{c,mn} = \frac{c}{2}\sqrt{\left(\frac{m}{a}\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{n}{b}\right)^{2}}$$

**(b) Numerical evaluation — 7 marks**

Given $a = 2.286$ cm, $b = 1.016$ cm, $c = 3\times10^{8}$ m/s.

**Dominant mode TE$_{10}$:** $m=1, n=0$

$$f_{c,10} = \frac{3\times10^{8}}{2\times0.02286}\sqrt{\left(\frac{1}{1}\right)^{2}+0} = 6.557\,\text{GHz}$$

**Next higher mode TE$_{20}$:** $m=2, n=0$

$$f_{c,20} = \frac{6.557}{2}\times 2 = \frac{c}{a} = 13.114\,\text{GHz}$$

**Next higher mode TE$_{01}$:** $m=0, n=1$

$$f_{c,01} = \frac{c}{2b} = \frac{3\times10^{8}}{2\times0.01016} = 14.764\,\text{GHz}$$

**Single-mode operation at 8 GHz:**

The single-mode band exists from $f_{c,10} = 6.557$ GHz up to $f_{c,20} = 13.114$ GHz.
Since $6.557 < 8 < 13.114$, the guide supports **only the TE$_{10}$ mode** at 8 GHz → **single-mode operation is YES**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget that the **upper edge of the single-mode band is the cutoff of the *next* higher mode** (TE$_{20}$ for a rectangular guide), not the cutoff of TE$_{11}$ or TM$_{11}$. Always state the next mode that *will* be excited, not the next *TE* mode in general.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Explain the concept of dominant mode in a rectangular waveguide. Derive expressions for guide wavelength, phase velocity, and group velocity for a TE mode. For an air-filled guide of dimensions $a = 4$ cm, $b = 2$ cm operating at 5 GHz in the dominant mode, calculate (i) the cutoff frequency, (ii) the guide wavelength, and (iii) the phase velocity.**

**Solution Outline:**

**(a) Theory of dominant mode, $\lambda_g$, $v_p$, $v_g$ — 7 marks**

1. The **dominant mode** is the propagating mode with the *lowest* cutoff frequency. For a rectangular waveguide, because $a > b$, the dominant mode is **TE$_{10}$**, with $f_{c,10} = c/(2a)$.
2. From the dispersion relation $\beta^{2} = \omega^{2}\mu\varepsilon - k_{c}^{2}$ and the substitutions $\lambda_g = 2\pi/\beta$, $v_p = \omega/\beta$, $v_g = d\omega/d\beta$, derive:

$$\lambda_{g} = \frac{\lambda_{0}}{\sqrt{1-(f_{c}/f)^{2}}}, \qquad v_{p} = \frac{c}{\sqrt{1-(f_{c}/f)^{2}}}, \qquad v_{g} = c\sqrt{1-(f_{c}/f)^{2}}$$

3. Show the product $v_p v_g = c^2$ (a fundamental result for all waveguides).

**(b) Numerical — 7 marks**

Given $a = 4$ cm $= 0.04$ m, $b = 2$ cm $= 0.02$ m, $f = 5$ GHz.

**(i) Cutoff frequency (TE$_{10}$):**

$$f_{c,10} = \frac{3\times10^{8}}{2\times0.04} = 3.75\,\text{GHz}$$

**Propagation check:** $f = 5 > f_c = 3.75$ GHz → mode propagates.

**(ii) Guide wavelength:**

Free-space wavelength: $\lambda_0 = c/f = 3\times10^{8}/5\times10^{9} = 0.06$ m $= 6$ cm

$$\lambda_{g} = \frac{0.06}{\sqrt{1-(3.75/5)^{2}}} = \frac{0.06}{\sqrt{1-0.5625}} = \frac{0.06}{0.6614} \approx 0.0907\,\text{m} \approx 9.07\,\text{cm}$$

**(iii) Phase velocity:**

$$v_{p} = \frac{3\times10^{8}}{0.6614} \approx 4.536\times10^{8}\,\text{m/s}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** A common error is to write $v_p = c \cdot \lambda_g / \lambda_0$ and then forget to keep the units consistent. Always quote $v_p$ in m/s and $\lambda_g$ in m. Also remember that **$v_p$ exceeds $c$** inside a waveguide — this is normal, as it is the *signal energy* (group velocity) that obeys relativity, not the phase fronts.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid-Revision Checklist — Read this in the last 10 minutes before the exam.**

- **Waveguide:** Hollow, single-conductor metallic pipe that guides EM waves at microwave frequencies.
- **No TEM mode** — only **TE** (transverse electric) and **TM** (transverse magnetic) modes can exist.
- **Rectangular waveguide dimensions:** $a$ = broad wall, $b$ = narrow wall, with $a > b$.
- **Dominant mode = TE$_{10}$** with cutoff $f_{c,10} = c/(2a)$ and $\lambda_{c,10} = 2a$.
- **General cutoff formula (rectangular):** $f_{c,mn} = (c/2)\sqrt{(m/a)^2 + (n/b)^2}$.
- **Single-mode band:** $f_c(\text{TE}_{10}) < f < f_c(\text{TE}_{20})$ for a standard rectangular guide.
- **Guide wavelength:** $\lambda_g = \lambda_0 / \sqrt{1 - (f_c/f)^2}$ — always **larger** than the free-space wavelength.
- **Phase velocity:** $v_p = c / \sqrt{1 - (f_c/f)^2}$ — always **greater** than $c$.
- **Group velocity:** $v_g = c \cdot \sqrt{1 - (f_c/f)^2}$ — always **less** than $c$.
- **Fundamental identity:** $v_p \cdot v_g = c^2$.
- **TE mode wave impedance:** $Z_{TE} = 377 / \sqrt{1 - (f_c/f)^2} \Omega$ — *greater* than $377\,\Omega$.
- **TM mode wave impedance:** $Z_{TM} = 377 \cdot \sqrt{1 - (f_c/f)^2} \Omega$ — *less* than $377\,\Omega$.
- **Circular waveguide dominant mode = TE$_{11}$** with Bessel root $x'_{11} \approx 1.841$, $f_c \approx 0.293 c / r_0$.
- **Ridged waveguide:** Lower cutoff, wider bandwidth (~3:1), reduced power handling.
- **Dielectric waveguide:** Used at optical frequencies; the foundation of optical-fiber communication.
- **Power transmitted** in TE$_{10}$ scales with $E_0^2$ and with $\sqrt{1 - (f_c/f)^2}$ — operation close to cutoff drastically reduces power handling.
- **Cutoff phenomenon:** Below $f_c$ the wave is *evanescent* and decays exponentially as $e^{-\alpha z}$ with $\alpha = \sqrt{k_c^2 - \omega^2\mu\varepsilon}$.
- **Standard sizes:** WR-90 ($a = 2.286$ cm, X-band), WR-62 ($a = 1.58$ cm, Ku-band), WR-42 ($a = 1.07$ cm, K-band).
- **"WR" stands for "Waveguide Rectangular"** — the number equals the broad-wall dimension in hundredths of an inch (0.90 inch for WR-90).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principles of Radio Wave Propagation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT634 - COMMUNICATION SYSTEM AND TELEMETRY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Principles of Radio Wave Propagation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Radio Wave Propagation** is the phenomenon by which electromagnetic (EM) energy in the radio frequency (RF) band travels from a transmitting antenna to a receiving antenna through the Earth's atmosphere, the ground, or a combination of both. The mode in which these waves propagate is fundamentally determined by the carrier frequency, the physical characteristics of the Earth’s surface, the curvature of the Earth, and the ionized layers of the upper atmosphere collectively known as the **ionosphere**.

In the strict KTU 2024 Scheme vocabulary, radio wave propagation is categorized into three principal modes:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Three Modes of Propagation:**
> 1. **Ground Wave (Surface Wave) Propagation** — waves hugging the Earth's surface.
> 2. **Sky Wave (Ionospheric) Propagation** — waves refracted/reflected by the ionosphere.
> 3. **Space Wave (Line-of-Sight / Tropospheric) Propagation** — waves travelling in a straight line through the lower atmosphere.

## 1.2 Intuitive Conceptual Analogy

Imagine you drop a stone into a still pond. The energy ripples outwards in concentric circles. Now imagine three different scenarios:

1. **The Pond with Mud at the Edges (Ground Wave)** — If the ripples glide over a soft muddy shore, they lose energy quickly but still hug the surface. This is exactly what happens in **ground wave propagation**, where the radio wave clings to the Earth's contour and is guided by the conductive ground. Low-frequency AM broadcast signals (≈ 540 kHz – 1600 kHz) behave this way.

2. **The Pond with a Glass Ceiling Above (Sky Wave)** — Suppose you now put a transparent glass ceiling above the pond. The ripples hit the ceiling, bend (refract), and come back down at a distant point. The **ionosphere** — a collection of charged particle layers 60 km – 400 km above the Earth — acts exactly like that ceiling. Shortwave (HF, 3–30 MHz) signals bounce off it to enable intercontinental communication.

3. **The Open Sky with a Curved Floor (Space Wave)** — If you take the pond out into an open field and shine a torchlight from one hilltop to another, the light travels in a straight line. This is **space wave propagation**, the principle behind TV transmission, cellular 4G/5G, satellite links, and FM radio (VHF/UHF, 30 MHz – 3 GHz).

> [!NOTE]
> **The electromagnetic spectrum bands relevant to telemetry are: VLF, LF, MF, HF, VHF, UHF, SHF, EHF.** Each band selects a different propagation mode due to the interaction with ground and atmosphere.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants (KTU Reference Values)

- Speed of light in free space: $c = 3 \times 10^8 \;\text{m/s}$
- Earth radius: $R = 6371 \;\text{km}$ (≈ $4/3 \times 6371 \;\text{km}$ for radio refraction correction)
- Permittivity of free space: $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \;\text{F/m}$
- Permeability of free space: $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \;\text{H/m}$
- Wavelength–frequency relation: $\lambda = c / f$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mode 1 — Ground Wave (Surface Wave) Propagation

Ground waves travel along the Earth’s surface and are vertically polarized to induce currents in the ground, which then guide the wave. The wave is attenuated because part of its energy is absorbed by the ground (converted to heat). 

**Operational Logic — Step by Step:**
- The transmitted wave induces a surface current in the Earth.
- The Earth, being a lossy dielectric, both conducts and absorbs energy.
- Vertical polarization is mandatory for efficient ground-wave coupling — horizontally polarized waves are shorted out by the conductive surface.
- **Attenuation** increases sharply with **increasing frequency**.
- **Maximum practical range** is approximately limited to a few hundred kilometers at MF and below.

**Critical Engineering Insight:** Ground waves are reliable, unaffected by ionospheric disturbances, but their usable bandwidth and range are limited. They are the workhorse for maritime navigation, AM broadcasting, and standard time-signal broadcasting.

### 2.1.1 Surface Wave Attenuation Factor

For a smooth, homogeneous Earth with conductivity $\sigma$ and relative permittivity $\varepsilon_r$, the **numerical distance** $p$ and the **phase constant** $b$ are given by:

$$
p = \frac{\pi d}{\lambda \cdot X} \cdot \cos(b)
$$

$$
b = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\varepsilon_r + 1}{X}\right) \;\;\text{where}\;\; X = \frac{18 \times 10^9 \cdot \sigma}{f}
$$

When $p \to \infty$ (i.e., the wave has travelled far enough that the Earth behaves as a perfect dielectric), the attenuation factor reaches a stable value used in **broadcast coverage planning**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For **land/soil** ($\sigma \approx 10^{-2}\;\text{S/m}$) and **sea water** ($\sigma \approx 4\;\text{S/m}$), sea water offers far less attenuation, so maritime MF/HF signals can travel thousands of kilometres over the ocean.

## 2.2 Mode 2 — Sky Wave (Ionospheric) Propagation

The ionosphere contains layers of free electrons and ions produced by solar ultraviolet and X-ray radiation. These layers are designated:

- **D Layer** (60 – 90 km): Exists only during daytime. **Absorbs** HF waves strongly. No refraction; it’s a sink.
- **E Layer** (90 – 150 km): Refracts HF waves (≈ 10 MHz during day, lower at night).
- **F1 Layer** (150 – 250 km): Forms during day; merges into F2 at night.
- **F2 Layer** (250 – 400 km): The most important refracting layer — supports long-distance HF communication.

### 2.2.1 The Physics of Refraction

An electromagnetic wave entering a region with free electron density $N$ (electrons per m³) experiences a refractive index:

$$
n = \sqrt{1 - \frac{f_c^2}{f^2}} \;\;\text{where}\;\; f_c = 9\sqrt{N_{\max}}
$$

Because the electron density increases with height, $n$ decreases with height, and the wave bends back towards Earth (similar to light bending in a graded-index fibre).

### 2.2.2 Critical Frequency

The **critical frequency** $f_c$ is the highest frequency that can be reflected back **vertically** from the ionosphere:

$$
\boxed{\,f_c = 9 \sqrt{N_{\max}}\;\;\text{Hz}\,}
$$

where $N_{\max}$ is the **maximum electron density** per m³ in the layer. The constant 9 carries the square-root of the plasma-frequency conversion factors $\left( \sqrt{e^2 / (4\pi^2 m \varepsilon_0)} \right)$.

### 2.2.3 Maximum Usable Frequency (MUF)

For oblique incidence at angle $\theta$ (measured from vertical), the **Secant Law** governs the highest frequency that the layer can return:

$$
\boxed{\,\text{MUF} = f_c \cdot \sec(\theta)\,}
$$

### 2.2.4 Skip Distance

The **skip distance** $D_s$ is the minimum ground distance from the transmitter at which the sky wave returns. Using a flat-Earth approximation with **virtual height** $h'$:

$$
\boxed{\,D_s = 2 h' \sqrt{\sec^2(\theta) - 1} = 2 h' \tan(\theta)\,}
$$

The area between the ground-wave range and the skip distance is called the **skip zone** — no signal is received there.

## 2.3 Mode 3 — Space Wave (Line-of-Sight) Propagation

Space wave consists of:
- The **direct wave** (straight line between antennas).
- The **ground-reflected wave**.
- Sometimes a **tropospheric-refracted** component.

### 2.3.1 Line-of-Sight Range

For two antennas of heights $h_t$ and $h_r$ (meters) above a spherical Earth of effective radius $4R/3$:

$$
\boxed{\,d_{\max} = \sqrt{17 h_t} + \sqrt{17 h_r}\;\;\text{km}\,}
$$

where 17 comes from $2 \cdot \sqrt{2 R_{\text{eff}}} \approx \sqrt{17}$ with $R_{\text{eff}} = 4R/3 \approx 8493$ km, and the factor of 2 accounts for the two-way distance.

### 2.3.2 Friis Free-Space Transmission Equation

The received power in free space (no obstructions, no reflections):

$$
\boxed{\,P_r = P_t \, G_t \, G_r \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2\,}
$$

### 2.3.3 Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL)

$$
\boxed{\,\text{FSPL} = \left(\frac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{4\pi d f}{c}\right)^2\,}
$$

In decibels, the **most-tested KTU form**:

$$
\boxed{\,\text{FSPL (dB)} = 32.44 + 20 \log_{10}(d_{\text{km}}) + 20 \log_{10}(f_{\text{MHz}})\,}
$$

### 2.3.4 Electric Field Strength

The received field strength at distance $d$ for an isotropic radiator with $P_t$ watts:

$$
E = \frac{\sqrt{30 \, G_t \, P_t}}{d} \;\;\text{V/m}
$$

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is a one-stop summary. Memorize the boxed expressions — they appear in nearly every KTU 2024 ESE paper for this module.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Typical Value |
|---|---------|---------|------------------------|
| 1 | Wavelength | $\lambda = c / f$ | m |
| 2 | Critical frequency | $f_c = 9 \sqrt{N_{\max}}$ | Hz |
| 3 | MUF (Secant Law) | $\text{MUF} = f_c \sec(\theta)$ | Hz |
| 4 | Skip distance | $D_s = 2 h' \tan(\theta)$ | m |
| 5 | LOS range | $d_{\max} = \sqrt{17 h_t} + \sqrt{17 h_r}$ | km, h in m |
| 6 | Free-space path loss | $\text{FSPL (dB)} = 32.44 + 20\log(d_{\text{km}}) + 20\log(f_{\text{MHz}})$ | dB |
| 7 | Friis equation | $P_r = P_t G_t G_r (\lambda / 4\pi d)^2$ | W |
| 8 | E-field isotropic | $E = \sqrt{30\,G_t P_t} / d$ | V/m |
| 9 | Numerical distance | $p = (\pi d \cos b) / (\lambda X)$ | dimensionless |
| 10 | Surface conductivity constant | $X = 1.8 \times 10^{10} \sigma / f$ | dimensionless |
| 11 | Earth effective radius (radio) | $R_{\text{eff}} = 4R/3 \approx 8493$ | km |
| 12 | Refractive index of iono layer | $n = \sqrt{1 - f_c^2 / f^2}$ | dimensionless |

> Engineering real-world utility: these formulas are used in **HF link design for defence radio**, **broadcast coverage planning for All India Radio (AIR)**, **cellular tower height optimization**, **satellite link budget for ISRO missions**, and **telemetry links in launch vehicles**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Critical Frequency of an Ionospheric Layer

### 3.1.1 Physical Setup

Consider a radio wave incident **vertically** on an ionospheric layer with electron density $N$ (electrons per m³). The refractive index of a collisionless plasma for an EM wave is:

$$
n = \sqrt{1 - \frac{N e^2}{4 \pi^2 m \varepsilon_0 f^2}}
$$

where $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C is the electron charge and $m = 9.11 \times 10^{-31}$ kg is the electron mass.

### 3.1.2 The Reflection Condition

A wave returns to Earth only if it meets a layer where its frequency matches the **local plasma frequency**:

$$
f_p = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{N e^2}{m \varepsilon_0}}
$$

The wave is reflected when $f = f_p$ at the peak electron density of the layer. Therefore, for vertical incidence:

$$
f_c = f_p(N_{\max}) = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{N_{\max} e^2}{m \varepsilon_0}}
$$

### 3.1.3 Substitute Numerical Constants

We now substitute the SI constants:

- $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C
- $m = 9.11 \times 10^{-31}$ kg
- $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m

$$
f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{(1.602 \times 10^{-19})^2 \cdot N_{\max}}{(9.11 \times 10^{-31})(8.854 \times 10^{-12})}}
$$

Evaluating the denominator first:

$$
(9.11 \times 10^{-31})(8.854 \times 10^{-12}) = 8.066 \times 10^{-42}
$$

The numerator:

$$
(1.602 \times 10^{-19})^2 = 2.566 \times 10^{-38}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\frac{N_{\max} \cdot 2.566 \times 10^{-38}}{8.066 \times 10^{-42}} = N_{\max} \cdot 3181.5
$$

$$
f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{3181.5 \, N_{\max}}
$$

$$
f_c \approx 8.98 \, \sqrt{N_{\max}} \;\;\text{Hz}
$$

This rounds to the well-known textbook form:

$$
\boxed{\,f_c \approx 9\sqrt{N_{\max}}\;\text{Hz}\,}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Maximum Usable Frequency (Secant Law)

### 3.2.1 Geometry of Oblique Incidence

Let the wave strike the ionosphere at an angle $\theta_0$ from the vertical at height $h = 0$. It penetrates the layer and is refracted following Snell’s law for a spherically stratified ionosphere (Appleton’s relation):

$$
f \cdot \sec(\theta_0) = f_p(N_{\max}) = f_c
$$

at the point where the wave is just barely reflected.

### 3.2.2 Applying Snell’s Law for Ionosphere

For a spherically symmetric ionosphere of effective radius $R$ and a layer at virtual height $h'$, the angle of incidence at the layer is related to the ground angle $\theta_0$ by:

$$
f = f_c \cos(\theta_{\text{layer}})
$$

Combining with the geometry:

$$
f_{\max}(\theta_0) = \frac{f_c}{\cos(\theta_0)} = f_c \sec(\theta_0)
$$

Thus:

$$
\boxed{\,\text{MUF} = f_c \sec(\theta_0)\,}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Skip Distance (Flat-Earth Approximation)

### 3.3.1 Geometric Construction

Place the transmitter at $T$ on the ground. The wave goes up to the virtual reflection point $V$ at height $h'$, and comes back to receiver at $R$. By symmetry of the right triangles $TVP$ and $RVP$ (where $P$ is the foot of perpendicular from $V$), the total skip distance is twice the ground projection of the slant path:

$$
\frac{D_s}{2} = h' \tan(\theta_0)
$$

Therefore:

$$
D_s = 2 h' \tan(\theta_0)
$$

### 3.3.2 Connecting Skip Distance to MUF

Since $\sec(\theta_0) = \text{MUF}/f_c$, we have $\tan^2(\theta_0) = \sec^2(\theta_0) - 1 = (\text{MUF}/f_c)^2 - 1$. Thus an alternate form:

$$
\boxed{\,D_s = 2 h' \sqrt{\left(\frac{\text{MUF}}{f_c}\right)^2 - 1}\,}
$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Free-Space Path Loss (Friis)

### 3.4.1 Start with Isotropic Radiator

An isotropic antenna radiates total power $P_t$ uniformly over a sphere of radius $d$. The power flux density (Poynting vector magnitude) is:

$$
S = \frac{P_t}{4\pi d^2} \;\;\text{W/m}^2
$$

### 3.4.2 Received Power

A receiving antenna of effective aperture $A_e$ intercepts a fraction of this flux. For any antenna, $A_e = G_r \lambda^2 / (4\pi)$. So:

$$
P_r = S \cdot A_e = \frac{P_t}{4\pi d^2} \cdot \frac{G_r \lambda^2}{4\pi}
$$

Combining both gains:

$$
\boxed{\,P_r = \frac{P_t \, G_t \, G_r \, \lambda^2}{(4\pi)^2 \, d^2}\,}
$$

### 3.4.3 Free-Space Path Loss

The path loss (ratio $P_t / P_r$ for isotropic isotropic case) is:

$$
\text{FSPL} = \left(\frac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)^2
$$

In dB, with $d$ in km and $f$ in MHz:

$$
\text{FSPL}_{\text{dB}} = 20\log\!\left(\frac{4\pi \cdot d \cdot f}{c}\right)
$$

Substituting $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s and converting:

$$
\frac{4\pi}{c} = \frac{12.566}{3 \times 10^8} = 4.19 \times 10^{-8}
$$

For $d$ in km and $f$ in MHz, $d \cdot f$ is in units of $10^3 \cdot 10^6 = 10^9$ m·Hz. We need:

$$
20\log(4.19 \times 10^{-8} \cdot 10^9 \cdot d_{\text{km}} \cdot f_{\text{MHz}}) = 32.44 + 20\log(d_{\text{km}}) + 20\log(f_{\text{MHz}})
$$

This confirms the **standard KTU formula**:

$$
\boxed{\,\text{FSPL (dB)} = 32.44 + 20 \log(d_{\text{km}}) + 20 \log(f_{\text{MHz}})\,}
$$

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Line-of-Sight Range over Spherical Earth

### 3.5.1 Setup

Two antennas of heights $h_t$ and $h_r$ stand on a sphere of radius $R$. The maximum LOS distance $d$ corresponds to the line that just grazes the Earth's surface at the tangent point. The geometry gives two right triangles; for each:

$$
d_t = \sqrt{2 R h_t + h_t^2} \approx \sqrt{2 R h_t}\;\;(\text{since } h_t \ll R)
$$

### 3.5.2 Combining Both Ends

Total range is the sum of the two contributions:

$$
d_{\max} = \sqrt{2 R h_t} + \sqrt{2 R h_r}
$$

### 3.5.3 Substituting Effective Earth Radius

To account for atmospheric refraction, use $R_{\text{eff}} = (4/3) R \approx 8493$ km:

$$
2 R_{\text{eff}} \approx 16986 \;\text{km}, \quad \sqrt{16986} \approx 130.3
$$

But since $h$ is in meters and we want $d$ in km, we need to adjust units. With $h$ in metres:

$$
\sqrt{2 R_{\text{eff}} h} \;\;\text{km} = \sqrt{2 \cdot 8493 \cdot h / 1000} = \sqrt{16.986 \, h}
$$

Using the standard textbook approximation (which factors in the $4/3$ Earth):

$$
d_{\max} = \sqrt{17 h_t} + \sqrt{17 h_r} \;\;\text{km}
$$

This is the **KTU-ready formula**:

$$
\boxed{\,d_{\max}\,(\text{km}) = \sqrt{17 h_t}\, + \sqrt{17 h_r}\,,\;\; h_t, h_r \;\text{in metres}\,}
$$

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A transmitter operates at 100 MHz with 10 kW ERP and a receiver antenna has gain 6 dB at a distance of 50 km. Compute (a) Free-space path loss, (b) Received power in dBm, (c) E-field strength.

**Solution:**

**(a)** FSPL:

$$
\text{FSPL} = 32.44 + 20\log(50) + 20\log(100) = 32.44 + 33.98 + 40 = 106.42 \;\text{dB}
$$

**(b)** $P_t = 10\;\text{kW} = 70\;\text{dBm}$. With $G_t = 0\;\text{dBi}$ (since ERP includes transmitter gain), $G_r = 6\;\text{dBi}$:

$$
P_r = P_t + G_t + G_r - \text{FSPL} = 70 + 0 + 6 - 106.42 = -30.42 \;\text{dBm}
$$

**(c)** E-field with $G_t = 1$ (isotropic reference):

$$
E = \frac{\sqrt{30 \cdot 1 \cdot 10{,}000}}{50{,}000} = \frac{\sqrt{300{,}000}}{50{,}000} = \frac{547.7}{50{,}000} \approx 10.95 \;\text{mV/m}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Modes of Radio Wave Propagation

The following Mermaid diagram presents a hierarchical block-level architecture of the three principal modes of radio wave propagation, the medium each interacts with, and the typical frequency bands.

```mermaid
graph TD
    RWP["Radio Wave Propagation"]
    RWP --> GW["Ground Wave Surface Wave Mode"]
    RWP --> SW["Sky Wave Ionospheric Mode"]
    RWP --> SPW["Space Wave Line of Sight Mode"]

    GW --> GWa["Guided by Earth Surface Conduction"]
    GW --> GWb["Frequency Below 2 MHz Typical"]
    GW --> GWc["Used For AM Broadcast Maritime Radio"]

    SW --> SWa["Refracted by Ionosphere Layers"]
    SW --> SWb["Frequency 3 to 30 MHz HF"]
    SW --> SWc["Used For Long Distance HF Communication"]
    SWa --> IONO["Ionosphere Layers D E F1 F2"]

    SPW --> SPWa["Direct and Ground Reflected Components"]
    SPW --> SPWb["Frequency VHF UHF SHF Above 30 MHz"]
    SPW --> SPWc["Used For TV FM Cellular Satellite Links"]

    IONO --> DL["D Layer 60 to 90 km Daytime Absorber"]
    IONO --> EL["E Layer 90 to 150 km Sporadic E"]
    IONO --> F1L["F1 Layer 150 to 250 km Daytime"]
    IONO --> F2L["F2 Layer 250 to 400 km Most Useful"]
```

## 4.2 Functional Flow — Ionospheric Refraction Geometry

```mermaid
graph LR
    TX["Transmitter on Ground"]
    TX -->|"Oblique Ray at Angle theta0"| ION["Ionosphere Layer at Virtual Height h prime"]
    ION -->|"Refracted Back"| RX["Receiver at Skip Distance Ds"]
    RX -.->|"Skip Zone No Signal"| TX

    ION --> CRIT["Critical Frequency fc equals 9 root Nmax"]
    CRIT --> MUF["MUF equals fc sec theta0"]
    MUF --> DS["Ds equals 2h prime tan theta0"]
```

## 4.3 Functional Block — Free-Space Link Budget

```mermaid
graph LR
    PT["Pt Transmit Power dBm"]
    PT --> GT["Plus Gt Tx Antenna Gain dBi"]
    GT --> FSPL["Minus FSPL equals 32.44 plus 20log dkm plus 20log fMHz"]
    FSPL --> GR["Plus Gr Rx Antenna Gain dBi"]
    GR --> PR["Pr Received Power dBm"]

    PR --> SENS["Compare Against Receiver Sensitivity"]
    SENS --> MARGIN["Link Margin in dB"]
```

## 4.4 Layered Ionosphere — Functional Cross-Section (Flowchart Form)

```mermaid
graph TB
    SPACE["Outer Space Above 400 km"]
    F2["F2 Layer 250 to 400 km Main Refractor for HF"]
    F1["F1 Layer 150 to 250 km Exists by Day Merges into F2 at Night"]
    E["E Layer 90 to 150 km Refracts Lower HF"]
    D["D Layer 60 to 90 km Daytime Only Absorbs HF"]
    GND["Earth Surface 0 km"]

    SPACE --- F2
    F2 --- F1
    F1 --- E
    E --- D
    D --- GND
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### **Q1. Define critical frequency in ionospheric propagation. Why is it called critical?** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The **critical frequency** $f_c$ is the highest frequency of a radio wave that, when transmitted vertically upward, is just returned to Earth by an ionospheric layer. It is called *critical* because any frequency slightly above $f_c$ will escape through the layer into space, while any frequency at or below $f_c$ is reflected back. Mathematically:

$$
f_c = 9 \sqrt{N_{\max}} \;\;\text{Hz}
$$

where $N_{\max}$ is the peak electron density of the layer (electrons/m³). It is determined by ionosonde measurements (vertical sounding).

### **Q2. Distinguish between ground wave and sky wave propagation. State two applications of each.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Ground Wave | Sky Wave |
|---------|-------------|----------|
| Guiding medium | Earth's surface (conduction) | Ionosphere (refraction) |
| Polarization | Vertical (mandatory) | Either |
| Frequency range | Below ≈ 2 MHz (MF/LF) | 3 – 30 MHz (HF) |
| Range | Few hundred km (limited by attenuation) | Thousands of km (single hop or multi-hop) |
| Time dependency | Almost independent of day/night | Strongly dependent (D-layer absorption by day) |
| Reliability | Very reliable | Variable (fades, MUF changes) |

**Ground Wave Applications:** AM broadcast (MW), maritime coastal radio, Loran-C navigation, standard time signal (e.g., ATA, RNS).
**Sky Wave Applications:** International shortwave broadcast (AIR, BBC), defence HF communication, over-the-horizon (OTH) radar, amateur radio DX contacts.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks each, Internal Choice)

> **Module Internal Choice:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **critical frequency** of the ionospheric layer in terms of the maximum electron density. Explain how the **Maximum Usable Frequency (MUF)** and the **skip distance** are related to the critical frequency. (7 marks)

**(b)** A 20 MHz wave is transmitted at an angle of 30° from the vertical and reflected from the F2 layer whose virtual height is 250 km. Given the critical frequency of the F2 layer is 8 MHz, calculate (i) MUF, (ii) skip distance, and (iii) the maximum electron density of the layer. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of Critical Frequency:** (See detailed derivation in Section 3.1 above.)

- Begin with plasma refractive index: $n = \sqrt{1 - N e^2 / (4\pi^2 m \varepsilon_0 f^2)}$.
- Setting $n = 0$ at the point of reflection gives $f = f_p$.
- For vertical incidence: $f_c = 9 \sqrt{N_{\max}}$ Hz.
- For oblique incidence at angle $\theta_0$: $\text{MUF} = f_c \sec(\theta_0)$ (Secant Law).
- Skip distance from geometry: $D_s = 2 h' \tan(\theta_0)$.
- Combining: $D_s = 2 h' \sqrt{(\text{MUF}/f_c)^2 - 1}$.
- This shows that **higher MUF** (i.e., higher operating frequency or larger $\theta_0$) **increases skip distance**.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $f_{\text{op}} = 20$ MHz, $\theta_0 = 30°$, $h' = 250$ km, $f_c = 8$ MHz.

**(i) MUF:**

$$
\text{MUF} = f_c \sec(\theta_0) = 8 \times \sec(30°) = 8 \times \frac{1}{\cos(30°)} = 8 \times 1.1547 = 9.238 \;\text{MHz}
$$

**(ii) Skip Distance:**

$$
D_s = 2 h' \tan(\theta_0) = 2 \times 250 \times \tan(30°) = 500 \times 0.5774 = 288.7 \;\text{km}
$$

**(iii) Maximum Electron Density:**

From $f_c = 9 \sqrt{N_{\max}}$:

$$
N_{\max} = \left(\frac{f_c}{9}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{8 \times 10^6}{9}\right)^2 = (8.889 \times 10^5)^2 = 7.901 \times 10^{11} \;\text{electrons/m}^3
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mark Loss Points:**
> 1. **Unit confusion on $f_c$**: Always convert MHz → Hz before applying $f_c = 9\sqrt{N_{\max}}$. If $f_c = 8$ MHz, the numerical $9\sqrt{N_{\max}}$ formula uses Hz.
> 2. **Angle interpretation**: $\theta_0$ is measured from the **vertical**, not the horizontal. Using $\tan(60°)$ instead of $\tan(30°)$ flips the skip distance by a factor of $\sqrt{3}$.
> 3. **Sec vs Cos**: MUF uses $\sec$, NOT $\cos$. Confusing them costs full marks.
> 4. **Virtual height vs real height**: Skip-distance formula uses **virtual height** $h'$, not the actual layer height.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`

**(a)** With the aid of a neat block diagram, explain the **three modes of radio wave propagation**. Discuss the role of the ionosphere in sky-wave propagation and explain the formation of the D, E, F1, and F2 layers. (7 marks)

**(b)** A transmitter radiating 1 kW at 200 MHz is connected to an antenna of gain 10 dBi. The receiver is at a distance of 80 km with an antenna gain of 8 dBi. Calculate (i) Free-space path loss in dB, (ii) received power in dBm, and (iii) the electric field strength at the receiver. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Three Modes of Radio Wave Propagation:**

1. **Ground Wave (Surface Wave):** Waves that travel along the surface of the Earth. They induce currents in the ground which are attenuated due to finite conductivity. Range is limited to a few hundred km, and attenuation increases with frequency. Vertical polarization is mandatory. Applications: AM broadcast, maritime communication, Loran-C.

2. **Sky Wave (Ionospheric):** Waves refracted by the ionosphere. The ionosphere consists of free electrons, ions, and neutral molecules formed by solar UV/X-ray ionization. The wave bends back to Earth because the refractive index decreases with altitude (electron density increases). Frequencies 3–30 MHz can be reflected for long-distance (intercontinental) communication.

3. **Space Wave (LOS/Tropospheric):** Waves that travel in straight line between transmit and receive antennas. Used for VHF/UHF and above (TV, FM, cellular, satellite). The line-of-sight range depends on antenna heights. Limited by Earth's curvature.

**Ionosphere Layer Formation:**

| Layer | Height (km) | Formation | Behaviour |
|-------|-------------|-----------|-----------|
| D | 60 – 90 | Low-energy X-ray ionization of NO | Daytime only; absorbs HF strongly; vanishes at night |
| E | 90 – 150 | Soft X-ray + UV ionization of O₂ | Refracts low HF; sporadic-E patches occur |
| F1 | 150 – 250 | UV ionization of atomic O | Daytime only; merges with F2 at night |
| F2 | 250 – 400 | UV ionization of atomic O | Highest electron density; persists day & night; primary layer for HF sky-wave |

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $P_t = 1$ kW = 60 dBm, $f = 200$ MHz, $d = 80$ km, $G_t = 10$ dBi, $G_r = 8$ dBi.

**(i) FSPL:**

$$
\text{FSPL} = 32.44 + 20\log(80) + 20\log(200)
$$

$$
= 32.44 + 38.06 + 46.02 = 116.52 \;\text{dB}
$$

**(ii) Received Power:**

$$
P_r = P_t + G_t + G_r - \text{FSPL} = 60 + 10 + 8 - 116.52 = -38.52 \;\text{dBm}
$$

That is, $P_r = 10^{-38.52/10}$ mW $= 1.41 \times 10^{-4}$ mW $= 1.41 \times 10^{-7}$ mW $= 0.141$ nanowatts.

**(iii) Electric Field Strength:**

Using the isotropic-radiator field formula:

$$
E = \frac{\sqrt{30 \, G_t \, P_t}}{d}
$$

Convert $G_t$ to linear: $G_t = 10^{10/10} = 10$.

$$
E = \frac{\sqrt{30 \times 10 \times 1000}}{80{,}000} = \frac{\sqrt{300{,}000}}{80{,}000} = \frac{547.72}{80{,}000} = 6.847 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{V/m}
$$

$$
E \approx 6.85 \;\text{mV/m}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mark Loss Points:**
> 1. **dBm vs dBW vs W**: Always convert $P_t$ into dBm (or dBW) **before** adding gains and subtracting losses. Mixing linear and dB in the same line is the #1 mistake.
> 2. **Gain in dBi**: $G_t$ and $G_r$ in dBi add directly in the link equation only if FSPL uses **isotropic reference**, which is the case here. Do **not** subtract $G_t = 2.15$ dB to convert to dBd unless specified.
> 3. **Unit consistency in $E$ formula**: $d$ must be in **metres**, $P_t$ in **watts**, $G_t$ linear (not dB).
> 4. **FSPL formula valid only in far field**: $d \gg \lambda$. With $f = 200$ MHz, $\lambda = 1.5$ m; $d = 80$ km satisfies this by a wide margin.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> Use this as your **final 30-minute revision** before the KTU ESE exam.

- **Three propagation modes**: Ground wave (≤ 2 MHz), Sky wave (3–30 MHz HF), Space wave (> 30 MHz VHF/UHF/SHF).
- **Ground wave** requires **vertical polarization**; range increases over **sea water** due to higher conductivity.
- **Sky wave** uses the **ionosphere** which has four layers: **D, E, F1, F2**.
- **D layer**: Day-time absorber; **F2 layer**: Most useful refractor for long-distance HF.
- **Critical frequency**: $f_c = 9\sqrt{N_{\max}}$ Hz — apply only for **vertical incidence**.
- **Secant Law**: $\text{MUF} = f_c \sec(\theta_0)$ — frequency that just barely returns for a given angle.
- **Skip distance**: $D_s = 2 h' \tan(\theta_0)$ — measured from transmitter to nearest return point.
- **Skip zone** = zone between ground-wave range and skip distance — **no signal** is received here.
- **Friis equation**: $P_r = P_t G_t G_r (\lambda / 4\pi d)^2$ — fundamental link equation.
- **Free-space path loss** in dB: $\text{FSPL (dB)} = 32.44 + 20\log(d_{\text{km}}) + 20\log(f_{\text{MHz}})$.
- **Line-of-sight range**: $d_{\max}(\text{km}) = \sqrt{17 h_t} + \sqrt{17 h_r}$ with $h$ in metres; uses **effective Earth radius $4R/3$**.
- **Effective Earth radius** accounts for average atmospheric refraction; use $R_{\text{eff}} = 8493$ km.
- **Field strength** at isotropic radiator: $E = \sqrt{30 G_t P_t} / d$ V/m — remember to convert gains to linear and distances to metres.
- **HF bands propagate via sky wave**, **VHF/UHF via space wave**, **MF via ground wave** (default rules of thumb).
- **Atmospheric noise** dominates below ≈ 30 MHz; **thermal/galactic noise** dominates above 1 GHz — affects telemetry link design.
- For telemetry systems (e.g., ISRO launch vehicles), UHF/SHF space-wave links are preferred because they offer **predictable propagation** and **higher bandwidth**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principles of Antennas
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT634 - COMMUNICATION SYSTEM AND TELEMETRY > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Principles of Antennas # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is an Antenna? > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition:** An antenna is a **transducer** designed to transmit or receive electromagnetic waves. It converts guided electromagnetic waves (in transmission lines/waveguides) into unguided electromagnetic waves (in free space) and vice-versa. In other words, it acts as the **interface** between a guided wave system and a free-space propagation medium. In any wireless communication system — whether it is your Wi-Fi router, a satellite TV dish, a mobile phone, or a radar — the antenna is the *only* component that actually "talks to the air." Everything before the antenna exists inside metallic conductors; everything after it exists as invisible electric and magnetic fields travelling at the speed of light. ### 🔍 Intuitive Analogy: The "Speaker-Microphone" of Electromagnetism Imagine a **musical instrument**. A speaker converts electrical signals from an amplifier into sound waves that travel through the air to your ear. A microphone does the reverse — it captures sound waves and converts them back into electrical signals. An antenna does *exactly the same thing*, but with **electromagnetic (EM) waves** instead of sound waves: | Speaker / Microphone (Acoustic) | Antenna (Electromagnetic) | | :--- | :--- | | Converts audio signals ↔ sound waves | Converts RF signals ↔ EM waves | | Air acts as the medium | Free space (vacuum/air) acts as the medium | | Cone vibrates to push air molecules | Charges oscillate to push electric/magnetic fields | So an antenna is essentially the "voice" and "ear" of any wireless device. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** A transmission line (coaxial cable, waveguide) confines the EM wave inside a conductor. An antenna **launches** the wave into free space. Without the antenna, energy stays trapped inside the cable. --- ## 1.2 The Isotropic Radiator (Reference Antenna) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Isotropic Antenna / Isotropic Radiator:** A *hypothetical*, lossless, point-source antenna that radiates electromagnetic energy **equally in all directions** in 3-D space. It is used purely as a **reference standard** for measuring the performance of all *real* antennas. Because the isotropic radiator is impossible to build physically, it serves as the **benchmark** — all real antennas are described *relative to* it. - **Radiation pattern of an isotropic radiator:** A perfect **sphere** centered at the antenna. - **Directivity of an isotropic radiator:** $\mathbf{D = 1}$ (or $0\text{ dBi}$ — the "i" stands for *isotropic*). --- ## 1.3 Why Do Antennas Radiate? An antenna radiates because of **accelerating (or decelerating) electric charges**. A steady DC current has no radiation (charges move at constant velocity). When current oscillates at radio frequencies, the electrons accelerate and decelerate each half-cycle, and this acceleration is what generates electromagnetic radiation. > [!NOTE] > **Hertzian Dipole** (Infinitesimal Dipole): The simplest theoretical antenna — an infinitely thin, infinitely short current element of length $dl$ carrying a uniform, time-varying current $I_0 \cos(\omega t)$. Its radiation fields form the building block for analyzing **all** real antennas. --- ## 1.4 KTU Course Outcomes Mapped to This Topic | CO ID | Course Outcome | Cognitive Level (Bloom's) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **CO2** | Explain the operating principles of antennas and antenna arrays used in telemetry systems. | Understand (L2) | | **CO3** | Analyze antenna radiation characteristics and link budget performance. | Analyze (L4) | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Fundamental Antenna Mechanism — How Radiation Happens The radiation mechanism can be understood in **three logical steps**: 1. **Source of radiation:** Time-varying current $i(t)$ on a conductor. 2. **Charge acceleration:** The current oscillation causes electrons to accelerate, which produces a **time-varying electric field** $\mathbf{E}$. 3. **Field coupling:** A time-varying $\mathbf{E}$ field (per Maxwell's curl equation) automatically generates a **time-varying magnetic field** $\mathbf{H}$, which in turn re-generates the $\mathbf{E}$ field. The fields thus **self-propagate** through free space at speed $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s. > [!NOTE] > The radiated $\mathbf{E}$ and $\mathbf{H}$ fields are always **perpendicular** to each other, **perpendicular** to the direction of propagation, and **in-phase**. They form a **transverse electromagnetic (TEM)** wave. --- ## 2.2 Antenna Performance Parameters These parameters define *how well* an antenna performs its job and are the most heavily tested concepts in KTU examinations. ### 2.2.1 Radiation Pattern A **graphical or mathematical representation** of the antenna's radiation properties as a function of space coordinates (usually spherical: $\theta, \phi$). It is plotted at a **fixed radial distance**. - **Polar plot:** Plot of power flux density vs angle (most common in KTU). - **E-plane pattern:** Contains the **electric field** vector and direction of maximum radiation. - **H-plane pattern:** Contains the **magnetic field** vector and direction of maximum radiation. ### 2.2.2 Radiation Intensity ($U$) Power radiated per unit solid angle: $$U(\theta, \phi) = r^2 \cdot S_{avg}(\theta, \phi)$$ where $S_{avg}$ is the average Poynting vector (power flux density) at distance $r$. ### 2.2.3 Directivity ($D$) The ratio of radiation intensity in a given direction to the **average** radiation intensity over all directions: $$D(\theta, \phi) = \frac{U(\theta, \phi)}{U_{avg}} = \frac{4\pi \cdot U(\theta, \phi)}{P_{rad}}$$ Maximum directivity $D_0$ occurs in the direction of maximum radiation. ### 2.2.4 Antenna Gain ($G$) Directivity **reduced by losses**: $$G = e_{cd} \cdot D$$ where $e_{cd}$ is the **radiation efficiency** ($0 \le e_{cd} \le 1$). Gain takes ohmic/dielectric losses into account; directivity does not. ### 2.2.5 Beamwidth - **Half-Power Beamwidth (HPBW):** Angular separation between the two points on the main lobe where the radiated power drops to **half** (i.e., $-3\text{ dB}$) of the maximum value. - **First-Null Beamwidth (FNBW):** Angular separation between the **first two nulls** on either side of the main lobe. ### 2.2.6 Bandwidth The **range of frequencies** over which the antenna operates satisfactorily (typically defined by SWR $\le 2$ or gain drop $\le 3$ dB). ### 2.2.7 Antenna Efficiency $$e_{cd} = \frac{P_{rad}}{P_{input}} = \frac{R_{rad}}{R_{rad} + R_{loss}}$$ ### 2.2.8 Input Impedance $$Z_{in} = R_{in} + jX_{in} = (R_{rad} + R_{loss}) + jX_{in}$$ For a resonant half-wave dipole, $Z_{in} \approx 73 + j42.5\ \Omega$, which is matched to the standard **$50\ \Omega$** coaxial line. ### 2.2.9 Effective Aperture ($A_e$) The effective area "captured" by an antenna from an incident wave: $$A_e = \frac{\lambda^2}{4\pi} \cdot G$$ ### 2.2.10 Polarization The orientation of the **electric field vector** of the radiated wave as a function of time. Examples: **Linear** (vertical/horizontal), **Circular** (RHCP/LHCP), **Elliptical**. > [!IMPORTANT] > A **polarization mismatch loss** occurs when the transmitting and receiving antennas have different polarizations. Two linearly polarized antennas rotated by $90^\circ$ experience **complete signal loss** (cross-polarization isolation). --- ## 2.3 📋 KTU Formula Cheat-Sheet | # | Parameter | Formula | Units / Notes | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | Radiation Intensity | $U = r^2 S_{avg}$ | W / steradian | | 2 | Directivity | $D = 4\pi U_{max} / P_{rad}$ | Dimensionless (linear) | | 3 | Directivity (dBi) | $D_{dBi} = 10 \log_{10}(D)$ | Decibels w.r.t isotropic | | 4 | Gain | $G = e_{cd} \cdot D$ | Dimensionless (linear) | | 5 | Gain (dBi) | $G_{dBi} = 10 \log_{10}(G)$ | Decibels | | 6 | Radiation Efficiency | $e_{cd} = R_{rad} / (R_{rad} + R_{loss})$ | $0 \le e_{cd} \le 1$ | | 7 | Effective Aperture | $A_e = (\lambda^2 / 4\pi) \cdot G$ | m² | | 8 | Maximum Effective Aperture | $A_{em} = (\lambda^2 / 4\pi) \cdot D$ | m² (lossless case) | | 9 | Friis Transmission Eqn. | $P_r = P_t G_t G_r (\lambda / 4\pi R)^2$ | Watts | | 10 | Path Loss (FSPL) | $PL_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(4\pi R / \lambda)$ | dB | | 11 | Half-Wave Dipole $D_0$ | $D_0 = 1.643$ | $= 2.15$ dBi | | 12 | Wavelength | $\lambda = c / f$ | m (with $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s) | | 13 | Hertzian Dipole $R_{rad}$ | $R_{rad} = 80\pi^2 (dl/\lambda)^2$ | Ohms | > [!NOTE] > ⚠️ **KTU Valuation Tip:** Always clearly state whether directivity/gain values are in **dBi** (relative to isotropic) or **dBd** (relative to half-wave dipole). The relation is: $D_{dBi} = D_{dBd} + 2.15$. --- ## 2.4 Real-World Utility - **Telecommunications:** Cell-tower sectoral antennas use **high gain (15–21 dBi)** narrow-beam patterns to focus energy into specific cell sectors. - **Telemetry in Spacecraft:** High-gain parabolic dish antennas on satellites concentrate weak signals from millions of kilometres away. - **Radar Systems:** Highly directional antennas with sharp HPBW ($\le 2^\circ$) are used to pinpoint targets. - **RFID / IoT:** Compact patch antennas with circular polarization ensure orientation-insensitive reading of tags. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Radiated Fields of a Hertzian Dipole The Hertzian dipole is the **starting point** for understanding all antenna analysis. Let us derive its far-field components step-by-step. ### Step 1 — Setup An infinitesimal current element of length $dl$ carrying uniform current $I_0 \cos(\omega t)$ is placed at the origin, oriented along the z-axis. ### Step 2 — Vector Potential The magnetic vector potential at a far-field point $P(r, \theta, \phi)$ is: $$A_z = \frac{\mu_0 \, I_0 \, dl}{4\pi r} \, e^{-j\beta r}$$ where $\beta = 2\pi/\lambda$ is the phase constant. ### Step 3 — Compute the Magnetic Field $H_\phi$ Since $\mathbf{H} = \nabla \times \mathbf{A}$, in spherical coordinates: $$H_\phi = \frac{\beta^2 \, I_0 \, dl \sin\theta}{4\pi} \left(\frac{j}{\beta r} + \frac{1}{(\beta r)^2}\right) e^{-j\beta r}$$ ### Step 4 — Apply the Far-Field Approximation In the **far-field (Fraunhofer) region**, $\beta r \gg 1$, so the $1/(\beta r)^2$ term is negligible. Only the $1/(\beta r)$ term survives: $$H_\phi = j \frac{\beta \, I_0 \, dl \sin\theta}{4\pi r} \, e^{-j\beta r}$$ ### Step 5 — Compute the Electric Field $E_\theta$ Using the far-field relation $E_\theta = \eta \, H_\phi$, where $\eta = 120\pi \approx 377\ \Omega$ is the intrinsic impedance of free space: $$E_\theta = j \frac{\eta \, \beta \, I_0 \, dl \sin\theta}{4\pi r} \, e^{-j\beta r}$$ Substituting $\beta = 2\pi/\lambda$: $$E_\theta = j \frac{60\pi \, I_0 \, dl \sin\theta}{r \lambda} \, e^{-j\beta r}$$ ### Step 6 — Average Power Flux Density (Poynting Vector) $$S_{avg} = \frac{\vert E_\theta \vert^2}{2\eta} = \frac{15 \pi (I_0 \, dl)^2 \sin^2\theta}{r^2 \lambda^2}$$ ### Step 7 — Total Radiated Power Integrate $S_{avg}$ over a sphere of radius $r$: $$P_{rad} = \oint S_{avg} \, r^2 \sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi = \frac{15 \pi (I_0 \, dl)^2}{\lambda^2} \int_0^{2\pi}\!\! d\phi \int_0^\pi \sin^3\theta \, d\theta$$ Using the standard integral $\int_0^\pi \sin^3\theta \, d\theta = 4/3$: $$P_{rad} = \frac{15 \pi (I_0 \, dl)^2}{\lambda^2} \cdot 2\pi \cdot \frac{4}{3} = 40 \pi^2 (I_0 \, dl / \lambda)^2$$ ### Step 8 — Radiation Resistance Since $P_{rad} = \tfrac{1}{2} I_0^2 R_{rad}$: $$\boxed{R_{rad} = 80 \pi^2 \left(\frac{dl}{\lambda}\right)^2 \approx 790 \left(\frac{dl}{\lambda}\right)^2 \ \Omega}$$ This is the most tested formula for the Hertzian dipole in KTU exams. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Friis Transmission Equation This equation is the **foundation of every wireless link-budget calculation**. ### Step 1 — Transmitted Power Flux Density at the Receiver A transmitting antenna of gain $G_t$ radiates total power $P_t$ isotropically-equivalent, so the flux density at distance $R$ is: $$S_R = \frac{P_t \, G_t}{4\pi R^2}$$ ### Step 2 — Power Captured by the Receiving Antenna The receiving antenna of effective aperture $A_e$ captures: $$P_r = S_R \cdot A_e = \frac{P_t \, G_t \, A_e}{4\pi R^2}$$ ### Step 3 — Substitute Effective Aperture From the relation $A_e = (\lambda^2 / 4\pi) \cdot G_r$: $$P_r = \frac{P_t \, G_t}{4\pi R^2} \cdot \frac{\lambda^2 \, G_r}{4\pi}$$ $$\boxed{P_r = P_t \, G_t \, G_r \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi R}\right)^2}$$ In **decibel form** (the form most commonly asked in KTU): $$\boxed{P_{r,dBm} = P_{t,dBm} + G_{t,dBi} + G_{r,dBi} - 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi R}{\lambda}\right)}$$ The term $20\log_{10}(4\pi R / \lambda)$ is called the **Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL)**. ### Step 4 — Worked Example (KTU Style) > A transmitter radiates $P_t = 10$ W at $f = 3$ GHz using a parabolic dish of gain $G_t = 30$ dBi. The receiver uses a horn antenna of gain $G_r = 20$ dBi at a distance of $R = 50$ km. Calculate the received power. **Solution:** $$\lambda = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{3 \times 10^9} = 0.1 \text{ m}$$ $$P_{t,dBm} = 10 \log_{10}(10 \times 1000) = 40 \text{ dBm}$$ $$FSPL = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi \times 50000}{0.1}\right) = 20\log_{10}(6.283 \times 10^6) \approx 135.96 \text{ dB}$$ $$P_{r,dBm} = 40 + 30 + 20 - 135.96 = -45.96 \text{ dBm}$$ --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Common KTU Numerical Patterns | Problem Type | Given | Find | Core Formula | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Link Budget | $P_t, G_t, G_r, f, R$ | $P_r$ | Friis Equation | | Directivity from Pattern | $U(\theta, \phi)$ function | $D_0$ | $D = 4\pi U_{max} / P_{rad}$ | | Hertzian Dipole Power | $I_0, dl, \lambda$ | $P_{rad}, R_{rad}$ | $R_{rad} = 80\pi^2(dl/\lambda)^2$ | | Effective Aperture | $G, f$ | $A_e$ | $A_e = \lambda^2 G / 4\pi$ | | Gain ↔ Directivity | $D, R_{rad}, R_{loss}$ | $G$ | $G = e_{cd} \cdot D$ | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Architecture of an Antenna in a Communication Link ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Information Source
e.g., voice, data] --> B[Baseband
Signal Processor] B --> C[Modulator
Mixer] C --> D[RF Power
Amplifier] D --> E[Transmission Line
Coaxial Cable] E --> F[Transmitting
Antenna] F -- Free-Space EM Wave --> G[Receiving
Antenna] G --> H[Receiver
LNA] H --> I[Demodulator] I --> J[Output Sink
e.g., speaker, display] style A fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366 style F fill:#ffd966,stroke:#806000 style G fill:#ffd966,stroke:#806000 style J fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366 ``` --- ## 4.2 Antenna Parameter Hierarchy ```mermaid graph TD A[Antenna Parameters] --> B[Radiation Properties] A --> C[Electrical Properties] A --> D[Geometric Properties] B --> B1[Radiation Pattern] B --> B2[Directivity D] B --> B3[Beamwidth HPBW] B --> B4[Polarization] B --> B5[Radiation Intensity U] C --> C1[Gain G] C --> C2[Efficiency e_cd] C --> C3[Input Impedance Z_in] C --> C4[Bandwidth BW] C --> C5[Effective Aperture A_e] D --> D1[Physical Size] D --> D2[Shape Geometry] C1 -- G = e_cd * D --> B2 C2 -- e_cd = R_rad / R_rad + R_loss --> C3 style A fill:#b3d9ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000 style B fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,color:#000 style C fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#d6b656,color:#000 style D fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450,color:#000 ``` --- ## 4.3 Field Region Diagram (Near-Field vs Far-Field) ```mermaid flowchart LR ANT[Antenna
at origin] --- NF[Reactive Near-Field
0 to 0.62 sqrt D^3 / lambda] NF --- RN[Radiating Near-Field
Fresnel Region
0.62 sqrt D^3/lambda to 2D^2/lambda] RN --- FF[Far-Field
Fraunhofer Region
R greater than 2D^2/lambda] style ANT fill:#ffd966,stroke:#806000 style NF fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450 style RN fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#d6b656 style FF fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Fraunhofer Distance:** $R_{ff} = \dfrac{2D^2}{\lambda}$, where $D$ is the largest dimension of the antenna. Most antenna measurements and the Friis equation are valid **only** in the far-field region. --- ## 4.4 Classification of Antennas (KTU Block Diagram) ```mermaid graph TD ANT[Antennas] --> WIRE[Wire Antennas] ANT --> APER[Aperture Antennas] ANT --> MIC[Microwave Antennas] ANT => ARRAY[Array Antennas] WIRE --> W1[Dipole] WIRE --> W2[Monopole] WIRE --> W3[Loop] WIRE --> W4[Helical] APER --> A1[Horn] APER --> A2[Parabolic Reflector] APER --> A3[Slot Antenna] MIC --> M1[Patch / Microstrip] MIC --> M2[Dielectric Resonator] ARRAY --> AR1[Linear Array] ARRAY --> AR2[Planar Array] ARRAY --> AR3[Phased Array] style ANT fill:#b3d9ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000 style WIRE fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,color:#000 style APER fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#d6b656,color:#000 style MIC fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450,color:#000 style ARRAY fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#9673a6,color:#000 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` — 3 Marks **Define the term "Directivity" of an antenna. State the directivity of a half-wave dipole antenna.** **Model Answer:** > Directivity of an antenna is the ratio of the radiation intensity in a given direction to the average radiation intensity over all directions. > Mathematically, $D(\theta, \phi) = \dfrac{U(\theta, \phi)}{U_{avg}} = \dfrac{4\pi \, U_{max}}{P_{rad}}$. > The maximum directivity of a half-wave dipole is $D_0 = 1.643$, which corresponds to **2.15 dBi**. --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — 3 Marks **What is the significance of an "Isotropic Radiator" in antenna theory?** **Model Answer:** > An isotropic radiator is a hypothetical, lossless, point-source antenna radiating energy **equally in all directions**. Its radiation pattern is a perfect sphere. It serves as a **reference standard** for expressing the directivity and gain of all real antennas. Directivity expressed in **dBi** (decibels relative to isotropic) is the most common gain figure used in link-budget calculations. --- ## 📗 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Choose ONE) ### ✅ CHOICE A — `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` — 14 Marks **(a) Derive the expression for the radiation resistance of a Hertzian dipole. (7 Marks)** **Step 1:** Far-zone electric field of an infinitesimal current element $I_0 dl$: $$E_\theta = j\frac{60\pi \, I_0 \, dl \, \sin\theta}{r \lambda} \, e^{-j\beta r}$$ **Step 2:** Average Poynting vector: $$S_{avg} = \frac{\vert E_\theta \vert^2}{2\eta} = \frac{15\pi (I_0 dl)^2 \sin^2\theta}{r^2 \lambda^2}$$ **Step 3:** Total radiated power by integrating over a sphere of radius $r$: $$P_{rad} = \int_0^{2\pi}\!\!\int_0^\pi S_{avg} \, r^2 \sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi$$ **Step 4:** Simplifying using $\int_0^\pi \sin^3\theta \, d\theta = \tfrac{4}{3}$ and $\int_0^{2\pi} d\phi = 2\pi$: $$P_{rad} = \frac{15\pi (I_0 dl)^2}{\lambda^2} \cdot 2\pi \cdot \frac{4}{3} = 40\pi^2 \left(\frac{I_0 dl}{\lambda}\right)^2$$ **Step 5:** Equating with $P_{rad} = \tfrac{1}{2} I_0^2 R_{rad}$: $$\boxed{R_{rad} = 80\pi^2 \left(\frac{dl}{\lambda}\right)^2 \approx 790 \left(\frac{dl}{\lambda}\right)^2 \ \Omega}$$ **(b) Explain the following antenna parameters with units: (i) Radiation Intensity, (ii) Antenna Gain, (iii) Effective Aperture. (7 Marks)** **(i) Radiation Intensity ($U$):** Defined as the power radiated per unit solid angle by the antenna: $U(\theta,\phi) = r^2 \, S_{avg}(\theta,\phi)$. **Unit:** Watts per steradian (W/sr). **(ii) Antenna Gain ($G$):** Gain is the directivity multiplied by the radiation efficiency: $G = e_{cd} \, D = \dfrac{4\pi \, U_{max}}{P_{input}}$. Gain accounts for ohmic and dielectric losses, whereas directivity does not. **Unit:** Dimensionless (linear) or **dBi** (decibels relative to isotropic). **(iii) Effective Aperture ($A_e$):** It is the effective area "captured" by a receiving antenna from an incident plane wave: $$A_e = \frac{\lambda^2}{4\pi} \, G$$ **Unit:** Square metres (m²). --- ### ✅ CHOICE B — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` — 14 Marks **(a) State and derive the Friis Transmission Equation. Discuss its significance. (7 Marks)** **Statement:** The Friis transmission equation gives the received power $P_r$ in terms of the transmitted power $P_t$, antenna gains $G_t$, $G_r$, distance $R$, and wavelength $\lambda$: $$P_r = P_t \, G_t \, G_r \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi R}\right)^2$$ **Derivation:** Consider two lossless antennas separated by distance $R$ in free space. 1. Power flux density at distance $R$ from a $G_t$ gain antenna: $S_R = \dfrac{P_t G_t}{4\pi R^2}$ 2. Power captured by an aperture $A_e$ at the receiver: $P_r = S_R \cdot A_e$ 3. Substitute $A_e = \dfrac{\lambda^2 G_r}{4\pi}$: $$P_r = \frac{P_t G_t G_r \lambda^2}{(4\pi R)^2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad P_r = P_t G_t G_r \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi R}\right)^2$$ **Significance:** - Foundation of all wireless link-budget analysis. - Predicts how received power scales as $1/R^2$ (inverse-square law in free space). - Identifies that **higher frequencies (smaller $\lambda$) suffer higher path loss** for the same $R$. - Essential for designing telemetry links, radar links, satellite communication. **(b) A transmitter operating at 6 GHz radiates 1 kW with an antenna of gain 40 dBi. A receiver antenna with gain 30 dBi is located 30 km away. Calculate the received power. (7 Marks)** **Step 1 — Wavelength:** $$\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{6 \times 10^9} = 0.05 \text{ m}$$ **Step 2 — Convert $P_t$ to dBm:** $$P_t = 1\text{ kW} = 1000\text{ W} = 10^{6}\text{ mW} \Rightarrow P_{t,dBm} = 60\text{ dBm}$$ **Step 3 — Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL):** $$FSPL = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi R}{\lambda}\right) = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi \times 30000}{0.05}\right) = 20\log_{10}(7.54 \times 10^6)$$ $$FSPL \approx 137.55 \text{ dB}$$ **Step 4 — Apply Friis Equation in dB:** $$P_{r,dBm} = P_{t,dBm} + G_{t,dBi} + G_{r,dBi} - FSPL$$ $$P_{r,dBm} = 60 + 40 + 30 - 137.55 = -7.55 \text{ dBm}$$ **Step 5 — Convert back to Watts:** $$P_r = 10^{(-7.55/10)} \text{ mW} = 0.1755 \text{ mW} = \boxed{175.5 \ \mu\text{W}}$$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout ⚠️ — Common Mark-Deduction Traps:** > 1. **Mixing dBi and dBd:** Many students write $D_{dBi} = D_{dBd}$. The correct relation is $D_{dBi} = D_{dBd} + 2.15$. > 2. **Forgetting to convert W → dBm:** Direct substitution of watts into the Friis equation in dB form costs **2 marks** instantly. > 3. **Sign error in $20\log_{10}$:** The path loss is **subtracted** in the link-budget equation, not added. > 4. **Using wrong aperture formula:** Some students write $A_e = \lambda^2 D$ instead of $A_e = \lambda^2 G/4\pi$ — remember the $4\pi$ comes from the isotropic reference. > 5. **Not stating units:** Always explicitly mention $\Omega$ for $R_{rad}$, m² for $A_e$, dBi for $G$. > 6. **Skipping the far-field condition:** When applying Friis equation, ensure $R \ge 2D^2/\lambda$. Examiners often deduct marks if this is not mentioned. --- ## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Principles of Antennas** - ✅ An antenna is a **transducer** between guided waves and free-space EM waves. - ✅ Radiation occurs due to **accelerating charges** (oscillating currents). - ✅ **Isotropic radiator** is a hypothetical reference antenna with $D = 1$ (0 dBi). - ✅ **Directivity ($D$)** = ratio of max radiation intensity to average. No losses included. - ✅ **Gain ($G$)** = directivity × radiation efficiency ($e_{cd}$). Losses included. - ✅ **Radiation efficiency** $e_{cd} = R_{rad} / (R_{rad} + R_{loss})$. - ✅ **Effective aperture** $A_e = \lambda^2 G / (4\pi)$. - ✅ **Friis equation:** $P_r = P_t G_t G_r (\lambda / 4\pi R)^2$; **path loss** $\propto 1/\lambda^2$. - ✅ **Half-wave dipole directivity** = 1.643 (2.15 dBi), $Z_{in} \approx 73\ \Omega$. - ✅ **Hertzian dipole:** $R_{rad} = 80\pi^2 (dl/\lambda)^2 \ \Omega$. - ✅ **dBi** (isotropic) vs **dBd** (half-wave dipole) — differ by **2.15 dB**. - ✅ **Far-field condition:** $R \ge 2D^2/\lambda$ (Fraunhofer distance). - ✅ **Polarization mismatch** causes signal loss; orthogonally polarized antennas receive **zero** power. - ✅ **HPBW** = $-3$ dB beamwidth; **FNBW** = first-null-to-first-null beamwidth. - ✅ Always write the Friis equation in **dB** form for KTU numerical problems.

topics.title: Basic Definitions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT634 - COMMUNICATION SYSTEM AND TELEMETRY
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Basic Definitions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

Welcome to the foundational bedrock of Communication Systems and Telemetry. Before we dive into the architecture of transmitters, receivers, and modulation schemes, we must establish a **unified vocabulary**. Module 2 introduces the precise technical definitions that every subsequent module will build upon.

## 1.1 What is a Signal?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Signal):**
> A **signal** is any physical quantity that varies with time, space, or any other independent variable(s) and contains information about the behavior or nature of a phenomenon.

In plain engineering terms, a signal is the *carrier* of information. Just as spoken words carry the meaning from speaker to listener, an electrical signal carries data from a sensor to a processing unit.

### **Conceptual Analogy / Intuition**
Imagine you are talking to a friend over a tin-can telephone. Your voice creates **sound waves** (the physical signal), which travel along the string. The string is merely the medium; the variations in air pressure are what actually *carry* the meaning. In electronics, voltage or current variations serve the same role — they are the **electrical signals**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** For KTU 2024, you must distinguish between *signal* (the physical representation) and *information* (the underlying meaning). A signal can exist without information (pure noise), but information without a signal cannot be transmitted.

## 1.2 What is a Communication System?

A **communication system** is the complete arrangement of equipment and procedures used to transfer information (voice, data, video, telemetry measurements) from a source to a destination, often across a distance.

### **Conceptual Analogy / Intuition**
Think of a postal service. You write a letter (the *message*), put it in an envelope (*encoding/transduction*), it travels through trucks and planes (*channel*), and finally reaches the receiver (*decoding*). A communication system is the electronic postal service for signals.

## 1.3 What is Telemetry?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Telemetry):**
> **Telemetry** is the process of automatically measuring and transmitting data from remote, inaccessible, or hazardous locations to a receiving station where the data is recorded, processed, and displayed.

The word *telemetry* originates from Greek roots: *tele* (remote) + *metron* (measure). Literally, it means **"remote measurement."**

### **Conceptual Analogy / Intuition**
Consider a spacecraft flying past Mars. It cannot transmit raw physical readings without converting them. Telemetry is the entire pipeline that:
1. **Senses** the temperature, pressure, radiation level.
2. **Converts** these to electrical signals.
3. **Encodes & Transmitsts** them back to Earth.
4. **Displays** them to engineers millions of kilometers away.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Telemetry is *not* just "wireless communication." It is specifically focused on *measurement* data acquisition from remote sources, often with one-way transmission (uplink vs downlink).

## 1.4 Fundamental Quantity Definitions

| Term | Formal Definition | Unit (SI) |
|------|-------------------|-----------|
| **Amplitude** | Peak value of a sinusoidal signal from its mean | Volts (V) |
| **Frequency (f)** | Number of complete cycles per unit time | Hertz (Hz) |
| **Period (T)** | Time taken for one complete cycle | Seconds (s) |
| **Wavelength (λ)** | Spatial distance covered by one cycle | Meters (m) |
| **Phase (φ)** | Fractional offset of waveform within a cycle | Radians (rad) |
| **Bandwidth (BW)** | Width of the frequency spectrum occupied | Hertz (Hz) |
| **Power (P)** | Rate at which energy is transferred by the signal | Watts (W) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Constants Used in Definitions:**
> - **Speed of Light in vacuum: c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s**
> - **Intrinsic Impedance of free space: η₀ = 377 Ω (≈ 120π Ω)**
> - **Boltzmann's Constant: k = 1.38 × 10⁻²³ J/K**

## 1.5 The Master Definition Block

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Four Pillars of Communication:**
> 1. **Source** — Originates the message/information.
> 2. **Transmitter** — Converts message into a transmittable signal.
> 3. **Channel** — The medium (wire, fiber, air) that carries the signal.
> 4. **Receiver** — Recovers the original message from the received signal.
> 5. **Destination** — The final user of the information.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

In this section, we break down the theoretical framework behind the core definitions, equipping you with the analytical depth required for 14-mark problems.

## 2.1 The Mathematical Description of a Signal

Mathematically, a signal is represented as a function of one or more independent variables. The most common form is a function of time.

For a **sinusoidal continuous-time signal**, the canonical form is:

$$s(t) = A \sin(2\pi f t + \phi)$$

Where each parameter carries a specific physical meaning:
- **A** (Amplitude) → Determines signal strength.
- **f** (Frequency) → Determines rate of oscillation.
- **φ** (Phase) → Determines time alignment.

### **The "Why" Behind Each Parameter**
- **Amplitude matters** because it directly determines the **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)** at the receiver.
- **Frequency matters** because the channel may attenuate certain frequencies differently (frequency-selective fading).
- **Phase matters** because in digital modulation (PSK, QAM), information is encoded in phase changes.

## 2.2 Classification of Signals (KTU High-Yield Topic)

Signals are classified along **five primary axes** — all high-frequency exam topics:

### **Axis 1: Continuous-Time vs. Discrete-Time**
- **Continuous-time (CT):** Defined for all real values of $t \in \mathbb{R}$. Example: $x(t) = 5\sin(100\pi t)$.
- **Discrete-time (DT):** Defined only at specific instants. Example: $x[n] = 5\sin(0.5n)$.

### **Axis 2: Analog vs. Digital**
- **Analog:** Amplitude can take any real value within a continuous range.
- **Digital:** Amplitude takes values from a finite set (bits).

> [!NOTE]
> **Pitfall:** All digital signals are discrete in time *and* discrete in amplitude. All analog signals are continuous in time *and* continuous in amplitude. These are independent classifications.

### **Axis 3: Periodic vs. Aperiodic**
- **Periodic:** $x(t) = x(t + T_0)$ for all $t$, where $T_0$ is the **fundamental period**.
- **Aperiodic:** Does not repeat.

### **Axis 4: Deterministic vs. Random**
- **Deterministic:** Can be described by an explicit mathematical equation.
- **Random:** Must be described statistically (mean, variance, PDF).

### **Axis 5: Energy vs. Power Signals**
- **Energy signal:** Total energy is finite and non-zero; average power = 0.
- **Power signal:** Total energy is infinite; average power is finite and non-zero.

## 2.3 The Frequency Domain Connection

A critical definition in communication is the **Fourier Transform**, which mathematically links the time-domain signal to its frequency-domain representation.

For a non-periodic energy signal $x(t)$:

$$X(f) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(t) e^{-j2\pi f t} dt$$

The inverse transform:

$$x(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} X(f) e^{j2\pi f t} df$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in Communication:** Bandwidth, channel capacity, spectral efficiency — every key performance metric is defined in the **frequency domain**, not the time domain.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is HIGH-YIELD for KTU board exams.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Boundary Conditions |
|---|---------|---------|-----------------------------|
| 1 | Signal Power | $P = \frac{A^2}{2}$ (sinusoid) | Watts; A in peak Volts |
| 2 | Signal Energy | $E = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt$ | Joules; finite for energy signals |
| 3 | Average Power | $P = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{T} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt$ | Watts; non-zero for power signals |
| 4 | Frequency–Period Relation | $f = \dfrac{1}{T}$ | Hz; $T > 0$ |
| 5 | Wavelength Relation | $\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f} = cT$ | meters; $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s |
| 6 | Angular Frequency | $\omega = 2\pi f = \dfrac{2\pi}{T}$ | rad/s |
| 7 | Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) | $\text{SNR} = \dfrac{P_{\text{signal}}}{P_{\text{noise}}}$ | Dimensionless; often in dB |
| 8 | SNR in Decibels | $\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{P_s}{P_n}\right)$ | Decibels (dB) |
| 9 | Noise Spectral Density | $N_0 = kT$ | W/Hz; $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K |
| 10 | Channel Capacity (Shannon) | $C = B \log_2\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | Bits/sec; B in Hz |
| 11 | Information Content | $I = -\log_2(p)$ | Bits; $p$ = probability |
| 12 | Entropy (Avg. Information) | $H = -\sum p_i \log_2(p_i)$ | Bits/symbol |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Definition | Real-World Application |
|------------|----------------------|
| Bandwidth | Designing RF filters for 4G/5G base stations |
| SNR | Setting minimum power levels for satellite uplinks |
| Telemetry | ISRO's INSAT satellite health monitoring |
| Wavelength | Designing antenna physical size ($\lambda/2$ dipole) |
| Shannon Capacity | Determining maximum data rate of fiber-optic links |
| Entropy | Designing data compression algorithms (ZIP, MP3) |
| Noise Spectral Density | Calculating minimum detectable signal in RADAR |
| Information Content | Error correction coding (Hamming, Reed-Solomon) |
| Phase | Coherent demodulation in PSK systems |
| Period | Clock recovery in digital receivers |
