# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

In this section, we perform **complete, line-by-line derivations** of the foundational formulas. No steps are skipped — this is the level of rigor expected in a 14-mark KTU ESE answer.

## 3.1 Derivation: Energy and Power of a Sinusoidal Signal

> [!NOTE]
> **Why derive this?** Signal energy and power are foundational to SNR calculations, which appear in *every* communication and telemetry problem.

### **Step 1 — Start with the standard definition of instantaneous power.**

For any electrical signal $x(t)$ across a 1 Ω resistor, the **instantaneous power** is defined as:

$$p(t) = \frac{\vert x(t) \vert^2}{R}$$

We normalize to $R = 1$ Ω (a standard convention in signal theory), so:

$$p(t) = \vert x(t) \vert^2$$

### **Step 2 — Define the total energy of the signal.**

The total energy is the time-integral of instantaneous power over all time:

$$E = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^2 dt$$

### **Step 3 — Define the average power over an interval of length 2T.**

$$P_T = \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{T} \vert x(t) \vert^2 dt$$

### **Step 4 — Take the limit as T → ∞ to obtain the true average power.**

$$P = \lim_{T \to \infty} P_T = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{T} \vert x(t) \vert^2 dt$$

### **Step 5 — Apply these definitions to the sinusoid $x(t) = A\sin(2\pi f_0 t + \phi)$.**

First, compute the squared magnitude:

$$\vert x(t) \vert^2 = A^2 \sin^2(2\pi f_0 t + \phi)$$

### **Step 6 — Use the trigonometric identity.**

Recall that $\sin^2(\theta) = \dfrac{1 - \cos(2\theta)}{2}$. Therefore:

$$\vert x(t) \vert^2 = \frac{A^2}{2} - \frac{A^2}{2}\cos(2(2\pi f_0 t + \phi))$$

$$\vert x(t) \vert^2 = \frac{A^2}{2} - \frac{A^2}{2}\cos(4\pi f_0 t + 2\phi)$$

### **Step 7 — Integrate over one full period $T_0 = \dfrac{1}{f_0}$.**

The cosine term integrates to zero over a complete period (it is orthogonal to the DC term):

$$\int_{0}^{T_0} \cos(4\pi f_0 t + 2\phi) \, dt = 0$$

Therefore:

$$\int_{0}^{T_0} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt = \int_{0}^{T_0} \frac{A^2}{2} dt = \frac{A^2 T_0}{2}$$

### **Step 8 — Compute the average power.**

$$P = \frac{1}{T_0} \cdot \frac{A^2 T_0}{2} = \frac{A^2}{2}$$

### **Conclusion**

$$\boxed{P_{\text{avg}} = \frac{A^2}{2} \quad \text{(for a sinusoid of peak amplitude } A\text{)}}$$

This is the **average power delivered by a sinusoid across a 1 Ω load**. It is independent of frequency and phase — a critical insight for KTU problems.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Signal-to-Noise Ratio in Decibels

> [!NOTE]
> **Why derive this?** KTU frequently tests conversion between linear and logarithmic SNR, especially in link budget calculations for telemetry.

### **Step 1 — Begin with the linear definition of SNR.**

$$\text{SNR}_{\text{linear}} = \frac{P_s}{P_n}$$

where $P_s$ is the signal power and $P_n$ is the noise power.

### **Step 2 — Apply the definition of the decibel.**

The decibel is defined for a power ratio as:

$$\text{Value in dB} = 10 \log_{10}(\text{Power Ratio})$$

This arises from the **Bel** (named after Alexander Graham Bell), where 1 Bel = 10 decibels. The factor of 10 converts Bels to decibels; the logarithm converts a ratio to a manageable scale.

### **Step 3 — Substitute the SNR expression.**

$$\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_s}{P_n}\right)$$

### **Step 4 — Use logarithm properties to expand.**

$$\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10\left[\log_{10}(P_s) - \log_{10}(P_n)\right]$$

$$\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10\log_{10}(P_s) - 10\log_{10}(P_n)$$

### **Step 5 — Define dBm (decibels relative to 1 milliwatt) for absolute power.**

If we want to express absolute power in dBm:

$$P_{\text{dBm}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P}{1\,\text{mW}}\right)$$

Then SNR in dB is simply:

$$\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = P_{s,\text{dBm}} - P_{n,\text{dBm}}$$

### **Conclusion**

$$\boxed{\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_s}{P_n}\right) = P_{s,\text{dBm}} - P_{n,\text{dBm}}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Voltage vs Power dB Conversion:** For voltage ratios across the same impedance, use $20\log_{10}(V_s/V_n)$ — the factor is 20, not 10, because power scales as $V^2$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Noise Power and Spectral Density

Thermal noise is omnipresent in all electronic systems. Its definition requires **Boltzmann's constant** and the **system temperature**.

### **Step 1 — Define noise spectral density.**

The single-sided **noise power spectral density** (PSD) is the noise power per unit bandwidth:

$$N_0 = k T \quad \text{(in Watts/Hz)}$$

where $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K and $T$ is the absolute temperature in Kelvin.

### **Step 2 — Derive total noise power over a bandwidth B.**

The total noise power is the integral of PSD over the bandwidth:

$$P_n = \int_{0}^{B} N_0 \, df = N_0 \cdot B$$

### **Step 3 — Substitute the PSD expression.**

$$P_n = k T B$$

### **Step 4 — Express noise power in dBm.**

$$P_{n,\text{dBm}} = 10\log_{10}(kTB) + 30$$

The "+30" converts watts to milliwatts ($10\log_{10}(1000) = 30$).

### **Conclusion**

$$\boxed{P_n = kTB \quad \text{and} \quad P_{n,\text{dBm}} = -174 + 10\log_{10}(B_{\text{Hz}}) \text{ at } T = 290 \text{ K}}$$

The **-174 dBm/Hz** figure is the **noise floor at room temperature** — one of the most important numbers in wireless communication.

> [!NOTE]
> **Room Temperature:** $T_0 = 290$ K is the standard IEEE reference. $10\log_{10}(kT_0) = -204$ dBW/Hz = **-174 dBm/Hz**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Wavelength–Frequency Relation

### **Step 1 — Start from the universal wave equation.**

All electromagnetic waves propagate at the speed of light $c$ in vacuum. In one period $T$, the wave travels a distance equal to one wavelength $\lambda$:

$$\lambda = c \cdot T$$

### **Step 2 — Substitute $T = 1/f$.**

$$\lambda = \frac{c}{f}$$

### **Step 3 — Express in a more practical form.**

$$\lambda(\text{m}) = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{f(\text{Hz})}$$

For example:
- $f = 1$ GHz $\Rightarrow \lambda = 0.3$ m = 30 cm
- $f = 100$ MHz $\Rightarrow \lambda = 3$ m

### **Conclusion**

$$\boxed{\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = cT \quad \text{with } c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}}$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Information Content of a Symbol

> [!NOTE]
> **Why derive this?** This is the cornerstone of source coding (data compression) and source-coding theorems in KTU Module 2/3.

### **Step 1 — Define information intuitively.**

If an event has probability $p$ of occurring, its **information content** (or **surprisal**) is the logarithm of the inverse probability:

$$I(p) = \log_2\!\left(\frac{1}{p}\right) = -\log_2(p)$$

**Reasoning:** The rarer an event, the more "surprising" and informative it is when it occurs. If $p \to 1$, $I \to 0$ (no surprise). If $p \to 0$, $I \to \infty$ (maximum surprise).

### **Step 2 — Average over all possible symbols to obtain entropy.**

$$H = \sum_{i=1}^{N} p_i I(p_i) = -\sum_{i=1}^{N} p_i \log_2(p_i)$$

### **Step 3 — Special case: binary equiprobable source.**

For $N = 2$, $p_1 = p_2 = 0.5$:

$$H = -[0.5 \log_2(0.5) + 0.5 \log_2(0.5)] = -[0.5(-1) + 0.5(-1)] = 1 \text{ bit/symbol}$$

This is the maximum entropy of a binary source — **1 bit per symbol**.

### **Conclusion**

$$\boxed{I = -\log_2(p) \quad \text{and} \quad H = -\sum p_i \log_2(p_i)}$$

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

This section contains model questions in the exact KTU 2024 Scheme format — Part A (3 marks) and Part B (14 marks with internal choice).

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Define the following terms with suitable examples:**
1. **Deterministic signal**
2. **Energy signal**
3. **Periodic signal**

**Model Answer (3 marks, ~80 words):**

1. **Deterministic Signal:** A signal whose value can be uniquely determined for all time using a mathematical function or rule. *Example:* $x(t) = 5\cos(100\pi t)$. There is no uncertainty about its past or future values.

2. **Energy Signal:** A signal with finite, non-zero total energy and zero average power. *Example:* $x(t) = e^{-at}u(t)$ for $a > 0$, whose energy is $1/(2a)$ joules. Such signals cannot exist for infinite duration with finite energy unless they decay.

3. **Periodic Signal:** A signal that repeats itself exactly after a fixed interval $T_0$, satisfying $x(t) = x(t + T_0)$ for all $t$. *Example:* $x(t) = A\sin(2\pi f t)$, where $T_0 = 1/f$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse *energy* with *power* signals. A periodic signal (like a sinusoid) is a **power signal** with infinite total energy — not an energy signal. State this clearly to avoid losing 1 mark.

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Define bandwidth. What is the relationship between wavelength and frequency for an electromagnetic wave propagating in free space?**

**Model Answer (3 marks, ~70 words):**

**Bandwidth (BW):** Bandwidth is the width of the range of frequencies occupied by a signal or available in a channel. Mathematically, for a signal with frequency components from $f_1$ to $f_2$:

$$\text{BW} = f_2 - f_1$$

**Wavelength–Frequency Relationship:** In free space, an EM wave propagates at the speed of light $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s. The relationship is:

$$\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = cT$$

where $T$ is the period of the wave.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Bandwidth must always be expressed in **Hz**, not in rad/s. Angular frequency $\omega = 2\pi f$ — be careful not to confuse the two. The question asks for $f$, not $\omega$.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Each Part B question has internal choice — students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each choice has sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks.

---

### **Question A — Choice 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q. (a)** Define a communication system. With the aid of a neat block diagram, explain the function of each block in a generic communication system.

**Q. (b)** Define Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR). A communication system receives a signal of power 50 µW with a noise power of 0.5 µW. Calculate the SNR in dB.

---

#### **Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks**

A **communication system** is an arrangement of electronic and electromechanical equipment designed to transfer information (voice, data, video, telemetry) from a source to a destination. The basic components are:

**Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    info[Information Source] --> tx[Transmitter]
    tx --> ch[Channel]
    ch --> noise[Noise]
    noise --> rx[Receiver]
    rx --> dst[Destination]
    style info fill:#FFE4B5
    style ch fill:#FFB6C1
    style noise fill:#FF6347
    style dst fill:#90EE90
```

**Functions of each block:**

1. **Information Source:** Produces the original message (audio, video, data, sensor reading). Example: microphone, camera, temperature sensor.

2. **Transmitter:** Converts the message into a form suitable for transmission. Includes transducer, amplifier, modulator, and oscillator. It performs modulation to shift the signal to a higher frequency band.

3. **Channel:** The physical medium through which the signal propagates — copper wire, optical fiber, free space (radio), or underwater acoustic path. The channel introduces attenuation, distortion, and noise.

4. **Noise:** Unwanted random signals that get added during transmission. Includes thermal noise, shot noise, and interference from other sources.

5. **Receiver:** Performs the inverse operation of the transmitter — amplification, filtering, demodulation, and decoding — to recover the original message.

6. **Destination:** The final output device that delivers the message to the user — speaker, display, recorder, or control system.

---

#### **Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks**

**Definition:** SNR is the ratio of signal power to noise power at a given point in the system. It quantifies how much the desired signal stands above the background noise.

$$\text{SNR} = \frac{P_s}{P_n}$$

In decibels:

$$\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_s}{P_n}\right)$$

**Numerical Calculation:**

Given:
- $P_s = 50$ µW $= 50 \times 10^{-6}$ W
- $P_n = 0.5$ µW $= 0.5 \times 10^{-6}$ W

**Step 1 — Compute linear SNR.**

$$\text{SNR} = \frac{50 \times 10^{-6}}{0.5 \times 10^{-6}} = 100$$

**Step 2 — Convert to decibels.**

$$\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10\log_{10}(100) = 10 \times 2 = 20 \text{ dB}$$

**Answer:** SNR = **20 dB**.

---

### **Question B — Choice 2 (Alternative to Question A)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q. (a)** Define telemetry. Explain the functional blocks of a typical telemetry system with a block diagram.

**Q. (b)** A sinusoidal signal has peak amplitude 10 V and frequency 1 kHz. Calculate its (i) angular frequency, (ii) period, (iii) RMS value, (iv) average power across 50 Ω.

---

#### **Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** Telemetry is the automated process of measuring physical quantities at a remote or inaccessible location and transmitting the measurement data to a central monitoring station.

**Block Diagram of a Telemetry System:**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    s1[Primary Sensor] --> con[Signal Conditioner]
    s2[Secondary Sensor] --> con
    con --> mux[Multiplexer]
    mux --> enc[Encoder]
    enc --> mod[Modulator]
    mod --> tx[Transmitter]
    tx --> ch[Channel]
    ch --> rx[Receiver]
    rx --> demod[Demodulator]
    demod --> dec[Decoder]
    dec --> demux[Demultiplexer]
    demux --> dsp[Display and Recording]
```

**Functions of each block:**

1. **Primary/Secondary Sensors:** Convert physical quantities (temperature, pressure, flow, vibration) into electrical signals (typically low-level voltages).
2. **Signal Conditioner:** Amplifies, linearizes, and filters the sensor output to a usable level.
3. **Multiplexer (MUX):** Time-shares multiple sensor channels over a single transmission path.
4. **Encoder:** Adds redundancy for error detection/correction (channel coding).
5. **Modulator:** Maps the digital bits onto a carrier waveform (ASK/FSK/PSK).
6. **Transmitter:** Upconverts, filters, and amplifies the signal for the channel.
7. **Channel:** Wireless link (often RF or satellite).
8. **Receiver/Demodulator:** Recovers baseband data.
9. **Decoder/Demultiplexer:** Reverse of encoding/multiplexing.
10. **Display:** Plots, charts, computer screen — human-machine interface.

---

#### **Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks**

**Given:** $V_p = 10$ V (peak amplitude), $f = 1$ kHz, $R = 50$ Ω.

**(i) Angular Frequency:**

$$\omega = 2\pi f = 2\pi \times 1000 = 2000\pi \text{ rad/s} \approx 6283.19 \text{ rad/s}$$

**(ii) Period:**

$$T = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{1000} = 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 1 \text{ ms}$$

**(iii) RMS Value:**

$$V_{\text{rms}} = \frac{V_p}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{10}{\sqrt{2}} = 5\sqrt{2} \approx 7.071 \text{ V}$$

**(iv) Average Power across 50 Ω:**

$$P_{\text{avg}} = \frac{V_{\text{rms}}^2}{R} = \frac{(5\sqrt{2})^2}{50} = \frac{50}{50} = 1 \text{ W}$$

**Equivalently:** $P_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{V_p^2}{2R} = \dfrac{100}{100} = 1$ W. ✓

**Answers:**
- $\omega = 2000\pi$ rad/s
- $T = 1$ ms
- $V_{\text{rms}} \approx 7.07$ V
- $P = 1$ W

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning:** A common student mistake is using the *peak* voltage instead of *RMS* in the power formula. This yields $P = 100/50 = 2$ W — **wrong**. Always use $V_{\text{rms}}$ for average power calculations, or use the equivalent formula $P = V_p^2 / (2R)$ for sinusoids. Either method gives the correct answer of 1 W.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2 — Basic Definitions**

- [ ] **Signal** = physical quantity carrying information; varies with time/space.
- [ ] **Continuous-time signal** defined for all $t$; **discrete-time** defined only at instants $t = nT$.
- [ ] **Analog** = continuous in both amplitude and time. **Digital** = discrete in both.
- [ ] **Periodic** if $x(t) = x(t + T_0)$ for all $t$.
- [ ] **Deterministic** if described by an explicit formula; **Random** if described by statistics.
- [ ] **Energy signal**: finite $E$, zero $P$. **Power signal**: infinite $E$, finite $P$.
- [ ] **Frequency** $f = 1/T$; **Angular frequency** $\omega = 2\pi f$; **Wavelength** $\lambda = c/f$.
- [ ] **Sinusoid average power**: $P = A^2 / 2$ across 1 Ω. Across $R$ Ω: $P = A^2 / (2R)$.
- [ ] **RMS value** of sinusoid: $V_{\text{rms}} = V_p / \sqrt{2}$.
- [ ] **SNR** (linear) $= P_s/P_n$. **SNR (dB)** $= 10\log_{10}(P_s/P_n)$.
- [ ] **Thermal noise**: $P_n = kTB$, where $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K.
- [ ] **Noise floor at 290 K** = **-174 dBm/Hz**.
- [ ] **Channel capacity (Shannon)**: $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$.
- [ ] **Information content**: $I = -\log_2(p)$ bits.
- [ ] **Entropy**: $H = -\sum p_i \log_2(p_i)$ bits/symbol.
- [ ] **Communication system**: Source → Transmitter → Channel (+Noise) → Receiver → Destination.
- [ ] **Telemetry**: Remote measurement + transmission of physical quantities.
- [ ] **Telemetry pipeline**: Sensors → Conditioner → MUX → ADC → Encoder → Modulator → Channel → Demod → Decoder → Display.
- [ ] **Bandwidth** is always measured in Hz, never rad/s.
- [ ] **dB conversion**: $10\log_{10}$ for power, $20\log_{10}$ for voltage.
- [ ] **Speed of light**: $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s in vacuum/free space.

> [!WARNING]
> **Final Examiner's Note:** Always state the **units** of any quantity you compute. KTU examiners typically deduct 0.5 mark per missing unit. Also, distinguish carefully between **linear SNR** (a pure ratio) and **SNR in dB** (logarithmic). Mixing these is the #1 reason students lose marks in Part B numerical questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Antennas
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT634 - COMMUNICATION SYSTEM AND TELEMETRY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Types of Antennas**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEICT634 – Communication System and Telemetry)**, an **antenna** is defined as a transitional structure between free-space and a guiding device (typically a transmission line or waveguide), used for the efficient radiation and reception of electromagnetic (EM) waves. The classification of **Types of Antennas** is broadly performed based on:

1. **Physical structure and geometry** (Wire, Aperture, Array, Microstrip, Reflector).
2. **Radiation behavior** (Isotropic, Directional, Omni-directional).
3. **Frequency of operation** (HF, VHF, UHF, Microwave, mm-Wave).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Students must master the construction, radiation mechanism, and application domains of at least the following classes: **Dipole, Loop, Helical, Horn, Parabolic Reflector, Microstrip Patch, Yagi-Uda, and Log-Periodic Dipole Array (LPDA)**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of an antenna as the **"mouth and ear" of a radio system**:
- When you speak into a megaphone, the megaphone doesn't create the sound—it *shapes* and *directs* the voice coming from your mouth into a narrow, far-reaching beam. An antenna does the same for electrical currents.
- A **loud speaker** in a stadium throws sound in every direction (omni-directional), but a **spotlight** in a theatre focuses light in one narrow direction (highly directional). Different antennas mimic these behaviors at radio frequencies.
- The **transmission line** delivers the electrical signal, the **antenna** converts that guided energy into free-space EM waves, and the surrounding **atmosphere/space** becomes the medium of propagation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Constant:** The speed of light $c \approx \mathbf{3 \times 10^8 \; m/s}$ governs all antenna behavior, linking frequency $f$, wavelength $\lambda$, and physical dimensions through $c = f\lambda$. Every antenna's size is scaled relative to $\lambda$.

## 1.3 The Three Fundamental Antenna Parameters (KTU Recurring Theme)

Before classifying antenna types, you must internalize these three metrics used to *compare* any two antennas:

| Parameter | Symbol | Definition | Unit |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :---: |
| Radiation Intensity | $U$ | Power radiated per unit solid angle | W/steradian |
| Directivity | $D$ | Ratio of radiation intensity in a given direction to the average value | dimensionless |
| Gain | $G$ | Directivity multiplied by radiation efficiency ($\eta$) | dimensionless (dBi) |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification Tree of Antennas

Antennas are categorized into **five major families**, each suited to a specific frequency band and application.

### 2.1.1 Family 1 — Wire Antennas
Wire antennas are the oldest and simplest class. They are fabricated from conducting wires or rods.

- **Half-Wave Dipole:** The canonical reference antenna. Total length $L = \lambda/2$. Fed at the center. Produces a **figure-8 radiation pattern** in the E-plane and a circular pattern in the H-plane.
- **Monopole:** Half of a dipole placed over a conducting ground plane. Length $L = \lambda/4$. Radiates only into the upper hemisphere, providing a gain of **3 dBi** over a dipole.
- **Loop Antenna:** The conductor is formed into a loop (circular or square). *Small loops* (perimeter $\ll \lambda$) behave as magnetic dipoles; *large loops* (perimeter $\approx \lambda$) behave as electric dipoles.
- **Helical Antenna:** A conducting wire wound in a helix. Operates in **normal mode** (broadside, when circumference $C \ll \lambda$) or **axial mode** (end-fire, when $C \approx \lambda$ and pitch $\approx \lambda/4$). Produces **circular polarization**.

### 2.1.2 Family 2 — Aperture Antennas
Used at microwave frequencies ($\geq 1$ GHz) where wire antennas become impractical.

- **Horn Antenna:** A flared extension of a rectangular or circular waveguide. Simple, moderate gain (10–25 dBi), used as a feed for reflectors and in radar systems.
- **Waveguide Slot:** A slot cut into the wall of a waveguide. Radiates when current lines are interrupted.

### 2.1.3 Family 3 — Array Antennas
Multiple identical radiating elements arranged in space and excited with specific amplitudes/phases to shape the overall radiation pattern.

- **Broadside Array:** Maximum radiation perpendicular to the array axis.
- **End-fire Array:** Maximum radiation along the array axis.
- **Yagi-Uda Array:** A driven dipole plus a reflector and one or more directors. The *most widely used* TV reception antenna.
- **Log-Periodic Dipole Array (LPDA):** Dipoles of progressively changing length. Provides **frequency-independent** behavior over a wide band (e.g., 30–1000 MHz).

### 2.1.4 Family 4 — Reflector Antennas
High-gain antennas used in satellite communication, radio astronomy, and deep-space probes.

- **Parabolic Reflector:** A paraboloid of revolution that converts a spherical wavefront from a feed horn at the focus into a plane wave, producing a pencil beam. Gain can exceed **60 dBi** for large dishes.

### 2.1.5 Family 5 — Microstrip (Patch) Antennas
A metallic patch on a grounded dielectric substrate. Low profile, lightweight, conformal to surfaces. Used in mobile phones, GPS, and 5G mm-wave arrays.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Classification

The classification exists because **different physics dominate at different frequencies**:

$$f < 30 \text{ MHz} \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \lambda > 10 \text{ m} \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \text{Wire antennas dominate}$$

$$f > 1 \text{ GHz} \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \lambda < 30 \text{ cm} \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \text{Aperture/Microstrip dominate}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** As frequency rises, wavelengths shrink, and the precision of metal shaping (etching, printing) improves, making planar geometries more attractive than 3D wire structures.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $L = \dfrac{\lambda}{2}$ | Length of a half-wave dipole | meters |
| 2 | $L = \dfrac{\lambda}{4}$ | Length of a quarter-wave monopole over ground plane | meters |
| 3 | $D = \dfrac{4\pi}{\Omega_A}$ | Directivity from beam solid angle $\Omega_A$ | dimensionless |
| 4 | $G = \eta \cdot D$ | Gain equals efficiency times directivity | $\eta \le 1$ |
| 5 | $A_e = \dfrac{G \lambda^2}{4\pi}$ | Effective aperture of an antenna | $m^2$ |
| 6 | $R_{rad} = 73 \; \Omega$ | Radiation resistance of a thin half-wave dipole | ohms |
| 7 | $HPBW \approx \dfrac{70 \lambda}{D_{dish}}$ | Half-Power Beamwidth of a parabolic dish of diameter $D_{dish}$ | degrees |
| 8 | $AF = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} a_n \, e^{j n \psi}$ | Array Factor for a uniform linear array | $\psi = \beta d \cos\theta + \alpha$ |
| 9 | $AR = \dfrac{\text{Major axis}}{\text{Minor axis}}$ | Axial Ratio; AR = 1 $\Rightarrow$ circular polarization | $\ge 1$ |
| 10 | $P_r = P_t G_t G_r \left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi R}\right)^2$ | Friis Transmission Equation | Watts |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe Symbol Trap:** When writing absolute value or beam solid angle in a markdown table, never use the keyboard pipe `|`. Always use LaTeX `\vert` or `\mid` to prevent table syntax corruption. Example: write $\vert \theta \vert$, not $| \theta |$.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Directivity of a Half-Wave Dipole

The directivity is the ratio of maximum radiation intensity $U_{max}$ to the average radiation intensity $U_{avg}$.

**Step 1 — Write the average radiation intensity.**

$$U_{avg} = \frac{P_{rad}}{4\pi}$$

**Step 2 — Express the directivity in terms of the beam solid angle $\Omega_A$.**

By definition, the beam solid angle is:

$$\Omega_A = \frac{P_{rad}}{U_{max}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad U_{max} = \frac{P_{rad}}{\Omega_A}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the directivity formula.**

$$D_0 = \frac{U_{max}}{U_{avg}} = \frac{P_{rad} / \Omega_A}{P_{rad} / (4\pi)} = \frac{4\pi}{\Omega_A}$$

**Step 4 — Apply to the half-wave dipole.** The normalized radiation intensity of a thin $\lambda/2$ dipole is:

$$U(\theta) = U_{max} \sin^3 \theta$$

Integrating over the sphere to find the beam solid angle:

$$\Omega_A = \int_{0}^{2\pi} \int_{0}^{\pi} \sin^3 \theta \sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi = 2\pi \int_{0}^{\pi} \sin^3 \theta \, d\theta$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the integral.**

$$\int_{0}^{\pi} \sin^3 \theta \, d\theta = \int_{0}^{\pi} \sin\theta (1 - \cos^2\theta) d\theta = \frac{4}{3}$$

**Step 6 — Final directivity calculation.**

$$\Omega_A = 2\pi \cdot \frac{4}{3} = \frac{8\pi}{3}$$

$$D_0 = \frac{4\pi}{8\pi/3} = \frac{3}{2} = 1.5 \quad (\approx 1.76 \text{ dBi})$$

**Conclusion:** The half-wave dipole has a directivity of **1.64 (2.15 dBi)** when the exact current distribution and far-field equations are used. The simplified derivation above yields the canonical textbook figure of 1.5, which is the value KTU examiners typically expect.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Array Factor for a Two-Element Array

Consider two isotropic radiators separated by distance $d$, with progressive phase shift $\alpha$.

**Step 1 — Write the total far-field.** With element 1 at origin and element 2 at distance $d$ along the z-axis:

$$E_{total} = E_0 \left(1 + e^{j \psi}\right)$$

where $\psi = \beta d \cos\theta + \alpha$ and $\beta = 2\pi/\lambda$.

**Step 2 — Factor out the term.**

$$E_{total} = E_0 \, e^{j\psi/2} \left(e^{-j\psi/2} + e^{j\psi/2}\right) = 2 E_0 \, e^{j\psi/2} \cos\left(\frac{\psi}{2}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Take the magnitude to obtain the Array Factor.**

$$AF = 2 \left\vert \cos\left(\frac{\psi}{2}\right) \right\vert$$

**Step 4 — Determine the condition for maximum radiation.** The array factor is maximum when $\cos(\psi/2) = \pm 1$, i.e., when $\psi/2 = n\pi$, so $\psi = 2n\pi$.

$$\beta d \cos\theta_{max} + \alpha = 2n\pi$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $\alpha$ given a desired $\theta_{max}$.**

$$\alpha = -\beta d \cos\theta_{max} + 2n\pi$$

> [!NOTE]
> **For broadside radiation** ($\theta_{max} = 90°$): $\cos\theta_{max} = 0$, so $\alpha = 0$ (no progressive phase needed).
> **For end-fire radiation** ($\theta_{max} = 0°$ or $180°$): $\alpha = \mp \beta d$.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Effective Aperture from Friis Equation

Start with the **Friis Transmission Equation**:

$$P_r = P_t G_t G_r \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi R}\right)^2$$

**Step 1 — Define received power in terms of effective aperture.** By definition:

$$P_r = S \cdot A_e$$

where $S$ is the power flux density at the receiver at distance $R$:

$$S = \frac{P_t G_t}{4\pi R^2}$$

**Step 2 — Equate and solve for $A_e$.**

$$P_t G_t \frac{A_e}{4\pi R^2} = P_t G_t G_r \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi R}\right)^2$$

$$A_e = G_r \frac{\lambda^2}{4\pi}$$

**Step 3 — Generalize.** For an antenna with gain $G$:

$$\boxed{A_e = \frac{G \lambda^2}{4\pi}}$$

**Step 4 — Sanity check (isotropic antenna).** $G = 1$, so $A_e = \lambda^2 / 4\pi$. For a 1 GHz signal ($\lambda = 0.3$ m), this is approximately $0.00716 \; m^2$, which matches known textbook values.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Quick-Reference)

| Antenna Type | Typical Frequency Band | Typical Gain | Polarization | Key Application |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| Half-Wave Dipole | HF / VHF / UHF | 2.15 dBi | Linear | Reference / FM broadcast |
| Quarter-Wave Monopole | HF / VHF | 2.15 dBi (over ground) | Linear | Vehicle whip, mobile radio |
| Small Loop | LF / MF / HF | $\le 0$ dBi | Linear | Direction finding, RFID |
| Helical (Axial mode) | VHF / UHF | 10–20 dBi | Circular | Satellite TV, GPS, ISM |
| Horn (Pyramidal) | Microwave | 10–25 dBi | Linear | Radar, anechoic chamber feeds |
| Yagi-Uda | VHF / UHF | 6–15 dBi | Linear | TV reception, amateur radio |
| LPDA | HF / VHF / UHF | 6–8 dBi | Linear | Wideband surveillance, TV |
| Parabolic Reflector | UHF / SHF / EHF | 20–60 dBi | Linear / Circular | Satellite comm, radio astronomy |
| Microstrip Patch | UHF / SHF | 5–8 dBi | Linear / Circular | Mobile handsets, GNSS, 5G |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Antenna Family Classification (Conceptual Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0["ANTENNA TYPES"] --> B0["WIRE ANTENNAS"]
    A0 --> C0["APERTURE ANTENNAS"]
    A0 --> D0["ARRAY ANTENNAS"]
    A0 --> E0["REFLECTOR ANTENNAS"]
    A0 --> F0["MICROSTRIP PATCH"]

    B0 --> B1["Half Wave Dipole"]
    B0 --> B2["Quarter Wave Monopole"]
    B0 --> B3["Loop Antenna"]
    B0 --> B4["Helical Antenna"]

    C0 --> C1["Horn Antenna"]
    C0 --> C2["Waveguide Slot"]
    C0 --> C3["Reflector Backed Aperture"]

    D0 --> D1["Broadside Array"]
    D0 --> D2["End Fire Array"]
    D0 --> D3["Yagi Uda"]
    D0 --> D4["Log Periodic Dipole Array"]

    E0 --> E1["Parabolic Dish"]
    E0 --> E2["Cassegrain"]
    E0 --> E3["Gregorian"]

    F0 --> F1["Rectangular Patch"]
    F0 --> F2["Circular Patch"]
    F0 --> F3["Slotted Patch"]

    B1 --> G1["FM Broadcast Reference"]
    B2 --> G2["Mobile Whip"]
    B3 --> G3["Direction Finding"]
    B4 --> G4["Satellite TV"]
    C1 --> G5["Radar Feeds"]
    D3 --> G6["TV Reception"]
    D4 --> G7["Wideband Monitoring"]
    E1 --> G8["Deep Space Telemetry"]
    F1 --> G9["5G and IoT Devices"]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Yagi-Uda Antenna Signal Flow

The Yagi-Uda is the most frequently asked wire-array antenna in KTU papers. The block diagram below shows how each element contributes to the directional pattern.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    RF["RF Feed Line 50 ohm"] --> DRV["Driven Element Lambda by 2"]
    DRV --> REFL["Reflector Slightly longer than Lambda by 2"]
    DRV --> DIR1["Director 1 Slightly shorter than Lambda by 2"]
    DR1["Director 1"] --> DIR2["Director 2"]
    DR2["Director 2"] --> DIR3["Director 3"]

    REFL -.->|"Mutual Coupling Re radiates Phase delayed"| DRV
    DIR1 -.->|"Mutual Coupling Re radiates Phase advanced"| DRV
    DIR2 -.->|"Mutual Coupling Re radiates Phase advanced"| DR1
    DIR3 -.->|"Mutual Coupling Re radiates Phase advanced"| DR2

    DRV ==> BEAM["Highly Directive Beam Towards Directors End Fire Mode"]
    REFL -.->|"Suppresses Rear Lobe"| BEAM
```

**Explanation of the diagram:**
- The **driven element** is the only one electrically connected to the feed.
- The **reflector** (longer) re-radiates with a phase delay, pushing the wave back toward the directors.
- The **directors** (shorter, progressively) re-radiate with phase advance, "pulling" the wave forward.
- Net effect: a **highly directional end-fire beam** with gain typically 7–12 dBi over a dipole.

## 4.3 Functional Architecture — Parabolic Reflector Telescope-Style Feed

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TX["Transmitter"] --> WG["Waveguide Feed Line"]
    WG --> FH["Feed Horn Located at Focus F"]
    FH -->|"Spherical Wavefront"| PAR["Paraboloid Reflector Diameter D"]
    PAR ==>|"Reflected and Collimated"| PW["Planar Wavefront Narrow Beam"]
    PW --> TAR["Target Direction Far Field"]

    RX["Incoming Plane Wave"] --> PAR
    PAR -->|"Focused to Point F"| FH
    FH --> WG
    WG --> RX2["Receiver Low Noise Amplifier"]
```

**Operating Principle:**
- A point source (feed horn) at the focus of a paraboloid creates a spherical wave. The paraboloid converts it to a plane wave (transmit mode).
- Conversely, an incoming plane wave is focused to the focal point (receive mode).
- The **focal-length-to-diameter ratio** $f/D$ is critical: typical value is **0.3 to 0.5** for minimum spillover and best gain.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question Bank, 2024 Scheme]
**Differentiate between a half-wave dipole and a quarter-wave monopole antenna. Mention the role of the ground plane in the monopole.**

**Model Answer:**

A **half-wave dipole** is a symmetric antenna with two conducting arms, each of length $\lambda/4$, giving a total length of $\lambda/2$. It is fed at the center and radiates in a figure-8 pattern with a directivity of about **2.15 dBi** and a radiation resistance of **73 Ω**.

A **quarter-wave monopole** has only one arm of length $\lambda/4$ mounted vertically over a **conducting ground plane**. The ground plane acts as an *image plane*: it creates a virtual mirror image of the monopole, effectively doubling the radiated field in the upper hemisphere. This results in a **3 dB gain improvement** over a dipole, yielding an effective directivity of **5.15 dBi** above the ground.

**Key Differences (3 marks should cover):**

| Feature | Half-wave Dipole | Quarter-wave Monopole |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Length | $\lambda/2$ | $\lambda/4$ |
| Ground plane | Not required | Mandatory |
| Radiation resistance | 73 Ω | 36.5 Ω |
| Radiation pattern | Figure-8 (full sphere) | Half figure-8 (upper hemisphere) |
| Gain | 2.15 dBi | 2.15 dBi (over perfect ground) |

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question Bank, 2024 Scheme]
**What is a Yagi-Uda antenna? State the function of the reflector and directors.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Yagi-Uda antenna** is a parasitic linear array antenna consisting of a **driven element** (a half-wave dipole fed directly by the transmission line), one **reflector** (slightly longer than $\lambda/2$), and one or more **directors** (progressively shorter than $\lambda/2$).

- **Reflector:** Slightly longer ($\approx 0.5\lambda + 5\%$) than the driven element. It is *not* electrically connected to the feed. Due to mutual coupling, it is excited by the driven element and re-radiates with a **phase delay of about 180°**, which cancels the rear lobe and reinforces the forward direction.

- **Directors:** Slightly shorter ($\approx 0.5\lambda - 5\%$ each) than the driven element. They re-radiate with a **phase advance**, "pulling" the wavefront forward, creating a highly directional end-fire beam. Each additional director increases the gain by roughly 1 dB.

The antenna is widely used in **VHF/UHF television reception** because of its high gain (6–15 dBi), low cost, and simple construction.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the construction and working of a **parabolic reflector antenna**. Define the terms *focal length* and *focal-point feed*. Derive the expression for its **half-power beamwidth (HPBW)** in terms of the dish diameter $D$ and wavelength $\lambda$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A parabolic reflector antenna operates at **6 GHz**. The dish diameter is **2.4 m** and the aperture efficiency is **0.55**. Calculate (i) the **gain in dBi** and (ii) the **HPBW in degrees**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Construction and Working:**

A parabolic reflector antenna consists of a **large metallic paraboloid** of revolution, with a small **feed horn** placed at its focal point $F$. The paraboloid is described by the equation:

$$y^2 = 4 f x$$

where $f$ is the focal length. The feed horn illuminates the dish; rays from $F$ striking the dish are reflected parallel to the **axis of symmetry**, producing a pencil beam.

**Derivation of HPBW:**

The HPBW of a uniformly illuminated circular aperture of diameter $D$ is:

$$HPBW = \frac{58.4 \; \lambda}{D} \quad \text{[degrees]}$$

For typical tapered illumination (aperture efficiency $\varepsilon_{ap}$), the empirical formula is:

$$HPBW \approx \frac{70 \; \lambda}{D} \quad \text{[degrees]}$$

The **focal length** $f$ is the distance from the vertex of the paraboloid to the focus. The **focal-point feed** is the small antenna (usually a horn) placed at $F$ that illuminates the dish.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** $f = 6 \text{ GHz} \Rightarrow \lambda = c/f = 3\times10^8 / 6\times10^9 = 0.05 \text{ m}$, $D = 2.4 \text{ m}$, $\varepsilon_{ap} = 0.55$.

**(i) Gain:**

$$G = \varepsilon_{ap} \left(\frac{\pi D}{\lambda}\right)^2 = 0.55 \times \left(\frac{\pi \times 2.4}{0.05}\right)^2$$

$$G = 0.55 \times (150.796)^2 = 0.55 \times 22739.45 = 12506.7$$

$$G_{dBi} = 10 \log_{10}(12506.7) \approx 40.97 \text{ dBi}$$

**(ii) HPBW:**

$$HPBW = \frac{70 \times 0.05}{2.4} = \frac{3.5}{2.4} \approx 1.46°$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the **array factor** of a uniform linear array (ULA) of $N$ isotropic elements. Derive the **condition for broadside** and **end-fire** radiation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 4-element uniform linear array has element spacing $d = \lambda/2$ and progressive phase shift $\alpha = 0°$. Plot (or describe) the radiation pattern and find the **HPBW**. If $\alpha$ is changed to $90°$, what type of array does it become? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Array Factor Derivation:**

Consider $N$ isotropic elements along the z-axis with uniform spacing $d$ and progressive phase $\alpha$. The array factor is:

$$AF = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} e^{j n \psi} = \frac{\sin(N\psi/2)}{\sin(\psi/2)}$$

where $\psi = \beta d \cos\theta + \alpha$ and $\beta = 2\pi/\lambda$.

**Maximum condition:** $\psi = 2m\pi$, giving $P_{max} = N^2$.

**Broadside condition:** Maximum at $\theta = 90° \Rightarrow \cos\theta = 0$.

$$\beta d \cdot 0 + \alpha = 2m\pi \quad \Rightarrow \quad \alpha = 0$$

**End-fire condition:** Maximum at $\theta = 0°$ or $180° \Rightarrow \cos\theta = \pm 1$.

$$\beta d (\pm 1) + \alpha = 2m\pi \quad \Rightarrow \quad \alpha = \mp \beta d = \mp \frac{2\pi d}{\lambda}$$

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** $N = 4$, $d = \lambda/2$, $\alpha = 0°$.

Since $\alpha = 0$, the array operates in **broadside mode** (max radiation perpendicular to array axis).

$$AF = \frac{\sin(2\psi)}{\sin(\psi/2)}, \quad \psi = \pi\cos\theta$$

**First null** occurs when $N\psi/2 = \pm\pi \Rightarrow \psi = \pm\pi/2$.

$$\pi \cos\theta = \pm \pi/2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \cos\theta = \pm 0.5 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta = 60° \text{ or } 120°$$

**HPBW** $\approx 120° - 60° = 60°$ (approximate, exact value from $\sin(N\psi/2)/\sin(\psi/2) = \sqrt{2}$ yields about 32° for a 4-element array — verify using the standard $0.886 \lambda / (Nd)$ approximation).

For small arrays: $HPBW \approx 0.886 \dfrac{\lambda}{Nd} \cdot \left(\dfrac{180}{\pi}\right) \approx 25.4°$.

**If $\alpha = 90°$ and $d = \lambda/2$:** $\alpha = 90° \ne \mp \beta d = \mp \pi$, so it is **not** a standard end-fire. The main beam **tilts off broadside** in the direction given by $\cos\theta_{max} = -\alpha/(\beta d) = -90°/180° = -0.5$, i.e., $\theta_{max} = 120°$. This is a **phased-array scanning** configuration, useful in electronically steered radar.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do not confuse gain and directivity.** Gain = $\eta \cdot D$. For a lossless antenna $\eta = 1$, but in practice always state the efficiency.
> 2. **Beamwidth units:** HPBW is in *degrees*, beam solid angle $\Omega_A$ in *steradians*. Mixing them costs marks.
> 3. **Array Factor is for *isotropic* elements.** The total field is $E = E_{element} \times AF$ (pattern multiplication). Forgetting the element pattern in Yagi-Uda is a common mistake.
> 4. **Monopole gain quoted over ground plane is NOT 2.15 dBi absolute** — it is 2.15 dBi *relative to a perfect ground*; the absolute dBi can be up to ~5.15 dBi. Read the question carefully.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Five families of antennas:** Wire, Aperture, Array, Reflector, Microstrip. Each dominates a specific frequency band.
- **Half-wave dipole** is the universal reference: $L = \lambda/2$, $R_{rad} = 73\;\Omega$, $D = 1.5$ (1.76 dBi).
- **Monopole** requires a perfect ground plane; gain is **3 dB higher** than a dipole *in the upper hemisphere*.
- **Helical antenna** in axial mode ($C \approx \lambda$, pitch $\approx \lambda/4$) produces **circular polarization** — used in satellite TV and GPS.
- **Yagi-Uda** has one driven element, one reflector (longer), and N directors (shorter); end-fire, linear polarization, 6–15 dBi gain.
- **LPDA** is *frequency-independent* over a wide band; element lengths follow a geometric ratio $\tau$.
- **Parabolic reflector** is a high-gain, narrow-beam antenna: $G = \varepsilon_{ap}(\pi D/\lambda)^2$, $HPBW \approx 70\lambda/D$.
- **Microstrip patch** is low-profile, low-cost, and ideal for mobile and 5G mm-wave applications.
- **Array Factor** formula $AF = \sin(N\psi/2)/\sin(\psi/2)$; broadside requires $\alpha = 0$; end-fire requires $\alpha = \mp\beta d$.
- **Effective aperture** $A_e = G\lambda^2/(4\pi)$ is a fundamental link between gain and receiving capability.
- **Friis transmission equation** $P_r = P_t G_t G_r (\lambda/4\pi R)^2$ — appears in nearly every KTU paper under communication system design.
- Always state **polarization** (linear/circular), **bandwidth**, and **VSWR** when describing an antenna type.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Antennas for Telemetry
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT634 - COMMUNICATION SYSTEM AND TELEMETRY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Antennas for Telemetry

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **antenna** is a transducer that converts guided electromagnetic waves propagating through a transmission line into unguided (free-space) electromagnetic waves, or vice versa, in the radio frequency (RF) spectrum. In the context of telemetry, an **antenna for telemetry** is a specially engineered radiating structure designed to reliably transmit (and often receive) measurement data from a remote, inaccessible, or moving platform to a ground-based or central monitoring station with the required gain, polarization, bandwidth, and radiation-pattern characteristics.

According to IEEE Standard 145-2013, an antenna is defined as *"a means for radiating or receiving radio waves."* In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the antenna forms the **front-end transducer** of every telemetry link, sitting at the boundary between the guided circuit and the propagation medium.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> For Module 2, the antenna section focuses on the radiation principles, antenna parameters (gain, directivity, beamwidth, polarization, effective aperture), and the specific classes of antennas used in terrestrial, mobile, airborne, and spaceborne telemetry links.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Megaphone of the RF World"

Imagine you are standing in the middle of a noisy football stadium and you need to tell a friend in the topmost stand exactly what the score is. If you simply speak, your voice gets drowned in noise. But if you speak into a **megaphone (or use a parabolic reflector)**, all your sound energy gets concentrated and beamed toward your friend — they hear you clearly, even from far away.

An **antenna does exactly the same thing for radio waves**:

- The **transmission line** carries the weak telemetry signal from the sensor/encoder.
- The **antenna** "shouts" this signal into free space, shaping and directing it so it reaches the receiver with sufficient strength.
- At the receiver, the antenna "listens" with the same directional sensitivity, gathering the weak telemetry signal from the noise.

Different telemetry applications need different "shouts":
- A **biomedical telemetry capsule** needs a tiny, low-profile antenna that radiates equally in all directions (like a small whisper in a room).
- A **satellite telemetry downlink** needs a high-gain dish antenna that focuses energy into a narrow pencil beam pointed at Earth (like a powerful megaphone).

> [!NOTE]
> **Reciprocity Principle:** Any antenna works identically for transmission and reception. If an antenna has a high gain in a particular direction when transmitting, it will also have a high gain in that same direction when receiving. This is a fundamental property used extensively in telemetry link-budget calculations.

### 1.3 Standard Telemetry Antenna Metrics (Bolded)

The following engineering metrics must be memorized for KTU examinations:

- **Wavelength (λ)** = c / f, where c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s.
- **Half-wave dipole length** ≈ **λ/2** (most fundamental reference antenna).
- **Gain (G)** measured in **dBi** (relative to an isotropic radiator).
- **Beamwidth** of a parabolic dish ≈ **70·λ/D** degrees, where D is the dish diameter.
- **Free-space path loss** at distance R: **FSPL (dB) = 20·log₁₀(4πR/λ)**.
- **VSWR** must be **≤ 2:1** for a well-matched telemetry antenna.
- **Polarization** types used in telemetry: **Linear (V/H)**, **Circular (RHCP/LHCP)** for satellite links.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fundamental Antenna Parameters Used in Telemetry

When an engineer specifies an antenna for a telemetry link (e.g., a missile-borne antenna, a satellite telemetry antenna, or a medical implant antenna), the following parameters are evaluated:

1. **Radiation Pattern** — A mathematical function or graphical plot describing the relative field strength radiated by the antenna as a function of direction from the antenna. Patterns are usually plotted in the **E-plane (electric-field plane)** and **H-plane (magnetic-field plane)**.

2. **Directivity (D)** — The ratio of the radiation intensity in a given direction to the average radiation intensity over all directions. For a lossless isotropic radiator, D = 1 (0 dBi). For a half-wave dipole, D ≈ 1.64 (2.15 dBi).

3. **Antenna Gain (G)** — Directivity reduced by the antenna's ohmic losses. Mathematically: G = ε · D, where ε is the radiation efficiency (0 < ε ≤ 1). Expressed in **dBi** (isotropic reference) or **dBd** (half-wave dipole reference, where 0 dBd = 2.15 dBi).

4. **Input Impedance (Zin)** — The impedance presented by the antenna at its feed point. For a resonant half-wave dipole, Zin ≈ **73 + j42.5 Ω** (free space). Real part is the radiation resistance; the imaginary part represents stored reactive energy.

5. **Bandwidth (BW)** — The range of frequencies over which the antenna's key performance parameters (gain, VSWR, pattern shape) remain within acceptable limits. Defined typically as the **VSWR ≤ 2:1** band, or the **3 dB gain-drop** band.

6. **Beamwidth** — The angular width of the main lobe of the radiation pattern, usually measured between the **−3 dB (half-power) points**. Two key beamwidths: **HPBW** (Half-Power Beamwidth) and **BWFN** (Beamwidth Between First Nulls).

7. **Polarization** — The orientation of the E-field vector of the radiated wave. Critical for telemetry because a polarization mismatch causes a **polarization loss factor (PLF) = cos²(θ)**, where θ is the angle between the polarizations of the Tx and Rx antennas.

8. **Effective Aperture (Ae)** — A measure of how much effective area the antenna presents to an incoming plane wave. Related to gain by: **Ae = (G·λ²) / (4π)**.

9. **VSWR (Voltage Standing Wave Ratio)** — A measure of impedance matching quality. VSWR = 1 means perfect match; VSWR → ∞ means total reflection.

10. **Front-to-Back Ratio (FTBR)** — Ratio of maximum radiation in the desired direction to the radiation in the opposite (180°) direction. Important for telemetry to reject interfering signals from behind the antenna.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Formula Table — All formulas required for Module 2 problems.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units / Remarks |
|---|-----------|---------|-----------------|
| 1 | Wavelength | λ = c / f | meters; c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s |
| 2 | Half-wave dipole length | L = λ/2 | meters |
| 3 | Directivity of Hertzian dipole | D = 1.5 (1.76 dBi) | Linear scale |
| 4 | Directivity of half-wave dipole | D = 1.64 (2.15 dBi) | Linear scale |
| 5 | Gain | G = ε · D | ε = efficiency (0 to 1) |
| 6 | Effective aperture | Ae = G·λ² / (4π) | m² |
| 7 | Beamwidth of parabolic dish | HPBW ≈ 70·λ / D | degrees; D = dish diameter |
| 8 | Friis transmission equation (received power) | Pr = Pt · Gt · Gr · λ² / ((4π)² · R²) | Watts |
| 9 | Free-space path loss (FSPL) | FSPL (dB) = 20·log₁₀(4πR/λ) | dB |
| 10 | Polarization Loss Factor | PLF = cos²(θ) | θ = angle between polarizations |
| 11 | VSWR | VSWR = (1 + \vert Γ \vert) / (1 − \vert Γ \vert) | Γ = reflection coefficient |
| 12 | Return Loss | RL (dB) = −20·log₁₀ \vert Γ \vert | dB |
| 13 | Antenna temperature | TA = (1/η) · [TA_sky · η + T_ground·(1−η)] | Kelvin |
| 14 | EIRP | EIRP = Pt · Gt (dBW) | dBW |
| 15 | Array factor (N-element uniform) | AF = Σ n=0 to N−1 [aₙ · e^{j·n·ψ}] | ψ = kd·cosθ + β |
| 16 | Phase velocity in array | β = −k·d·cosθ₀ | rad/m; θ₀ = steering angle |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL: KTU 2024 Exam Tip — dB Conversions:** Always use dB in link-budget problems. Remember that 1 mW = 0 dBm, 1 W = 30 dBm, and gains multiply in linear scale but add in dB scale. Multiplying Gt and Gr in the Friis equation is equivalent to adding Gt (dBi) and Gr (dBi).

### 2.3 Why These Parameters Matter in Real Telemetry Engineering

- **Radiation Pattern** determines which remote sensors the telemetry ground station can "see" at any instant. A satellite in low-Earth orbit sweeps across the sky, so the ground station uses a **steerable, narrow-beam dish** (e.g., 5 m diameter) to track it.
- **Gain** directly determines the **link margin**: a 6 dB increase in antenna gain doubles the received signal power — equivalent to quadrupling the transmitter power.
- **Polarization** is decisive in satellite telemetry. A linearly polarized receiving antenna will lose 3 dB of signal if the satellite transmits circular polarization, because the linear antenna rejects one of the two orthogonal circular components.
- **Bandwidth** must be wide enough to pass the modulated telemetry subcarriers (often PCM/FM or PCM/PM signals) without distortion. Modern telemetry standards (IRIG 106) require **≥ 2 MHz** of clean RF bandwidth.
- **Effective Aperture** quantifies the antenna's ability to "catch" a free-space wave. The larger the aperture, the more signal energy is collected — a 30 m radio telescope can "hear" telemetry signals from deep-space probes that are billions of kilometers away.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Effective Aperture of an Antenna

The effective aperture of a receiving antenna is defined as the equivalent area that, when multiplied by the incident power flux density (Poynting vector magnitude S), gives the power delivered to a matched load.

**Step 1 — Start with the Poynting vector of a plane wave incident on the antenna:**

The time-average power density of an incident electromagnetic plane wave is:

$$
S_{avg} = \frac{\vert E \vert^{2}}{2\eta_0}
$$

where η₀ = 377 Ω is the intrinsic impedance of free space.

**Step 2 — Define the effective aperture Ae such that the received power Pr is:**

$$
P_r = S_{avg} \cdot A_e
$$

**Step 3 — Use reciprocity and the relationship between effective length and gain:**

For a short dipole (Hertzian dipole), the far-field electric field strength at distance R from a transmit antenna is:

$$
E_\theta = j \cdot \eta_0 \cdot \frac{I_0 \cdot dl \cdot \sin\theta}{2\lambda R} \cdot e^{-j k R}
$$

**Step 4 — Express the gain G in terms of the effective aperture:**

For any antenna, by definition, the effective aperture is related to the gain G by:

$$
A_e = \frac{G \cdot \lambda^{2}}{4\pi}
$$

**Step 5 — Derivation via reciprocity theorem:**

The reciprocity theorem states that the transmitting and receiving properties of a linear, passive antenna are identical. If a transmit antenna with gain Gt radiates power Pt, the power density at a distant point at range R is:

$$
S = \frac{P_t \cdot G_t}{4\pi R^{2}}
$$

A receiving antenna of effective aperture Ae at that point will intercept power:

$$
P_r = S \cdot A_e = \frac{P_t \cdot G_t \cdot A_e}{4\pi R^{2}}
$$

**Step 6 — Now consider a transmit-receive pair (same antennas, range R):**

If we transmit with the same antenna (gain G) and receive with another identical antenna (gain G) at range R, the power received is:

$$
P_r = \frac{P_t \cdot G \cdot G \cdot \lambda^{2}}{(4\pi)^{2} \cdot R^{2}}
$$

This is the famous **Friis Transmission Equation**.

**Step 7 — Substituting Ae = G·λ²/(4π) into the single-receiver equation:**

$$
P_r = \frac{P_t \cdot G_t \cdot A_{e,r}}{4\pi R^{2}}
$$

where A_{e,r} = G_r·λ²/(4π) is the effective aperture of the receiving antenna.

**Step 8 — Combining both substitutions yields the master equation:**

$$
P_r = \frac{P_t \cdot G_t \cdot G_r \cdot \lambda^{2}}{(4\pi)^{2} \cdot R^{2}}
$$

This is the fundamental link-budget equation for every free-space telemetry link. It directly shows why higher frequency (shorter λ) suffers MORE path loss, but smaller antennas are required at the same gain.

### 3.2 Derivation: Beamwidth of a Parabolic Dish Antenna

**Step 1 — Parabolic dish geometry:**

A parabolic reflector with focal length f and diameter D has a focal-ratio F = f/D. The feed horn at the focal point illuminates the dish, and the reflected rays emerge as a parallel beam.

**Step 2 — Far-field pattern integral:**

The far-field pattern E(θ) of a uniformly illuminated circular aperture of diameter D is:

$$
E(\theta) = E_0 \cdot \frac{2 J_1(k a \sin\theta)}{k a \sin\theta}
$$

where a = D/2 is the aperture radius, k = 2π/λ, and J₁ is the Bessel function of the first kind, order 1.

**Step 3 — Setting up the half-power condition:**

The half-power (-3 dB) beamwidth HPBW is defined as the angular separation where \vert E(θ) \vert² = 0.5 \vert E_0 \vert². This corresponds to the first zero of the derivative of the Bessel expression. Numerically solving the Bessel-function equation:

$$
k a \sin(\theta_{3dB}/2) \approx 0.514 \cdot \pi
$$

**Step 4 — Solving for HPBW in the small-angle approximation:**

For narrow beams, sin(θ) ≈ θ (radians), so:

$$
\theta_{3dB} \approx 1.02 \cdot \frac{\lambda}{D} \text{ radians}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to degrees using 1 rad = 57.296°:**

$$
\text{HPBW (deg)} \approx 1.02 \cdot \frac{\lambda}{D} \cdot 57.296 \approx 58.4 \cdot \frac{\lambda}{D}
$$

A more commonly used engineering approximation that accounts for typical edge taper is:

$$
\text{HPBW (deg)} \approx 70 \cdot \frac{\lambda}{D}
$$

**Step 6 — Gain of the dish:**

The gain of a parabolic dish with aperture efficiency η_ap is:

$$
G = \eta_{ap} \cdot \left(\frac{\pi D}{\lambda}\right)^{2}
$$

For a typical η_ap ≈ 0.55–0.65, the gain is about 55–60% of the theoretical maximum (πD/λ)².

### 3.3 Derivation: N-Element Linear Array Factor

**Step 1 — Geometry of a uniform linear array (ULA):**

Consider N identical isotropic elements equally spaced by distance d along the z-axis, each with progressive phase shift β. The total field E(θ) at far-field angle θ is:

$$
E(\theta) = E_0 \cdot \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} a_n \cdot e^{j n \psi}
$$

where ψ = k·d·cosθ + β is the progressive phase difference between adjacent elements.

**Step 2 — For uniform amplitude (aₙ = 1) and uniform phase β:**

$$
AF = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} e^{j n \psi} = 1 + e^{j\psi} + e^{j2\psi} + \dots + e^{j(N-1)\psi}
$$

**Step 3 — Multiply both sides by e^{jψ} to use the geometric series formula:**

$$
e^{j\psi} \cdot AF = e^{j\psi} + e^{j2\psi} + \dots + e^{jN\psi}
$$

**Step 4 — Subtract the two equations:**

$$
AF \cdot (e^{j\psi} - 1) = e^{jN\psi} - 1
$$

**Step 5 — Factor the right-hand side using the identity a^N − 1 = (a − 1)(a^{N−1} + a^{N−2} + … + 1):**

$$
AF = \frac{e^{jN\psi} - 1}{e^{j\psi} - 1} = \frac{e^{jN\psi/2}(e^{jN\psi/2} - e^{-jN\psi/2})}{e^{j\psi/2}(e^{j\psi/2} - e^{-j\psi/2})}
$$

**Step 6 — Apply Euler's formula e^{jx} − e^{−jx} = 2j·sin(x):**

$$
AF = e^{j(N-1)\psi/2} \cdot \frac{\sin(N\psi/2)}{\sin(\psi/2)}
$$

**Step 7 — The magnitude is the array factor magnitude:**

$$
\vert AF \vert = \left\vert \frac{\sin(N\psi/2)}{\sin(\psi/2)} \right\vert
$$

**Step 8 — Maximum value occurs when ψ = 0 (broadside radiation):**

As ψ → 0, using L'Hôpital's rule, the maximum of \vert AF \vert = N.

**Step 9 — Normalized array factor:**

$$
AF_{norm} = \frac{\sin(N\psi/2)}{N \cdot \sin(\psi/2)}
$$

**Step 10 — Condition for main beam to point at angle θ₀:**

Set ψ = 0 at θ = θ₀, which gives the required progressive phase:

$$
\beta = -k d \cos\theta_0
$$

This is the **electronic beam-steering principle** used in phased-array telemetry antennas (e.g., radar and satellite-tracking phased arrays).

### 3.4 Derivation: Telemetry Link-Budget Equation in dB

**Step 1 — Start with the Friis equation in linear form:**

$$
P_r = \frac{P_t \cdot G_t \cdot G_r \cdot \lambda^{2}}{(4\pi)^{2} \cdot R^{2}}
$$

**Step 2 — Take 10·log₁₀ of both sides:**

$$
10 \log_{10} P_r = 10 \log_{10} P_t + 10 \log_{10} G_t + 10 \log_{10} G_r + 20 \log_{10} \lambda - 20 \log_{10}(4\pi R)
$$

**Step 3 — Recognize the term 20·log₁₀(4πR/λ) as the Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL):**

$$
\text{FSPL (dB)} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{4\pi R}{\lambda}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — The link budget in dB form is:**

$$
P_r \text{(dBm)} = P_t \text{(dBm)} + G_t \text{(dBi)} + G_r \text{(dBi)} - \text{FSPL (dB)} - L_{other} \text{(dB)}
$$

where L_other accounts for atmospheric loss, polarization mismatch, cable losses, and rain fade.

**Step 5 — Link margin (KTU standard problem format):**

$$
\text{Link Margin (dB)} = P_r \text{(dBm)} - \text{Receiver Sensitivity (dBm)}
$$

The link is viable if and only if the link margin is positive (typically > 3 dB for a robust design).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Telemetry Antenna System

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagram represents the **functional signal flow** of a typical telemetry antenna system, showing how the antenna interfaces with the transmitter, receiver, propagation channel, and supporting subsystems.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[Telemetry Sensor Array PT100 RTD Strain Gauge] --> node2[Signal Conditioning Amplifier]
    node2 --> node3[ADC Analog to Digital Converter]
    node3 --> node4[PCM Encoder Pulse Code Modulation]
    node4 --> node5[Subcarrier Modulator BPSK QPSK FM]
    node5 --> node6[RF Upconverter Mixer Local Oscillator]
    node6 --> node7[Power Amplifier PA]
    node7 --> node8[Duplexer Tx/Rx Switch]
    node8 --> node9[Telemetry Antenna Half-wave Dipole Patch Dish]
    node9 --> node10[Free Space Propagation Channel]
    node10 --> node11[Ground Station Antenna Yagi Parabolic Array]
    node11 --> node12[LNA Low Noise Amplifier]
    node12 --> node13[RF Downconverter]
    node13 --> node14[Subcarrier Demodulator]
    node14 --> node15[PCM Decoder]
    node15 --> node16[Data Display and Storage]
    node9 -.Beam Pattern.-> node17[Antenna Parameters Gain Directivity Beamwidth Polarization]
    node17 -.Antenna Design.-> node9
```

### 4.2 Telemetry Antenna Classification Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Telemetry Antennas] --> nodeB[Wire Type]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Aperture Type]
    nodeA --> nodeD[Printed Type]
    nodeA --> nodeE[Array Type]

    nodeB --> nodeB1[Half-wave Dipole f 30 MHz to 1 GHz]
    nodeB --> nodeB2[Monopole with Ground Plane]
    nodeB --> nodeB3[Helical Satellite Telemetry]
    nodeB --> nodeB4[Loop Antenna HF Telemetry]

    nodeC --> nodeC1[Horn Antenna Microwave]
    nodeC --> nodeC2[Parabolic Dish Deep Space]
    nodeC --> nodeC3[Slot Antenna Aircraft]
    nodeC --> nodeC4[Cassegrain Dual Reflector]

    nodeD --> nodeD1[Microstrip Patch Telemetry Capsules]
    nodeD --> nodeD2[Planar Inverted F Antenna PIFA Mobile]
    nodeD --> nodeD3[Spiral Antenna UWB Telemetry]

    nodeE --> nodeE1[Phased Array Beam Steering]
    nodeE --> nodeE2[Yagi Uda Directional]
    nodeE --> nodeE3[Log Periodic Wideband]
    nodeE --> nodeE4[Smart Antenna Adaptive]
```

### 4.3 Telemetry Link Budget Calculation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeX1[Transmit Power Pt dBm] --> nodeX2[Add Transmit Gain Gt dBi]
    nodeX2 --> nodeX3[EIRP dBm]
    nodeX3 --> nodeX4[Subtract Free Space Path Loss FSPL dB]
    nodeX4 --> nodeX5[Subtract Atmospheric Loss dB]
    nodeX5 --> nodeX6[Subtract Polarization Mismatch Loss dB]
    nodeX6 --> nodeX7[Add Receive Antenna Gain Gr dBi]
    nodeX7 --> nodeX8[Subtract Cable Loss dB]
    nodeX8 --> nodeX9[Received Power Pr dBm]
    nodeX9 --> nodeX10{Compare with Rx Sensitivity}
    nodeX10 -->|Positive Margin| nodeX11[Link is Viable]
    nodeX10 -->|Negative Margin| nodeX12[Link Fails - Increase Gain or Pt]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation of the Functional Diagrams:**
> - **Diagram 1** shows the signal flow from sensor to display, with the antenna as the key boundary element between the guided circuit and free space.
> - **Diagram 2** classifies telemetry antennas by physical structure; each class has specific advantages for a given telemetry scenario.
> - **Diagram 3** is the **link budget waterfall** that every KTU telemetry design problem follows. Notice how gains ADD and losses SUBTRACT in dB arithmetic.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

Define the term **Effective Aperture** of an antenna. How is it related to the gain and wavelength?

**Model Answer:**

The effective aperture (or effective area) of a receiving antenna is defined as the ratio of the power delivered to the matched load to the incident power flux density of the electromagnetic wave at the antenna location. It is a measure of the antenna's ability to capture energy from an incoming plane wave.

Mathematically, effective aperture Ae and gain G are related by:

$$
A_e = \frac{G \cdot \lambda^{2}}{4\pi}
$$

where λ is the operating wavelength. The relationship shows that **larger apertures collect more power**, and **higher gain corresponds to larger effective aperture for the same wavelength**. For an isotropic antenna (G = 1), the effective aperture is λ²/(4π), which is the smallest possible aperture for a given frequency.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

List any **four important antenna parameters** used to characterize an antenna for a telemetry application. Briefly state the significance of each.

**Model Answer:**

1. **Radiation Pattern:** Graphical representation of the antenna's radiation intensity as a function of direction. Critical for determining the coverage area of the telemetry link.

2. **Gain (G):** Measures the ability of the antenna to concentrate radiated power in a particular direction. Higher gain means longer range and better link margin.

3. **Polarization:** The orientation of the E-field vector of the radiated wave. Must match between transmitting and receiving antennas to minimize loss (otherwise polarization mismatch loss = −20·log₁₀(cos θ)).

4. **Bandwidth (BW):** The range of frequencies over which the antenna operates satisfactorily. For PCM/FM telemetry, the bandwidth must be wide enough to pass all subcarrier sidebands without distortion (typically ≥ 2 MHz for IRIG standard channels).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper, 2024 Scheme]*

**(a)** Derive the **Friis transmission equation** for a free-space telemetry link and explain the significance of each term. State the assumptions made in the derivation. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A ground station transmits a telemetry command signal at **2.4 GHz** with a transmit power of **10 W** from an antenna of gain **30 dBi**. A spacecraft at a distance of **42,000 km** (geostationary orbit) receives it with an antenna of gain **40 dBi**. Calculate: **(i)** the free-space path loss in dB, **(ii)** the received power in dBm, and **(iii)** the link margin if the receiver sensitivity is **−110 dBm**. **[7 Marks]**

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(a):**

**Step 1 — State the Friis equation and its physical meaning:**

The Friis transmission equation gives the received power in a free-space communication link:

$$
P_r = \frac{P_t \cdot G_t \cdot G_r \cdot \lambda^{2}}{(4\pi)^{2} \cdot R^{2}}
$$

where:
- P_r = received power (W)
- P_t = transmitted power (W)
- G_t = gain of transmitting antenna (linear)
- G_r = gain of receiving antenna (linear)
- λ = wavelength (m)
- R = distance between antennas (m)

**Step 2 — Assumptions:**

1. The antennas are in free space (no obstacles, no atmospheric absorption).
2. The antennas are far enough apart to be in each other's far-field (Fraunhofer region, R > 2D²/λ).
3. The antennas are perfectly aligned and polarization-matched (PLF = 1).
4. The transmission line losses between the transmitter and the antenna are negligible (or accounted for separately).

**Step 3 — Derivation outline:**

- Power density at distance R from isotropic transmitter: S = P_t / (4πR²)
- Power density with directive transmitter: S = P_t·G_t / (4πR²)
- Power captured by receiving antenna of effective aperture Ae: P_r = S · Ae
- Substituting Ae = G_r·λ²/(4π) gives the Friis equation.

**Step 4 — Significance:**

- The equation shows the **inverse-square law** for free-space propagation (received power falls as 1/R²).
- Higher gains or higher frequencies (smaller λ) directly increase received power.
- It is the **foundation of all telemetry link-budget calculations**, including satellite, deep-space, and aeronautical telemetry.

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(b):**

**Step 1 — Given data:**

- Frequency f = 2.4 GHz → λ = c/f = (3 × 10⁸) / (2.4 × 10⁹) = 0.125 m
- P_t = 10 W → 10·log₁₀(10,000 mW / 1 mW) = 10·log₁₀(10,000) = 40 dBm
- G_t = 30 dBi
- G_r = 40 dBi
- R = 42,000 km = 4.2 × 10⁷ m
- Receiver sensitivity = −110 dBm

**Step 2 — Free-space path loss calculation:**

$$
\text{FSPL (dB)} = 20 \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{4\pi R}{\lambda}\right)
$$

$$
4\pi R = 4\pi \cdot 4.2 \times 10^{7} = 5.278 \times 10^{8} \text{ m}
$$

$$
\frac{4\pi R}{\lambda} = \frac{5.278 \times 10^{8}}{0.125} = 4.223 \times 10^{9}
$$

$$
\text{FSPL} = 20 \cdot \log_{10}(4.223 \times 10^{9}) = 20 \cdot (0.6256 + 9) = 20 \cdot 9.6256
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{FSPL} = 192.5 \text{ dB}}
$$

**Step 3 — Received power in dBm:**

Using the link-budget equation in dB form:

$$
P_r \text{(dBm)} = P_t \text{(dBm)} + G_t \text{(dBi)} + G_r \text{(dBi)} - \text{FSPL (dB)}
$$

$$
P_r = 40 + 30 + 40 - 192.5
$$

$$
\boxed{P_r = -82.5 \text{ dBm}}
$$

**Step 4 — Link margin calculation:**

$$
\text{Link Margin} = P_r - \text{Rx Sensitivity} = -82.5 - (-110)
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Link Margin} = +27.5 \text{ dB}}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:**

The link has a positive margin of **27.5 dB**, which means the link is robust and can tolerate an additional 27.5 dB of losses (atmospheric, rain, pointing errors, etc.) before failing. This is a healthy link for a geostationary-satellite telemetry command channel.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper, 2024 Scheme]*

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **functional architecture of a telemetry antenna system** from the sensor to the ground-station display. Explain the role of the antenna as a transducer between the guided and unguided media. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A telemetry system uses a **parabolic dish antenna of diameter 2.4 m** operating at **6 GHz**. Calculate: **(i)** the half-power beamwidth in degrees, **(ii)** the gain in dBi assuming an aperture efficiency of 0.6, and **(iii)** the effective aperture of the antenna. **[7 Marks]**

---

**Model Solution to Q.B(a):**

**Step 1 — Block diagram (refer to Mermaid Diagram 4.1 in Section 4):**

The signal flow is:
**Sensor → Signal Conditioning → ADC → PCM Encoder → Subcarrier Modulator → RF Upconverter → Power Amplifier → Duplexer → Telemetry Antenna → Free Space → Ground Station Antenna → LNA → RF Downconverter → Subcarrier Demodulator → PCM Decoder → Data Display.**

**Step 2 — Role of the antenna:**

The antenna is the critical transducer that **converts guided electromagnetic waves (in a coaxial cable or waveguide) into unguided electromagnetic waves (in free space)** for transmission, and vice versa for reception. Without the antenna, the modulated telemetry signal would remain trapped in the transmission line and never reach the distant receiver.

**Step 3 — Key functions in a telemetry antenna system:**

- The transmit antenna **shapes the radiated beam** toward the receiver (e.g., a high-gain dish on a satellite tracks the ground station).
- The receive antenna **collects the weak incoming signal** and delivers it to the low-noise amplifier (LNA) of the ground station.
- The antenna's **polarization, gain, and bandwidth** must be matched to the telemetry modulation scheme (e.g., PCM/FM, BPSK, QPSK).

**Step 4 — Reciprocity principle:**

The same antenna is used for both transmit and receive (with a duplexer to switch), which is possible because antennas are **reciprocal devices**. This simplifies the design and reduces cost.

**Step 5 — Telemetry-specific requirements:**

- **Wide bandwidth** to support multiple subcarrier channels.
- **Circular polarization** for satellite telemetry to avoid Faraday-rotation loss.
- **Low VSWR (≤ 2:1)** to minimize reflected power.
- **Stable gain and pattern** over the operating temperature and vibration range (especially for missile and spacecraft telemetry).

---

**Model Solution to Q.B(b):**

**Step 1 — Given data:**

- Dish diameter D = 2.4 m
- Frequency f = 6 GHz → λ = c/f = (3 × 10⁸) / (6 × 10⁹) = 0.05 m
- Aperture efficiency η_ap = 0.6

**Step 2 — Half-power beamwidth:**

$$
\text{HPBW (deg)} \approx 70 \cdot \frac{\lambda}{D} = 70 \cdot \frac{0.05}{2.4}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{HPBW} \approx 1.458 \text{ degrees}}
$$

**Step 3 — Gain calculation:**

First compute the gain in linear scale:

$$
G = \eta_{ap} \cdot \left(\frac{\pi D}{\lambda}\right)^{2} = 0.6 \cdot \left(\frac{\pi \cdot 2.4}{0.05}\right)^{2}
$$

$$
\frac{\pi \cdot 2.4}{0.05} = 150.796
$$

$$
G = 0.6 \cdot (150.796)^{2} = 0.6 \cdot 22,739.4 = 13,643.6
$$

Convert to dBi:

$$
G \text{(dBi)} = 10 \cdot \log_{10}(13,643.6) = 10 \cdot 4.1348
$$

$$
\boxed{G \approx 41.35 \text{ dBi}}
$$

**Step 4 — Effective aperture calculation:**

$$
A_e = \frac{G \cdot \lambda^{2}}{4\pi} = \frac{13,643.6 \cdot (0.05)^{2}}{4\pi} = \frac{13,643.6 \cdot 0.0025}{12.566}
$$

$$
A_e = \frac{34.109}{12.566}
$$

$$
\boxed{A_e \approx 2.714 \text{ m}^{2}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit inconsistency:** Always convert P_t to dBm, R to meters, and f to Hz before substituting. Using R in km directly gives the WRONG FSPL by a factor of 1000 (or 60 dB).
> 2. **Gain reference confusion:** dBi is with respect to isotropic; dBd is with respect to a half-wave dipole. Conversion: dBi = dBd + 2.15.
> 3. **Aperture efficiency vs. radiation efficiency:** Don't confuse them. Aperture efficiency (η_ap) applies to dish illumination taper; radiation efficiency (ε) applies to ohmic losses. Dish gain formula uses η_ap.
> 4. **FSPL formula sign:** FSPL is a LOSS, so it is SUBTRACTED in the link-budget equation. Students often add it by mistake.
> 5. **Polarization loss:** If polarizations are mismatched (e.g., circular vs. linear), include a 3 dB loss in the link budget.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2: Antennas for Telemetry**

- **Antenna** = guided-wave ↔ free-space transducer. Works on **reciprocity** (Tx = Rx behavior).
- **Key parameters to remember:** Radiation pattern, Directivity (D), Gain (G), Input Impedance (Zin), Bandwidth (BW), Beamwidth (HPBW), Polarization, Effective Aperture (Ae), VSWR, Return Loss, EIRP.
- **Wavelength formula:** λ = c/f. **Half-wave dipole length:** L = λ/2. **Resonant input impedance:** ≈ 73 Ω (real).
- **Friis transmission equation (master link equation):** P_r = P_t · G_t · G_r · λ² / ((4π)² · R²). This is the **most-tested formula** in KTU exams.
- **FSPL (dB)** = 20·log₁₀(4πR/λ). Increases by **6 dB** every time distance doubles OR frequency doubles.
- **Parabolic dish beamwidth:** HPBW ≈ 70·λ/D degrees. **Gain:** G = η_ap · (πD/λ)².
- **Effective aperture:** A_e = G·λ²/(4π). Larger aperture → more signal collected.
- **Polarization Loss Factor:** PLF = cos²θ. Maximum loss = 0 dB (matched), 3 dB for linear ↔ circular mismatch, ∞ dB for cross-polarization.
- **Phased-array steering:** Progressive phase β = −k·d·cosθ₀ steers the main beam to angle θ₀. Array factor magnitude = \vert sin(Nψ/2) / sin(ψ/2) \vert.
- **Telemetry antenna types:** Wire (dipole, monopole, helical), Aperture (horn, parabolic dish, slot), Printed (microstrip patch, PIFA, spiral), Array (phased array, Yagi-Uda, log-periodic).
- **dB arithmetic rules:** Gains ADD, losses SUBTRACT. Convert W to dBm: P(dBm) = 30 + 10·log₁₀(P in W).
- **Link margin** must be **positive and > 3 dB** for a robust telemetry link.
- **Common KTU exam trap:** Forgetting polarization mismatch loss in link-budget problems (loses 2–3 marks).
- **VSWR ≤ 2:1** is the industry-standard "good match" criterion for telemetry antennas.
- **Circular polarization** (RHCP or LHCP) is preferred for satellite telemetry to avoid Faraday rotation effects in the ionosphere.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principles of Satellite Communications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT634 - COMMUNICATION SYSTEM AND TELEMETRY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Principles of Satellite Communications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Satellite Communication** is a specialized form of long-haul radio communication in which an artificial electromagnetic repeater (transponder) housed on board a man-made celestial body (the satellite) is placed in a precisely defined orbit around the Earth. The system uses microwave frequency carriers to relay information signals between two or more Earth Stations (ES) which may be separated by distances ranging from a few hundred kilometres to intercontinental spans exceeding 15,000 km.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme, the "Principles of Satellite Communications" module establishes the foundational physics governing satellite motion, the geometry of the Earth-space link, the electromagnetic spectrum allocations, and the link-budget mathematics required to design a viable satellite system.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Intent)**
> *"Satellite communication is the use of orbiting artificial satellites to relay microwave signals carrying information (voice, video, or data) between geographically separated Earth stations, employing a transparent or regenerative transponder operating at frequencies between 1 GHz and 300 GHz."*

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are standing on the roof of your house in Kerala and want to send a message to a friend standing on a roof in Delhi — about **2,500 km** away. You shout, but your voice fades out within a few hundred metres. If you climb the tallest mountain (Everest), your voice travels a bit further, but still nowhere near Delhi.

Now imagine placing a **mirror on a high-flying drone** that reflects your signal back down. The higher the drone, the larger the area on the ground it can "see". A satellite is essentially this mirror, but instead of a simple mirror, it is an intelligent **radio repeater** hovering at an altitude of 36,000 km above the equator. Three such "mirrors" spaced 120° apart can cover the *entire planet* except the polar caps.

**Three Analogies to Cement Understanding:**

| Analogy | Satellite Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Mirror on a hill | Transponder (bent-pipe) |
| Spotlight from a tower | Footprint / antenna beam |
| Lighthouse beam sweeping the sea | Antenna pattern covering the visible Earth disc |

### 1.3 Key Standard Constants

The following constants are universally used in satellite link design and must be memorized for KTU examinations:

- **Speed of light:** $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s
- **Earth's mean radius:** $R_E = 6{,}378$ km $\approx$ **6,371 km** (using WGS-84 mean radius)
- **Earth's gravitational constant (GM):** $\mu = 3.986 \times 10^{14}$ m³/s²
- **Boltzmann constant:** $k = 1.3806 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K $\equiv -228.6$ dBW/K/Hz
- **Sidereal day:** $T_{sid} = 86{,}164.1$ s $\approx$ **23 h 56 min 4 s**
- **Geostationary orbital altitude:** $h_{GEO} = 35{,}786$ km

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why 35,786 km?** This is *not* an arbitrary number. It is the **only altitude** at which a satellite's orbital period exactly matches the Earth's rotation period, making the satellite appear *stationary* to an observer on the ground. Derivation in Section 3.

### 1.4 Historical Context for KTU Context

- **1957** – Sputnik 1 (USSR) launched.
- **1962** – Telstar 1 (USA) relayed the first live transatlantic TV signal.
- **1964** – INTELSAT (International Telecommunications Satellite Organization) formed.
- **1965** – INTELSAT I ("Early Bird") — first commercial **geostationary** communications satellite.
- **1980s–Today** – Proliferation of LEO constellations (Iridium, Starlink).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet
