### 2.1 Kepler's Three Laws of Planetary Motion

These laws form the bedrock of orbital mechanics. Johannes Kepler formulated them in the early 17th century purely from astronomical observation; Isaac Newton later proved them mathematically using his law of universal gravitation.

**Law 1 — The Law of Ellipses:**
Every satellite (or planet) moves in an elliptical orbit, with the central body (Earth) located at one of the two foci.

**Law 2 — The Law of Equal Areas:**
The line joining the satellite and the Earth (the **radius vector**) sweeps out equal areas in equal intervals of time.

*Operational consequence:* The satellite moves **fastest** at **perigee** (closest point) and **slowest** at **apogee** (farthest point). Angular momentum is conserved.

**Law 3 — The Harmonic Law (T² ∝ a³):**
The square of the orbital period is proportional to the cube of the semi-major axis of the orbit.

### 2.2 Newton's Derivation of Kepler's Third Law

For a satellite in a *circular* orbit (a special case of an ellipse where the foci coincide), we equate centripetal force to gravitational attraction.

$$\text{Centripetal Force} = \text{Gravitational Force}$$
$$\frac{m v^2}{r} = \frac{G M_E m}{r^2}$$

Since the satellite moves in a circle, its velocity can be expressed as $v = \frac{2\pi r}{T}$, where $T$ is the orbital period. Substituting and simplifying yields the iconic relationship.

### 2.3 Orbital Classifications (The Three Sacred Altitudes)

| Orbit Class | Altitude Range | Period | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **LEO** (Low Earth Orbit) | 160 – 2,000 km | 88 – 127 min | Starlink, Iridium, Earth observation |
| **MEO** (Medium Earth Orbit) | 2,000 – 35,786 km | 2 – 24 hr | GPS (Navstar), GLONASS, Galileo |
| **GEO** (Geostationary Orbit) | **35,786 km** exactly | **23h 56m 4s** | DBS TV (DD Direct+, Tata Sky), VSAT, Inmarsat |
| **HEO** (Highly Elliptical Orbit) | Variable | 12 hr | Molniya, Tundra (used over Russia/Canada) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** A *circular equatorial orbit at 35,786 km* is called **Geostationary**. If the orbit is circular but inclined or eccentric, the satellite is merely **Geosynchronous** — it has the same period as Earth's rotation but is not stationary with respect to a ground observer.

### 2.4 Satellite Frequency Bands

The KTU syllabus and ITU Radio Regulations mandate the following primary allocations for commercial satellite services:

| Band | Frequency Range | Wavelength | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **L** | 1 – 2 GHz | 30 – 15 cm | Mobile satellite (Inmarsat), GPS |
| **S** | 2 – 4 GHz | 15 – 7.5 cm | NASA, TT&C, MSS |
| **C** | 4 – 8 GHz | 7.5 – 3.75 cm | Fixed satellite (FSS), Intelsat, TV uplink |
| **X** | 8 – 12 GHz | 3.75 – 2.5 cm | Military, radar |
| **Ku** | 12 – 18 GHz | 2.5 – 1.67 cm | DBS TV, VSAT |
| **K** | 18 – 27 GHz | 1.67 – 1.11 cm | Uplink in some systems |
| **Ka** | 27 – 40 GHz | 1.11 – 0.75 cm | High-throughput (Starlink, ViaSat) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rain Attenuation Rule (KTU Favourite):** Higher frequencies suffer greater atmospheric/rain attenuation. **Ku and Ka bands** are particularly vulnerable; **C and L bands** are the most robust. This is the *physical reason* C-band is still preferred for tropical regions like Kerala.

### 2.5 Satellite System Block Architecture

A satellite link comprises three primary segments:

1. **Uplink Segment** — Earth station transmitter → Satellite receiver (uplink frequency).
2. **Satellite (Space) Segment** — Transponder (LNA → Mixer → HPA → Antenna).
3. **Downlink Segment** — Satellite transmitter → Earth station receiver (downlink frequency).

> [!NOTE]
> The uplink and downlink use **different frequencies** to prevent feedback oscillation at the satellite (e.g., **uplink 6 GHz / downlink 4 GHz** for C-band; **uplink 14 GHz / downlink 12 GHz** for Ku-band).

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are examinable. Use `\vert` for any absolute values to comply with markdown table rules.

| # | Concept | Formula | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Kepler's Third Law (Newton's form)** | $T^2 = \dfrac{4\pi^2}{\mu} a^3$ | T in s, a in m, $\mu = 3.986 \times 10^{14}$ m³/s² |
| 2 | Orbital period (circular) | $T = 2\pi\sqrt{\dfrac{r^3}{\mu}}$ | r is orbital radius from Earth centre |
| 3 | Geostationary radius | $r_{GEO} = \left(\dfrac{\mu T_{sid}^2}{4\pi^2}\right)^{1/3}$ | ≈ 42,164 km |
| 4 | Geostationary altitude | $h_{GEO} = r_{GEO} - R_E$ | ≈ **35,786 km** |
| 5 | Orbital velocity | $v = \sqrt{\dfrac{\mu}{r}}$ | m/s |
| 6 | Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL) | $L_{FS} = \left(\dfrac{4\pi d f}{c}\right)^2$ | dimensionless ratio |
| 7 | FSPL in dB (practical) | $L_{FS}\,(dB) = 32.45 + 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz})$ | d in km, f in MHz |
| 8 | EIRP | $\text{EIRP} = P_t + G_t - L_{ft}$ | dBW |
| 9 | Figure of Merit | $\dfrac{G}{T} = G_{rx} - T_{sys}$ | dB/K |
| 10 | Carrier-to-Noise Density | $\dfrac{C}{N_0} = \text{EIRP} + \dfrac{G}{T} - L_{FS} - L_{misc} - k$ | dB·Hz |
| 11 | Carrier-to-Noise Ratio | $\dfrac{C}{N} = \dfrac{C}{N_0} - 10\log_{10}(B)$ | dB |
| 12 | Link budget equation (uplink) | $\dfrac{C}{N_0}_u = \text{EIRP}_{ES} + \dfrac{G}{T}_{sat} - L_{FS,u} - k$ | dB·Hz |
| 13 | Slant range (central angle method) | $d = \sqrt{R_E^2 + r^2 - 2 R_E r \cos\gamma}$ | km |
| 14 | Central angle | $\gamma = \arccos\!\left(\cos L_e \cos \beta\right)$ | radians / degrees |
| 15 | Longitude difference | $\beta = \vert L_s - L_e \vert$ | degrees |
| 16 | Elevation angle | $\tan E_l = \dfrac{\cos\gamma - R_E / r}{\sin\gamma}$ | degrees |
| 17 | Azimuth angle (from north) | $A_z = 180° + \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{\tan\beta}{\sin L_e}\right)$ | degrees |
| 18 | Doppler shift | $\Delta f = \dfrac{v_r f_c}{c}$ | Hz |
| 19 | Round-trip light time (GEO) | $\Delta t = \dfrac{2 h_{GEO}}{c} \approx 0.24$ s | seconds |
| 20 | Total CNR (cascaded) | $\dfrac{1}{C/N_{tot}} = \dfrac{1}{C/N_{up}} + \dfrac{1}{C/N_{down}}$ | power sum |

### 2.7 Engineering Utility

The principles established here directly govern the design of:
- **Global Navigation Satellite Systems (GNSS)** — GPS, IRNSS (NavIC).
- **Direct Broadcast Satellite Television (DBS-TV)** — DTH services.
- **Very Small Aperture Terminals (VSAT)** for corporate networks.
- **Telemetry, Tracking & Command (TT&C)** links for satellite operators.
- **Emergency communication** during disasters (e.g., ISRO's GSAT series for the PM-WANI initiative).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Kepler's Third Law from Newton's Law of Gravitation

**Given:**
- Newton's Universal Law of Gravitation: $F = \dfrac{G M_E m}{r^2}$
- Newton's Second Law for circular motion: $F = \dfrac{m v^2}{r}$

**Step 1:** Equate the two expressions for force on a satellite of mass $m$ in a circular orbit of radius $r$.

$$\frac{m v^2}{r} = \frac{G M_E m}{r^2}$$

**Step 2:** Cancel $m$ on both sides and multiply both sides by $r$.

$$v^2 = \frac{G M_E}{r}$$

**Step 3:** Recall that $\mu = G M_E = 3.986 \times 10^{14}$ m³/s² for Earth.

$$v^2 = \frac{\mu}{r}$$

**Step 4:** The satellite completes one full revolution in period $T$, travelling a distance $2\pi r$. Hence $v = \dfrac{2\pi r}{T}$. Substitute:

$$\left(\frac{2\pi r}{T}\right)^2 = \frac{\mu}{r}$$

**Step 5:** Square the left side and rearrange.

$$\frac{4\pi^2 r^2}{T^2} = \frac{\mu}{r}$$

**Step 6:** Cross-multiply to isolate $T^2$.

$$T^2 = \frac{4\pi^2 r^2 \cdot r}{\mu} = \frac{4\pi^2 r^3}{\mu}$$

**Step 7:** Replacing the radius $r$ with the semi-major axis $a$ for an elliptical orbit (Newton's generalisation) yields the final universal form.

$$\boxed{\,T^2 = \frac{4\pi^2}{\mu} a^3\,}$$

> **Interpretation:** This is the *mathematical proof* of Kepler's Third Law. It tells us that the *farther* a satellite is, the *much longer* its orbital period (since $T \propto a^{3/2}$).

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Radius and Altitude of the Geostationary Orbit

**Given:**
- Sidereal day: $T_{sid} = 86{,}164.1$ s
- Earth's GM: $\mu = 3.986 \times 10^{14}$ m³/s²
- Earth's equatorial radius: $R_E = 6{,}378{,}137$ m

**Step 1:** Rearrange Kepler's third law to solve for the orbital radius.

$$r = \left(\frac{\mu T^2}{4\pi^2}\right)^{1/3}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute numerical values.

$$r = \left(\frac{3.986 \times 10^{14} \times (86{,}164.1)^2}{4 \pi^2}\right)^{1/3}$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate the numerator.

$$\mu T^2 = 3.986 \times 10^{14} \times 7.428 \times 10^{9} = 2.961 \times 10^{24}$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate the denominator.

$$4\pi^2 = 39.478$$

**Step 5:** Compute the ratio.

$$\frac{\mu T^2}{4\pi^2} = \frac{2.961 \times 10^{24}}{39.478} = 7.500 \times 10^{22} \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 6:** Take the cube root.

$$r_{GEO} = \left(7.500 \times 10^{22}\right)^{1/3} = 4.2164 \times 10^{7} \text{ m} = 42{,}164 \text{ km}$$

**Step 7:** Subtract the Earth's radius to find the altitude above mean sea level.

$$h_{GEO} = r_{GEO} - R_E = 42{,}164 - 6{,}378 = 35{,}786 \text{ km}$$

$$\boxed{\,h_{GEO} \approx 35{,}786 \text{ km}\,}$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Free-Space Path Loss (Friis Transmission Equation in Free Space)

**Given:** A transmitter with gain $G_t$ radiates isotropically into free space. The power flux density at distance $d$ is:

$$S = \frac{P_t G_t}{4\pi d^2}$$

**Step 1:** The receiving antenna of effective aperture $A_e$ captures this flux. By definition, $A_e = \dfrac{G_r \lambda^2}{4\pi}$.

**Step 2:** The received power is therefore:

$$P_r = S \cdot A_e = \frac{P_t G_t}{4\pi d^2} \cdot \frac{G_r \lambda^2}{4\pi}$$

**Step 3:** For a lossless unity-gain isotropic reference, define the **Free-Space Path Loss** as the ratio $L_{FS} = \dfrac{P_t}{P_r}$ when $G_t = G_r = 1$.

$$L_{FS} = \left(\frac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{4\pi d f}{c}\right)^2$$

**Step 4:** Convert to decibels using $\lambda = c/f$.

$$L_{FS}\,(dB) = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi d f}{c}\right) = 32.45 + 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz})$$

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Look Angle Geometry (Slant Range)

Consider an Earth station at latitude $L_e$ and longitude $L_{e,\lambda}$ looking up at a geostationary satellite at longitude $L_{s,\lambda}$.

**Step 1:** Define the longitude difference $\beta = L_{s,\lambda} - L_{e,\lambda}$.

**Step 2:** The angle subtended at the Earth's centre between the Earth station and the satellite (the **central angle**) is found from spherical trigonometry:

$$\gamma = \arccos\!\left(\cos L_e \cdot \cos\beta\right)$$

**Step 3:** Apply the law of cosines in the plane containing the Earth centre (E), the Earth station (A), and the satellite (S). The triangle EAS has sides $R_E$, $r_{GEO}$, and $d$ (the slant range).

$$d^2 = R_E^2 + r_{GEO}^2 - 2 R_E r_{GEO} \cos\gamma$$

$$\boxed{\,d = \sqrt{R_E^2 + r_{GEO}^2 - 2 R_E r_{GEO} \cos\gamma}\,}$$

**Step 4:** Apply the law of sines to obtain the elevation angle $E_l$ at the Earth station.

$$\frac{\sin(90° + E_l)}{r_{GEO}} = \frac{\sin\gamma}{d}$$

$$\boxed{\,\tan E_l = \frac{\cos\gamma - R_E / r_{GEO}}{\sin\gamma}\,}$$

**Step 5:** The azimuth angle (measured clockwise from true North) is:

$$\boxed{\,A_z = 180° + \arctan\!\left(\frac{\tan\beta}{\sin L_e}\right)\,}$$

### 3.5 Derivation 5 — Link Budget and Carrier-to-Noise Ratio

The end-to-end performance of a satellite link is captured by $C/N_0$. Starting from the standard link equation:

**Step 1:** The flux density at the satellite from an Earth station of EIRP $\Phi$ at range $d$ is:

$$S = \frac{\Phi}{4\pi d^2}$$

**Step 2:** The carrier power captured by the satellite's receiving antenna (gain $G_{rs}$) is:

$$C = S \cdot \frac{\lambda^2}{4\pi} G_{rs} = \frac{\Phi G_{rs}}{L_{FS}}$$

**Step 3:** The system noise power in bandwidth $B$ is:

$$N = k T_{sys} B$$

**Step 4:** Form the carrier-to-noise-density ratio:

$$\frac{C}{N_0} = \frac{\Phi G_{rs} / L_{FS}}{k T_{sys}} = \text{EIRP} + \frac{G}{T} - L_{FS} - k$$

**Step 5:** In decibels (with $k$ in dBW/K/Hz):

$$\boxed{\,\frac{C}{N_0}\,(dB\cdot Hz) = \text{EIRP}\,(dBW) + \frac{G}{T}\,(dB/K) - L_{FS}\,(dB) - k\,(dBW/K/Hz)\,}$$

where $k = -228.6$ dBW/K/Hz. So the constant term is $-(-228.6) = +228.6$.

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Examples (KTU-Style)

#### **Example 1: Geostationary Altitude Re-derivation**

A satellite orbits Earth in a circular equatorial orbit with $T = 86{,}164$ s. Find its altitude.

$$\begin{aligned}
r &= \left(\frac{\mu T^2}{4\pi^2}\right)^{1/3} \\
&= \left(\frac{3.986 \times 10^{14} \times (86{,}164)^2}{4 \pi^2}\right)^{1/3} \\
&= \left(7.50 \times 10^{22}\right)^{1/3} \\
&= 42{,}164 \text{ km}
\end{aligned}$$

$$h = r - R_E = 42{,}164 - 6{,}378 = \boxed{35{,}786 \text{ km}}$$

#### **Example 2: Free-Space Loss at Geostationary Distance**

Find the free-space path loss for a 6 GHz downlink to a slant range of $d = 38{,}500$ km.

$$L_{FS}(dB) = 32.45 + 20\log_{10}(38{,}500) + 20\log_{10}(6{,}000)$$

$$= 32.45 + 91.71 + 75.56 = \boxed{199.72 \text{ dB}}$$

#### **Example 3: Look Angles for an Earth Station in Trivandrum**

Earth station at $L_e = 8.5°$ N, $L_{e,\lambda} = 76.9°$ E. Satellite at $L_{s,\lambda} = 83°$ E (typical INSAT position).

$$\begin{aligned}
\beta &= 83° - 76.9° = 6.1° \\
\gamma &= \arccos(\cos 8.5° \cdot \cos 6.1°) \\
&= \arccos(0.9891 \times 0.9943) \\
&= \arccos(0.9835) = 10.45° \\
d &= \sqrt{6378^2 + 42164^2 - 2 \times 6378 \times 42164 \times \cos 10.45°} \\
&\approx \boxed{37{,}943 \text{ km}} \\
\tan E_l &= \frac{\cos 10.45° - 6378/42164}{\sin 10.45°} \\
&= \frac{0.9834 - 0.1513}{0.1815} = 4.585 \\
E_l &= \boxed{77.7°}
\end{aligned}$$

The very high elevation confirms that Trivandrum, being close to the equator, has near-zenith look angles — a key reason India chose the **INSAT** system over **Molniya** for tropical latitudes.

#### **Example 4: Carrier-to-Noise Density**

A satellite downlink has EIRP = 50 dBW, $G/T = 5$ dB/K, FSPL = 200 dB, and additional losses 2 dB.

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{C}{N_0} &= 50 + 5 - 200 - 2 + 228.6 \\
&= \boxed{81.6 \text{ dB·Hz}}
\end{aligned}$$

For a transponder bandwidth of 36 MHz:

$$\frac{C}{N} = 81.6 - 10\log_{10}(36 \times 10^6) = 81.6 - 75.56 = 6.04 \text{ dB}$$

#### **Example 5: Round-Trip Light Time (Why GEO Cannot Support Real-Time Voice Without Delay)**

$$\Delta t = \frac{2 h_{GEO}}{c} = \frac{2 \times 35{,}786{,}000}{3 \times 10^8} \approx 0.24 \text{ s}$$

A one-way voice link over GEO thus adds **240 ms** of one-way delay — well above the 150 ms threshold for natural conversation. This is why LEO constellations (Starlink: ~25 ms latency) are disruptive.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — End-to-End Satellite Communication System

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the signal flow from the originating Earth station, through the satellite transponder, to the destination Earth station. All node identifiers are alphanumeric (no reserved keywords) and labels are clean uppercase alphanumeric text only.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[ES TX 1 ORIGINATING EARTH STATION] --> A2[MODULATOR AND UPCONVERTER]
    A2 --> A3[HPA HIGH POWER AMPLIFIER TWTA]
    A3 --> A4[FEED AND ANTENNA GAIN GT]
    A4 -->|UPLINK 6 GHZ| B0[FREE SPACE PATH LOSS LFS UPLINK]
    B0 --> B1[SATELLITE RECEIVING ANTENNA]
    B1 --> B2[LNA LOW NOISE AMPLIFIER]
    B2 --> B3[DOWNCONVERTER IF PROCESSING]
    B3 --> B4[HPA SSPA OR TWTA ONBOARD]
    B4 --> B5[BEAM FORMING NETWORK AND COVERAGE]
    B5 -->|DOWNLINK 4 GHZ| C0[FREE SPACE PATH LOSS LFS DOWNLINK]
    C0 --> C1[ES RX 2 DESTINATION EARTH STATION]
    C1 --> C2[LNA GROUND RECEIVER]
    C2 --> C3[DEMODULATOR AND DECODER]
    C3 --> C4[USER DATA OUTPUT VOICE VIDEO DATA]
    B5 --> B6[TT AND C SUBSYSTEM TELEMETRY TRACKING AND COMMAND]
    B6 --> B7[ORBIT CONTROL AND STATION KEEPING]

    B6 -. TT AND C DOWNLINK .-> A1
```

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question A1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
*Define a geostationary satellite. What are the conditions required for a satellite to be geostationary?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A geostationary satellite is one that appears stationary relative to an observer on Earth. For a satellite to be geostationary, three conditions must be simultaneously satisfied:
1. The orbital period must equal the Earth's sidereal rotation period ($T = 86{,}164.1$ s).
2. The orbit must be **circular** (eccentricity $e = 0$).
3. The orbit must lie in the **equatorial plane** (inclination $i = 0°$).
The resulting orbit has a radius of 42,164 km and an altitude of 35,786 km above mean sea level. *(3 marks — 1 for definition + 2 for conditions.)*

#### **Question A2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
*List any six satellite frequency bands and state one application of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — table format expected):**

| Band | Range | Application |
|---|---|---|
| L | 1–2 GHz | Mobile satellite (Inmarsat), GPS |
| S | 2–4 GHz | NASA TT&C |
| C | 4–8 GHz | FSS, Intelsat |
| Ku | 12–18 GHz | DBS-TV |
| Ka | 27–40 GHz | Starlink HTS |

*(Any six correct pairs = 3 marks.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Derive the orbital period of a geostationary satellite and show that its altitude above the Earth's surface is approximately 35,786 km. Assume Earth's mass $M_E = 5.98 \times 10^{24}$ kg, $G = 6.67 \times 10^{-11}$ N·m²/kg², and $R_E = 6{,}378$ km. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A satellite downlink operates at 4 GHz with a slant range of $40{,}000$ km. The Earth station has an antenna gain of 50 dBi and a system noise temperature of 150 K. The satellite's EIRP is 40 dBW. Compute (i) the free-space path loss in dB, (ii) the carrier-to-noise ratio $C/N$ in a 36 MHz bandwidth. *(7 marks)*

#### **Model Solution A(a):**

**Step 1:** For a circular orbit, equate gravitational and centripetal forces.

$$\frac{G M_E m}{r^2} = \frac{m v^2}{r} \Rightarrow v^2 = \frac{G M_E}{r}$$

**Step 2:** The orbital period $T = \dfrac{2\pi r}{v}$, so $v = \dfrac{2\pi r}{T}$.

$$\left(\frac{2\pi r}{T}\right)^2 = \frac{G M_E}{r}$$

**Step 3:** Rearrange for $r$.

$$r = \left(\frac{G M_E T^2}{4\pi^2}\right)^{1/3}$$

**Step 4:** For geostationary, $T = 86{,}164$ s.

$$\begin{aligned}
GM_E &= 6.67 \times 10^{-11} \times 5.98 \times 10^{24} \\
&= 3.986 \times 10^{14} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}^2 \\
r &= \left(\frac{3.986 \times 10^{14} \times 7.428 \times 10^9}{39.478}\right)^{1/3} \\
&= (7.50 \times 10^{22})^{1/3} \\
&= 42{,}164 \text{ km}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5:** Subtract Earth's radius.

$$h = 42{,}164 - 6{,}378 = 35{,}786 \text{ km} \quad \blacksquare$$

#### **Model Solution A(b):**

**(i)** FSPL:
$$L_{FS} = 32.45 + 20\log_{10}(40{,}000) + 20\log_{10}(4{,}000)$$
$$= 32.45 + 92.04 + 72.04 = \boxed{196.53 \text{ dB}}$$

**(ii)** Carrier-to-noise density:
$$\frac{C}{N_0} = \text{EIRP} + \frac{G}{T} - L_{FS} + 228.6$$

Compute $G/T$:

$$\frac{G}{T} = 50 - 10\log_{10}(150) = 50 - 21.76 = 28.24 \text{ dB/K}$$

$$\frac{C}{N_0} = 40 + 28.24 - 196.53 + 228.6 = 100.31 \text{ dB·Hz}$$

Then in 36 MHz bandwidth:

$$\frac{C}{N} = 100.31 - 10\log_{10}(36 \times 10^6) = 100.31 - 75.56 = \boxed{24.75 \text{ dB}}$$

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** Define and distinguish between LEO, MEO, and GEO orbits. Compare them in terms of altitude, period, propagation delay, and typical applications. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** An Earth station is located at $L_e = 13°$ N, $L_{e,\lambda} = 80°$ E. A geostationary satellite is located at $L_{s,\lambda} = 83°$ E. Calculate the (i) central angle $\gamma$, (ii) slant range $d$, and (iii) elevation angle $E_l$. Take $R_E = 6{,}378$ km and $r_{GEO} = 42{,}164$ km. *(7 marks)*

#### **Model Solution B(a):**

| Parameter | LEO | MEO | GEO |
|---|---|---|---|
| Altitude | 160 – 2,000 km | 2,000 – 35,786 km | **35,786 km** |
| Period | 88 – 127 min | 2 – 24 hr | **23h 56m 4s** |
| One-way delay | 1 – 10 ms | 20 – 80 ms | **~240 ms** |
| Footprint | Small | Moderate | **Huge (~1/3 Earth)** |
| Examples | Starlink, Iridium | GPS, GLONASS, NavIC | INSAT, Inmarsat |
| Visibility | Few minutes | Few hours | **Continuous** |

LEOs require *constellations* for continuous coverage (handover needed). MEOs are used primarily for navigation (since three satellites at ~20,200 km give global coverage). GEOs are preferred for broadcasting because a single satellite covers a continent with no handover. *(7 marks — 1 per row + 2 for distinction narrative.)*

#### **Model Solution B(b):**

**(i) Longitude difference:**
$$\beta = 83° - 80° = 3°$$

**Central angle:**
$$\gamma = \arccos(\cos 13° \cdot \cos 3°) = \arccos(0.9744 \times 0.9986) = \arccos(0.9730) = 13.26°$$

**(ii) Slant range** (apply law of cosines):
$$d = \sqrt{6378^2 + 42164^2 - 2 \times 6378 \times 42164 \times \cos 13.26°}$$
$$= \sqrt{4.068 \times 10^7 + 1.778 \times 10^9 - 2 \times 2.690 \times 10^8 \times 0.9732}$$
$$= \sqrt{1.778 \times 10^9 + 4.068 \times 10^7 - 5.235 \times 10^8}$$
$$= \sqrt{1.295 \times 10^9} = 35{,}993 \text{ km}$$

**(iii) Elevation angle:**
$$\tan E_l = \frac{\cos 13.26° - 6378/42164}{\sin 13.26°} = \frac{0.9732 - 0.1513}{0.2294} = 3.585$$
$$E_l = \boxed{74.4°}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark-Loss Zones:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch:** Confusing $d$ in km with frequency in Hz in the FSPL formula. Always use $d_{km}$ and $f_{MHz}$ (the constants 32.45 and 20 are derived for these units).
> 2. **Sidereal vs Solar day:** A common error is using $T = 24$ hr instead of $T = 86{,}164.1$ s. Using 24 hr gives altitude ~35,888 km (close but still wrong, and KTU strict markers will deduct 1 mark).
> 3. **Missing +228.6:** When converting $C/N_0$ to $C/N$ via the Boltzmann constant, students often forget to add $+228.6$. This costs ~2 marks.
> 4. **Look angle sign:** Earth station latitude is **positive** for North, negative for South. Always specify.
> 5. **Cosine argument:** In $\gamma = \arccos(\cos L_e \cos\beta)$, students sometimes write $\cos(L_e \cdot \beta)$ — this is incorrect multiplication.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Geostationary** = circular + equatorial + period = sidereal day → altitude **35,786 km**.
- [x] **Kepler's Third Law (Newton):** $T^2 = \dfrac{4\pi^2}{\mu} a^3$.
- [x] **Earth's GM:** $\mu = 3.986 \times 10^{14}$ m³/s²; **Boltzmann:** $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K.
- [x] **EIRP** (dBW) = $P_t + G_t - L_{feed}$ (transmitter output).
- [x] **FSPL** (dB) = $32.45 + 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz})$.
- [x] **CNR link budget:** $C/N_0 = \text{EIRP} + G/T - L_{FS} - L_{misc} + 228.6$ (dB·Hz).
- [x] **GEO one-way delay ≈ 240 ms** → too large for natural voice telephony.
- [x] **Look angles:** central angle uses $\gamma = \arccos(\cos L_e \cos\beta)$.
- [x] **Higher frequency = higher rain attenuation** → C-band preferred in tropical Kerala.
- [x] **Uplink ≠ Downlink frequency** → prevents feedback oscillation at the transponder.
- [x] **LEO advantages:** low delay, low FSPL. **Disadvantages:** short visibility, needs many satellites.
- [x] **GEO advantage:** single-satellite coverage of a continent, no handover.
- [x] **Total cascaded CNR:** $\dfrac{1}{C/N_{tot}} = \dfrac{1}{C/N_{up}} + \dfrac{1}{C/N_{down}}$ (power-domain sum).
- [x] **Round-trip GEO light time ≈ 0.48 s** → important for radar altimetry and ranging.
- [x] **Doppler shift formula:** $\Delta f = \dfrac{v_r f_c}{c}$ (significant for LEO, negligible for GEO).
- [x] **Orbital velocity at GEO:** $v = \sqrt{\mu/r} \approx 3.07$ km/s — matches Earth's surface rotation at equator.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Tip:** When asked to "show that altitude is 35,786 km" in an exam, **always show the intermediate cube root step explicitly**. Examiners award partial marks for correct intermediate values. Never skip to the final answer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Geostationary Orbits
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT634 - COMMUNICATION SYSTEM AND TELEMETRY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Geostationary Orbits

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition

A **Geostationary Orbit (GEO)** is a unique circular, equatorial, and direct (prograde) orbit around the Earth in which a satellite appears **stationary relative to a fixed point on the Earth's surface**. This remarkable property is achieved by matching the satellite's **orbital period** to the Earth's **sidereal rotation period** (≈ **23 hours 56 minutes 4 seconds**, or 86164 seconds) and placing the satellite in the **equatorial plane** with **zero inclination** and **zero eccentricity**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Mapping):**
> A geostationary orbit is a circular orbit at an altitude where the orbital period equals the Earth's rotational period. For the satellite to remain stationary relative to Earth, three conditions must be satisfied simultaneously:
> 1. **Orbital period** = **Sidereal day** (23 h 56 min 4 s)
> 2. **Orbital eccentricity** = **0** (perfectly circular)
> 3. **Orbital inclination** = **0°** (lies in the equatorial plane)

The standard reference altitude of a geostationary satellite is **35,786 km** above mean sea level, placing the satellite at a geocentric radius of approximately **42,164 km**.

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a child sitting on a **slow-moving merry-go-round (carousel)** holding a long stick that extends vertically upward. The child rotates with the carousel, but the top of the stick traces a **circle in the air**. Now, place a small toy airplane at the tip of the stick, but the airplane flies forward at **exactly the same angular speed** as the carousel rotates. To an observer standing on the ground far away, the airplane appears to **hover in one spot**, even though it is actually moving at enormous speed (~3.07 km/s).

That hovering toy airplane is a **geostationary satellite**. The carousel is the rotating Earth, and the airplane's forward motion is its orbital velocity — perfectly synchronized so that as the Earth turns one way, the satellite "falls" around the Earth at the same angular rate.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants (KTU Mandatory Memorization):**
> - Gravitational Constant: **G = 6.674 × 10⁻¹¹ N·m²/kg²**
> - Mass of Earth: **M_E = 5.972 × 10²⁴ kg**
> - Earth's Mean Radius: **R_E = 6,378 km** (equatorial: 6,378.137 km)
> - Geocentric Gravitational Parameter: **μ = GM_E = 3.986 × 10¹⁴ m³/s²**
> - Sidereal Day: **T_s = 86,164 s** (≈ 23.934 hours)
> - Solar Day: **T_solar = 86,400 s** (24 hours)

## Why Geostationary Orbits Matter in Telemetry

Telemetry systems require **continuous, uninterrupted data relay** from remote platforms (rockets, weather balloons, buoys, spacecraft). A geostationary satellite acts as a **permanent relay tower in the sky**, providing:
- **Continuous visibility** to ~42% of Earth's surface from a single satellite
- **No Doppler shift** for stationary or near-stationary transmitters
- **Simplified ground antenna tracking** (fixed-pointing dishes)
- **Predictable signal path loss** for link-budget calculations

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Sanity Check for the Exam:**
> If a question mentions a satellite "appearing fixed in the sky from the ground," it is a **geostationary** satellite. If a question mentions a satellite "passing overhead periodically," it is a **non-geostationary** satellite (LEO/MEO/HEO).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of a Geostationary Orbit

### Pillar 1 — Equatorial Plane (Inclination i = 0°)

For a satellite to appear stationary, its orbital plane must coincide with the Earth's equatorial plane. If the inclination is non-zero, the satellite traces a **figure-eight analemma** in the sky relative to a ground observer, oscillating north and south each day. Only when **i = 0°** does the ground track collapse to a single point.

### Pillar 2 — Circular Orbit (Eccentricity e = 0)

A non-zero eccentricity causes the satellite's altitude to vary, which in turn makes its **angular velocity vary** (per Kepler's 2nd law). The satellite would appear to drift east-west each day, defeating the "stationary" property. Thus, **e = 0** is required.

### Pillar 3 — Synchronous Period (T = Sidereal Day)

The orbital period must equal the Earth's rotation period relative to the **fixed stars** (sidereal), not the Sun. This is because "stationary" means fixed with respect to the rotating Earth frame, and the Earth rotates 360° relative to the stars in one sidereal day.

## 2.2 The Balancing Act: Gravity vs. Centrifugal Force

For any satellite in circular orbit, the **centripetal force required** to keep it moving in a circle is provided entirely by **Earth's gravitational pull**:

$$F_{\text{centripetal}} = F_{\text{gravity}}$$

$$\frac{mv^2}{r} = \frac{GM_E m}{r^2}$$

The satellite's mass cancels, yielding a mass-independent orbital velocity:

$$v = \sqrt{\frac{GM_E}{r}}$$

For the **geostationary** case, the velocity must be such that one full orbit takes exactly one sidereal day:

$$v = \frac{2\pi r}{T_s}$$

## 2.3 Orbital Mechanics — Kepler's 3rd Law

Kepler's Third Law for circular orbits around Earth states:

$$T^2 = \frac{4\pi^2 r^3}{GM_E}$$

This is the **single most important equation** in orbital mechanics for KTU examinations. It allows us to find the **orbital radius** corresponding to any desired period.

Solving for r:

$$r = \left(\frac{GM_E T^2}{4\pi^2}\right)^{1/3}$$

## 2.4 Look Angles — Pointing a Ground Antenna

A ground station must know two angles to point its antenna accurately at a geostationary satellite:
- **Elevation Angle (El):** The angle above the local horizontal plane.
- **Azimuth Angle (Az):** The compass bearing (measured clockwise from North).

The elevation angle depends on the satellite's orbital radius and the ground station's latitude:

$$\sin(\theta_{El}) = \frac{r \cos(\lambda) - R_E}{r}$$

where:
- **θ_El** = Elevation angle
- **λ** = Ground station latitude (absolute value)
- **r** = Geocentric orbital radius of satellite
- **R_E** = Earth's mean radius

> [!NOTE]
> **Why λ must be in absolute value:** Geostationary satellites lie over the equator. The geometry is symmetric about the equator, so the formula uses the magnitude of latitude.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Orbital Period (Kepler's 3rd) | $T^2 = \dfrac{4\pi^2 r^3}{GM_E}$ | s² (when r in m) | **Master Equation** |
| 2 | Orbital Radius (solved) | $r = \left(\dfrac{GM_E T^2}{4\pi^2}\right)^{1/3}$ | m | For T = 86,164 s → r = 42,164 km |
| 3 | Altitude Above Earth | $h = r - R_E$ | m | Standard GEO altitude: **35,786 km** |
| 4 | Orbital Velocity | $v = \sqrt{\dfrac{GM_E}{r}}$ | m/s | GEO velocity: **3.07 km/s** |
| 5 | Orbital Angular Velocity | $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi}{T}$ | rad/s | GEO: $\omega = 7.2921 \times 10^{-5}$ rad/s |
| 6 | Elevation Angle | $\sin(\theta_{El}) = \dfrac{r\cos\lambda - R_E}{r}$ | degrees | λ is absolute latitude |
| 7 | Azimuth Angle | $\cos(\theta_{Az}) = \dfrac{\tan\lambda}{\sin\Delta\phi}$ | degrees | Δφ = longitude difference |
| 8 | Slant Range | $d = \sqrt{R_E^2 + r^2 - 2R_E r \cos\lambda \cos\Delta\phi}$ | m | Used for path-loss |
| 9 | Free-Space Path Loss | $FSPL = 32.45 + 20\log d_{(km)} + 20\log f_{(MHz)}$ | dB | Telemetry link budget |
| 10 | Centripetal Accn. | $a_c = \omega^2 r = \dfrac{v^2}{r}$ | m/s² | For circular orbit |

> [!TIP]
> **Memorization Tip for KTU 2024 ESE:**
> The four golden numbers you must recall cold: **r = 42,164 km**, **h = 35,786 km**, **T = 86,164 s**, **v = 3.07 km/s**. Any one of these can be the seed for a 14-mark derivation question.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Geostationary orbits are the **backbone of modern communication and telemetry infrastructure**:
- **INSAT/GSAT series (India):** Weather imaging, DTH television, VSAT communications
- **GOES (USA):** Continuous weather monitoring for the western hemisphere
- **Meteosat (Europe):** Telemetry of atmospheric data
- **Telemetry, Tracking & Command (TTC):** Spacecraft beyond LEO use GEO as a relay hub (e.g., Chandrayaan, Mars Orbiter Mission used DSN via GEO)

The **ISRO Master Control Facility (MCF) at Hassan** maintains the health of India's GEO fleet through **station-keeping maneuvers** (north-south and east-west), which we will examine in Section 3.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Geostationary Orbital Radius

We begin with Newton's law of gravitation and the requirement for uniform circular motion.

**Step 1 — Write the gravitational force between Earth (mass $M_E$) and satellite (mass $m$) at distance $r$:**

$$F_g = \frac{GM_E m}{r^2}$$

**Step 2 — Write the required centripetal force for the satellite to follow a circular path of radius $r$ at angular velocity $\omega$:**

$$F_c = m\omega^2 r$$

**Step 3 — Equate $F_c$ and $F_g$ (gravity supplies the centripetal force):**

$$m\omega^2 r = \frac{GM_E m}{r^2}$$

**Step 4 — Cancel $m$ and solve for $\omega^2$:**

$$\omega^2 = \frac{GM_E}{r^3}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi}{T}$ (one full revolution in time T):**

$$\left(\frac{2\pi}{T}\right)^2 = \frac{GM_E}{r^3}$$

**Step 6 — Rearrange (Kepler's Third Law for circular orbits):**

$$T^2 = \frac{4\pi^2 r^3}{GM_E}$$

$$r^3 = \frac{GM_E T^2}{4\pi^2}$$

**Step 7 — Cube-root both sides:**

$$r = \left(\frac{GM_E T^2}{4\pi^2}\right)^{1/3}$$

**Step 8 — Plug in the geostationary period T = 86,164 s:**

$$r = \left(\frac{(3.986 \times 10^{14}) \times (86{,}164)^2}{4\pi^2}\right)^{1/3}$$

Let us evaluate the numerator and denominator inside the parentheses:

$$GM_E \cdot T^2 = (3.986 \times 10^{14}) \times (7.4296 \times 10^9)$$

$$GM_E \cdot T^2 = 2.9614 \times 10^{24} \text{ m}^3$$

$$4\pi^2 = 4 \times (9.8696) = 39.4784$$

$$\frac{GM_E T^2}{4\pi^2} = \frac{2.9614 \times 10^{24}}{39.4784} = 7.5007 \times 10^{22} \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 9 — Take the cube root:**

$$r = (7.5007 \times 10^{22})^{1/3}$$

$$r = 4.2164 \times 10^7 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{r \approx 42{,}164 \text{ km}}$$

**Step 10 — Subtract Earth's mean radius to get altitude:**

$$h = r - R_E = 42{,}164 \text{ km} - 6{,}378 \text{ km}$$

$$\boxed{h \approx 35{,}786 \text{ km}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Geostationary Orbital Velocity

Starting from the force balance (Step 3 of Derivation 1):

$$\frac{mv^2}{r} = \frac{GM_E m}{r^2}$$

$$v^2 = \frac{GM_E}{r}$$

$$v = \sqrt{\frac{GM_E}{r}}$$

**Substituting $GM_E = 3.986 \times 10^{14}$ m³/s² and $r = 4.2164 \times 10^7$ m:**

$$v = \sqrt{\frac{3.986 \times 10^{14}}{4.2164 \times 10^7}}$$

$$v = \sqrt{9.4528 \times 10^6}$$

$$\boxed{v \approx 3.07 \text{ km/s}}$$

**Equivalently, using $v = \dfrac{2\pi r}{T}$:**

$$v = \frac{2\pi \times 4.2164 \times 10^7}{86{,}164}$$

$$v = \frac{2.6491 \times 10^8}{86{,}164} = 3.074 \times 10^3 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\boxed{v \approx 3.07 \text{ km/s}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Elevation Angle from Ground Station

**Step 1 — Draw the geometry: Earth center O, ground station G (latitude λ on the surface), satellite S (on the line of the geostationary orbit directly above the equator's intersection with the meridian of the station).**

**Step 2 — Identify the triangle O-G-S:**
- Side OG = R_E (Earth's radius)
- Side OS = r (geocentric orbital radius)
- The angle at G between the local vertical (toward O) and the line to S is $(90° - \theta_{El})$.

**Step 3 — The angle $\angle GOS$** is the geocentric angle between the ground station's radial line and the satellite's radial line. For a station at latitude λ directly under the satellite's meridian, this angle equals λ.

**Step 4 — Apply the Law of Sines to triangle OGS:**

$$\frac{\sin(\theta_{El})}{\sin(\angle GOS)} = \frac{\sin(\theta_{El})}{\sin\lambda} = \frac{r}{OS}\cdot\frac{1}{\text{something}}$$

**More directly, apply the Law of Cosines on side GS:**

$$GS^2 = R_E^2 + r^2 - 2 R_E r \cos\lambda$$

**Step 5 — For the elevation angle, consider the projection along the local horizontal at G. The line GS makes an angle $\theta_{El}$ above the horizontal. The component of GS along the radial direction OG is:**

$$GS \cos(90° - \theta_{El}) = GS \sin(\theta_{El})$$

**But this radial component is also:**

$$r \cos\lambda - R_E$$

**Therefore:**

$$GS \sin(\theta_{El}) = r\cos\lambda - R_E$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $\theta_{El}$:**

$$\sin(\theta_{El}) = \frac{r\cos\lambda - R_E}{GS}$$

**For the maximum-elevation case (satellite directly overhead, Δφ = 0):**

$$GS = r - R_E$$

$$\sin(\theta_{El,\max}) = \frac{r\cos\lambda - R_E}{r - R_E}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Special Case:** For a station on the equator (λ = 0°), $\sin(\theta_{El,\max}) = 1$, so $\theta_{El,\max} = 90°$. The satellite is directly overhead — true zenith viewing.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Time of Flight Around a Geostationary Orbit

| Segment of Orbit | Distance Travelled | Time Elapsed |
|------------------|--------------------|--------------|
| Quarter orbit (90°) | $\dfrac{\pi r}{2} = 66{,}236$ km | $\dfrac{T}{4} = 21{,}541$ s (≈ 5.98 h) |
| Half orbit (180°) | $\pi r = 132{,}472$ km | $\dfrac{T}{2} = 43{,}082$ s (≈ 11.97 h) |
| Full orbit (360°) | $2\pi r = 264{,}944$ km | $T = 86{,}164$ s (≈ 23.93 h) |

**Numerical sanity:** $2\pi r = 2 \times 3.14159 \times 42{,}164 = 264{,}942$ km. This is the **circumference** of the geostationary orbit — useful for satellite spacing and frequency-reuse calculations.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** A communication satellite is to be placed in a geostationary orbit over the Indian Ocean at 82°E longitude. Calculate (a) the orbital radius, (b) the altitude above mean sea level, and (c) the orbital velocity. (Given: $GM_E = 3.986 \times 10^{14}$ m³/s², $R_E = 6{,}378$ km, $T = 86{,}164$ s.)

**Solution (a) — Orbital Radius:**

$$r = \left(\frac{GM_E T^2}{4\pi^2}\right)^{1/3}$$

$$r = \left(\frac{(3.986 \times 10^{14}) \times (86{,}164)^2}{39.4784}\right)^{1/3}$$

$$r = (7.5007 \times 10^{22})^{1/3} = 4.2164 \times 10^7 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{r = 42{,}164 \text{ km}}$$

**Solution (b) — Altitude:**

$$h = r - R_E = 42{,}164 - 6{,}378 = 35{,}786 \text{ km}$$

**Solution (c) — Orbital Velocity:**

$$v = \frac{2\pi r}{T} = \frac{2\pi \times 4.2164 \times 10^7}{86{,}164}$$

$$v = 3.07 \times 10^3 \text{ m/s} = 3.07 \text{ km/s}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Define a geostationary orbit. State the three essential conditions a satellite must satisfy to be in a geostationary orbit.**

**Model Answer:**
A geostationary orbit is a circular orbit around the Earth in which a satellite appears stationary relative to a point on the Earth's surface.

The three essential conditions are:
1. The orbital period must equal the **sidereal rotation period of the Earth (86,164 s)**.
2. The orbit must be **circular** (eccentricity e = 0).
3. The orbit must lie in the **equatorial plane** (inclination i = 0°).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write "24 hours" instead of "86,164 s (sidereal day)". The 24-hour figure is the *solar* day. The sidereal day is shorter because the Earth also moves along its orbit. Full 3 marks requires mentioning **sidereal** specifically.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Why is a geostationary satellite placed at an altitude of approximately 35,786 km above the Earth's surface? Justify with the relevant orbital period equation.**

**Model Answer:**
A geostationary satellite is placed at 35,786 km because this altitude gives an orbital period equal to the Earth's sidereal rotation period (86,164 s).

From Kepler's Third Law:

$$T^2 = \frac{4\pi^2 r^3}{GM_E}$$

For $T = 86{,}164$ s and $GM_E = 3.986 \times 10^{14}$ m³/s²:

$$r = \left(\frac{GM_E T^2}{4\pi^2}\right)^{1/3} = 42{,}164 \text{ km}$$

$$h = r - R_E = 42{,}164 - 6{,}378 = 35{,}786 \text{ km}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Marks are lost when students quote the altitude without deriving it. Even a 3-mark answer benefits from a one-line statement of Kepler's law and the substitution.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2023]

**(a)** Derive an expression for the orbital radius of a geostationary satellite. Using the result, calculate the altitude and orbital velocity of a geostationary satellite. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define elevation and azimuth angles. A ground station is located at a latitude of 13° N and longitude of 77° E. A geostationary satellite is located at 82° E longitude. Calculate the elevation angle of the satellite as seen from the ground station, assuming the satellite is on the same meridian. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

Starting from gravitational force = centripetal force:

$$\frac{GM_E m}{r^2} = m\omega^2 r$$

With $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi}{T}$:

$$r^3 = \frac{GM_E T^2}{4\pi^2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad r = \left(\frac{GM_E T^2}{4\pi^2}\right)^{1/3}$$

Substituting $T = 86{,}164$ s:

$$r = \left(\frac{(3.986 \times 10^{14}) \times (86{,}164)^2}{39.478}\right)^{1/3} = 42{,}164 \text{ km}$$

**Altitude:**

$$h = r - R_E = 42{,}164 - 6{,}378 = 35{,}786 \text{ km}$$

**Orbital Velocity:**

$$v = \frac{2\pi r}{T} = \frac{2\pi \times 42{,}164}{86{,}164} = 3.07 \text{ km/s}$$

**Model Solution (b):**

**Elevation angle (θ_El):** The angle between the local horizontal at the ground station and the line of sight to the satellite.

**Azimuth angle (θ_Az):** The compass bearing of the satellite measured clockwise from true North.

For a ground station at latitude λ and the satellite on the same meridian (Δφ = 0):

$$\sin(\theta_{El}) = \frac{r\cos\lambda - R_E}{r - R_E}$$

Substituting $r = 42{,}164$ km, $R_E = 6{,}378$ km, $\lambda = 13°$:

$$\sin(\theta_{El}) = \frac{42{,}164 \cos 13° - 6{,}378}{42{,}164 - 6{,}378}$$

$$\cos 13° = 0.9744$$

$$\sin(\theta_{El}) = \frac{42{,}164 \times 0.9744 - 6{,}378}{35{,}786} = \frac{41{,}085 - 6{,}378}{35{,}786}$$

$$\sin(\theta_{El}) = \frac{34{,}707}{35{,}786} = 0.9699$$

$$\theta_{El} = \sin^{-1}(0.9699) = \boxed{75.96° \approx 76°}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Common errors: (1) using $\sin\lambda$ instead of $\cos\lambda$ — **no marks**; (2) forgetting to use the **absolute** value of latitude; (3) omitting the slant range substitution when Δφ ≠ 0. The "same meridian" simplification must be stated explicitly.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**(a)** Explain the orbital perturbation effects that cause a geostationary satellite to drift from its nominal position. What is meant by "station keeping"? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Three geostationary satellites are placed at longitudes 60° E, 90° E, and 120° E. Calculate the minimum slant range from a ground station at Trivandrum (8.5° N, 77° E) to the satellite at 90° E, and comment on the minimum number of satellites needed to provide global (excluding polar) coverage. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

**Orbital Perturbations affecting geostationary satellites:**

1. **Lunar and Solar gravitational perturbations** — The Moon and Sun exert tidal forces that cause the orbital inclination to increase by about **0.85°/year**. This makes the satellite oscillate north-south each day.

2. **Solar radiation pressure** — Photons from the Sun exert a continuous force on the satellite, especially on large solar panels, causing east-west drift.

3. **Earth's gravitational anomalies (J2 effect)** — Earth's oblateness (J2 coefficient) causes a slow regression of the orbital plane (a precession).

4. **Triaxiality of Earth** — Earth's slight "pear shape" creates preferred longitudes (stable at 75.2° E and 104.7° W, unstable at 165.3° E and 14.7° W).

**Station keeping** is the active process of firing onboard thrusters (chemical or ion) to correct the satellite's drift and keep it within its assigned longitude/latitude box. Typical station-keeping budgets:
- **North-south station keeping** — every 2 weeks
- **East-west station keeping** — every 2-3 months

**Model Solution (b):**

**Slant Range** (using the law of cosines on the triangle O-G-S):

$$d = \sqrt{R_E^2 + r^2 - 2 R_E r \cos\lambda \cos\Delta\phi}$$

Where:
- $R_E = 6{,}378$ km
- $r = 42{,}164$ km
- $\lambda = 8.5°$ (latitude of Trivandrum)
- $\Delta\phi = 90° - 77° = 13°$ (longitude difference)

**Compute each term:**

$$R_E^2 = (6{,}378)^2 = 4.068 \times 10^7 \text{ km}^2$$

$$r^2 = (42{,}164)^2 = 1.7778 \times 10^9 \text{ km}^2$$

$$2 R_E r \cos\lambda \cos\Delta\phi = 2 \times 6{,}378 \times 42{,}164 \times \cos 8.5° \times \cos 13°$$

$$\cos 8.5° = 0.9890, \quad \cos 13° = 0.9744$$

$$2 R_E r \cos\lambda \cos\Delta\phi = 2 \times 6{,}378 \times 42{,}164 \times 0.9890 \times 0.9744$$

$$= 5.3782 \times 10^8 \times 0.9636 = 5.1825 \times 10^8 \text{ km}^2$$

**Substituting:**

$$d^2 = 4.068 \times 10^7 + 1.7778 \times 10^9 - 5.1825 \times 10^8$$

$$d^2 = 1.3002 \times 10^9 \text{ km}^2$$

$$d = \sqrt{1.3002 \times 10^9} = \boxed{36{,}058 \text{ km}}$$

**Comment on global coverage:** A single geostationary satellite can see approximately **42% of Earth's surface** (out to a minimum elevation of 0°). **Three satellites** spaced 120° apart can provide continuous coverage of the entire equatorial belt and most populated regions, except the polar caps. Hence, the minimum number for "near-global" (excluding poles) coverage is **three**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Common mistakes: (1) Using $\sin\lambda$ instead of $\cos\lambda$ in the slant range equation; (2) Forgetting that $\Delta\phi$ is the **difference** in longitude, not the absolute longitude; (3) Confusing the minimum number of satellites for "global" vs. "equatorial" coverage. A clear statement that **3 satellites** provide near-global (non-polar) coverage is essential for full marks.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Read Before Every Exam):**

- **Definition:** A geostationary orbit is a **circular, equatorial, prograde** orbit with a period equal to the **sidereal day (86,164 s)**.
- **Three Conditions:** $T = 86{,}164$ s, $e = 0$, $i = 0°$. **Memorize these three.**
- **Master Equation:** $T^2 = \dfrac{4\pi^2 r^3}{GM_E}$ — this is Kepler's 3rd law and the gateway to all numerical problems.
- **Golden Numbers to Memorize:** $r = 42{,}164$ km, $h = 35{,}786$ km, $T = 86{,}164$ s, $v = 3.07$ km/s, $f = 3.07 \times 10^{-3}$ Hz.
- **Orbital Velocity Formula:** $v = \sqrt{GM_E / r}$ — can also use $v = 2\pi r / T$.
- **Elevation Angle:** $\sin(\theta_{El}) = \dfrac{r\cos\lambda - R_E}{r}$ (for Δφ = 0). Lower latitude → higher elevation. Polar stations get lower elevations.
- **Azimuth Angle:** Depends on the relative positions of the ground station and the satellite's longitude.
- **Slant Range:** $d = \sqrt{R_E^2 + r^2 - 2 R_E r \cos\lambda \cos\Delta\phi}$ — needed for path-loss calculations in telemetry link budgets.
- **Station Keeping:** Required to counteract **lunar/solar perturbations (inclination drift)**, **solar radiation pressure (east-west drift)**, and **Earth's J2 effect**. Uses **NSSK (North-South)** and **EWSK (East-West)** maneuvers.
- **Coverage:** A single GEO satellite covers **~42%** of Earth's surface. **Three GEO satellites** spaced 120° apart provide near-global (non-polar) coverage.
- **Applications:** DTH TV, VSAT, weather imaging, telemetry relay, TTC for deep-space missions.
- **KTU Common Traps:** Confusing **sidereal** and **solar** day; using $\sin\lambda$ instead of $\cos\lambda$ in elevation formula; forgetting to subtract $R_E$ to get altitude; mixing up inclination and eccentricity in the "three conditions" question.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Mantra:** Whenever a question on geostationary orbits appears, immediately write down the four golden numbers ($r, h, T, v$) and Kepler's 3rd law. The examiner can then see your foundational knowledge before you tackle the derivation — this often earns **partial credit** even if your derivation has a numerical slip.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Attitude Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT634 - COMMUNICATION SYSTEM AND TELEMETRY > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Attitude Control # Attitude Control — Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition > [!NOTE] > **Attitude Control** of a spacecraft is the precise regulation of its **orientation** (angular position) and **angular rate** with respect to a chosen **reference frame**, achieved through the application of internal or external torques. In telemetry engineering, it is the closed-loop mechanism that keeps an antenna pointed at Earth, a solar array aligned with the Sun, or a sensor directed toward a target. The **attitude** of a satellite is mathematically described by three independent angular parameters (commonly the **Euler angles** — *roll $\phi$, pitch $\theta$, yaw $\psi$*) that uniquely map the **body-fixed frame** $\{ \hat{b}_1, \hat{b}_2, \hat{b}_3 \}$ onto the **orbital / inertial reference frame** $\{ \hat{o}_1, \hat{o}_2, \hat{o}_3 \}$. The governing rigid-body rotational dynamics follow **Euler's Equation**: $$\vec{T}_{ext} + \vec{T}_{ctl} \;=\; \frac{d\vec{H}}{dt} \;=\; \mathbf{I}\,\dot{\vec{\omega}} \;+\; \vec{\omega}\times(\mathbf{I}\,\vec{\omega})$$ where $\vec{T}_{ctl}$ is the **control torque** generated by the actuator subsystem, and $\mathbf{I}$ is the **inertia tensor** of the spacecraft. ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!IMPORTANT] > **Analogy — The Spinning Top in Still Water:** > Imagine a remote-controlled toy boat floating on a perfectly still pond. It has no rudder; instead, a small internal flywheel spins. By accelerating or braking the flywheel, the boat turns. The boat's *heading* (its orientation on the water) is its **attitude**, the pond is the **orbital frame**, and the flywheel is the **reaction wheel actuator**. Now imagine the same boat on a windy pond — wind pushes it off-course, just like *gravity gradient torque* or *solar radiation pressure* push a satellite off its desired pointing. The flywheel plus a *compass* (sensor) form a *closed-loop attitude control system*. > > In essence: **Attitude Control = Sensing orientation + Computing error + Applying corrective torque.** ## 1.3 Why Attitude Control Matters in Telemetry For a **communication satellite**: - Antenna gain $G \propto 1/\theta_{pointing}^2$ — a pointing error of just **0.1°** at Ku-band can cause **dB-scale** link-budget loss. - Solar arrays must track the Sun to maintain bus power. - Heat radiators must avoid direct Sun exposure to prevent thermal runaway. For a **scientific / remote-sensing satellite**: - Imagery line-of-sight must be stable to fractions of an **arc-second** for sub-meter resolution. ## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Highlighted) - Earth's gravitational constant $\mu = 3.986 \times 10^{14}\ \text{m}^3/\text{s}^2$ - Earth rotation rate $\omega_E = 7.2921 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{rad/s}$ - 1 AU $= 1.496 \times 10^{11}\ \text{m}$ (for solar radiation pressure) - Standard **pointing accuracy** classes: coarse (1°–5°), medium (0.1°–1°), fine (< 0.1° / arc-sec) - **J2** oblateness coefficient of Earth $\approx 1.08263 \times 10^{-3}$ # Attitude Control — Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Reference Frames Used in Attitude Kinematics | Frame | Symbol | Origin | Used For | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Inertial (ECI) | $\mathcal{I}$ | Earth's centre | Newton's laws valid; star-tracker outputs | | Earth-Centred Earth-Fixed (ECEF) | $\mathcal{E}$ | Earth's centre | Ground antenna tracking | | Orbital | $\mathcal{O}$ | Satellite CoM | Most natural frame for *gravity-gradient* & *aerodynamic* torques | | Body-fixed | $\mathcal{B}$ | Satellite CoM | Where sensors and actuators physically live | ## 2.2 Operational Concept — Stepwise Logic > [!NOTE] > **The Six Functional Stages of an Attitude Control Subsystem (ACS):** 1. **Command Ingestion** — Ground station uploads desired attitude quaternion $\vec{q}_{cmd}$ via S-band uplink. 2. **Attitude Determination** — Onboard sensors (star tracker, sun sensor, magnetometer, gyroscope) estimate current attitude $\vec{q}_{est}$. 3. **Error Computation** — Quaternion difference $\delta\vec{q} = \vec{q}_{cmd} \otimes \vec{q}_{est}^{-1}$ yields the small-angle error vector $\delta\vec{\theta}$. 4. **Control Law Evaluation** — A PID / state-feedback law computes the required torque $\vec{T}_{ctl} = \mathbf{K}_p\,\delta\vec{\theta} + \mathbf{K}_d\,\vec{\omega}_{err} + \mathbf{K}_i\int\delta\vec{\theta}\,dt$. 5. **Actuator Command Distribution** — Torque is realized via reaction wheels, thrusters, or magnetorquers. 6. **Telemetry Downlink** — Attitude telemetry (quaternion, rates, mode, wheel speeds) is beaconed to ground for monitoring. ## 2.3 Classification of Attitude Control Techniques | Method | Type | Torque Source | Typical Accuracy | Use Case | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Spin Stabilization** | Passive | Angular momentum storage | 1°–5° | Early spinners (e.g., Pioneer) | | **Dual-Spin** | Passive / Hybrid | Rotor momentum | 0.1°–1° | GEO comsats (Intelsat IV) | | **Gravity Gradient (GG)** | Passive | $\nabla$ of Earth's gravity | 1°–5° | LEO small sats | | **Magnetic (Passive)** | Passive | Hysteresis rods vs $B_E$ | Several degrees | CubeSats | | **Three-Axis Active** | Active | Reaction wheels / CMG / Thrusters | 0.001°–0.1° | Modern comsats, EO | | **Magnetorquer (Active)** | Active | $B_E$ interaction | 1°–5° | CubeSats, ADCS desaturation | ## 2.4 Sources of Disturbance Torques | Disturbance | Magnitude Order (LEO) | Mitigation | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Gravity-gradient | $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-3}$ N·m | Use boom/damper, or exploit it (GG) | | Solar radiation pressure | $10^{-5}$ N·m | Trim vanes, careful area balancing | | Aerodynamic drag | $10^{-6}$ to $10^{-4}$ N·m | Increases with altitude loss | | Magnetic (residual dipole) | $10^{-6}$ N·m | Magnetorquer desaturation | ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **The following table lists every equation you must memorize for KTU ESE on Attitude Control.** All symbols use SI units unless explicitly stated. | # | Equation | Symbol Meaning | Typical Application | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | F1 | $I_x\ddot{\phi} - (I_y - I_z)\dot{\theta}\dot{\psi} = T_x$ | Euler's equation, roll axis | Spin & 3-axis dynamics | | F2 | $I_y\ddot{\theta} - (I_z - I_x)\dot{\phi}\dot{\psi} = T_y$ | Euler's equation, pitch axis | 3-axis dynamics | | F3 | $I_z\ddot{\psi} - (I_x - I_y)\dot{\phi}\dot{\theta} = T_z$ | Euler's equation, yaw axis | 3-axis dynamics | | F4 | $\vec{T}_{GG} = 3\omega_o^2\,(\hat{o}_3 \times \mathbf{I}\,\hat{o}_3)$ | Gravity-gradient torque vector | GG boom design | | F5 | $T_{GG,b} = 3\omega_o^2(I_z - I_y)\sin 2\theta$ | GG torque in pitch | Boom inertia sizing | | F6 | $\vec{T}_{mag} = \vec{m} \times \vec{B}_E$ | Magnetic dipole torque | Magnetorquer sizing | | F7 | $\vec{T}_{SRP} = \int (P/c)\,(\hat{n}\times\vec{r})\cos^2\alpha\,dA$ | Solar radiation torque | Trim-tab design | | F8 | $\dot{\vec{H}} = \vec{T}_{ext}$ (for wheel) | Wheel momentum exchange | Reaction wheel sizing | | F9 | $\mathbf{R}(\phi,\theta,\psi) = \mathbf{R}_z(\psi)\mathbf{R}_y(\theta)\mathbf{R}_x(\phi)$ | 3-2-1 Euler rotation matrix | Attitude kinematics | | F10 | $\omega_o = \sqrt{\mu/r^3}$ | Orbital angular rate | Orbital frame definition | | F11 | $q = [\cos(\alpha/2),\ \hat{e}\sin(\alpha/2)]^T$ | Quaternion definition | Modern ACS implementation | | F12 | $h_{wheel} = I_w\,\Omega_{wheel}$ | Reaction wheel angular momentum | Momentum budget | > **Boundary/Units reminder:** All angular quantities in **radians**, torques in **N·m**, inertias in **kg·m²**, $\omega_o$ in **rad/s**. A satellite must satisfy the **dual-spinner stability condition** $I_T > I_S$ (transverse moment $>$ spin moment) for stable rotation about its long axis. # Attitude Control — Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Euler's Rotational Equations of Motion > [!NOTE] > **Objective:** Starting from Newton's 2nd law for rotation, derive the coupled nonlinear equations F1, F2, F3 of the formula sheet. ### Step 1 — Start with Angular Momentum For a rigid body, the angular momentum vector about its centre of mass is $$\vec{H} = \int \vec{r} \times (\vec{\omega}\times\vec{r})\,dm = \mathbf{I}\,\vec{\omega}$$ where $\mathbf{I}$ is the inertia tensor. ### Step 2 — Apply Newton's Second Law for Rotation The sum of all external and control torques equals the time-derivative of angular momentum, evaluated **in the inertial frame**: $$\vec{T}_{ext} + \vec{T}_{ctl} = \frac{d\vec{H}}{dt}\bigg|_{\mathcal{I}}$$ ### Step 3 — Convert the Inertial Derivative to the Body Frame Using the transport theorem, since the body frame rotates at $\vec{\omega}$ relative to inertial: $$\frac{d\vec{H}}{dt}\bigg|_{\mathcal{I}} = \frac{d\vec{H}}{dt}\bigg|_{\mathcal{B}} + \vec{\omega}\times\vec{H}$$ Substituting $\vec{H}=\mathbf{I}\,\vec{\omega}$ and noting $\mathbf{I}$ is constant in body frame: $$\vec{T}_{ext} + \vec{T}_{ctl} = \mathbf{I}\,\dot{\vec{\omega}} + \vec{\omega}\times(\mathbf{I}\,\vec{\omega})$$ ### Step 4 — Express Component-Wise in the Body Frame Writing out components $(\hat{b}_1,\hat{b}_2,\hat{b}_3)$ and using the inertia matrix $$\mathbf{I} = \begin{bmatrix} I_x & -I_{xy} & -I_{xz} \\ -I_{xy} & I_y & -I_{yz} \\ -I_{xz} & -I_{yz} & I_z \end{bmatrix}$$ and for the **principal axes** assumption $I_{xy}=I_{yz}=I_{xz}=0$, we obtain the celebrated Euler equations: $$ \begin{aligned} T_x &= I_x\,\dot{\omega}_x - (I_y - I_z)\,\omega_y\,\omega_z \\ T_y &= I_y\,\dot{\omega}_y - (I_z - I_x)\,\omega_z\,\omega_x \\ T_z &= I_z\,\dot{\omega}_z - (I_x - I_y)\,\omega_x\,\omega_y \end{aligned} $$ > **Conversion Logic:** The cross-product term $\vec{\omega}\times(\mathbf{I}\vec{\omega})$ is what couples the three scalar equations — this is the **gyroscopic coupling** responsible for nutation in spinning spacecraft. --- ## 3.2 Derivation of Gravity-Gradient Torque (F4 & F5) ### Step 1 — Gravitational Potential of a Non-Spherical Body For a body treated as two point masses separated along the pitch axis by $2l$, the differential gravitational pull between near-side and far-side produces a torque. The exact potential to first order in $(l/r)^2$ is $$U = -\frac{\mu m}{r} - \frac{\mu}{2r^3}\,(3(\hat{r}\cdot\mathbf{I}\hat{r}) - \text{tr}(\mathbf{I}))$$ ### Step 2 — Gradient Gives the Torque $$\vec{T}_{GG} = -\hat{b}_1\,T_{x,GG} - \hat{b}_2\,T_{y,GG} - \hat{b}_3\,T_{z,GG} = \nabla U \times \hat{r}$$ Resulting in the compact vector form: $$\vec{T}_{GG} = 3\,\omega_o^2\,(\hat{o}_3 \times \mathbf{I}\,\hat{o}_3)$$ ### Step 3 — Specialize to Pitch (Boom-Down Configuration) Let pitch angle $\theta$ be the deviation of the long axis $\hat{b}_3$ from the local vertical $\hat{o}_3$. Then $\hat{b}_3 \cdot \hat{o}_3 = \cos\theta$, and the pitch component reduces to: $$T_{GG,\theta} = 3\,\omega_o^2\,(I_z - I_y)\,\sin\theta\,\cos\theta = \frac{3}{2}\,\omega_o^2\,(I_z - I_y)\,\sin 2\theta$$ > **Stability Insight:** Equilibrium $\theta=0$ (boom down) is stable **iff** $I_z > I_y > I_x$, i.e., the satellite is **long and slender** along the local vertical. --- ## 3.3 Derivation of Reaction Wheel Control Torque A reaction wheel is a motor-driven inertial disc with spin inertia $I_w$ spinning at angular speed $\Omega_w$ relative to the spacecraft. The wheel's angular momentum in inertial frame is $$\vec{H}_w = I_w\,(\Omega_w + \omega_s)\,\hat{b}_s$$ where $\omega_s$ is the spacecraft rate projected on the wheel spin axis. Differentiating: $$\frac{d\vec{H}_w}{dt}\bigg|_{\mathcal{I}} = I_w\,\dot{\Omega}_w\,\hat{b}_s + I_w\,(\Omega_w + \omega_s)\,\dot{\hat{b}}_s$$ For an axisymmetric spacecraft with $I_s = I_t$ (wheel on symmetry axis) and small nutation, the second term contributes only through $\omega \times$ coupling. **Conservation of total angular momentum** in absence of external torque requires the spacecraft body to react: $$\vec{T}_{ctl} = -\,\frac{d\vec{H}_w}{dt}\bigg|_{\mathcal{B}} = -I_w\,\dot{\Omega}_w\,\hat{b}_s$$ Thus, **accelerating the wheel decelerates the spacecraft by reaction**, giving the linearized control law: $$\dot{\Omega}_w = -\frac{T_{ctl}}{I_w} \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad T_{ctl} = -I_w\,\dot{\Omega}_w$$ --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Disturbance vs. Mitigation Effectiveness | Disturbance | Mathematical Form | Dominant at | Mitigation Strategy | Mitigation Effectiveness | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Gravity-gradient | $T_{GG} = (3\omega_o^2/2)\Delta I\sin 2\theta$ | LEO, all orbits | Boom design / 3-axis | 90% | | Solar radiation | $T_{SRP} \propto (P/c)(A\,l)\sin\alpha$ | All orbits | Centre-of-pressure $\approx$ CoM | 80% | | Aerodynamic | $T_{aero} \propto \frac{1}{2}\rho\,V^2\,C_d\,A\,l$ | LEO < 500 km | Altitude $\uparrow$ | Mission-dependent | | Magnetic residual | $T_{mag} = \vec{m}_{res}\times\vec{B}$ | All orbits | Magnetic cleanliness, hysteresis dampers | 70% | --- ## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style) > **Problem:** A 500 kg LEO satellite at $h = 600$ km has principal moments of inertia $I_x = 280$, $I_y = 320$, $I_z = 110$ kg·m². The pitch axis is misaligned by $\theta = 5°$ from local vertical. Find the gravity-gradient restoring torque. **Solution:** $$r = R_E + h = 6378 + 600 = 6978\ \text{km} = 6.978\times 10^6\ \text{m}$$ $$\omega_o = \sqrt{\mu/r^3} = \sqrt{\frac{3.986\times 10^{14}}{(6.978\times 10^6)^3}} \approx 1.135\times 10^{-3}\ \text{rad/s}$$ $$T_{GG,\theta} = \frac{3}{2}\omega_o^2\,(I_z - I_y)\,\sin 2\theta$$ $$T_{GG,\theta} = 1.5 \times (1.135\times 10^{-3})^2 \times (110-320) \times \sin(10°)$$ $$ \begin{aligned} T_{GG,\theta} &= 1.5 \times 1.288\times 10^{-6} \times (-210) \times 0.1736 \\ &= -7.05\times 10^{-5}\ \text{N·m} \end{aligned} $$ > **Interpretation:** Negative sign indicates a **restoring torque** (acts to reduce $\theta$), as expected for stable equilibrium. # Attitude Control — Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Complete Attitude Control Subsystem (ACS) The figure below maps the **closed-loop functional architecture** of a typical three-axis stabilized satellite ACS, including the telemetry back-channel. It treats the spacecraft as a *plant* and the ground station as the *supervisor*. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Ground Command Uplink
S band TTC] --> B[Command Decoder
and Authenticator] B --> C[Attitude Reference

Determination Filter
EKF] G[Gyroscope IMU] --> F H[Sun Sensor] --> F I[Magnetometer] --> F F --> J[Current Attitude
Quaternion Qest] J --> D D --> K[Control Law
PID or LQR] K --> L[Actuator

and Anomaly Detection] T --> A ``` ## 4.2 Functional Mode State Machine The satellite's ACS operates in several modes. Below is the state-transition diagram showing valid mode handovers. ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> SafeHold SafeHold --> Detumble : B field torque exceeds threshold Detumble --> SunAcquire : Angular rate below 0.5 deg per s SunAcquire --> EarthPoint : Sun sensor locks and Earth sensor available EarthPoint --> NormalOps : All 3 axes within deadband of target NormalOps --> EarthPoint : Star tracker dropout detected EarthPoint --> SafeHold : Wheel momentum saturation above 80 percent NormalOps --> SunPoint : Payload eclipse entry commanded SunPoint --> NormalOps : Ground command or eclipse exit NormalOps --> [*] : Mission end disposal burn ``` ## 4.3 Sensor-to-Actuator Coverage Map | Sensor | Roll | Pitch | Yaw | Update Rate | Drift | | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- | | Magnetometer | Yes | Yes | Yes | 10 Hz | None | | Coarse Sun Sensor | Yes | Yes | Limited | 1 Hz | None | | Fine Sun Sensor (1-axis) | Yes | Yes | No | 10 Hz | None | | Earth Horizon Sensor | No | Yes | Yes | 1 Hz | None | | Star Tracker | Yes | Yes | Yes | 1–10 Hz | None | | Gyro (rate) | Yes | Yes | Yes | 100 Hz | Yes (drift) | | Actuator | Authority | Continuous? | Typical Use | | :--- | :--- | :---: | :--- | | Reaction Wheel | $\approx 10^{-3}$ to $10^{-1}$ N·m | Yes | Precision 3-axis | | CMG | $\approx 1$ to $10^{3}$ N·m | Yes | Large agile sats | | Magnetorquer | $\approx 10^{-4}$ N·m | Yes | CubeSats, desaturation | | Cold-gas Thruster | $\approx 0.01$ to $1$ N·m | No | Wheel desaturation | | Solar vane / trim tab | Disturbance only | Passive | Augment GG or SRP | # Attitude Control — Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` **Define attitude control of a satellite. List any four types of attitude control techniques.** **Model Answer:** Attitude control is the process of orienting a spacecraft to a desired angular position and maintaining it against disturbance torques using internal or external actuation. Four types: (i) **Spin stabilization**, (ii) **Dual-spin stabilization**, (iii) **Gravity-gradient stabilization**, (iv) **Three-axis active stabilization** using reaction wheels, control moment gyroscopes (CMG), or thrusters. *(3 marks: 1 for definition + 0.5 each for four types.)* --- ### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]` **What is gravity-gradient stabilization? State the condition for its stability.** **Model Answer:** Gravity-gradient stabilization is a **passive** attitude control method that exploits the differential gravitational pull on a long boom-mounted satellite to align its long axis with the local vertical. The **stability condition** is that the pitch-axis moment of inertia must be the largest and the radial-axis moment the smallest: $$I_z > I_y > I_x \quad\text{(long, slender, boom-down configuration)}$$ For this configuration, any small pitch deviation produces a **restoring torque** proportional to $\sin 2\theta$. *(3 marks: 1 for explanation + 2 for condition.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) ### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 — Adapted]` **(a)** Derive Euler's rotational equations of motion for a rigid spacecraft in the principal-axis body frame. **\[7 Marks\]** **(b)** Explain the working principle of a **reaction wheel** attitude control system with a neat block diagram. Derive the relation between commanded torque and wheel angular acceleration. **\[7 Marks\]** #### **Model Solution — (a)** 1. Begin with rigid-body angular momentum: $\vec{H} = \mathbf{I}\vec{\omega}$, where $\mathbf{I}$ is the inertia tensor in body frame. 2. Newton's 2nd law for rotation (inertial frame): $\vec{T}_{ext}+\vec{T}_{ctl}=\dfrac{d\vec{H}}{dt}\Big|_{\mathcal{I}}$. 3. Apply transport theorem to convert inertial derivative to body frame: $$\frac{d\vec{H}}{dt}\Big|_{\mathcal{I}} = \frac{d\vec{H}}{dt}\Big|_{\mathcal{B}} + \vec{\omega}\times\vec{H}$$ 4. With principal axes ($I_{xy}=I_{yz}=I_{xz}=0$) and constant $\mathbf{I}$ in body frame, expand component-wise: $$ \begin{aligned} T_x + T_{ctl,x} &= I_x\,\dot{\omega}_x - (I_y - I_z)\,\omega_y\omega_z \\ T_y + T_{ctl,y} &= I_y\,\dot{\omega}_y - (I_z - I_x)\,\omega_z\omega_x \\ T_z + T_{ctl,z} &= I_z\,\dot{\omega}_z - (I_x - I_y)\,\omega_x\omega_y \end{aligned} $$ The cross-product terms $(I_y-I_z)\omega_y\omega_z$ etc. represent **gyroscopic coupling**. **(7 marks distributed: 2 for starting relations + 2 for transport theorem + 3 for final expansion.)** #### **Model Solution — (b)** **Working Principle:** A reaction wheel is a motor-driven inertial disc spinning at high RPM along a body-mounted axis. By Newton's third law, any torque the motor applies to speed up or slow down the wheel is reacted on the spacecraft body, producing **attitude control torque** without expending propellant. **Block Diagram (textual):** ``` Error θ_e → [Controller K(s)] → T_cmd → [Motor Drive] → I_w dΩ/dt → Reaction Wheel → T_reaction → Spacecraft Body ω_gyro (feedback) ←——————————————————————— Body rate ω ←——————————————————————————————————————— ``` **Derivation:** From conservation of total angular momentum $\vec{H}_{total}=\vec{H}_{body}+\vec{H}_{wheel}$ in absence of external torque: $$\vec{T}_{ext} = \frac{d\vec{H}_{body}}{dt} + \frac{d\vec{H}_{wheel}}{dt} = 0$$ For a wheel on body axis $\hat{b}_s$: $$\vec{H}_{wheel} = I_w\,(\Omega_w + \omega_s)\,\hat{b}_s$$ Differentiating in the body frame (treating $\hat{b}_s$ as approximately fixed) and setting external torque zero: $$\vec{T}_{ctl} = -I_w\,\dot{\Omega}_w\,\hat{b}_s$$ Thus, **the commanded control torque is linearly proportional to the wheel angular acceleration**: $$T_{ctl} = -I_w\,\dot{\Omega}_w \quad\Rightarrow\quad \dot{\Omega}_w = -\frac{T_{ctl}}{I_w}$$ **(7 marks: 2 for principle + 2 for block diagram + 3 for derivation.)** --- ### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 — Adapted]` **(a)** With the help of a labelled sketch, explain **gravity-gradient stabilization**. Derive the expression for the gravity-gradient torque in pitch. State the stability condition. **\[7 Marks\]** **(b)** Define **attitude** and **attitude quaternion**. A satellite uses two magnetorquers aligned along body $\hat{b}_1$ and $\hat{b}_2$ axes. If Earth's magnetic field in body frame is $\vec{B}_E = [B_1,\ B_2,\ B_3]^T$ and the magnetorquers produce dipoles $\vec{m} = [m_1,\ 0,\ 0]^T + [0,\ m_2,\ 0]^T$, find the magnetic control torque vector. **\[7 Marks\]** #### **Model Solution — (a)** **Sketch (described):** ``` ● Mass m_top (mass at end of boom) | | Long slender boom (length l) | ____|____ / Satellite \ \ Body bus / ‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾ ● Mass m_bot ``` The Earth is "below" the satellite along $-\hat{o}_3$ (local vertical). Both masses feel gravitational pull toward Earth, but the closer mass $m_{bot}$ is pulled harder, generating a torque that aligns the long axis with the vertical. **Derivation:** Consider two point masses $m_{bot}$ and $m_{top}$ separated by $2l$ along pitch axis $\hat{b}_3$, tilted at angle $\theta$ from local vertical $\hat{o}_3$. Distance of each mass from Earth's centre: $$r_{bot} = r - l\cos\theta,\quad r_{top} = r + l\cos\theta$$ Differential gravitational force in pitch direction produces a torque about the centre of mass: $$T_{GG,\theta} = \frac{GM_E m\,(2l)\,\sin\theta}{(r-l\cos\theta)(r+l\cos\theta)} \approx \frac{2GM_E\,m\,l\,\sin\theta}{r^3} = 3\omega_o^2\,m\,l^2\,\sin\theta\cos\theta$$ Generalising to a continuous rigid body with $I_y < I_z$: $$\boxed{T_{GG,\theta} = \frac{3}{2}\omega_o^2\,(I_z - I_y)\,\sin 2\theta}$$ **Stability Condition:** For $\theta=0$ to be a stable equilibrium, $\partial T_{GG,\theta}/\partial\theta < 0$, which requires: $$\boxed{I_z > I_y > I_x}$$ That is, the pitch (longitudinal) axis must have the **largest moment of inertia** and the radial (lateral) axis the **smallest**. **(7 marks: 2 for sketch + 3 for derivation + 2 for stability condition.)** #### **Model Solution — (b)** **Attitude** is the orientation of a rigid body in three-dimensional space, described relative to a chosen reference frame. **Attitude quaternion** is a four-element unit-norm complex number $\vec{q} = [q_0,\ q_1,\ q_2,\ q_3]^T$ that encodes a rotation by angle $\alpha$ about a unit axis $\hat{e}$: $$\vec{q} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos(\alpha/2) \\ e_x\,\sin(\alpha/2) \\ e_y\,\sin(\alpha/2) \\ e_z\,\sin(\alpha/2) \end{bmatrix}, \quad \|\vec{q}\|=1$$ Quaternions avoid the **gimbal-lock singularity** of Euler angles. **Magnetic Torque Calculation:** The torque produced by a magnetic dipole $\vec{m}$ in Earth's magnetic field $\vec{B}_E$ is given by the cross product: $$\vec{T}_{mag} = \vec{m} \times \vec{B}_E$$ With $\vec{m} = (m_1,\,m_2,\,0)^T$ and $\vec{B}_E = (B_1,\,B_2,\,B_3)^T$: $$ \begin{aligned} \vec{T}_{mag} &= \begin{vmatrix} \hat{b}_1 & \hat{b}_2 & \hat{b}_3 \\ m_1 & m_2 & 0 \\ B_1 & B_2 & B_3 \end{vmatrix} \\ &= \hat{b}_1\,(m_2 B_3 - 0\cdot B_2) - \hat{b}_2\,(m_1 B_3 - 0\cdot B_1) + \hat{b}_3\,(m_1 B_2 - m_2 B_1) \end{aligned} $$ $$\boxed{\vec{T}_{mag} = (m_2 B_3)\,\hat{b}_1 - (m_1 B_3)\,\hat{b}_2 + (m_1 B_2 - m_2 B_1)\,\hat{b}_3}$$ > **Key Limitation:** Notice that the magnetic torque vector is always **perpendicular to $\vec{B}_E$** — magnetorquers can only produce torque in the plane orthogonal to Earth's field, so they cannot fully control 3 axes simultaneously. **(7 marks: 2 for attitude definition + 2 for quaternion definition + 3 for cross-product torque derivation.)** --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks on Attitude Control Questions:** > > 1. **Forgetting the gyroscopic coupling terms** $(I_y - I_z)\omega_y\omega_z$ in Euler's equations — they are not zero and must be shown. > 2. **Confusing reference frames** — gravity-gradient torque is naturally computed in the **orbital frame**, but control torque is in the **body frame**. State frame explicitly. > 3. **Wrong sign in reaction wheel derivation** — the control torque on the spacecraft is **opposite** to the wheel acceleration torque. > 4. **Quaternions vs. Euler angles** — KTU examiners award 1 mark just for stating the gimbal-lock advantage. > 5. **Stability condition of gravity-gradient** — students often only write $I_z > I_y$ and miss $I_y > I_x$; both are required. > 6. **Units** — mixing rad/s with deg/s; $\omega_o^2$ value at 600 km gives $1.29\times 10^{-6}$ rad²/s² — compute carefully. > 7. **Magnetorquer** — forgetting that control authority **vanishes along $\vec{B}_E$ direction**; this is why magnetorquers alone cannot do full 3-axis control. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Revision Checklist for Attitude Control (Module 2)** - **Attitude** = orientation of body frame $\mathcal{B}$ w.r.t. reference frame; parameterised by 3 angles or a unit quaternion. - **Euler's equations** (principal-axis form): $$T_i = I_i\dot{\omega}_i - (I_j - I_k)\omega_j\omega_k, \quad i \neq j \neq k$$ - **Six functional ACS stages**: command → determination → error → control law → actuator → telemetry. - **Three reference frames** to remember: Inertial (ECI), Orbital (LVLH), Body-fixed. - **Euler 3-2-1 rotation order**: yaw-pitch-roll gives $\mathbf{R} = \mathbf{R}_z(\psi)\,\mathbf{R}_y(\theta)\,\mathbf{R}_x(\phi)$. - **Quaternion advantage**: no gimbal lock; $\vec{q} = [\cos(\alpha/2),\, \hat{e}\sin(\alpha/2)]^T$. - **Gravity-gradient torque**: $\vec{T}_{GG} = 3\omega_o^2(\hat{o}_3\times\mathbf{I}\hat{o}_3)$; stable iff **long slender boom** with $I_z > I_y > I_x$. - **Reaction wheel torque**: $T_{ctl} = -I_w\dot{\Omega}_w$; saturated wheels must be **desaturated** via thrusters or magnetorquers. - **CMG** vs reaction wheel: CMG gives torque by **gimbaling** a constant-speed wheel; provides much higher torque but is non-linear and singular. - **Magnetic torque**: $\vec{T}_{mag} = \vec{m}\times\vec{B}_E$; **always perpendicular to $\vec{B}_E$** (cannot control the field-aligned axis). - **Solar radiation torque**: $\propto (P/c)\,A\,l\,\sin\alpha$; minimized by **centre-of-pressure at centre-of-mass**. - **Aerodynamic torque**: dominant below 500 km; grows as the orbit decays. - **Disturbance magnitudes (LEO)**: gravity-gradient and SRP dominate; all scale with boom length / characteristic area. - **Spin stabilization**: simple, robust, but antenna must despun; payload gets less sky coverage. - **Three-axis active**: highest accuracy (arc-sec class); required for Earth-pointing high-gain antennas. - **Telemetry**: attitude quaternion + wheel speeds + mode flags downlinked at 1–10 Hz typically. - **Always mention units** in derivations: rad/s, N·m, kg·m²; be consistent throughout. > [!TIP] > **Quick Mnemonic for Euler's Equations — "ZYX rule":** For each axis, the inertia in front of $\dot{\omega}$ is the principal moment of that axis; the gyroscopic term uses the **other two** inertias, multiplied in **cyclic** order (Z→Y→X for x-axis equation, X→Z→Y for y-axis, Y→X→Z for z-axis).

topics.title: Satellite Station Keeping
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT634 - COMMUNICATION SYSTEM AND TELEMETRY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Satellite Station Keeping

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

**Satellite Station Keeping** is the precise set of orbital maneuvers and propulsion operations performed periodically to maintain a communication satellite in its designated orbital slot, particularly within the **Geostationary Earth Orbit (GEO)** belt. Because no orbit is naturally perfect and several external perturbation forces continuously tug on a spacecraft, station keeping counteracts these disturbances so that the satellite remains within the assigned longitudinal and latitudinal tolerance window (typically **±0.05° to ±0.1°** in longitude and **±0.05°** in inclination for modern Ku/Ka-band operators).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** Station keeping is the *active in-orbit maintenance activity* that ensures a geostationary satellite stays inside its allocated longitude slot and on the equatorial plane, compensating for long-period perturbations caused by the Moon, Sun, Earth's oblateness (J2), and solar radiation pressure.

### The Two Sub-Operations (KTU High-Yield Taxonomy)

| Sub-System | Target Drift Corrected | Plane | Typical Period |
|---|---|---|---|
| **North–South Station Keeping (NSSK)** | Inclination drift (out of equatorial plane) | Polar (N–S) | Every 2 weeks (≈ 14 days) |
| **East–West Station Keeping (EWSK)** | Longitude drift / libration | Equatorial (E–W) | Every 2 to 4 weeks |

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Drifting Ship on a Calm Sea"

Imagine your satellite as a luxury cruise ship that must remain **anchored at exactly one square meter of a fixed spot** in the ocean — even though ocean currents, winds, and tides constantly push it sideways.

- The **wind and currents** are like the Moon, Sun, and solar photons pushing the satellite.
- The **anchor chain** is the propulsion system (thrusters).
- The **captain's compass reading** is the telemetry / ranging signal from Earth.
- The act of *throwing fuel backward* to stay in place = **station-keeping burn**.

Without active correction, a geostationary satellite would oscillate north–south by about **±7°** over 26.6 years and drift east–west along the gravity well, ruining coverage, increasing path loss jitter, and creating interference with neighbouring satellites spaced only **2° apart** in the crowded GEO arc.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Tolerance Figures (Bold for KTU Board Recall):** Modern INTELSAT / ISRO norms demand ±0.05° E–W and ±0.05° N–S. Ku-band HTS operators even demand ±0.02°. The lifetime ΔV budget typically ranges from **45 m/s to 50 m/s per year** for chemical propulsion, or as low as **5–10 m/s/year** for electric (ion) propulsion.

### Why GEO Specifically?

Because GEO satellites appear **stationary** to a ground antenna (orbital period = Earth's rotation period = **23 h 56 min 4 s** = one *sidereal day*), they only deliver uninterrupted communications if they truly remain stationary. Any drift translates into:
