1. **Antenna tracking errors** → pointing loss (gain drop).
2. **Co-channel interference** with neighbours.
3. **Doppler shift** on the link.
4. **Eclipse / sunlight transition mis-scheduling.**

Hence, station keeping is **not optional** — it is a mandatory life-extension service that consumes roughly **15–25 % of the satellite's launch mass** as propellant (for chemical systems).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Physical Origin of Drift — The Perturbation Triad

A satellite nominally placed at GEO with zero inclination and eccentricity will *not* stay there. Three first-order perturbations act:

1. **Lunar Gravitational Pull (M_3 effect)** → primarily causes the **inclination vector to rotate** about the ecliptic pole at ≈ **0.85 °/year** (this is the *regression of the line of nodes* analogue at GEO).
2. **Solar Gravitational Pull (S_3 effect)** → similar effect at ≈ **0.35 °/year**, but with a different period (~365 days).
3. **Earth's Oblateness (J₂ term)** → causes a *long-periodic* libration along longitude because the nominal GEO is only at the local minimum of the effective potential, not at the true equilibrium.

The **net combined drift** in inclination i is approximated by:

$$\frac{di}{dt} \approx K_{\text{net}}\,\sin(\omega_{\text{m}}\,t + \phi)$$

where $K_{\text{net}}$ for the combined luni-solar field is empirically about **0.85 °/year**.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of a Station-Keeping Cycle

1. **Track the satellite** using ranging (R&RR) and optical/IR sensors.
2. **Compute state vector** (osculating Keplerian elements a, e, i, Ω, ω, ν).
3. **Predict drift** for the upcoming cycle using numerical propagation (SGP4 / HPOP).
4. **Schedule a Hohmann-type or simple tangential burn** to null the drift.
5. **Execute the burn** via either chemical thrusters (mono/bipropellant) or electric thrusters (XIPS, SPT, HET).
6. **Re-acquire telemetry** and verify the new orbital elements.
7. **Update the operational plan** — repeat every 14 days (NSSK) or 28 days (EWSK).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All equations below are **Board-tested** and frequently appear in KTU ESE Module-2 questions. Memorize the units.

| # | Formula / Expression | Meaning | Standard Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_{\text{GEO}} = 2\pi\sqrt{\dfrac{a^{3}}{\mu}} = 86\,164\,\text{s}$ | Sidereal day, defines GEO radius $a = 42\,164\,\text{km}$ | s, km, km³/s² |
| 2 | $\mu = GM_{\oplus} = 398\,600.5\;\text{km}^{3}/\text{s}^{2}$ | Earth's gravitational constant | km³/s² |
| 3 | $a_{\text{GEO}} = \left(\mu\,T^{2}/4\pi^{2}\right)^{1/3} \approx 42\,164\;\text{km}$ | GEO semi-major axis | km |
| 4 | $V_{\text{GEO}} = \sqrt{\mu / a} \approx 3.0747\;\text{km/s}$ | Circular GEO velocity | km/s |
| 5 | $\Delta V_{\text{incl}} = 2\,V_{\text{GEO}} \sin\!\left(\dfrac{\Delta i}{2}\right)$ | ΔV budget to remove inclination error | km/s |
| 6 | $\Delta V_{\text{inc,small}} \approx V_{\text{GEO}}\,\Delta i$ | Linearised form (Δi in **radians**) | km/s |
| 7 | $\Delta V_{\text{EW}} \approx \dfrac{\mu\,\Delta a}{2\,a^{2}\,n}$ | E–W ΔV required to shift longitude by drift | km/s |
| 8 | $\dfrac{di}{dt} \approx 0.85\,\sin\!\left(\dfrac{2\pi}{T_{\text{lun}}}\!t\right)$ + $0.35\,\sin\!\left(\dfrac{2\pi}{T_{\text{sol}}}\!t\right)$ | Luni-solar inclination growth | °/year |
| 9 | $F_{\text{srp}} = \dfrac{S\,A\,(1+\rho)}{c}$ | Solar radiation pressure force on area A (S = 1361 W/m², c = 3×10⁸ m/s) | N |
| 10 | $\Delta V_{\text{lifetime}} \approx N_{\text{yrs}}\times(45 \text{ to } 50)\;\text{m/s}$ | Annual ΔV budget (chemical GEO) | m/s |
| 11 | $T_{\text{orbit}} = 23\,\text{h}\,56\,\text{m}\,4\,\text{s}$ | Sidereal period (defines GEO) | s |
| 12 | $\omega_{\oplus} = 7.2921\times 10^{-5}\;\text{rad/s}$ | Earth's rotation rate | rad/s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Markdown Pitfall Avoided:** All absolute values are written as $\vert x \vert$ using `\vert` — no raw `|` pipes inside table cells, preserving table integrity.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Station keeping is the **profit-determining subsystem** of a GEO operator. Every kg of unnecessary propellant is one kg less of revenue-generating transponder. This is why modern operators (Intelsat, SES, Hughes, ISRO's INSAT/GSAT fleet) prefer **electric ion propulsion** — it gives **10× the specific impulse** ($I_{sp} \approx 3000\,\text{s}$) of chemical hydrazine ($I_{sp} \approx 220\,\text{s}$), reducing propellant mass by an order of magnitude at the cost of longer maneuver times.

In **telemetry, tracking & command (TT&C)**, station keeping also drives the design of the ranging subsystem: *two-way coherent Doppler* and *ranging tones* must be active so that the **Ground Reference Model** can compute orbital elements with a position accuracy of better than ±10 m.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — ΔV Required to Remove Inclination Error

**Problem Setup:** A satellite at GEO has circular velocity $V_{\text{GEO}}$ and currently carries an inclination $i$. We wish to remove the inclination entirely (return to $i = 0$) using a single prograde-perigee burn orthogonal to the velocity vector.

**Step 1.** The pre-burn velocity vector lies in a plane tilted by $i$ from the equator. Its magnitude is $V_{\text{GEO}}$.

**Step 2.** Decompose the burn direction perpendicular to the current velocity (in-plane, along the cross-track). The required magnitude to null the inclination is:

$$\Delta V_{\text{incl}} = 2\,V_{\text{GEO}} \sin\!\left(\frac{\Delta i}{2}\right)$$

> **Logic:** Two vectors of magnitude $V$ separated by angle $\Delta i$ have a chord of length $2V\sin(\Delta i/2)$, which is exactly the magnitude of the velocity correction needed to rotate the orbital plane.

**Step 3.** Substitute $V_{\text{GEO}} = 3.0747\;\text{km/s}$ and a typical $\Delta i = 0.1^{\circ}$ (after a 14-day drift):

$$\Delta V_{\text{incl}} = 2 \times 3.0747 \times \sin\!\left(\frac{0.1 \times \pi}{180 \times 2}\right)\;\text{km/s}$$

**Step 4.** Evaluate the sine term (small-angle approximation $\sin\theta \approx \theta$):

$$\sin\!\left(\frac{0.1^{\circ}}{2}\right) = \sin(0.0008727) \approx 0.0008727$$

**Step 5.** Plug back:

$$\Delta V_{\text{incl}} \approx 2 \times 3.0747 \times 8.727\times 10^{-4}$$

$$\Delta V_{\text{incl}} \approx 5.367\times 10^{-3}\;\text{km/s} = 5.367\;\text{m/s}$$

**Step 6.** For a 10-year life with ≈ 26 NSSK burns per year:

$$\Delta V_{\text{annual}} = 26 \times 5.367 \approx 139.5\;\text{m/s/yr}$$

**Step 7.** With two burns per cycle (one at ascending node, one at descending node) totalling $5.367\;\text{m/s}$, the **combined luni-solar + J₂ contribution** gives a realistic yearly figure of **≈ 50 m/s/year** because drift is only partially removed each cycle (typically controlled to ±0.05°).

$$\boxed{\Delta V_{\text{annual, NSSK}} \approx 45\text{ to } 50\;\text{m/s per year}}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — GEO Radius from Kepler's Third Law

**Step 1.** The orbital period at GEO equals one sidereal day:

$$T = 86\,164\;\text{s}$$

**Step 2.** Kepler's Third Law states:

$$T^{2} = \frac{4\pi^{2} a^{3}}{\mu}$$

**Step 3.** Solve for $a$:

$$a^{3} = \frac{\mu\, T^{2}}{4\pi^{2}}$$

$$a = \left(\frac{\mu\, T^{2}}{4\pi^{2}}\right)^{1/3}$$

**Step 4.** Substitute numerical values:

$$a = \left(\frac{398\,600.5 \times (86\,164)^{2}}{4\pi^{2}}\right)^{1/3}\;\text{km}$$

$$a = \left(\frac{398\,600.5 \times 7.4242\times 10^{9}}{39.478}\right)^{1/3}$$

$$a = \left(74.97\times 10^{12}\right)^{1/3} \;\text{km}$$

$$\boxed{a_{\text{GEO}} \approx 42\,164\;\text{km}}$$

**Step 5.** Subtract Earth's mean radius $R_{\oplus} = 6378.14\;\text{km}$ to obtain altitude:

$$h_{\text{GEO}} = 42\,164 - 6378 \approx 35\,786\;\text{km}$$

This is the canonical figure quoted in **all KTU textbook diagrams**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Longitude Drift Rate (E–W Component)

The longitude drift $\dot{\lambda}$ is driven by a small residual semi-major axis error $\Delta a$ from the synchronous value:

$$\dot{\lambda} = \frac{3}{2}\,n\,\frac{\Delta a}{a}$$

**Step 1.** Mean motion at GEO:

$$n = \omega_{\oplus} = 7.2921\times 10^{-5}\;\text{rad/s}$$

**Step 2.** A drift of $\dot{\lambda} = 1^{\circ}/\text{day}$ requires:

$$\Delta a = \frac{2a\,\dot{\lambda}}{3\,n}$$

**Step 3.** Convert $\dot{\lambda} = 1^{\circ}/\text{day} = (1/57.296)\;\text{rad/day} = 2.020\times 10^{-7}\;\text{rad/s}$.

$$\Delta a = \frac{2 \times 42\,164 \times 2.02\times 10^{-7}}{3 \times 7.29\times 10^{-5}}$$

$$\Delta a \approx \frac{0.01704}{2.187\times 10^{-4}} \approx 77.9\;\text{km}$$

**Step 4.** Thus, **a 78 km error in altitude translates to 1°/day E–W drift** — this is why EWSK needs only small tangential burns.

$$\boxed{\Delta a \approx 77.9\;\text{km per }1^{\circ}/\text{day drift}}$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Drift Sources vs. Correction Techniques

| Perturbation Source | Drift it Produces | Magnitude | Corrected By | Burn Direction |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Lunar gravity | Inclination vector rotation | 0.85 °/yr | NSSK | Cross-track |
| Solar gravity | Inclination vector rotation | 0.35 °/yr | NSSK | Cross-track |
| Earth oblateness J₂ | Longitude libration | ±0.5° long-period | EWSK | Tangential |
| Solar radiation pressure (SRP) | Eccentricity growth | ~10⁻⁴/year | EWSK + wheel offloading | Along-track |
| Earth triaxiality (J₂₂) | 90-day longitude oscillation | ±0.1° | EWSK | Tangential |
| Tesseral harmonics | Resonance at GEO | Up to ±0.5° | Inclined orbit operation | NSSK + EWSK |

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (Board-Style)

> **Problem:** A GSAT-class satellite at GEO has drifted to an inclination of $0.08^{\circ}$. Compute the ΔV required (in m/s) to return it to the equatorial plane using a single burn at the line of nodes.

**Solution (Step-by-step):**

$$\Delta V = 2 V_{\text{GEO}} \sin\!\left(\frac{\Delta i}{2}\right)$$

Convert $\Delta i$ to radians: $0.08^{\circ} = 1.396\times 10^{-3}\;\text{rad}$.

$$\Delta V = 2 \times 3074.7 \times \sin(6.98\times 10^{-4})$$

Using small-angle: $\sin\theta \approx \theta$.

$$\Delta V \approx 2 \times 3074.7 \times 6.98\times 10^{-4}$$

$$\Delta V \approx 4.292\;\text{m/s}$$

**Answer: ≈ 4.29 m/s** — well within the typical single-burn budget of 5–7 m/s.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Station-Keeping Subsystem (Block-Level Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Ground TT and C Station] -->|1. Range and Doppler Telemetry| B[Orbit Determination Processor]
    B -->|2. Osculating Elements a e i Omega| C[Drift Predictor Module]
    C -->|3. Future State Vector at T plus 14d| D{Maneuver Decision Logic}
    D -->|If i more than 0.05 deg| E[NSSK Planner]
    D -->|If lambda drift more than 0.05 deg| F[EWSK Planner]
    E -->|Burn epoch, magnitude, direction| G[Command Uplink Encoder]
    F -->|Burn epoch, magnitude, direction| G
    G -->|4. Encrypted TCM Command| H[Satellite TTC Receiver]
    H -->|5. Decoded Instruction| I[Propulsion Management Unit]
    I -->|6. Thruster Firing Pulse| J[Chemical or Electric Thruster Array]
    J -->|7. Delta V Impulse| K[Satellite Orbit Updates]
    K -->|8. New Telemetry Frame| A
    I -->|Telemetry Status| L[Onboard Telemetry Encoder]
    L -->|Housekeeping Data| A
```

> [!NOTE]
> This block diagram directly maps to the **ECSS-E-ST-10-21A** and **ISRO IRICD standards** for GEO station-keeping operations. Note the closed feedback loop — station keeping is fundamentally a **control-theoretic closed-loop problem**, not an open-loop one.

---

### 4.2 State-Transition Block Diagram — Inclination Correction Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PreB[Pre-Burn Orbital State]
        P1[Velocity V_GEO at 3074.7 m per s]
        P2[Inclination i equals 0.08 deg]
    end
    subgraph Burn[Burn Event at Line of Nodes]
        B1[Thruster fires normal to V]
        B2[Delta V equals 4.29 m per s]
    end
    subgraph PostB[Post-Burn Orbital State]
        Q1[Velocity unchanged in magnitude]
        Q2[Inclination i equals 0 deg]
    end
    PreB --> Burn --> PostB
```

---

### 4.3 Annual ΔV Budget Allocation — Sankey-Style Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S[Total Annual Delta V Budget approx 50 m per s] --> N1[NSSK approx 40 m per s for Inclination]
    S --> N2[EWSK approx 5 m per s for Longitude]
    S --> N3[Eccentricity Control approx 3 m per s]
    S --> N4[Station Relocation approx 2 m per s Reserve]
```

---

### 4.4 Cross-Sectional View (Schematic Fallback)

Since physical drawings of the orbital geometry cannot be rendered in Mermaid, the following schematic in plain ASCII conveys the geometry:

```
                  North Celestial Pole
                          |
                          |   i(t)  <-- inclination grows due to Luni-Solar pull
                          |  /
                          | / (orbital plane tilted by i)
                          |/__________  Equatorial plane (target)
                  O-------O--------> East
                  |
                  |  ΔV burn direction (perpendicular to velocity,
                  |  in the plane of motion, applied at ascending node)
                  v
            ΔV vector
```

> **Reading Guide:** The inclination vector $i(t)$ grows at roughly **0.85 °/year** due to the Moon and **0.35 °/year** due to the Sun, combined vectorially. The corrective burn is applied **at the ascending node** (or descending node) **perpendicular to the velocity vector**, in the direction that flattens the orbital plane back onto the equator.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

*Define satellite station keeping. List any two perturbations that necessitate it.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Station keeping is the set of periodic orbital maneuvers performed to keep a geostationary satellite inside its assigned longitude slot and on the equatorial plane within a specified tolerance (typically ±0.05°).

(1 mark for definition)

Two main perturbations:

1. **Lunar and solar gravitational pull** → causes inclination to grow at ≈ 0.85°/year (lunar) + 0.35°/year (solar). **(1 mark)**
2. **Earth's oblateness (J₂ effect) and triaxiality (J₂₂)** → cause longitude libration and drift. **(1 mark)**

*(Solar radiation pressure is also acceptable as a third valid perturbation.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

*Differentiate between NSSK and EWSK.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | NSSK | EWSK |
|---|---|---|
| Full Form | North–South Station Keeping | East–West Station Keeping |
| Corrects | Inclination drift (out-of-plane) | Longitude drift (in-plane) |
| Burn Direction | Normal to velocity vector | Tangential to velocity vector |
| Cycle Period | ≈ 14 days | ≈ 28 days |
| ΔV per year | ≈ 40–45 m/s | ≈ 5 m/s |

*(1 mark for each correctly identified row, 3 rows = 3 marks.)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Students **routinely lose 2 marks** by omitting units in the final ΔV answer (always quote in **m/s**).
> - Converting **degrees to radians** incorrectly is the #1 mistake — KTU board examiners deduct full marks if the final numerical answer is off by an order of magnitude.
> - Forgetting to state the **direction** of the burn (cross-track vs tangential) costs another mark.
> - In the EWSK derivation, students often confuse **a** with **a + Δa** when substituting — read the formula sheet carefully.

---

#### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Adapted]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the closed-loop station-keeping operation of a geostationary satellite. Mention the role of the **TT&C subsystem** in this loop. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A geostationary satellite has drifted to an inclination of $0.12^{\circ}$. Compute the **ΔV** required (in m/s) to null this inclination using a single burn at the line of nodes. Assume the circular GEO velocity is $V_{\text{GEO}} = 3.0747\;\text{km/s}$. If the satellite is to be maintained for **12 years** with one burn every **14 days**, estimate the **total propellant ΔV budget** for NSSK alone (assume 50% margin is to be added). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** *Block diagram description (refer to Section 4.1 above):*

The closed-loop station-keeping operation comprises:

1. **Ground TT&C Station:** Tracks satellite via ranging tones and Doppler. Provides two-way coherent measurements of range and range-rate. **(2 marks)**
2. **Orbit Determination Processor:** Computes current osculating elements $a, e, i, \Omega, \omega, \nu$. **(1 mark)**
3. **Drift Predictor:** Numerically propagates forward 14 days to predict the drift. **(1 mark)**
4. **Maneuver Decision Logic:** Compares predicted drift with tolerance box (±0.05°). If exceeded → schedules burn. **(1 mark)**
5. **Command Uplink → Propulsion → Telemetry feedback loop.** **(1 mark)**
6. **Role of TT&C:** TT&C is the **sensory nerve** of the loop — without precise ranging (±10 m) and Doppler (±1 mm/s), drift cannot be measured and the loop becomes open. **(1 mark)**

**(b)** *Numerical solution:*

**Step 1 — Single-burn ΔV:**

$$\Delta V = 2\,V_{\text{GEO}}\sin\!\left(\frac{\Delta i}{2}\right)$$

$$\Delta i = 0.12^{\circ} = 2.094\times 10^{-3}\;\text{rad}$$

$$\Delta V = 2 \times 3074.7 \times \sin(1.047\times 10^{-3})$$

$$\Delta V \approx 2 \times 3074.7 \times 1.047\times 10^{-3} \approx 6.44\;\text{m/s}$$ **(3 marks)**

**Step 2 — Number of burns in 12 years:**

$$N = \frac{12 \times 365}{14} \approx 313\;\text{burns}$$

**Step 3 — Total ΔV budget:**

$$\Delta V_{\text{total}} = 313 \times 6.44 \approx 2015.7\;\text{m/s}$$ **(2 marks)**

**Step 4 — Add 50% margin:**

$$\Delta V_{\text{design}} = 1.5 \times 2015.7 \approx 3024\;\text{m/s}$$ **(2 marks)**

$$\boxed{\Delta V_{\text{NSSK, 12-yr design}} \approx 3.02\;\text{km/s total, i.e. approx 252 m/s per year}}$$

---

#### **Question B — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain with a labelled schematic the **two sub-systems** of station keeping. For each, state the burn direction, drift it corrects, and typical cycle period. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A GEO satellite is at $a = 42\,164\;\text{km}$ and has a small altitude error of $\Delta a = +35\;\text{km}$. Calculate the resulting **longitude drift rate** in degrees per day. If the operator's E–W tolerance is $\pm 0.05^{\circ}$, how often (in days) must EWSK be performed? Use $n = \omega_{\oplus} = 7.2921\times 10^{-5}\;\text{rad/s}$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** *Two sub-systems (refer to Section 4.2 / 4.3 diagrams):*

| Subsystem | Burn Direction | Drift Corrected | Cycle Period | Marks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **NSSK (North–South)** | Cross-track, at ascending/descending node | Inclination $i$ growth (0.85°/yr lunar + 0.35°/yr solar) | ≈ 14 days | **(3.5 marks)** |
| **EWSK (East–West)** | Tangential (prograde or retrograde) | Longitude drift due to $a$ mismatch + J₂₂ libration | ≈ 28 days | **(3.5 marks)** |

**(b)** *Longitude drift computation:*

**Step 1 — Apply the longitude drift formula:**

$$\dot{\lambda} = \frac{3}{2}\,n\,\frac{\Delta a}{a}$$

$$\dot{\lambda} = 1.5 \times 7.2921\times 10^{-5} \times \frac{35}{42\,164}\;\text{rad/s}$$

$$\dot{\lambda} = 1.5 \times 7.2921\times 10^{-5} \times 8.30\times 10^{-4}$$

$$\dot{\lambda} \approx 9.08\times 10^{-8}\;\text{rad/s}$$ **(3 marks)**

**Step 2 — Convert to °/day:**

$$\dot{\lambda} = 9.08\times 10^{-8} \times \frac{180}{\pi} \times 86\,400\;\text{°/day}$$

$$\dot{\lambda} = 9.08\times 10^{-8} \times 57.296 \times 86\,400$$

$$\dot{\lambda} \approx 0.450\;\text{°/day}$$ **(2 marks)**

**Step 3 — Time to reach ±0.05°:**

$$T_{\text{EWSK}} = \frac{0.05}{0.450} \approx 0.111\;\text{days} \approx 2.67\;\text{hours}$$

This is unrealistically small because of the large assumed $\Delta a$. In practice, EWSK is performed every **2 to 4 weeks** because $\Delta a$ is held to within a few km by ongoing corrections. **(2 marks)**

$$\boxed{\dot{\lambda} \approx 0.45^{\circ}/\text{day for } \Delta a = 35\;\text{km}}$$

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exam**

- ✅ Station keeping = **NSSK + EWSK** working in tandem — never forget both.
- ✅ **NSSK corrects inclination**; burn is **cross-track** at the line of nodes.
- ✅ **EWSK corrects longitude**; burn is **tangential** (prograde/retrograde).
- ✅ GEO altitude is **35,786 km** above Earth's surface (semi-major axis **42,164 km**).
- ✅ GEO orbital period = **sidereal day = 86,164 s** (= 23 h 56 m 4 s).
- ✅ The **combined luni-solar inclination drift** is empirically ≈ **0.85 °/year** (Moon dominant).
- ✅ **Single-burn ΔV formula:** $\Delta V = 2 V_{\text{GEO}}\sin(\Delta i / 2) \approx V_{\text{GEO}} \cdot \Delta i$ (rad).
- ✅ Annual ΔV budget for NSSK ≈ **45–50 m/s/yr** (chemical) or **5–10 m/s/yr** (electric).
- ✅ EWSK cycle is **≈ 28 days**; NSSK cycle is **≈ 14 days**.
- ✅ A **78 km altitude error** causes **1 °/day** longitude drift (memorize this number).
- ✅ Station keeping is a **closed-loop** control system: TT&C → OD → Predict → Burn → Re-acquire.
- ✅ **Electric propulsion** (XIPS, SPT) gives **$I_{sp} \approx 3000$ s**, 10× chemical, but lower thrust.
- ✅ Total ΔV over a 15-year GEO life ≈ **600–750 m/s** (with margin); reserve propellant = ≈ **15–25 %** of launch mass.
- ✅ Always state **units (m/s)** and **burn direction** in KTU numerical answers — marks are deducted otherwise.

> **Final Tip:** When asked to "explain station keeping" in a 7-mark question, always structure your answer as **Definition → Two Sub-systems (NSSK + EWSK) → Block Diagram → ΔV Formulas → Practical Example**. This is the KTU high-scoring template.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Telemetry: Functional Blocks of Telemetry and Telecontrol Systems
topics.content: > **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT634 - COMMUNICATION SYSTEM AND TELEMETRY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Telemetry: Functional Blocks of Telemetry and Telecontrol Systems ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Telemetry — Formal Definition **Telemetry** is the process of automatic measurement and wireless (or wired) transmission of data from a remote, inaccessible, or hazardous source to a receiving, monitoring, and recording station. The word is derived from the Greek roots *tele* (remote) and *metron* (measure). In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, telemetry is treated as a measurement-and-communication discipline that forms the foundation of modern SCADA, IoT, industrial automation, satellite communication, and biomedical instrumentation systems. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Definition (Board Standard):** "Telemetry is the science and technology of automatically measuring a quantity, gathering the data at a remote location, and transmitting it to a receiving equipment where it is displayed, recorded, or processed." ### 1.2 Telecontrol — Formal Definition **Telecontrol** (also called *telemetry and command*, or *supervisory control*) is a two-way extension of telemetry in which measured data flows from the remote terminal to the control station, while **command/control signals** flow in the reverse direction to actuate devices (valves, motors, switches) at the remote end. Together, telemetry and teleconstitutute a closed-loop automation system. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Telemetry = One-way sensing (remote → control room).** > **Telecontrol = Two-way sensing + actuation (control room ↔ remote).** ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition Think of a **smart home security camera**: - The **camera sensor** (transducer) sees movement → converts light into an electrical signal. - The **signal** is **conditioned, encoded, and transmitted** through Wi-Fi to your **phone screen** (display). - This one-way data flow of "sensing at a distance" is **telemetry**. - Now, suppose you tap your phone and the front-door **smart lock clicks open** because your command traveled back to the lock actuator. That reverse path is **telecontrol**. A more industrial analogy: a **doctor monitoring a patient in an ICU** (telemetry — ECG, SpO₂ on a central monitor) versus a **robotic surgeon operating from another city** (telecontrol — control signals travel back to the robot arms). ### 1.4 Why These Systems Matter (Engineering Context) | Domain | Application | |---|---| | **Power grids** | Remote monitoring of transformer voltage, line current | | **Space research** | ISRO/NASA downlink of satellite health parameters | | **Oil & gas** | Pipeline pressure/temperature from offshore rigs | | **Defence** | Missile guidance, UAV telemetry | | **Biomedical** | Wireless patient monitoring (tele-ICU) | | **Industrial IoT** | Smart factories (Industry 4.0) | > [!NOTE] > **Standard Telemetry Frequency Band (CCIR / IRIG Standards):** **216–260 MHz** (VHF) is one classical allocation for analog/digital telemetry, alongside L-band **1435–1535 MHz** and S-band **2200–2290 MHz** for aerospace. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Generic Functional Block Architecture A telemetry/telecontrol system is a **signal-chain pipeline**. Each block performs one well-defined signal transformation, and the output of one stage becomes the input of the next. The chain is identical in principle whether the data is carried by a copper wire, an RF radio link, an optical fiber, or an IP network. The generic stages are: 1. **Primary Sensing Element (Transducer / Sensor)** — Converts the physical quantity (temperature, pressure, flow, strain, humidity, vibration, etc.) into an analogous electrical quantity (voltage, current, resistance, capacitance). *Example:* Thermocouple (T → mV), RTD (T → Ω), LVDT (displacement → V), piezoelectric crystal (force → charge). 2. **Signal Conditioning Unit (SCU)** — Performs amplification, linearization, filtering, cold-junction compensation (for thermocouples), and bridge excitation (for strain gauges). The output is a clean, standardized analog signal (typically **0–5 V** or **4–20 mA** in industrial systems). 3. **Multiplexer (MUX)** — Time-division switching of multiple conditioned channels onto a single transmission path. Two dominant architectures: - **TDM-PAM** (PAM Time Division Multiplexing) - **TDM-PCM** (PCM Time Division Multiplexing) — the modern KTU-relevant form. 4. **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** — Samples the multiplexed analog signal and quantizes it into binary words. Resolution is **N = 2ⁿ levels** for an *n*-bit ADC, giving quantization step $\Delta = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n - 1}$ and SNR$_q \approx (6.02n + 1.76)$ dB. 5. **Encoder / Channel Coder / Modulator** — Adds redundancy (block/convolution codes) for error protection, and modulates a carrier (ASK/FSK/PSK/QAM) to make the baseband signal compatible with the channel. 6. **Transmitter & Communication Channel** — Up-conversion, power amplification, and propagation through the chosen medium (twisted pair, coaxial cable, optical fiber, free-space RF, satellite, cellular 4G/5G). 7. **Receiver & Front-End** — Low-noise amplification, down-conversion, automatic gain control (AGC), matched filtering. 8. **Decoder / Demodulator** — Symbol detection, error correction, recovery of the digital bitstream. 9. **Demultiplexer (DEMUX)** — Reconstructs individual channel waveforms from the time-interleaved PCM stream. 10. **Data Processing, Display, and Storage** — Microprocessor/PC-based HMI, recorders, alarms, and analytics. This is the *Decision Centre*. 11. **Control Command Encoder (Telecontrol-Only Block)** — Generates the reverse-path command message and routes it through a parallel transmit chain. 12. **Actuator (Telecontrol-Only Block)** — Converts the decoded command into a physical action (closing a relay, opening a valve, driving a motor). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Tip:** When asked to "draw and explain the block diagram," always label **every block**, show **signal flow arrows in one direction (telemetry)** and **two directions (telecontrol)**, and explicitly mark the **boundary between the remote terminal and the control/monitoring station**. ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Equation / Quantity | Units / Notes | |---|---|---| | Number of quantization levels | $L = 2^n$ | $n$ = number of bits | | Quantization step size | $\Delta = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n - 1}$ | Volts | | Theoretical SQNR (full-scale sine) | $\text{SQNR} = 6.02\,n + 1.76$ | dB | | PCM bit rate | $R_b = n \cdot f_s$ | bits/s; $f_s \geq 2f_m$ (Nyquist) | | TDM frame period | $T_f = N \cdot T_s$ | seconds; $N$ = number of channels | | Channel bandwidth (PCM-NRZ) | $B \geq \dfrac{R_b}{2}$ | Hz (ideal Nyquist) | | Shannon channel capacity | $C = B \log_2\!\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | bits/s | | Bit Error Probability (BPSK, coherent) | $P_e = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)$ | dimensionless | | Standard analog loop current | $I = 4 \text{ to } 20\ \text{mA}$ | Industrial "live-zero" standard | | Standard voltage signal | $V = 0 \text{ to } 5\ \text{V}$ or $\pm 10\ \text{V}$ | Industrial analog standard | | Resolution (4–20 mA span) | $I_{span} = 16\ \text{mA}$ | Live-zero = 4 mA | ### 2.3 Classification of Telemetry Systems **(a) By signal nature** — *Analog telemetry* (continuous voltage/current) vs *Digital telemetry* (PCM-based, KTU-emphasis). **(b) By channel** — *Wired* (telemetry in power cables, PLC) vs *Wireless* (RF, satellite, cellular). **(c) By multiplexing** — *TDM*, *FDM*, *CDM*. **(d) By range** — *Short-range* (industrial), *Medium-range* (radio 30–300 MHz), *Long-range* (satellite). **(e) By application** — *Aerospace*, *Medical*, *Industrial*, *Scientific*, *Defence*. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility In the **Smart Grid**, every feeder line carries a **Data Acquisition (DAQ)** module whose front-end is precisely this block chain: CT/PT transducers → SCU → MUX → ADC → encoder → PLC/RF link → control centre. Without this chain, **SCADA** cannot exist. Similarly, an **ISRO satellite** downlinks hundreds of telemetry channels (battery voltage, panel temperature, payload health) through a single **S-band or X-band** downlink using exactly the same architecture — proving that this block diagram is **universal across all telemetry domains**. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Number of Quantization Levels vs Resolution **Statement:** For an $n$-bit ADC, the number of distinct quantization levels is $L = 2^n$. **Step 1 — Definition of resolution.** A unipolar ADC maps an analog range $[0, V_{FS}]$ to a digital code. Each codeword is a binary string of $n$ bits. **Step 2 — Counting codewords.** A binary string of $n$ bits can represent exactly $2 \times 2 \times 2 \cdots = 2^n$ distinct values (Fundamental Principle of Counting). **Step 3 — Conclusion.** Therefore, the number of quantization levels equals the number of possible codewords: $$ L = 2^{n} $$ **Step 4 — Quantization step derivation.** The analog range is divided into $L$ equal intervals, each of size $\Delta$: $$ \Delta = \frac{V_{FS}}{L} = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^{n}} $$ For a *bipolar* $\pm V_{FS}$ input the range doubles, so the denominator becomes $2^n - 1$ (the most negative and most positive codes are not symmetric), giving $$ \Delta = \frac{2 V_{FS}}{2^{n} - 1} $$ > [!NOTE] > KTU boards **accept both** $2^n$ (bipolar-symmetric) and $2^n - 1$ (true bipolar) in the denominator. Write the version that matches your input range statement. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio (SQNR) **Statement:** $\text{SQNR} = 6.02n + 1.76$ dB for a full-scale sine wave. **Step 1 — Quantization noise power.** The quantization error $e_q$ is uniformly distributed in $[-\Delta/2, +\Delta/2]$. Its mean-square value (noise variance) is $$ \sigma_{q}^{2} = \int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} e^{\,2}\,\frac{1}{\Delta}\,de = \frac{\Delta^{2}}{12} $$ **Step 2 — Full-scale sine-wave signal power.** For a sine wave of peak amplitude $V_{p} = V_{FS}/2$, the average power across a 1 Ω load is $$ P_{s} = \frac{V_{p}^{2}}{2} = \frac{V_{FS}^{2}}{8} $$ **Step 3 — Form the SQNR ratio.** Substituting $\Delta = V_{FS}/2^n$: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{SQNR}_{\text{linear}} &= \frac{P_s}{\sigma_q^2} = \frac{V_{FS}^{2}/8}{\Delta^{2}/12} = \frac{V_{FS}^{2}}{8} \cdot \frac{12}{V_{FS}^{2}/2^{2n}} \\ &= \frac{12}{8} \cdot 2^{2n} = \frac{3}{2} \cdot 4^{n} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Convert to decibels.** Using $10 \log_{10}(\cdot)$: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} &= 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{3}{2}\right) + 10 \log_{10}\!\left(4^{n}\right) \\ &= 10 \log_{10}\!\left(1.5\right) + 10 n \log_{10}\!\left(4\right) \\ &\approx 1.7609 + 10 n (0.60206) \\ &\approx 6.02\,n + 1.76 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Interpretation.** Each additional bit of ADC resolution yields a **6.02 dB** improvement in SQNR. Thus moving from 8-bit to 12-bit quantization gains $\approx 4 \times 6.02 = 24.08$ dB of dynamic range. --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — PCM Bit-Rate for an FDM/TDM Telemetry Frame **Statement:** For $N$ input channels each band-limited to $f_m$ Hz and sampled at the Nyquist rate $f_s = 2 f_m$, the serial PCM bit-rate is $R_b = N \cdot n \cdot 2 f_m$. **Step 1 — Per-channel Nyquist rate.** By the sampling theorem, $f_s \geq 2 f_m$. Choosing the minimum, $f_s = 2 f_m$ samples/s. **Step 2 — Per-channel bit rate after $n$-bit quantization.** Each sample produces $n$ bits, so the per-channel bit rate is $$ R_{b,\text{channel}} = n f_s = 2 n f_m $$ **Step 3 — Time-division multiplexing of $N$ channels.** TDM interleaves the channels in time, producing a frame that contains $N$ successive $n$-bit words. The aggregate bit rate is $$ \begin{aligned} R_b &= N \cdot R_{b,\text{channel}} \\ &= N \cdot 2 n f_m \\ &= 2 N n f_m \ \text{bits/s} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Frame period.** The frame duration is the time taken to transmit $N \cdot n$ bits at rate $R_b$: $$ T_f = \frac{N \cdot n}{R_b} = \frac{1}{2 f_m} $$ **Step 5 — Channel bandwidth.** For a rectangular NRZ line code the minimum Nyquist bandwidth is $$ B \geq \frac{R_b}{2} = N n f_m $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Numerical sanity check:** With $N = 12$, $f_m = 4\ \text{kHz}$ (voice), $n = 8$ bits: $R_b = 2 \cdot 12 \cdot 8 \cdot 4000 = 768\ \text{kbps}$. This is the classical T1-carrier rate (1.544 Mbps after framing). KTU frequently uses this exact number in problems. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Telemetry vs Telecontrol | Parameter | Telemetry | Telecontrol | |---|---|---| | **Direction of data flow** | One-way (remote → control) | Bi-directional | | **Purpose** | Measurement, monitoring, recording | Measurement + actuation | | **Actuator block** | Absent | Present at remote terminal | | **Feedback channel** | Not required | Required (control command uplink) | | **Latency tolerance** | Often relaxed (post-processing) | Tight (real-time control) | | **Failure consequence** | Data loss (recoverable) | System instability / safety risk | | **Loop structure** | Open-loop monitoring | Closed-loop supervisory control | | **Example** | Satellite downlink of temperatures | Smart-grid feeder load-shedding command | --- ### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style) **Problem:** A PCM telemetry system multiplexes $N = 16$ channels, each band-limited to $f_m = 3.4\ \text{kHz}$. The ADC uses $n = 8$ bits. Calculate (i) per-channel sampling rate, (ii) frame period, (iii) aggregate bit rate, (iv) minimum transmission bandwidth. **Solution:** (i) Per-channel sampling rate (Nyquist): $$ f_s = 2 f_m = 2 \times 3.4 = 6.8\ \text{kHz} $$ (ii) Frame period — time to gather one sample from each of 16 channels: $$ T_f = \frac{1}{f_s} = \frac{1}{6800} \approx 147.06\ \mu\text{s} $$ (iii) Aggregate bit rate: $$ R_b = N \cdot n \cdot f_s = 16 \times 8 \times 6800 = 870.4\ \text{kbps} $$ (iv) Minimum NRZ bandwidth (Nyquist): $$ B_{\min} = \frac{R_b}{2} = \frac{870\,400}{2} = 435.2\ \text{kHz} $$ --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Generic Telemetry System (One-Way) ```mermaid graph LR A[Physical Quantity] --> B[Primary Transducer] B --> C[Signal Conditioning Unit] C --> D[Analog Multiplexer] D --> E[Sample and Hold] E --> F[Analog to Digital Converter] F --> G[PCM Encoder] G --> H[Channel Encoder] H --> I[Modulator] I --> J[Transmitter] J --> K[Communication Channel] K --> L[Receiver] L --> M[Demodulator] M --> N[Channel Decoder] N --> O[PCM Decoder] O --> P[Demultiplexer] P --> Q[Display Recorder HMI] ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Telecontrol System (Bi-Directional, Closed Loop) ```mermaid graph LR A1[Remote Physical Quantity] --> B1[Transducer] B1 --> C1[Signal Conditioning] C1 --> D1[Multiplexer] D1 --> E1[ADC] E1 --> F1[PCM Encoder] F1 --> G1[Channel Encoder] G1 --> H1[Transmitter] H1 --> I1[Bi-Directional Channel] I1 --> J1[Receiver] J1 --> K1[Demodulator] K1 --> L1[Channel Decoder] L1 --> M1[PCM Decoder] M1 --> N1[Demultiplexer] N1 --> O1[Display and HMI] O1 --> P1[Controller CPU] P1 --> Q1[Command Encoder] Q1 --> R1[Modulator] R1 --> S1[Transmitter Uplink] S1 --> I1 I1 --> T1[Receiver Downlink at Remote] T1 --> U1[Command Decoder] U1 --> V1[Actuator] V1 --> W1[Physical Action on Process] W1 --> A1 ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Schematic — Data Acquisition Front-End (Industrial SCADA) ```mermaid graph TD S1[Temperature Sensor] --> SC1[Conditioning Amplifier] S2[Pressure Sensor] --> SC1 S3[Flow Sensor] --> SC1 S4[Level Sensor] --> SC1 SC1 --> MX1[4 Channel Analog MUX] MX1 --> SH1[Sample and Hold] SH1 --> AD1[12 bit ADC] AD1 --> MC1[Microcontroller CPU] MC1 --> TX1[RS485 or RF Transmitter] TX1 --> CH1[Channel Wired or Wireless] CH1 --> RX1[Receiver at Control Room] RX1 --> PC1[SCADA HMI PC] PC1 --> DB1[Database Historian] PC1 --> AL1[Alarm and Analytics] ``` ### 4.4 Mermaid Process Flow — Signal Transformation Across Blocks ```mermaid graph LR X1[Analog Physical Domain] --> X2[Analog Electrical Domain mV or mA] X2 --> X3[Standardized Analog 0 to 5 V or 4 to 20 mA] X3 --> X4[Sampled Analog Discrete Time] X4 --> X5[Digital PCM Bitstream] X5 --> X6[Modulated Passband Signal] X6 --> X7[Channel Affected Signal with Noise] X7 --> X8[Equalized Digital Bitstream] X8 --> X9[Reconstructed Analog Waveform] X9 --> X10[Displayed or Recorded Quantity] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Diagram-Specific Note:** The arrows in the diagrams are the KTU-expected **left-to-right data flow**. In the telecontrol diagram, the upper path is the **measuring/uplink** leg, the lower path is the **command/downlink** leg, and the **controller** is the decision-making element that closes the loop. Examiner’s marking scheme explicitly awards **2 marks for the direction of arrows being correct** and **1 mark for labeling the channel as "bi-directional"**. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Define telemetry. With a neat block diagram, explain the function of a transducer in a telemetry system. **Model Answer (3 marks):** Telemetry is the process of measuring a physical quantity at a remote/inaccessible location and transmitting the corresponding data to a receiving station where it is displayed or recorded. *(1 mark)* A **transducer** is the primary sensing element that converts the physical quantity (e.g., temperature, pressure, displacement) into a proportional electrical signal (voltage, current, resistance or capacitance). *(1 mark)* For example, a thermocouple converts temperature into millivolts, and an LVDT converts displacement into an AC voltage. The transducer output is then handed over to the **signal-conditioning** block for amplification and filtering. *(1 mark)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** Differentiate between telemetry and telecontrol. Mention any two applications of each. **Model Answer (3 marks):** Telemetry is **one-way** measurement and transmission of data from a remote site to a control/monitoring station. Telecontrol is **two-way**: it includes telemetry *plus* the reverse path of control commands to operate remote actuators. *(1 mark)* Telemetry applications: (i) ISRO satellite health-parameter downlink, (ii) wireless ICU patient monitoring. *(1 mark)* Telecontrol applications: (i) SCADA-based power-grid feeder switching, (ii) robotic telesurgery. *(1 mark)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — Module 3 Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) #### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1** **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]** *(a)* Draw the functional block diagram of a generalized PCM telemetry system and explain the function of each block. *(7 marks)* *(b)* A PCM telemetry system handles $N = 24$ voice channels, each band-limited to $f_m = 3.4\ \text{kHz}$. The system uses an 8-bit ADC and NRZ line coding. Determine (i) the per-channel sampling rate, (ii) the frame period, (iii) the aggregate bit rate, and (iv) the minimum transmission bandwidth. *(7 marks)* **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:** Draw the block chain *(2 marks)*: ``` Physical Quantity → Transducer → Signal Conditioner → Multiplexer → Sample & Hold → ADC → PCM Encoder → Channel Encoder → Modulator → Tx → Channel → Rx → Demodulator → Channel Decoder → PCM Decoder → Demultiplexer → Display/Recorder ``` Explanation of each block *(5 marks, 1 per key block)*: - **Transducer:** Converts the physical quantity (T, P, displacement) to a small electrical signal. - **Signal conditioner:** Amplifies, linearizes, and filters the transducer output to a standard range (0–5 V or 4–20 mA). - **Multiplexer:** Time-shares multiple channels onto one ADC to save hardware. - **Sample & Hold (S/H):** Holds the analog value constant during the ADC conversion interval to avoid aperture error. - **ADC:** Converts each held sample into an $n$-bit binary word (quantization). - **PCM Encoder:** Serializes the parallel bits and adds framing bits. - **Channel Encoder:** Adds error-correction redundancy. - **Modulator:** Maps bits to a passband waveform (ASK/FSK/PSK) for channel compatibility. - **Channel:** The physical medium — wire, RF, fiber, satellite. - **Receiver chain:** Performs the reverse operations — demodulation, decoding, demultiplexing, reconstruction. - **Display/Recorder:** HMI monitor, magnetic/SSD storage, alarm generation. **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:** (i) Per-channel Nyquist sampling rate *(2 marks)*: $$ f_s = 2 f_m = 2 \times 3.4 = 6.8\ \text{kHz} $$ (ii) Frame period — one sample per channel per frame *(1 mark)*: $$ T_f = \frac{1}{f_s} = \frac{1}{6800} \approx 147.06\ \mu\text{s} $$ (iii) Aggregate bit rate *(2 marks)*: $$ R_b = N \cdot n \cdot f_s = 24 \times 8 \times 6800 = 1.3056\ \text{Mbps} $$ (iv) Minimum NRZ bandwidth *(2 marks)*: $$ B_{\min} = \frac{R_b}{2} = \frac{1.3056 \times 10^{6}}{2} = 652.8\ \text{kHz} $$ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Marks Lost Most Often:** > 1. **Confusing TDM frame period with bit duration.** Frame period = $1/f_s$, not $1/R_b$. > 2. **Forgetting the 1/2 factor** for NRZ bandwidth. RC shaping needs more bandwidth — but KTU only asks for the ideal Nyquist minimum. > 3. **Dropping units** (kHz vs MHz). Examiners specifically dock 0.5 marks per missing unit. > 4. **Drawing the block diagram with arrows pointing both ways** in a one-way telemetry question — that is the **telecontrol** diagram. Wrong direction = 2 marks lost. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]** *(a)* With a neat block diagram, explain a telecontrol system. Distinguish clearly between the telemetry leg and the control leg. *(7 marks)* *(b)* An industrial SCADA link uses a 12-bit ADC and 8 channels each with $f_m = 2\ \text{kHz}$. Compute (i) the quantization step size for a unipolar $0$–$10\ \text{V}$ input range, (ii) the SQNR in dB, and (iii) the minimum NRZ bandwidth. *(7 marks)* **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:** Draw the bi-directional block diagram showing: - **Telemetry leg (uplink):** Transducer → SCU → MUX → ADC → Encoder → Tx → Channel → Rx → Decoder → DEMUX → Display. - **Telecontrol leg (downlink):** Controller → Command Encoder → Modulator → Tx (uplink channel shared) → Remote Rx → Command Decoder → Actuator → Process. *(3 marks for the diagram, 2 marks for arrow directions and labels.)* Explanation *(4 marks)*: - The **telemetry leg** is the one-way path carrying *measurement data* from the remote terminal to the central station. - The **control leg** is the reverse path carrying *actuation commands* generated by the central controller (CPU/PLC) to remote actuators (valves, motors, circuit breakers). - The two legs share a common **bi-directional channel** (half-duplex or full-duplex). - The **controller** in the centre is the *decision-making element*: it compares the measured value with the setpoint and generates the appropriate command. - This closed-loop architecture converts a passive monitoring system into an **active supervisory-control system**, the basis of SCADA. **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:** (i) Quantization step for a unipolar $0$–$10\ \text{V}$ range with $n = 12$ bits *(2 marks)*: $$ \Delta = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^{n}} = \frac{10}{4096} = 2.4414\ \text{mV} $$ (ii) SQNR in dB *(2 marks)*: $$ \text{SQNR} = 6.02 n + 1.76 = 6.02 \times 12 + 1.76 = 74.0\ \text{dB} $$ (iii) Minimum NRZ bandwidth *(3 marks)*: $$ \begin{aligned} f_s &= 2 f_m = 2 \times 2000 = 4000\ \text{Hz} \\ R_b &= N \cdot n \cdot f_s = 8 \times 12 \times 4000 = 384\ \text{kbps} \\ B_{\min} &= \frac{R_b}{2} = 192\ \text{kHz} \end{aligned} $$ > [!WARNING] > **Common Errors in Question B:** > 1. Writing $2^{n-1}$ or $2^{n} - 1$ in the denominator without checking whether the range is unipolar or bipolar — examiner deducts **1 full mark**. > 2. Computing SQNR as $6.02n$ only (omitting the $1.76$ dB term) — losing **0.5 marks**. > 3. Mixing up $N \cdot n \cdot f_s$ with $N \cdot f_s$ (forgetting that each sample carries $n$ bits) — losing **1.5 marks**. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - **Telemetry = measure at a distance**; **Telecontrol = measure + actuate at a distance** (closed loop). - The **universal signal chain** is: **Transducer → Signal Conditioner → Multiplexer → ADC → Encoder → Modulator → Channel → Demodulator → Decoder → DEMUX → Display**, with an **Actuator + Command Encoder** added for telecontrol. - **Quantization levels:** $L = 2^n$. **Step size:** $\Delta = V_{FS}/2^n$ (unipolar) or $2V_{FS}/(2^n - 1)$ (bipolar). - **SQNR for full-scale sine:** $\text{SQNR} = 6.02n + 1.76$ dB — derive, do not memorize blindly. - **Nyquist rate:** $f_s = 2 f_m$. **PCM bit rate:** $R_b = N \cdot n \cdot f_s$. **Frame period:** $T_f = 1/f_s$. - **Minimum NRZ bandwidth:** $B_{\min} = R_b/2$ (ideal Nyquist). - **Industrial standard current loop:** **4–20 mA** (live-zero allows detection of cable break). - **Shannon limit:** $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ — gives an absolute ceiling on bit rate. - **TDM ≠ FDM ≠ CDM** — TDM is the KTU-emphasis; FDM is used in older analog telemetry; CDM is used in modern spread-spectrum systems. - **Standard telemetry bands:** VHF 216–260 MHz, L-band 1435–1535 MHz, S-band 2200–2290 MHz. - **Examiner’s mantra:** Draw the block diagram with **single-line arrows for telemetry**, **double-line arrows for telecontrol**, **label the channel as bi-directional**, and **mention the controller/actuator explicitly** when the question is about telecontrol. - **Common KTU mistake to avoid:** Writing the block diagram for a "telemetry system" but including a feedback path with an actuator — that becomes telecontrol and **does not answer the asked question**. ---

topics.title: Telemetry Methods - Classical Ones - Pneumatic and Electrical Telemetry - Electrical Telemetry Systems - Voltage, Current, Position Telemetry - Optical Telemetry
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT634 - COMMUNICATION SYSTEM AND TELEMETRY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Telemetry Methods - Classical Ones - Pneumatic and Electrical Telemetry - Electrical Telemetry Systems - Voltage, Current, Position Telemetry - Optical Telemetry

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Definition of Telemetry

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Standard):**
> *Telemetry* is the automated process of **measuring, transmitting, and receiving data from remote or inaccessible points to an receiving station where the data can be analyzed, displayed, and recorded.** The word originates from the Greek roots *"tele"* (remote) and *"metron"* (measure).

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context for Communication Systems and Telemetry (PEICT634), telemetry forms the **backbone of industrial process control, aerospace, healthcare monitoring, and remote sensing applications**. It encompasses the complete chain of: **Sensor → Transducer → Signal Conditioning → Transmitter → Communication Channel → Receiver → Display/Recorder.**

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Medical Heart Monitor"**
> Imagine a patient in an ICU connected to a heart monitor. The sensors on the patient's chest detect electrical signals (ECG). These signals are too weak to travel far and are filled with noise. The telemetry system **amplifies, encodes, and transmits** these signals through wires (or wirelessly) to a nurses' station display, where doctors can monitor the patient's heart in real time — without being physically present.
> 
> **The patient = Remote Source | The cables/air = Communication Channel | The display = Receiver/Display**

This is exactly the principle behind every telemetry system: **convert a physical quantity into a transmittable signal, send it, and reconstruct it faithfully at a distant location.**

### 1.3 Standard Metrics in Telemetry

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Telemetry Performance Metrics (KTU High-Yield):**
> - **Accuracy:** ±0.1% to ±0.5% of full scale (industrial standard: **±0.1% FS**)
> - **Bandwidth:** **DC to 10 kHz** (typical analog process telemetry)
> - **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR):** Minimum **40 dB** for industrial grade
> - **Update Rate:** **1 to 10 samples/second** for process control
> - **Standard Signal Ranges:** 
>   - Current: **4–20 mA** (industry standard)
>   - Voltage: **0–10 V** or **1–5 V**
>   - Pneumatic: **3–15 psi**
> - **Operating Temperature:** **−40°C to +85°C** (industrial grade)

### 1.4 Classification of Telemetry Methods

> [!NOTE]
> **Hierarchical Classification (as per KTU 2024 Module 3):**
> 
> **Telemetry Methods**
> ├── **Classical Telemetry**
> │   ├── Pneumatic Telemetry (uses air pressure: 3–15 psi)
> │   ├── Electrical Telemetry
> │   │   ├── Voltage Telemetry (0–10 V, 1–5 V)
> │   │   ├── Current Telemetry (4–20 mA)
> │   │   └── Position Telemetry (Synchros, Servos, LVDT)
> │   └── Optical Telemetry (light-based transmission)

### 1.5 Why These Classical Methods Matter

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Significance:**
> Although modern digital telemetry (HART, Foundation Fieldbus, Industrial Ethernet) dominates today, the **classical methods form the foundational layer**. Every modern smart transmitter still uses **4–20 mA current loops** as a fallback. Pneumatic telemetry is still preferred in **explosive/hazardous environments** (oil refineries, chemical plants) because air cannot cause sparks. Position telemetry using synchros is the workhorse of **military radar, gun positioning, and aerospace navigation systems.**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Pneumatic Telemetry — Theoretical Analysis

**Pneumatic telemetry** uses **compressed air (or gas) pressure** as the information-carrying medium. The standard pressure range is **3–15 psi** (pounds per square inch), where:
- **3 psi** represents **0%** of the measured value (live zero)
- **15 psi** represents **100%** of the measured value

#### Operational Logic — Step-by-Step:

> [!NOTE]
> **Pneumatic Telemetry Working Principle:**
> 
> 1. **Sensing Element** (bellows, diaphragm, Bourdon tube) detects process variable (pressure, temperature, flow).
> 2. **Nozzle-Flapper Amplifier** converts small mechanical displacement into a proportional air pressure change. The flapper partially blocks a nozzle; small movements cause large back-pressure changes.
> 3. **Pilot Relay / Booster** amplifies the air signal to provide sufficient power for transmission over long pipe runs (up to **300 meters**).
> 4. **Pneumatic Transmission Line** carries the 3–15 psi signal to the receiver.
> 5. **Receiver (Indicating/Recording Instrument)** contains a pressure-sensitive element (bellows) that deflects a pointer or pen.

#### Mathematical Relationship:

The nozzle-flapper transfer function can be approximated as:

$$P_{out} = P_{s} \cdot \frac{K_{nf}}{1 + \tau_{nf} \cdot s} \cdot X_{flapper}$$

Where:
- $P_{out}$ = Output pressure (psi)
- $P_{s}$ = Supply pressure (**20 psi** standard)
- $K_{nf}$ = Nozzle-flapper gain
- $\tau_{nf}$ = Nozzle-flapper time constant
- $X_{flapper}$ = Flapper displacement (inches)

#### Real-World Utility:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Applications of Pneumatic Telemetry:**
> - **Oil & Gas Industry:** Refineries (intrinsically safe)
> - **Chemical Plants:** Where electrical sparks could cause explosions
> - **Power Plants:** Boiler control systems
> - **Pharmaceutical:** Sterile environments

**Advantages:** Intrinsically safe, simple, reliable, no electrical interference.  
**Disadvantages:** Slow response (**~1 Hz bandwidth**), requires clean dry air, limited to short distances.

---

### 2.2 Electrical Telemetry Systems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Standard):**
> *Electrical Telemetry* refers to telemetry systems that use **electrical quantities (voltage, current, or position-encoded signals)** as the information-carrying medium. It is the most widely used classical telemetry method in modern industry.

Electrical telemetry is classified into three sub-categories based on the type of electrical signal used:

---

### 2.3 Voltage Telemetry

**Voltage telemetry** uses a DC voltage proportional to the measured variable. Standard ranges:
- **0–10 V DC** (most common, single-ended)
- **1–5 V DC** (preferred for noise immunity — "live zero" avoids ambiguity)

#### Key Equations:

$$V_{out} = V_{min} + (V_{max} - V_{min}) \cdot \frac{MV - MV_{min}}{MV_{max} - MV_{min}}$$

Where:
- $V_{out}$ = Output voltage (V)
- $V_{min}, V_{max}$ = Range bounds (**0 V and 10 V**, or **1 V and 5 V**)
- $MV$ = Measured variable value
- $MV_{min}, MV_{max}$ = Measurement range limits

#### Transfer Function for 0–10V with 0–100°C input:

$$V_{out}(V) = 0.1 \cdot T(°C)$$

So at **50°C → 5.0 V**, at **100°C → 10.0 V**.

#### Voltage Telemetry Limitations:

> [!WARNING]
> **Why Voltage Telemetry Fails Over Long Distances:**
> 
> Voltage signals suffer from **IR drop** in the wire resistance. If line resistance is $R_{line}$ and current drawn is $I$:
> 
> $$V_{received} = V_{transmitted} - I \cdot R_{line}$$
> 
> For a **1000 ft** run of **#18 AWG copper wire** ($R \approx 0.0064 \Omega/\text{ft} \cdot 1000 = 6.4 \Omega$), with a **20 mA** signal current, the voltage drop is **128 mV** — a **1.28% error** in a 10V system. This is **unacceptable for precision telemetry**.

---

### 2.4 Current Telemetry (4–20 mA Loop)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Critical Concept — The 4–20 mA Current Loop:**
> The **4–20 mA current loop** is the **most important and widely deployed** telemetry standard in the world. It uses **current**, not voltage, as the information carrier. The "live zero" of **4 mA** (not 0 mA) provides:
> - **Disconnection detection:** A reading of 0 mA unambiguously indicates a broken wire.
> - **Power to the transmitter:** 4 mA supplies enough power for the field transmitter electronics.

#### Mathematical Formulation:

$$I_{loop}(mA) = 4 + 16 \cdot \frac{MV - MV_{min}}{MV_{max} - MV_{min}}$$

Where:
- $I_{loop}$ = Loop current (mA)
- $MV$ = Measured variable
- $MV_{min}, MV_{max}$ = Calibrated range

**Inverse (measured variable from current):**

$$MV = MV_{min} + (I_{loop} - 4) \cdot \frac{MV_{max} - MV_{min}}{16}$$

#### Why 4–20 mA Dominates Over Voltage Telemetry:

> [!NOTE]
> **Analysis of Current vs. Voltage Telemetry for 1000 ft loop:**
> 
> The **current in a series loop is identical at every point** (Kirchhoff's Current Law). Therefore, the received current is **independent of wire resistance, wire length, and contact resistance**. The voltage drop across the wire is irrelevant because the receiver senses current, not voltage.
> 
> **Maximum loop resistance for 24V supply:**
> 
> $$R_{max} = \frac{V_{supply} - V_{transmitter(min)}}{I_{max}} = \frac{24V - 12V}{20mA} = 600 \Omega$$
> 
> This allows transmission over **kilometers** of wire.

---

### 2.5 Position Telemetry (Synchro, Servo, LVDT)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:**
> *Position telemetry* transmits the **angular or linear position** of a remote shaft or object by encoding it into electrical signals. It uses **synchros, resolvers, servos, and LVDTs** as transducers.

#### 2.5.1 Synchro Transmitter-Receiver Pair

A **synchro** is a rotary electromechanical transducer that produces an electrical output proportional to its angular position. A pair of synchros acts as a **remote angular position indicator**.

**Construction:** Stator has three windings (Y-connected) displaced 120° apart. Rotor has a single winding excited by an AC reference (**400 Hz** standard for aircraft, **50 Hz** for industrial).

**Stator voltage equations** for synchro transmitter with rotor angle $\theta$:

$$V_{S1} = V_{m} \cdot \sin(\omega t) \cdot \sin(\theta)$$

$$V_{S2} = V_{m} \cdot \sin(\omega t) \cdot \sin(\theta - 120°)$$

$$V_{S3} = V_{m} \cdot \sin(\omega t) \cdot \sin(\theta + 120°)$$

Where:
- $V_{S1}, V_{S2}, V_{S3}$ = Stator terminal voltages
- $V_m$ = Peak amplitude
- $\omega$ = Excitation frequency rad/s
- $\theta$ = Rotor angular position (degrees)

#### 2.5.2 Synchro Control Transformer (CT) Operation

In a **CT (Control Transformer)**, the output is proportional to the **sine of the angle difference** between transmitter rotor and CT rotor:

$$V_{out} = K \cdot \sin(\theta_{TX} - \theta_{CT})$$

When $\theta_{CT} = \theta_{TX}$ (system aligned), $V_{out} = 0$ — called the **electrical null**.

#### 2.5.3 LVDT (Linear Variable Differential Transformer)

> [!NOTE]
> **LVDT Definition (KTU 2024):**
> A **Linear Variable Differential Transformer (LVDT)** is a passive inductive transducer that produces an electrical output proportional to the **linear displacement** of a movable ferromagnetic core.

**Output voltage equation:**

$$V_{out} = K_{LVDT} \cdot x$$

Where:
- $V_{out}$ = Differential output voltage (V)
- $K_{LVDT}$ = LVDT sensitivity (**V/mm**, typically 0.1 to 1 V/mm)
- $x$ = Core displacement from null position (mm)

For an **LVDT with two secondaries:**

$$V_{out} = V_{S1} - V_{S2} = K_1 \cdot x_1 - K_2 \cdot x_2$$

At null position: $x_1 = x_2$, so $V_{out} = 0$.

---

### 2.6 Optical Telemetry

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:**
> *Optical telemetry* uses **light** (visible, infrared, or laser) as the information carrier. The measured variable modulates a light beam that is transmitted through free space, optical fiber, or atmosphere to a photodetector at the receiver.

#### Key Transducers:

1. **Photovoltaic Cell (Silicon):** Generates a voltage proportional to incident light intensity. Open-circuit voltage up to **0.6 V**, short-circuit current proportional to light.
2. **Photoconductive Cell (CdS, LDR):** Resistance changes with light (dark: ~1 MΩ, bright: ~100 Ω).
3. **Phototransistor:** Current proportional to light intensity.
4. **Optical Fiber Sensors:** Intensity-modulated, wavelength-modulated, or phase-modulated.

#### Mathematical Model for Optical Link:

The received optical power in a free-space optical link follows the **inverse square law:**

$$P_{r} = P_{t} \cdot \frac{A_{r}}{(d \cdot \theta)^2} \cdot \tau_{atm}$$

Where:
- $P_r$ = Received power (W)
- $P_t$ = Transmitted power (W)
- $A_r$ = Receiver aperture area (m²)
- $d$ = Distance (m)
- $\theta$ = Beam divergence (radians)
- $\tau_{atm}$ = Atmospheric transmittance

#### Optical Fiber Attenuation:

$$P_{out} = P_{in} \cdot 10^{-\alpha L/10}$$

Where:
- $\alpha$ = Fiber attenuation coefficient (dB/km, typically **0.2 dB/km** for single-mode at 1550 nm)
- $L$ = Fiber length (km)

---

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **📋 KTU 2024 — High-Yield Formula Reference Table**

| # | System | Key Equation | Variables | Units | KTU Frequency |
|---|--------|--------------|-----------|-------|---------------|
| 1 | Pneumatic | $P_{out} = P_s \cdot \frac{K_{nf}}{1+\tau_{nf} s} \cdot X_f$ | $P_s$=20 psi standard | psi | High |
| 2 | Voltage (0–10V) | $V_{out} = 0.1 \cdot T$ (for 0–100°C) | $V_{out}$, $T$ | V, °C | High |
| 3 | Current (4–20mA) | $I = 4 + 16 \cdot \frac{MV - MV_{min}}{MV_{max} - MV_{min}}$ | $I$, $MV$ | mA, eng. units | Very High |
| 4 | Loop Resistance | $R_{max} = \frac{V_{supply} - V_{tx(min)}}{I_{max}}$ | $R_{max}$ | $\Omega$ | High |
| 5 | Synchro Stator | $V_{S1} = V_m \sin(\omega t) \sin(\theta)$ | $\theta$ = rotor angle | V, deg | Very High |
| 6 | Synchro CT | $V_{out} = K \sin(\theta_{TX} - \theta_{CT})$ | null at $\theta_{TX}=\theta_{CT}$ | V | Very High |
| 7 | LVDT | $V_{out} = K_{LVDT} \cdot x$ | $x$ = displacement | V, mm | High |
| 8 | Optical FSPL | $P_r = P_t \cdot \frac{A_r}{(d\theta)^2} \cdot \tau_{atm}$ | inverse square | W, m | Medium |
| 9 | Optical Fiber | $P_{out} = P_{in} \cdot 10^{-\alpha L/10}$ | $\alpha$ = dB/km | dB | Medium |
| 10 | Photovoltaic | $I_{sc} = k \cdot E$ | $E$ = illuminance (lux) | A, lux | Medium |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: 4–20 mA Loop Resistance Calculation

**Problem Statement:** A 4–20 mA pressure transmitter requires a minimum operating voltage of **12 V DC** and is connected to a 24 V DC power supply. The receiver (indicator) has an input resistance of **250 Ω**. Calculate the maximum allowable loop wire resistance.

**Given:**
- $V_{supply} = 24$ V
- $V_{tx(min)} = 12$ V (minimum transmitter voltage)
- $R_{receiver} = 250 \Omega$
- $I_{max} = 20$ mA = $0.020$ A

**Find:** $R_{wire(max)}$

#### Step 1: Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) around the loop

The supply voltage must overcome the transmitter drop, the wire drops (×2 for go and return), and the receiver drop:

$$V_{supply} = V_{tx} + I \cdot R_{wire(total)} + I \cdot R_{receiver}$$

Where $R_{wire(total)} = 2 \cdot R_{wire(one-way)}$ for the round-trip.

#### Step 2: Solve for the maximum total wire resistance

At $I = I_{max} = 20$ mA, and minimum transmitter voltage $V_{tx(min)}$:

$$R_{wire(total)} = \frac{V_{supply} - V_{tx(min)} - I_{max} \cdot R_{receiver}}{I_{max}}$$

#### Step 3: Substitute numerical values

$$R_{wire(total)} = \frac{24 - 12 - (0.020 \cdot 250)}{0.020}$$

$$R_{wire(total)} = \frac{24 - 12 - 5}{0.020} = \frac{7}{0.020}$$

$$R_{wire(total)} = 350 \Omega$$

#### Step 4: Convert to one-way wire resistance

$$R_{wire(one-way)} = \frac{R_{wire(total)}}{2} = \frac{350}{2} = 175 \Omega$$

#### Step 5: Determine maximum cable length

For **#18 AWG copper** ($R = 6.385 \Omega / 1000$ ft):

$$L_{max} = \frac{175 \Omega}{6.385 \Omega/1000ft} = 27.4 \times 10^3 \text{ ft} = 27{,}400 \text{ ft}$$

$$\boxed{L_{max} \approx 27{,}400 \text{ ft} \approx 8.35 \text{ km}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** A 4–20 mA loop on 24V supply can drive a transmitter over **8 km** of wire — vastly superior to voltage telemetry (typically limited to a few hundred meters). This is the **key engineering justification** for using current telemetry.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Synchro Control Transformer Output

**Problem:** A synchro transmitter (TX) is at angle $\theta_{TX} = 45°$. A synchro control transformer (CT) is at $\theta_{CT} = 30°$. The CT has a transfer constant $K = 11.8$ V/turn. Find the CT output voltage.

**Given:** $K = 11.8$ V (peak output per turn), $\theta_{TX} = 45°$, $\theta_{CT} = 30°$.

#### Step 1: Recall the CT output equation

$$V_{out} = K \cdot \sin(\theta_{TX} - \theta_{CT})$$

#### Step 2: Calculate the angular error

$$\Delta\theta = \theta_{TX} - \theta_{CT} = 45° - 30° = 15°$$

#### Step 3: Convert to radians for consistency

$$\Delta\theta_{rad} = 15° \cdot \frac{\pi}{180°} = 0.2618 \text{ rad}$$

#### Step 4: Apply the equation

$$V_{out} = 11.8 \cdot \sin(15°)$$

$$V_{out} = 11.8 \cdot 0.2588$$

$$V_{out} = 3.054 \text{ V (peak)}$$

#### Step 5: Convert to RMS if needed

$$V_{out(RMS)} = \frac{3.054}{\sqrt{2}} = 2.160 \text{ V RMS}$$

$$\boxed{V_{out} \approx 3.05 \text{ V (peak)} \text{ or } 2.16 \text{ V (RMS)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** This error voltage ($V_{out}$) drives a servo motor that rotates the CT shaft until $\theta_{CT} = \theta_{TX}$ and $V_{out} = 0$ (electrical null). This is the **fundamental principle of every synchro servo system** in radar, gun fire control, and aircraft autopilots.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: LVDT Sensitivity and Measurement

**Problem:** An LVDT used to measure displacement has a sensitivity of **0.5 V/mm**. The differential output is measured as **2.35 V**. The LVDT was calibrated such that **+V = positive displacement**. Find the displacement.

**Given:** $K_{LVDT} = 0.5$ V/mm, $V_{out} = +2.35$ V.

#### Step 1: Apply the LVDT equation

$$V_{out} = K_{LVDT} \cdot x$$

#### Step 2: Solve for $x$

$$x = \frac{V_{out}}{K_{LVDT}} = \frac{2.35 \text{ V}}{0.5 \text{ V/mm}}$$

#### Step 3: Calculate

$$x = 4.7 \text{ mm}$$

$$\boxed{x = +4.7 \text{ mm (positive displacement)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Convention in LVDT:** The differential output's **polarity** indicates the **direction** of displacement from the null position. If $V_{S2} > V_{S1}$ (i.e., $V_{out} < 0$ in a specific convention), the core has moved toward the secondary S2 (negative direction).

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Optical Free-Space Link Power Budget

**Problem:** An optical telemetry link operates at $\lambda = 850$ nm. Transmitter power $P_t = 10$ mW. Beam divergence $\theta = 1$ mrad. Receiver aperture diameter $D = 5$ cm. Distance $d = 500$ m. Atmospheric transmittance $\tau_{atm} = 0.85$. Find received power.

**Given:** $P_t = 10$ mW $= 0.010$ W, $\theta = 0.001$ rad, $D = 0.05$ m, $d = 500$ m, $\tau_{atm} = 0.85$.

#### Step 1: Calculate receiver aperture area

$$A_r = \pi \cdot \left(\frac{D}{2}\right)^2 = \pi \cdot (0.025)^2 = 1.963 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

#### Step 2: Apply the inverse square law

$$P_r = P_t \cdot \frac{A_r}{(d \cdot \theta)^2} \cdot \tau_{atm}$$

#### Step 3: Calculate the beam area at receiver

$$A_{beam} = (d \cdot \theta)^2 = (500 \cdot 0.001)^2 = (0.5)^2 = 0.25 \text{ m}^2$$

#### Step 4: Substitute all values

$$P_r = 0.010 \cdot \frac{1.963 \times 10^{-3}}{0.25} \cdot 0.85$$

$$P_r = 0.010 \cdot 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 0.85$$

$$P_r = 6.676 \times 10^{-5} \text{ W}$$

$$P_r \approx 66.76 \text{ μW} = -11.76 \text{ dBm}$$

#### Step 5: Verify against typical photodetector sensitivity

> [!NOTE]
> A silicon PIN photodiode has a typical sensitivity of **−30 dBm** at 850 nm with 1 Mbps data rate. Since $-11.76$ dBm $\gg -30$ dBm, the link has **~18 dB of margin** — adequate for reliable operation.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Analysis

> [!NOTE]
> **📊 Telemetry Method Comparison Matrix (KTU 2024 High-Yield)**

| Parameter | Pneumatic | Voltage | Current (4-20mA) | Position (Synchro) | Optical |
|-----------|-----------|---------|------------------|-------------------|---------|
| **Signal Range** | 3–15 psi | 0–10 V / 1–5 V | 4–20 mA | ±30°–360° angle | 0–10 mW light |
| **Max Distance** | ~300 m | ~50 m | **~8 km** | ~1 km (wire) | **~100 km** (fiber) |
| **Bandwidth** | ~1 Hz | ~100 Hz | ~DC–10 Hz | ~400 Hz (synchro) | **GHz** |
| **Accuracy** | ±0.5% | ±0.1% | **±0.05%** | ±0.1° | ±0.01% |
| **Intrinsic Safety** | **Yes** | No | No (24V) | No | **Yes** (fiber) |
| **Noise Immunity** | High | Low | **High** | Medium | **Very High** |
| **Cost** | Low | Very Low | Low | High | Medium |
| **KTU Importance** | ⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐ |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Generic Telemetry System

```mermaid
graph LR
    src1[Physical Quantity]
    sens1[Sensor]
    cond1[Signal Conditioner]
    enc1[Encoder]
    tx1[Transmitter]
    chan1[Communication Channel]
    rx1[Receiver]
    dec1[Decoder]
    disp1[Display or Recorder]
    src1 --> sens1
    sens1 --> cond1
    cond1 --> enc1
    enc1 --> tx1
    tx1 --> chan1
    chan1 --> rx1
    rx1 --> dec1
    dec1 --> disp1
```

### 4.2 Pneumatic Telemetry — Nozzle Flapper Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    ps[Pressure Supply 20 psi]
    nf[Nozzle Flapper Assembly]
    pr[Pilot Relay Booster]
    pl[Pneumatic Transmission Line]
    rx[Receiver Bellows]
    ind[Indicator Pointer]
    ps --> nf
    nf --> pr
    pr --> pl
    pl --> rx
    rx --> ind
    sensX[Process Variable] --> nf
```

### 4.3 4–20 mA Current Loop — Complete System

```mermaid
graph LR
    pv[Process Variable]
    tx[Smart Transmitter]
    ps[24V DC Supply]
    rw[Wire Resistance]
    rr[Receiver 250 ohm]
    disp[Display Indicator]
    pv --> tx
    ps --> tx
    tx --> rw
    rw --> rr
    rr --> disp
    disp --> ps
```

### 4.4 Synchro Telemetry — TX-CT Servo Loop

```mermaid
graph LR
    rpos[Remote Shaft Position]
    stx[Synchro Transmitter]
    lines[Three Wire Stator Lines]
    sct[Synchro Control Transformer]
    amp[Error Amplifier]
    servo[Servo Motor]
    gear[Gear Train]
    output[Output Shaft Follower]
    rpos --> stx
    stx --> lines
    lines --> sct
    sct --> amp
    amp --> servo
    servo --> gear
    gear --> output
    gear -.feedback.-> sct
```

### 4.5 LVDT Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    pos[Linear Displacement]
    core[Ferromagnetic Core]
    pri[Primary Coil]
    sec1[Secondary S1]
    sec2[Secondary S2]
    exc[AC Excitation]
    det[Differential Detector]
    out[Output Voltage]
    pos --> core
    core --> pri
    core --> sec1
    core --> sec2
    exc --> pri
    sec1 --> det
    sec2 --> det
    det --> out
```

### 4.6 Optical Telemetry Link — Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    meas[Measured Variable]
    mod[Light Modulator LED or Laser]
    opt[Optical Channel Fiber or Free Space]
    pd[Photodetector]
    amp1[Transimpedance Amplifier]
    dem[Demodulator]
    rec[Recorded or Displayed Output]
    meas --> mod
    mod --> opt
    opt --> pd
    pd --> amp1
    amp1 --> dem
    dem --> rec
```

### 4.7 Comparative Telemetry Method Selection Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    start[Telemetry Requirement]
    haz[Hazardous Area?]
    pn[Use Pneumatic 3 to 15 psi]
    long[Long Distance over 100 m?]
    vlt[Use Voltage 0 to 10 V]
    cur[Use Current 4 to 20 mA]
    ang[Angular Position?]
    syn[Use Synchro or Resolver]
    lin[Linear Position?]
    lvdt[Use LVDT or Optical Encoder]
    speed[High Bandwidth or EMI Area?]
    opt[Use Optical Fiber Telemetry]
    start --> haz
    haz -->|Yes| pn
    haz -->|No| long
    long -->|No under 100m| vlt
    long -->|Yes| cur
    ang -->|Yes| syn
    lin -->|Yes| lvdt
    speed -->|Yes| opt
```
