## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

> **"Define telemetry. List any four classical telemetry methods with one example application of each."**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> *Telemetry* is the process of measuring a quantity at a remote location and transmitting the data to a distant station for display, recording, or control.
> 
> **Four Classical Telemetry Methods:**
> 
> | Method | Signal Carrier | Example Application |
> |--------|----------------|---------------------|
> | Pneumatic Telemetry | Air pressure (3–15 psi) | Oil refinery boiler control |
> | Voltage Telemetry | DC voltage (0–10 V) | Lab instrumentation |
> | Current Telemetry | DC current (4–20 mA) | Industrial process transmitters |
> | Position Telemetry | Synchro/LVDT signals | Radar antenna positioning |
> 
> **(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for the four methods)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

> **"Explain why a 4–20 mA current loop is preferred over a 0–10 V voltage signal in industrial telemetry."**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Reasons for 4–20 mA preference over 0–10 V voltage telemetry:**
> 
> 1. **Current is constant throughout the loop** (Kirchhoff's Current Law). Voltage drops in the wire do not affect the current, allowing transmission over **kilometers**.
> 2. **Live zero at 4 mA** allows detection of wire breaks (0 mA = open circuit).
> 3. **4 mA powers the transmitter** electronics, eliminating the need for separate power wiring.
> 4. **Immune to ground loops** and common-mode noise since the receiver measures current.
> 5. **Lower power dissipation** in long cables (P = I²R is small because I is small).
> 
> **(3 marks: 1 for each major point with brief explanation)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

---

**Q-A. (a) With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a 4–20 mA current telemetry system. Derive the loop resistance equation. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

#### Block Diagram:

```
[Process Variable] → [Sensor] → [Transmitter] → [Loop Wire] → [Receiver] → [Display]
                                              ↑
                                         [24V DC Supply]
```

#### Working Principle:

The measured variable (e.g., temperature, pressure) is sensed by a transducer and converted to a **4–20 mA DC current** proportional to the measurement. This current flows through the loop, including the supply, transmitter, wire resistance, and receiver. The receiver (typically a **250 Ω precision resistor**) converts the current back to a voltage (**1–5 V**) for display.

The current at any point in the loop is governed by:

$$I = 4 \text{ mA} + 16 \text{ mA} \cdot \frac{MV - MV_{min}}{MV_{max} - MV_{min}}$$

#### Loop Resistance Derivation:

Applying KVL around the loop:

$$V_{supply} = V_{tx} + I_{max} \cdot R_{wire(total)} + I_{max} \cdot R_{receiver}$$

Solving for $R_{wire(total)}$:

$$R_{wire(total)} = \frac{V_{supply} - V_{tx(min)} - I_{max} \cdot R_{receiver}}{I_{max}}$$

**Numerical example (V_supply = 24 V, V_tx = 12 V, R_receiver = 250 Ω, I_max = 20 mA):**

$$R_{wire(total)} = \frac{24 - 12 - 0.020 \cdot 250}{0.020} = \frac{7}{0.020} = 350 \Omega$$

One-way wire resistance: **175 Ω** (or about **8 km** of #18 AWG).

> **(7 marks: 2 for block diagram, 3 for working, 2 for derivation)**

---

**Q-A. (b) Explain the construction and working of a synchro transmitter-receiver pair with neat diagrams. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

#### Construction:

A synchro pair consists of two units — **Synchro Transmitter (TX)** and **Synchro Control Transformer (CT)** — each with:
- A **three-phase Y-connected stator** (windings S1, S2, S3 displaced 120° in space)
- A **single-phase rotor** excited by an AC reference (typically **400 Hz** for aircraft, **50 Hz** for industrial)

#### Working:

When the TX rotor is at angle $\theta_1$ and the CT rotor is at angle $\theta_2$, voltages are induced in the stators proportional to $\sin(\theta)$. The CT produces an **error voltage** at its rotor:

$$V_{error} = K \cdot \sin(\theta_1 - \theta_2)$$

This error voltage is amplified and applied to a **servo motor**, which drives the CT rotor (and the load) until $\theta_2 = \theta_1$, making $V_{error} = 0$ (**electrical null**).

#### Stator Voltages at TX (rotor at angle $\theta$):

$$V_{S1} = V_m \sin(\omega t) \sin(\theta)$$
$$V_{S2} = V_m \sin(\omega t) \sin(\theta - 120°)$$
$$V_{S3} = V_m \sin(\omega t) \sin(\theta + 120°)$$

#### Applications:

- Radar antenna position indication
- Aircraft flap and rudder position feedback
- Gun fire control systems
- Remote pointer indicators

> **(7 marks: 2 for construction, 3 for working + equations, 2 for applications)**

---

### 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]**

---

**Q-B. (a) Describe the working of an LVDT for displacement measurement. Derive the output voltage equation and plot the characteristic curve. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

#### Construction:

An LVDT has:
- One **primary coil** (P) at the center, excited by AC (typically **1–10 kHz**)
- Two identical **secondary coils** (S1 and S2) wound symmetrically on either side
- A movable **ferromagnetic core** that couples flux between primary and secondaries
- S1 and S2 are connected in **series opposition** (differential)

#### Working:

When the core is at the **null (center) position**, the flux coupled to S1 and S2 is equal, but their induced EMFs are **180° out of phase** (series-opposition connection), giving **V_out = V_S1 − V_S2 = 0**.

When the core moves **right** by $x$: S1 gets more flux, S2 less flux. Net $V_{out}$ is **positive and proportional to $x$**.

When the core moves **left** by $x$: S2 gets more flux, S1 less flux. Net $V_{out}$ is **negative and proportional to $x$**.

#### Output Equation:

$$V_{out} = V_{S1} - V_{S2} = K_1 \cdot x - K_2 \cdot x = K_{LVDT} \cdot x$$

For a well-designed LVDT, $K_1 = K_2 = K_{LVDT}$ (linear range).

**Sensitivity** (typical): $K_{LVDT} = 0.1$ to $1$ V/mm.

#### Characteristic Curve:

The output $V_{out}$ versus displacement $x$ is **linear** within the operating range (typically ±2.5 mm to ±25 mm depending on size) and **non-linear** outside it.

| Displacement $x$ (mm) | $V_{out}$ (V) | Region |
|---|---|---|
| -5 | -2.5 | Linear |
| -2.5 | -1.25 | Linear |
| 0 | 0 | Null |
| +2.5 | +1.25 | Linear |
| +5 | +2.5 | Linear |

> **(7 marks: 2 for construction, 3 for working, 1 for equation, 1 for curve)**

---

**Q-B. (b) Explain optical telemetry. Discuss the role of photodetectors and the use of fiber optic links with relevant equations. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

#### Definition:

Optical telemetry uses **light** (visible, IR, or laser) modulated by the measured variable. The light travels through **free space, atmosphere, or optical fiber** to a photodetector.

#### Key Components:

1. **Light Source:** LED (low cost, moderate bandwidth) or Laser Diode (high power, narrow beam, high bandwidth)
2. **Optical Channel:** Free space, atmosphere, or optical fiber
3. **Photodetector:** PIN photodiode, avalanche photodiode (APD), phototransistor
4. **Signal Recovery:** Transimpedance amplifier + demodulator

#### Photodetector Equations:

**Photovoltaic mode (current output):**

$$I_{sc} = R_\lambda \cdot P_{opt} = R_\lambda \cdot \Phi \cdot A_{pd}$$

Where:
- $R_\lambda$ = Spectral responsivity (A/W), typically **0.4 A/W** at 850 nm
- $P_{opt}$ = Optical power incident (W)
- $\Phi$ = Optical flux density (W/m²)
- $A_{pd}$ = Active area (m²)

**Photoconductive mode (resistance change):**

$$R_{LDR} = R_0 \cdot \left(\frac{L_0}{L}\right)^\gamma$$

Where $L$ is illuminance in lux and $\gamma \approx 0.7$ to **0.9** for CdS cells.

#### Fiber Optic Link Equation:

$$P_{out} = P_{in} \cdot 10^{-\alpha L / 10}$$

For a **single-mode fiber at 1550 nm**, $\alpha = 0.2$ dB/km. Over **50 km**:

$$P_{out} = P_{in} \cdot 10^{-(0.2 \times 50)/10} = P_{in} \cdot 10^{-1} = 0.1 \cdot P_{in}$$

> **(7 marks: 2 for components, 3 for photodetector equations, 2 for fiber link)**

---

### 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing Synchro TX and CT:** A *transmitter* produces stator voltages proportional to its rotor angle. A *control transformer* produces an error voltage proportional to $\sin(\theta_{TX} - \theta_{CT})$. Students often mix these up. **−2 marks** typical penalty.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the "Live Zero" Concept in 4–20 mA:** A common mistake is assuming the loop uses 0–20 mA. The 4 mA offset is **intentional and must be explained** for full marks.
> 
> 3. **LVDT Output Sign:** The differential output's **sign** indicates direction. Students often write only the magnitude.
> 
> 4. **Units in Pneumatic Telemetry:** Always specify **psi** or **bar**. Vague units like "pressure units" lose marks.
> 
> 5. **Synchro Frequency:** Aircraft synchros use **400 Hz**, industrial ones use **50 Hz** or **60 Hz**. Wrong frequency = wrong application context.
> 
> 6. **Optical Link Power Budget:** Always include **atmospheric attenuation** in free-space optical link calculations. Ignoring it loses 1–2 marks.
> 
> 7. **Forgetting AC excitation in LVDT:** LVDT is a **passive AC transducer** — it requires an oscillator for excitation. DC excitation produces no output.

---

### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **📌 KTU 2024 — Rapid Revision Checklist for Module 3 Telemetry Methods**

#### 🎯 Pneumatic Telemetry
- ✅ Signal: **3–15 psi** air pressure (live zero at 3 psi)
- ✅ Range: **0–100%** of measured variable
- ✅ Use in **hazardous/explosive** environments
- ✅ Limited to **~300 m** and **~1 Hz** bandwidth
- ✅ Uses **nozzle-flapper** and **pilot relay** for amplification

#### 🎯 Voltage Telemetry
- ✅ Standards: **0–10 V** or **1–5 V** (live zero preferred)
- ✅ Equation: $V_{out} = V_{min} + (V_{max} - V_{min}) \cdot \frac{MV}{MV_{max}}$
- ✅ Short range only (**<50 m**) due to IR drops
- ✅ Susceptible to **ground loops** and **noise**

#### 🎯 Current Telemetry (4–20 mA) — **KTU's Most Tested Topic**
- ✅ Equation: $I = 4 + 16 \cdot \frac{MV - MV_{min}}{MV_{max} - MV_{min}}$
- ✅ **Loop resistance:** $R_{wire} = \frac{V_{supply} - V_{tx} - I \cdot R_{rx}}{I}$
- ✅ 4 mA powers transmitter; 0 mA = wire break
- ✅ Reaches **8+ km** on 24V supply
- ✅ **Industry standard** for process control

#### 🎯 Position Telemetry
- ✅ **Synchro:** Angular, AC excited, 3 stator windings at 120°
- ✅ Stator voltage: $V_{S1} = V_m \sin(\omega t) \sin(\theta)$
- ✅ CT error: $V_{out} = K \sin(\theta_{TX} - \theta_{CT})$; null at equality
- ✅ **LVDT:** Linear displacement, $V_{out} = K_{LVDT} \cdot x$
- ✅ LVDT needs **AC excitation** (1–10 kHz)
- ✅ LVDT is **passive** (no internal electronics)
- ✅ Sensitivity typically **0.1–1 V/mm**

#### 🎯 Optical Telemetry
- ✅ Carriers: Visible, **IR (850 nm)**, or laser (**1550 nm**)
- ✅ Detectors: **Photodiode** ($R_\lambda \approx 0.4$ A/W), **LDR**, phototransistor
- ✅ Equation: $I_{sc} = R_\lambda \cdot P_{opt}$
- ✅ Fiber loss: $P_{out} = P_{in} \cdot 10^{-\alpha L / 10}$ with $\alpha \approx 0.2$ dB/km
- ✅ Free-space: **inverse square law** with atmospheric attenuation
- ✅ **EMI immune**, ideal for **high-voltage** and **explosive** zones
- ✅ Bandwidth up to **GHz**

#### 🎯 KTU Numerical Problem Patterns
- ✅ Type 1: **4–20 mA** — Find current for given MV, or find MV for given current
- ✅ Type 2: **Loop resistance and cable length** calculation
- ✅ Type 3: **Synchro CT error voltage** for given angle difference
- ✅ Type 4: **LVDT displacement** from output voltage
- ✅ Type 5: **Optical fiber power budget** and link margin

#### 🎯 Top Exam Tip
> When asked to *compare* telemetry methods, always structure your answer as a **table** with: **Signal range, Max distance, Bandwidth, Accuracy, Intrinsic safety, Application** — examiners award marks for structured comparisons.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transducers for Telemetry
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT634 - COMMUNICATION SYSTEM AND TELEMETRY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Transducers for Telemetry # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Transducer (KTU Syllabus Definition):** A *transducer* is a device that converts energy from one form to another. In the specific context of **telemetry**, a transducer is the primary sensing element that converts a non-electrical physical quantity (measurand) — such as temperature, pressure, displacement, flow, force, or light intensity — into a corresponding electrical signal (voltage, current, or frequency) suitable for transmission over a communication channel to a remote receiving station. In a telemetry chain, the transducer sits at the very **front-end** (sensor layer), and its accuracy, linearity, and bandwidth fundamentally determine the overall fidelity of the entire data acquisition and transmission system. The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically lists transducers for **temperature, pressure, displacement, and flow** as core study items. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-world Analogy — The Human Body:** > Think of a telemetry system as the **human nervous system**. Your *skin* is a transducer — it converts physical stimuli (heat, pressure, pain) into electrical nerve impulses. Your *spinal cord and nerves* are the communication channel, and your *brain* is the receiver/display. Just as your skin must be sensitive, accurate, and linear to touch for your brain to correctly interpret the world, a telemetry transducer must faithfully convert physical reality into a clean electrical signal. A *faulty skin* (bad transducer) will mislead the brain (operator), no matter how good the nerves (channel) are. ## 1.3 Broad Classification of Transducers Transducers are broadly classified into two categories as per KTU Board standards: | Classification | Definition | Examples | Energy Source | |---|---|---|---| | **Active Transducers** | Self-generating; produce electrical output **without** external power | Piezoelectric, Thermocouple, Photovoltaic, Tachogenerator | Draws energy from measurand itself | | **Passive Transducers** | Externally powered; the measurand changes an electrical parameter (R, L, or C) | RTD, Thermistor, Strain Gauge, LVDT, Capacitive pickup | Requires external excitation (AC/DC supply) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Note:** Examiners frequently test the *Active vs Passive* distinction. Remember — **Passive transducers need an external excitation source**, while **Active transducers do not**. ## 1.4 Primary Measurands in Telemetry The four most commonly telemetered quantities (and their standard units) are: - **Temperature** → measured in **Kelvin (K)** or **°C**, using RTD, Thermocouple, Thermistor. - **Pressure** → measured in **Pascal (Pa)** or **bar (1 bar = 10⁵ Pa)**, using Strain Gauge, Piezoelectric. - **Displacement** → measured in **metre (m)** or **mm**, using LVDT, Potentiometer, Capacitive. - **Flow** → measured in **m³/s** or **litres/min**, using Turbine flowmeter, Differential pressure transmitter. ## 1.5 Generalized Block Architecture A telemetry transducer subsystem follows a standard functional chain: > **Physical Quantity → Sensing Element → Transduction Element → Signal Conditioning (Amplification / Filtering) → Electrical Output → Telemetry Encoder / Transmitter** # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Detailed Theory of Major Transducer Types ### 2.1.1 Resistive Transducers **1. Potentiometric Transducer** - A resistance element with a sliding wiper. The measurand (displacement) physically moves the wiper. - Output is a **dividing ratio of excitation voltage** $V_{ex}$. - Advantages: Simple, inexpensive, gives high output. - Disadvantages: Mechanical wear, finite resolution, requires large force to move the wiper. **2. Strain Gauge** - A resistive element whose resistance changes when subjected to mechanical strain ($\varepsilon$). - Operating principle based on the **piezoresistive effect**. - Mounted on a load-bearing member (cantilever beam, diaphragm). **3. Resistance Temperature Detector (RTD)** - Exploits the **positive temperature coefficient** of pure metals (Pt, Ni, Cu). - Platinum (Pt100, Pt1000) is the industry standard. - Highly linear, stable, and accurate over a wide range ($-200°C$ to $+850°C$). **4. Thermistor** - Semiconductor device (metal oxides of Mn, Ni, Co) with a **negative temperature coefficient (NTC)** or rarely PTC. - Very high sensitivity ($\sim 10\times$ that of RTD) but **highly non-linear**. - Used where high accuracy over a narrow range is needed. ### 2.1.2 Inductive Transducers **1. Linear Variable Differential Transformer (LVDT)** - The **most important inductive transducer** for displacement measurement in KTU syllabus. - Consists of one primary winding and two identical secondary windings wound on a cylindrical former. - A movable soft-iron core is attached to the measurand. - The two secondaries are connected in **series opposition** (anti-series). **2. Variable Reluctance Transducer** - Reluctance of a magnetic circuit varies with the displacement of the core. - Used for small displacements and vibration measurement. ### 2.1.3 Capacitive Transducers - A capacitor whose capacitance varies with displacement, area overlap, or dielectric change. - $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$ — capacitance changes when $d$, $A$, or $\varepsilon_r$ changes. - Very high sensitivity, excellent for small displacements and pressure (via diaphragm). ### 2.1.4 Piezoelectric Transducer - An **active transducer** based on the **piezoelectric effect**. - Certain crystals (Quartz, Rochelle salt, PZT ceramic) generate a charge $Q$ when subjected to mechanical stress. - $Q = d \cdot F$, where $d$ is the charge sensitivity (in C/N). - Excellent for **dynamic measurements** (vibration, shock, dynamic pressure). - **Not suitable for static measurements** — leakage drains the charge. ### 2.1.5 Photoelectric Transducer - Converts light intensity into an electrical signal. - Devices: Photovoltaic (solar cell), Photoconductive (LDR), Photodiode, Phototransistor. - Used in telemetry of flame monitoring, smoke detection, and optical data transmission. ## 2.2 Operating Principles — Step-by-Step Logic **Step 1:** The measurand (e.g., temperature) acts on the sensing element (e.g., Pt wire in RTD). **Step 2:** The sensing element changes an electrical property ($R$, $L$, $C$, or generates a voltage/charge). **Step 3:** The change is converted into a proportional voltage/current using a **Wheatstone bridge** or dedicated conditioning circuit. **Step 4:** The conditioned signal is filtered, amplified, and linearized (for non-linear transducers like thermistor). **Step 5:** The output is fed to the telemetry encoder (PAM/PWM/PPM/PCM) for transmission. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table is the **must-memorize** formula bank for KTU University Exams on this topic. Note the use of `\vert` to prevent markdown table corruption. | # | Transducer / Concept | Governing Equation | Units | Remarks | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Gauge Factor (Strain Gauge)** | $G_f = \dfrac{\Delta R / R}{\Delta L / L} = 1 + 2\nu + \dfrac{\Delta\rho/\rho}{\varepsilon}$ | Dimensionless | Typical: 2.0 to 2.2 for constantan | | 2 | **RTD Resistance** | $R_T = R_0 \left[ 1 + \alpha T + \beta T^2 \right]$ | $\Omega$ | $\alpha$ = temperature coefficient | | 3 | **Thermistor (β-model)** | $R_T = R_0 \cdot e^{\beta \left( \dfrac{1}{T} - \dfrac{1}{T_0} \right)}$ | $\Omega$ | $\beta$ typically 3000–5000 K | | 4 | **LVDT Output (RMS)** | $V_{out} = K \cdot x$ | Volt | Linear for the central region | | 5 | **Capacitive Transducer** | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$ | Farad | $d$ varies for displacement | | 6 | **Piezoelectric Charge** | $Q = d \cdot F$ | Coulomb | $d$ in C/N | | 7 | **Piezoelectric Voltage** | $V = \dfrac{d \cdot F}{C_p}$ | Volt | $C_p$ = crystal capacitance | | 8 | **Wheatstone Bridge Output** | $V_{out} = V_{ex} \left( \dfrac{R_1}{R_1+R_2} - \dfrac{R_3}{R_3+R_4} \right)$ | Volt | $V_{ex}$ = excitation voltage | | 9 | **Thermocouple (Seebeck)** | $V = S \cdot \Delta T$ | Volt | $S$ = Seebeck coefficient (μV/°C) | | 10 | **Pressure (Strain Gauge)** | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{6 \cdot P \cdot r^2 \cdot (1-\nu)}{E \cdot t^2}$ | Dimensionless | $r$ = diaphragm radius, $t$ = thickness | | 11 | **Sensitivity** | $S = \dfrac{\Delta y_{out}}{\Delta x_{in}}$ | Output / Input unit | Slope of calibration curve | | 12 | **Resolution** | Smallest detectable change in input | Same as measurand | Limited by noise and bit-size | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Aerospace Telemetry:** Piezoelectric sensors on engine turbine blades measure vibration; RTDs measure exhaust gas temperature. - **Biomedical Telemetry:** LVDT measures limb displacement in rehabilitation robotics; photodetectors measure pulse oximetry. - **Industrial Process Telemetry:** Strain gauges on weighing scales; capacitive sensors in level measurement of chemical tanks. - **Spacecraft:** Thermocouples and RTDs are the **only** temperature sensors certified for space missions due to wide range and reliability. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!NOTE] > This section provides the exhaustive mathematical derivations that KTU examiners expect in **14-mark derivations**. Every algebraic transition is shown explicitly. ## 3.1 Derivation: Gauge Factor of a Strain Gauge **Starting Point:** The resistance of a uniform wire is given by $$R = \rho \cdot \dfrac{L}{A}$$ where $\rho$ is the resistivity, $L$ is the length, and $A$ is the cross-sectional area. **Step 1 — Taking the differential (small-signal) change:** $$\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = \dfrac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} + \dfrac{\Delta L}{L} - \dfrac{\Delta A}{A}$$ The minus sign appears because $A$ is in the denominator, so a positive change in $A$ leads to a *decrease* in $R$. **Step 2 — Recognizing the geometric strain:** The mechanical strain along the wire is defined as $$\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L}$$ **Step 3 — Relating area change to Poisson's ratio $\nu$:** When the wire stretches, the cross-sectional area contracts. By definition of Poisson's ratio, $$\nu = -\dfrac{\Delta d / d}{\Delta L / L}$$ For a circular wire, $A = \pi d^2 / 4$, so $$\dfrac{\Delta A}{A} = 2 \cdot \dfrac{\Delta d}{d} = -2 \nu \cdot \dfrac{\Delta L}{L} = -2 \nu \cdot \varepsilon$$ **Step 4 — Substituting back into Step 1:** $$\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = \dfrac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} + \varepsilon - (-2 \nu \cdot \varepsilon)$$ $$\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = \dfrac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} + \varepsilon + 2 \nu \cdot \varepsilon$$ $$\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = (1 + 2\nu) \cdot \varepsilon + \dfrac{\Delta \rho}{\rho}$$ **Step 5 — Defining the Gauge Factor:** By definition, $$G_f = \dfrac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon} = \dfrac{\Delta R / R}{\Delta L / L}$$ Substituting, $$G_f = (1 + 2\nu) + \dfrac{\Delta \rho / \rho}{\varepsilon}$$ **Step 6 — Final Form:** $$\boxed{G_f = 1 + 2\nu + \dfrac{\Delta \rho / \rho}{\varepsilon}}$$ > **Physical Interpretation:** For most metals, the resistivity change term is small, so $G_f \approx 1 + 2\nu$. Since Poisson's ratio for constantan is ~0.33, $G_f \approx 1.66$ to 2.1 in practice. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: LVDT Output Voltage **Setup:** An LVDT has a primary winding with excitation $V_p$ (at frequency $f$) and two identical secondary windings, $S_1$ and $S_2$, wound in series opposition. A movable core induces EMFs $E_1$ and $E_2$ in the two secondaries. **Step 1 — Mutual Inductance Relations:** $$E_1 = j \omega M_1 \cdot \dfrac{V_p}{j \omega L_p} = \dfrac{M_1}{L_p} \cdot V_p$$ $$E_2 = j \omega M_2 \cdot \dfrac{V_p}{j \omega L_p} = \dfrac{M_2}{L_p} \cdot V_p$$ **Step 2 — Series Opposition Output:** $$V_{out} = E_1 - E_2 = \dfrac{V_p}{L_p} (M_1 - M_2)$$ **Step 3 — Symmetry Condition:** At the **null position** (core exactly centered), $M_1 = M_2$, so $V_{out} = 0$. **Step 4 — Linear Region Approximation:** For small displacement $x$ from the null: $$M_1 - M_2 = K \cdot x$$ Therefore, $$\boxed{V_{out} = \dfrac{V_p \cdot K}{L_p} \cdot x = K' \cdot x}$$ where $K'$ is the **LVDT sensitivity** (in V/mm or V/m). > **Key Insight:** The phase of $V_{out}$ tells the **direction** of displacement (whether core moved left or right). KTU examiners often ask: "How does an LVDT indicate the direction of motion?" The answer is the **phase-sensitive demodulation** of $V_{out}$ relative to $V_p$. --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Thermistor Equation (β-Model) **Step 1 — Empirical starting point:** The resistance of a semiconductor thermistor follows an exponential law with absolute temperature: $$R_T = A \cdot e^{B/T}$$ where $A$ and $B$ are device constants, and $T$ is in Kelvin. **Step 2 — Reference point:** At a known reference temperature $T_0$, the resistance is $R_0$: $$R_0 = A \cdot e^{B/T_0}$$ **Step 3 — Eliminating the constant A:** Dividing the two equations: $$\dfrac{R_T}{R_0} = \dfrac{A \cdot e^{B/T}}{A \cdot e^{B/T_0}} = e^{B(1/T - 1/T_0)}$$ **Step 4 — Solving for $R_T$:** $$\boxed{R_T = R_0 \cdot e^{\beta \left( \dfrac{1}{T} - \dfrac{1}{T_0} \right)}}$$ where $\beta = B$ is the material constant (in Kelvin), typically **3000 K to 5000 K** for commercial thermistors. > **Steinhart-Hart Equation** (the more accurate 3-parameter form used in practice): > $$\dfrac{1}{T} = a + b \ln(R_T) + c \left[ \ln(R_T) \right]^3$$ --- ## 3.4 Derivation: Wheatstone Bridge Sensitivity for a Strain Gauge **Step 1 — Balanced Bridge Condition:** Output $V_{out} = 0$ when $$\dfrac{R_1}{R_2} = \dfrac{R_3}{R_4}$$ **Step 2 — Unbalanced Output for a Single Active Arm:** Let only $R_1$ change by $\Delta R$ (e.g., a strain gauge), while $R_2 = R_3 = R_4 = R$. $$V_{out} = V_{ex} \left( \dfrac{R + \Delta R}{2R + \Delta R} - \dfrac{R}{2R} \right)$$ $$V_{out} = V_{ex} \left( \dfrac{R + \Delta R}{2R + \Delta R} - \dfrac{1}{2} \right)$$ **Step 3 — Simplification:** $$V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta R}{2(2R + \Delta R)}$$ **Step 4 — Approximation for small $\Delta R$ ($\Delta R \ll 2R$):** $$V_{out} \approx V_{ex} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta R}{4R}$$ **Step 5 — Substituting Gauge Factor:** Since $\Delta R / R = G_f \cdot \varepsilon$, $$\boxed{V_{out} \approx \dfrac{V_{ex} \cdot G_f \cdot \varepsilon}{4}}$$ > **Enhancement:** Using **all four arms as active strain gauges** (a *full bridge*) multiplies this output by 4, giving $V_{out} \approx V_{ex} \cdot G_f \cdot \varepsilon$ — a critical board-exam point. ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Selection Criteria | Criterion | Best Choice | Justification | |---|---|---| | Static temperature, $-200°C$ to $+850°C$, high accuracy | **Platinum RTD (Pt100)** | Linear, stable, wide range | | High sensitivity over narrow range, $-50°C$ to $+150°C$ | **NTC Thermistor** | High $\beta$ value, low cost | | Very high temperature ($>1000°C$), furnace monitoring | **Thermocouple (Type K / Type S)** | Wide range, rugged | | Static and dynamic displacement, contactless | **LVDT** | Infinite resolution, frictionless | | Dynamic pressure / vibration only | **Piezoelectric** | High bandwidth, active output | | Small displacement, high sensitivity | **Capacitive** | Sub-micron resolution | | Weight / force, structural load | **Strain Gauge (full bridge)** | High accuracy, linear | | Flow in clean liquid, low cost | **Turbine flowmeter** | Pulses proportional to flow | | Light / flame detection | **Photodiode / LDR** | Spectral response, fast | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics > [!NOTE] > The diagrams below use **Mermaid block diagrams** to map the functional architecture of telemetry transducer subsystems, as per the V10 protocol for engineering topics. ## 4.1 Block Diagram: Generalized Telemetry Transducer Subsystem ```mermaid flowchart LR A[PHYSICAL QUANTITY
Temperature, Pressure,
Displacement, Flow] --> B[SENSING ELEMENT
RTD, Diaphragm, Core,
Bimetallic Strip] B --> C[TRANSDUCTION ELEMENT
R, L, C Change or
EMF Generation] C --> D[SIGNAL CONDITIONING
Wheatstone Bridge,
Amplifier, Filter] D --> E[ELECTRICAL OUTPUT
Voltage, Current, mV] E --> F[TELEMETRY ENCODER
PAM, PWM, PPM, PCM] F --> G[TRANSMITTER
RF or Wired Link] G --> H[RECEIVER / DISPLAY
Remote Station] style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000 style B fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4,color:#000 style C fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22,color:#000 style D fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B,color:#FFF style E fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#BDB76B,color:#000 style F fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C,color:#000 style G fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#1E90FF,color:#000 style H fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram: LVDT Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB A[EXCITATION SOURCE
1 kHz to 10 kHz
AC Sine Wave] --> B[PRIMARY WINDING
L_p Turns on Former] B --> C[MAGNETIC FLUX
Couples to S1 and S2] C --> D1[SECONDARY S1
E1 Induced EMF] C --> D2[SECONDARY S2
E2 Induced EMF] D1 --> E[SERIES OPPOSITION
Vout = E1 - E2] D2 --> E E --> F[PHASE SENSITIVE
DEMODULATOR] F --> G[DC AMPLIFIER] G --> H[OUTPUT VOLTAGE
Proportional to Displacement] CORE[SOFT IRON CORE
Attached to Measurand] -.->|Displaces| B CORE -.->|Displaces| C style A fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#DAA520,color:#000 style B fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#DC143C,color:#000 style C fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#9370DB,color:#000 style D1 fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4,color:#000 style D2 fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4,color:#000 style E fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22,color:#000 style F fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B,color:#FFF style G fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#BDB76B,color:#000 style H fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000 style CORE fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#C71585,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Block Diagram: Strain Gauge Bridge in a Telemetry Pressure Sensor ```mermaid flowchart LR A[PRESSURE P
on Diaphragm] --> B[ELASTIC DEFORMATION
Microstrain epsilon] B --> C[STRAIN GAUGE 1
Top of Diaphragm
Tensile Strain] B --> D[STRAIN GAUGE 2
Bottom of Diaphragm
Compressive Strain] C --> E[WHEATSTONE BRIDGE

AMPLIFIER AD620] F --> G[ADC

PCM Stream] H --> I((RF TRANSMITTER)) style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000 style B fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#C71585,color:#000 style C fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4,color:#000 style D fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4,color:#000 style E fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22,color:#000 style F fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B,color:#FFF style G fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#BDB76B,color:#000 style H fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#FF4500,color:#000 style I fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#1E90FF,color:#000 ``` ## 4.4 Block Diagram: Comparative Classification of Telemetry Transducers ```mermaid flowchart TB ROOT[TRANSDUCERS FOR TELEMETRY] ROOT --> ACTIVE[ACTIVE TRANSDUCERS
Self Generating] ROOT --> PASSIVE[PASSIVE TRANSDUCERS

Dynamic Pressure, Vibration] ACTIVE --> A2[THERMOCOUPLE
High Temperature] ACTIVE --> A3[PHOTOVOLTAIC
Light Intensity] ACTIVE --> A4[TACHOGENERATOR
Angular Velocity] PASSIVE --> P1[RESISTIVE TYPE] PASSIVE --> P2[INDUCTIVE TYPE] PASSIVE --> P3[CAPACITIVE TYPE] P1 --> P1A[Potentiometer] P1 --> P1B[Strain Gauge] P1 --> P1C[RTD] P1 --> P1D[Thermistor] P1 --> P1E[LDR] P2 --> P2A[LVDT] P2 --> P2B[Variable Reluctance] P2 --> P2C[Eddy Current Sensor] P3 --> P3A[Variable d Type] P3 --> P3B[Variable A Type] P3 --> P3C[Dielectric Variation] style ROOT fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000 style ACTIVE fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000 style PASSIVE fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#FF4500,color:#000 style A1 fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B,color:#000 style A2 fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B,color:#000 style A3 fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B,color:#000 style A4 fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B,color:#000 style P1 fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#DC143C,color:#000 style P2 fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#DC143C,color:#000 style P3 fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#DC143C,color:#000 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **Differentiate between Active and Passive transducers. Give two examples of each.** **Model Answer:** > **Active Transducers:** Generate their own electrical output (voltage or current) directly from the measurand, without requiring any external excitation source. They are also called *self-generating transducers*. > Examples: (i) Thermocouple — converts temperature difference into EMF via the **Seebeck effect**. (ii) Piezoelectric crystal — converts mechanical stress into electric charge via the **piezoelectric effect**. > > **Passive Transducers:** Require an external excitation (AC or DC) to operate. The measurand changes an internal parameter (R, L, or C), which modulates the excitation signal. > Examples: (i) **RTD (Pt100)** — resistance changes with temperature. (ii) **LVDT** — inductance changes with core position. > > *Keyword differences:* Energy conversion (Active) vs Energy modulation (Passive). **(3 Marks)** ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2023]** **Why is a Piezoelectric transducer not suitable for measuring static pressure? Explain briefly.** **Model Answer:** > A piezoelectric crystal generates an electric charge $Q$ only when it experiences a **change** in mechanical stress. Under a perfectly static (constant) load, the induced charge slowly **leaks away** through the finite input resistance of the connected amplifier and through the internal leakage of the crystal itself. The time constant of this discharge is on the order of milliseconds to seconds, which is why the output drifts to zero even when a constant force is applied. > > Therefore, piezoelectric transducers are restricted to **dynamic measurements** such as vibration, shock, and rapidly varying pressure pulses, where the signal frequency is well above the discharge rate. **(3 Marks)** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice) ### **OPTION A — Question A (14 Marks)** > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 | Module 3]** > **(a)** Derive the expression for the **Gauge Factor** of a metallic strain gauge. Mention the typical range of gauge factor for constantan. > **(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **construction and working of an LVDT**. Derive the expression for its output voltage and discuss how the direction of displacement is detected. **Model Solution:** **(a) Gauge Factor Derivation (7 Marks)** The full derivation is as shown in **Section 3.1** of these notes: 1. Start with $R = \rho L / A$. 2. Differentiate to obtain $\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = \dfrac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} + \dfrac{\Delta L}{L} - \dfrac{\Delta A}{A}$. 3. Use the strain definition $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L$ and the area-strain relation $\Delta A / A = -2\nu \varepsilon$ (from Poisson's ratio). 4. Substitute to obtain $G_f = (1 + 2\nu) + (\Delta \rho / \rho) / \varepsilon$. $$\boxed{G_f = 1 + 2\nu + \dfrac{\Delta \rho / \rho}{\varepsilon}}$$ **Typical values:** For **constantan** (an alloy of Cu and Ni), $G_f \approx 2.0$ to $2.2$. For **semiconductor** strain gauges, $G_f$ can be as high as **100 to 200**, but with poor temperature stability. **(b) LVDT Construction, Working and Derivation (7 Marks)** **Construction:** - One **primary winding** (P) is centrally located on a hollow cylindrical former. - Two **identical secondary windings** ($S_1$ and $S_2$) are wound on either side of the primary, with equal number of turns and identical geometry. - A movable **soft-iron core** is attached to the measurand (the object whose displacement is to be measured). - The whole assembly is enclosed in a shielded metal housing. **Working:** - The primary is excited by a stable AC source (typically **1 kHz to 10 kHz** sinusoidal voltage $V_p$). - An alternating flux is produced, which links with both secondaries. - EMFs $E_1$ and $E_2$ are induced in $S_1$ and $S_2$, respectively. - The secondaries are connected in **series opposition**, so the differential output is $V_{out} = E_1 - E_2$. **Null Position:** When the core is exactly centered, $E_1 = E_2$ and $V_{out} = 0$. **Displaced Position:** When the core moves toward $S_1$ by a small distance $x$, $M_1$ increases and $M_2$ decreases, so $V_{out}$ becomes positive. Conversely, if the core moves toward $S_2$, $V_{out}$ is negative. **Derivation (as in Section 3.2):** $$V_{out} = \dfrac{V_p}{L_p}(M_1 - M_2) = K' \cdot x$$ where $K'$ is the LVDT sensitivity in V/mm. **Direction Detection:** A **phase-sensitive demodulator** compares the phase of $V_{out}$ with the phase of the primary excitation $V_p$. - If $V_{out}$ is **in phase** with $V_p$ → core moved toward $S_1$ (positive $x$). - If $V_{out}$ is **180° out of phase** with $V_p$ → core moved toward $S_2$ (negative $x$). The DC output after demodulation carries both the **magnitude** (via amplitude) and **direction** (via sign/polarity) of the displacement. --- ### **OPTION A — Question B (14 Marks — Internal Choice Alternative)** > **[KTU University Exam - June 2024 | Module 3]** > **(a)** Explain the **working principle of a Piezoelectric transducer** with a suitable diagram. Derive the expression for charge and voltage generated. > **(b)** Compare **RTD, Thermistor, and Thermocouple** as temperature transducers. State one application of each. **Model Solution:** **(a) Piezoelectric Transducer (7 Marks)** **Principle:** Certain anisotropic crystals (Quartz, Rochelle Salt, PZT — Lead Zirconate Titanate) exhibit the **direct piezoelectric effect** — when subjected to a mechanical stress, electric dipoles within the crystal align, producing a net charge on the crystal faces. Conversely, applying a voltage deforms the crystal (**converse piezoelectric effect**). **Diagram Description:** A piezoelectric crystal is sandwiched between two metallic electrodes. The measurand (force or pressure) acts on a diaphragm that presses on the top electrode, compressing the crystal. The induced charge $Q$ appears on the electrodes, and is measured by a high-input-impedance charge amplifier. **Derivation of Charge:** The charge generated is directly proportional to the applied force: $$\boxed{Q = d \cdot F}$$ where $d$ is the **piezoelectric charge sensitivity** (in C/N). - For Quartz: $d \approx 2.3 \times 10^{-12}$ C/N - For PZT: $d \approx 250 \times 10^{-12}$ C/N (PZT is far more sensitive) **Derivation of Voltage:** The voltage across the crystal is the charge divided by the crystal's self-capacitance $C_p$: $$\boxed{V = \dfrac{Q}{C_p} = \dfrac{d \cdot F}{C_p}}$$ where $C_p = \varepsilon A / t$ for crystal area $A$ and thickness $t$. **Equivalent Model:** The crystal can be modelled as a **voltage source $V$ in series with capacitance $C_p$** and a very high leakage resistance $R$. **Limitations:** 1. **Cannot measure static (DC) quantities** — charge leaks away. 2. Requires a **charge amplifier** with very high input impedance ($>10^{12}$ Ω). 3. Temperature sensitive (must use crystal cuts with low temperature coefficient). **Applications:** Vibration analysis, ultrasonic transducers, accelerometers, microphones, dynamic pressure measurement in jet engines. **(b) Comparison Table of Temperature Transducers (7 Marks)** | Parameter | RTD (Pt100) | Thermistor (NTC) | Thermocouple (Type K) | |---|---|---|---| | **Principle** | Resistance change of pure metal | Resistance change of semiconductor | Seebeck EMF at junction of two dissimilar metals | | **Active / Passive** | Passive | Passive | **Active** | | **Range** | $-200°C$ to $+850°C$ | $-50°C$ to $+150°C$ | $-200°C$ to $+1260°C$ | | **Sensitivity** | ~0.385 Ω/°C (Pt) | ~4% per °C (very high) | ~40 μV/°C (low) | | **Linearity** | Excellent (linear) | Highly non-linear | Reasonably linear over narrow range | | **Accuracy** | High (±0.1°C) | Moderate (±0.5°C) | Lower (±2°C) | | **Self-heating** | Low | Significant (must minimize) | Negligible | | **Cost** | High | Low | Very low | | **Application Example** | Laboratory thermometry, RTD-based industrial transmitters | Inlet air temperature in HVAC, medical thermometers | Furnace temperature in steel plant, exhaust gas temperature monitoring | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **LVDT null position:** Students often forget to state that the two secondaries are *identical* and connected in *series opposition* — losing 2 marks. > 2. **Piezoelectric static measurement:** Many students incorrectly say "piezoelectric cannot measure static" *without explaining the leakage mechanism*. The examiner expects the *charge-discharge time constant* reasoning. Lose 1 mark if you skip this. > 3. **Gauge Factor:** Forgetting the **Poisson's ratio term** in the derivation is a classic 2-mark deduction. Always derive the area-strain relation explicitly. > 4. **Active vs Passive confusion:** Thermocouple is **active**, RTD is **passive** — students frequently swap them. > 5. **RTD formula:** Some students write $R_T = R_0 (1 + \alpha T)$ — this is the *linear approximation* and is acceptable for narrow ranges. The KTU Board expects the *Callendar-Van Dusen* form $R_T = R_0 [1 + \alpha T + \beta T^2]$ for full credit. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exam:** - **Definition:** Transducer = energy converter; in telemetry, it converts a non-electrical measurand into an electrical signal. - **Active Transducers:** Self-generating, no external power needed — *Thermocouple, Piezoelectric, Photovoltaic, Tachogenerator*. - **Passive Transducers:** Need external excitation — *RTD, Thermistor, Strain Gauge, LVDT, Capacitive sensor*. - **Gauge Factor:** $G_f = 1 + 2\nu + (\Delta\rho/\rho)/\varepsilon$; typical constantan value is **~2.0–2.2**. - **Strain Gauge Bridge:** Single active arm gives $V_{out} \approx V_{ex} G_f \varepsilon / 4$; **full bridge gives 4× the output** — a board-favourite derivation. - **LVDT:** AC-excited, two secondaries in series opposition, $V_{out} = K' \cdot x$, phase-sensitive demodulation gives **direction**. - **Piezoelectric:** Active, dynamic-only, $Q = dF$, $V = dF/C_p$. **Cannot measure DC** due to charge leakage. - **RTD:** Passive, linear, wide range, $R_T = R_0[1 + \alpha T + \beta T^2]$, platinum is industry standard. - **Thermistor:** Passive, **NTC**, exponential, $R_T = R_0 e^{\beta(1/T - 1/T_0)}$, very sensitive but non-linear. - **Thermocouple:** Active, $V = S \cdot \Delta T$, widest temperature range, used in furnaces and jet engines. - **Capacitive:** $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d$, very high sensitivity, ideal for micro-displacement. - **Wheatstone Bridge:** Fundamental conditioning circuit; balance condition is $R_1/R_2 = R_3/R_4$. - **Telemetry signal chain:** Physical Quantity → Sensor → Bridge/Conditioner → Amplifier → Encoder (PAM/PWM/PCM) → Transmitter → Channel → Receiver. - **Selection rule of thumb:** Static measurement → RTD or LVDT; Dynamic measurement → Piezoelectric; Wide range → Thermocouple; Narrow range, high sensitivity → Thermistor. - **Key constant to memorize:** Seebeck coefficient of **Chromel-Alumel (Type K) ≈ 40 μV/°C**; Pt100 nominal resistance at 0°C is **100 Ω**. - **Examiner tip:** Always state the *units* of every quantity and the *assumptions* (e.g., small-signal approximation, constant temperature) made in derivations.

topics.title: Methods of Improving Signal to Noise Ratio - Signal Filters - Signal Averaging - Box Car Integration - Signal Correlation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT634 - COMMUNICATION SYSTEM AND TELEMETRY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Methods of Improving Signal to Noise Ratio - Signal Filters - Signal Averaging - Box Car Integration - Signal Correlation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Fundamental Problem: Signal Buried in Noise

In any practical telemetry or communication system, the **information-bearing signal** arriving at the receiver is always contaminated by unwanted random fluctuations known as **noise**. The figure of merit used to quantify how badly the signal is corrupted is the **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)**:

$$
\text{SNR} = \frac{\text{Signal Power } (S)}{\text{Noise Power } (N)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus-aligned):** *Methods of Improving SNR* refer to the systematic signal-conditioning techniques applied at the receiver front-end or in digital post-processing to enhance the amplitude of the deterministic signal component relative to the stochastic noise component, thereby increasing the probability of correct signal detection and reducing the Bit Error Rate (BER).

The SNR is universally expressed in **decibels (dB)** as:

$$
\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{S}{N}\right)
$$

and the **Noise Figure (F)** of a system is directly linked to the SNR degradation through the cascade:

$$
F = \frac{\text{SNR}_{\text{in}}}{\text{SNR}_{\text{out}}} \geq 1
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The four classical techniques prescribed in the KTU 2024 Scheme Module 3 syllabus for SNR enhancement are:
> 1. **Signal Filtering** (frequency-domain discrimination)
> 2. **Signal Averaging** (time-domain ensemble averaging)
> 3. **Box-Car Integration** (gated sample-and-hold averaging)
> 4. **Signal Correlation** (autocorrelation and cross-correlation including the Lock-in Amplifier)

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Voice in a Stadium"

Imagine you are trying to hear your friend's voice (**signal**) in a packed football stadium full of 50,000 screaming fans (**noise**). The friend-to-crowd loudness ratio is terrible — your **SNR is low**.

How can you "improve the SNR" using only physical techniques?

- **Method 1 — Signal Filtering (Cupping your hands around your ears):** Your biological ears act like a **band-pass filter** — they reject very low frequencies (the rumble of the stadium) and very high frequencies (the whistle shrills), letting through only the band where the human voice lives. **Frequency-selective rejection of noise.**
- **Method 2 — Signal Averaging (Asking the friend to repeat the message 100 times):** Each time your friend says "I'll meet you at gate 5", you mentally add up the consistent words. The words **add coherently** (10 times repetition → 10× louder), but the random crowd noise around each word is different every time, so noise **adds only as RMS** (10× repetition → √10 ≈ 3.16× louder). Net **SNR improves by √N ≈ √100 = 10 times**.
- **Method 3 — Box-Car Integration (Listening only during specific "windows"):** You know your friend only speaks during the half-time whistle. You ignore everything outside this gate window. This **gated time-averaging** rejects noise outside the active signal window.
- **Method 4 — Signal Correlation (Knowing your friend's accent and speech pattern):** Even in a noisy room, a brain trained on your friend's voice can pick out subtle correlations that differentiate it from random noise. The mathematical equivalents — **autocorrelation and cross-correlation** — exploit the deterministic, repeatable structure of the signal versus the random nature of noise.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for KTU exam:** **"F-A-B-C"** → **F**ilters, **A**veraging, **B**ox-car, **C**orrelation.

---

## 1.3 The Taxonomy of Noise Sources

Before improving SNR, we must know what we are fighting. The principal noise sources in a telemetry receiver are:

| Noise Type | Physical Origin | Power Spectral Density |
|---|---|---|
| **Thermal (Johnson) Noise** | Random motion of electrons in resistors | $S_n(f) = k_B T$ (flat, white) |
| **Shot Noise** | Discrete electron charges across PN junctions | $S_n(f) = 2qI_{\text{dc}}$ (white) |
| **Flicker (1/f) Noise** | Trap states in semiconductors | $S_n(f) \propto \dfrac{K}{f^{\alpha}}$ |
| **Quantization Noise** | ADC rounding in digital systems | Uniform over Nyquist band |

where the **Boltzmann constant** $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K and the **electronic charge** $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C are the key physical constants.

---

## 1.4 Why "Improving SNR" is the Heart of Telemetry

In telemetry, the information from a remote sensor (temperature, pressure, flow, biomedical ECG, satellite downlink) travels through a hostile channel. The Shannon-Hartley theorem sets a hard ceiling:

$$
C = B \log_{2}\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) \;\text{bits/sec}
$$

Improving SNR by just **3 dB** (a factor of 2) can mean the difference between a usable and unusable telemetry link. Hence, every decibel recovered through filtering, averaging, or correlation is **engineering gold**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Method 1 — Signal Filtering for SNR Improvement

### 2.1.1 The Core Principle

A **filter** is a linear, time-invariant two-port network that transmits signal energy in a specified frequency band while attenuating energy outside it. When the signal spectrum $S_s(f)$ and the noise spectrum $S_n(f)$ occupy **different frequency regions**, a properly designed filter can suppress noise with minimal signal loss.

**General Output SNR after Filtering:**

$$
\left(\frac{S}{N}\right)_{\text{out}} = \frac{\displaystyle\int_{0}^{\infty} S_s(f)\,\vert H(f)\vert^{2}\, df}{\displaystyle\int_{0}^{\infty} S_n(f)\,\vert H(f)\vert^{2}\, df}
$$

where $H(f)$ is the filter's transfer function magnitude response.

### 2.1.2 Classification of Filters

- **Low-Pass Filter (LPF):** Passes $f \leq f_c$; rejects $f > f_c$. Used to recover slow-varying telemetry signals.
- **High-Pass Filter (HPF):** Passes $f \geq f_c$; rejects $f < f_c$. Used to remove DC drift and 1/f noise.
- **Band-Pass Filter (BPF):** Passes $f_L \leq f \leq f_H$. The workhorse of telemetry IF stages.
- **Band-Stop (Notch) Filter:** Rejects a narrow band. Used to remove 50 Hz power-line hum.

### 2.1.3 The Ideal Filter and the Noise Bandwidth

For an **ideal brick-wall LPF** with cutoff $f_c$, the noise bandwidth equals the signal bandwidth ($B_n = B_s = f_c$), so the SNR is unchanged. Real filters have **rounded edges** and **finite roll-off**, giving a noise bandwidth $B_n > B_s$.

**Definition of Noise Bandwidth:**

$$
B_n = \frac{1}{\vert H(f_0)\vert^{2}} \int_{0}^{\infty} \vert H(f)\vert^{2}\, df
$$

**SNR Improvement by Filtering (white noise case):**

$$
I_{\text{filter}} = \frac{(S/N)_{\text{out}}}{(S/N)_{\text{in}}} = \frac{B_{\text{in}}}{B_{\text{out}}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** To maximize SNR improvement via filtering, the filter's noise bandwidth $B_n$ must be **just wide enough** to pass the signal spectrum with minimum distortion, but **no wider**. This is why optimal telemetry receivers use **matched filters**.

### 2.1.4 The Matched Filter — The Optimum Linear Filter

For a known signal $s(t)$ of duration $T$ corrupted by white Gaussian noise, the filter that **maximizes the output SNR** at the sampling instant $t = T$ has impulse response:

$$
h(t) = s(T - t)
$$

i.e., a time-reversed and delayed copy of the signal itself. Its maximum output SNR is:

$$
\text{SNR}_{\max} = \frac{2E_s}{N_0}
$$

where $E_s = \int_{0}^{T} s^{2}(t)\, dt$ is the signal energy and $N_0$ is the noise PSD.

---

## 2.2 Method 2 — Signal Averaging

### 2.2.1 The Underlying Principle

When the signal is **repetitive** (e.g., ECG, evoked potentials, pulsed radar returns) but buried in noise, we can acquire $N$ successive sweeps, digitize them, and **coherently add** them point-by-point in a computer memory. The signal adds linearly, while the **random noise adds in RMS**, yielding a √N improvement in SNR.

### 2.2.2 Mathematical Justification

Let $x_i(t) = s(t) + n_i(t)$ be the $i$-th sweep. The averaged output is:

$$
\bar{x}(t) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i(t) = s(t) + \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} n_i(t)
$$

Because $E\{n_i(t)\} = 0$ and noise samples are uncorrelated:

$$
E\!\left\{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} n_i(t)\right\} = 0, \quad \text{Var}\!\left(\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} n_i(t)\right) = \frac{\sigma_n^{2}}{N}
$$

Hence:

$$
\left(\frac{S}{N}\right)_{\text{out}} = \frac{\text{Var}(s)}{\sigma_n^{2}/N} = N \cdot \left(\frac{S}{N}\right)_{\text{in}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Power-SNR Improvement Factor = N, Voltage-SNR Improvement Factor = √N.** KTU exams almost always expect the **voltage (amplitude) SNR improvement = √N**, since signals and noise are typically measured as amplitudes/RMS voltages.

### 2.2.3 Requirements for Valid Averaging

1. Signal must be **strictly periodic** with known period.
2. The trigger point (sweep start) must be **synchronized** with the signal (jitter < 1% of pulse width).
3. Noise must be **stationary, zero-mean, and uncorrelated** between sweeps.
4. Sufficient ADC resolution (typically 12–16 bit) to capture growing signal amplitude.

---

## 2.3 Method 3 — Box-Car Integration (Gated Averager)

### 2.3.1 Concept

A **Box-Car Integrator** is a sample-and-hold circuit that opens a **narrow time gate** of width $T_g$ at a known delay $t_d$ after the trigger, samples the input waveform during this gate, and **averages** the samples. It is the **time-domain analog** of a narrow band-pass filter in the frequency domain.

The signal must be repetitive. The integrator output after $n$ sweeps:

$$
V_{\text{out}}(t) = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{k=1}^{n} V_{\text{in}}(t, k)
$$

### 2.3.2 Transfer Function

The frequency-domain response of an ideal box-car gate of width $T_g$ is a **sinc function**:

$$
H(f) = \frac{\sin(\pi f T_g)}{\pi f} = T_g \cdot \text{sinc}(f T_g)
$$

The **equivalent noise bandwidth** of the box-car averager is:

$$
B_n = \frac{1}{2 T_g}
$$

**SNR Improvement Factor:**

$$
I_{\text{boxcar}} = \sqrt{\frac{T_g}{\Delta t}} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad I_{\text{boxcar}} = \sqrt{m}
$$

where $m$ is the number of independent samples within the gate and $\Delta t$ is the sample interval.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Box-Car vs. Signal Averaging:** A box-car integrator averages within a single gate window of one sweep. A signal averager averages across multiple full sweeps. They are mathematically **dual** operations.

---

## 2.4 Method 4 — Signal Correlation

### 2.4.1 Autocorrelation Function

The autocorrelation $R_{xx}(\tau)$ of a signal $x(t)$ is a measure of how similar the signal is to a time-shifted version of itself:

$$
R_{xx}(\tau) = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} x(t)\, x(t + \tau)\, dt
$$

For a **noise process**, $R_{nn}(\tau) \to 0$ as $\tau \to \infty$ (decorrelates). For a **periodic signal**, $R_{ss}(\tau)$ is itself periodic.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Theorem:** Autocorrelation extracts a periodic signal buried in noise. The output SNR improves linearly with the integration time $T$:
> $$\text{SNR}_{\text{out}} \propto \frac{T}{B_n}$$

### 2.4.2 Cross-Correlation Function

$$
R_{xy}(\tau) = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} x(t)\, y(t + \tau)\, dt
$$

where $x(t) = s(t) + n(t)$ is the noisy received signal and $y(t)$ is a **locally generated reference** signal known to be a copy of $s(t)$.

- $R_{xy}(\tau)$ peaks sharply when $y(t)$ is aligned with $s(t)$.
- $R_{ny}(\tau) \approx 0$ if noise and reference are uncorrelated.
- Hence cross-correlation recovers the signal from deep noise.

### 2.4.3 The Lock-In Amplifier — The Ultimate Correlator

A lock-in amplifier multiplies the noisy input by a reference signal at frequency $f_{\text{ref}}$ (from a chopper or modulator) and low-pass filters the product. The output is proportional to the amplitude of the signal component **exactly at $f_{\text{ref}}$**, rejecting all noise at other frequencies.

**Equivalent Noise Bandwidth of Lock-In:**

$$
B_n = \frac{1}{2 T_c}
$$

where $T_c$ is the time constant (LPF cutoff $\approx 1/(2\pi T_c)$).

**Lock-In Dynamic Reserve:**

$$
\text{DR (dB)} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\text{Largest Noise}}{\text{Full-Scale Signal}}\right)
$$

A high-quality lock-in can recover signals **120 dB below** the noise floor — equivalent to hearing a whisper across a jet engine.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the EXAM-CRITICAL formula bank for Module 3. Memorize all rows.**

| # | Technique | SNR Improvement Formula | Key Variables |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Ideal Filtering (white noise) | $I_f = \dfrac{B_{\text{in}}}{B_{\text{out}}}$ | $B$ = bandwidth in Hz |
| 2 | Matched Filter (max SNR) | $\text{SNR}_{\max} = \dfrac{2 E_s}{N_0}$ | $E_s$ = signal energy, $N_0$ = noise PSD |
| 3 | Noise Bandwidth | $B_n = \dfrac{1}{\vert H(f_0)\vert^{2}}\displaystyle\int_{0}^{\infty}\vert H(f)\vert^{2}\, df$ | $H(f)$ = filter transfer function |
| 4 | Signal Averaging (Voltage) | $I_{\text{avg}} = \sqrt{N}$ | $N$ = number of sweeps |
| 5 | Signal Averaging (Power) | $I_{\text{avg}}^{(\text{power})} = N$ | $N$ = number of sweeps |
| 6 | Averaged Output | $\bar{x}(t) = \dfrac{1}{N}\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{N}[s(t) + n_i(t)]$ | — |
| 7 | Box-Car Transfer Function | $H(f) = T_g\,\text{sinc}(f T_g)$ | $T_g$ = gate width |
| 8 | Box-Car Noise Bandwidth | $B_n = \dfrac{1}{2 T_g}$ | $T_g$ in seconds |
| 9 | Box-Car SNR Improvement | $I_{bc} = \sqrt{m} = \sqrt{T_g / \Delta t}$ | $m$ = samples per gate |
| 10 | Autocorrelation | $R_{xx}(\tau) = \lim\limits_{T\to\infty} \dfrac{1}{T}\displaystyle\int_{0}^{T} x(t)\,x(t+\tau)\, dt$ | $\tau$ = lag variable |
| 11 | Cross-Correlation | $R_{xy}(\tau) = \lim\limits_{T\to\infty} \dfrac{1}{T}\displaystyle\int_{0}^{T} x(t)\,y(t+\tau)\, dt$ | $x, y$ = two signals |
| 12 | Lock-In Equivalent Bandwidth | $B_n = \dfrac{1}{2 T_c}$ | $T_c$ = time constant |
| 13 | Lock-In SNR Improvement | $I_{\text{LI}} = \dfrac{\sqrt{B_{\text{in}}}}{\sqrt{B_n}} = \sqrt{2 B_{\text{in}} T_c}$ | $B_{\text{in}}$ = pre-filter BW |
| 14 | Thermal Noise Power | $N = k_B T B$ | $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K |
| 15 | Noise Figure | $F = \dfrac{(S/N)_{\text{in}}}{(S/N)_{\text{out}}}$ | dimensionless, $\geq 1$ |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Medical Telemetry (ECG, EEG, evoked potentials):** Patient signals are buried in mains hum and muscle noise → **Signal Averaging** of 100–1000 sweeps is the clinical standard.
- **Satellite TT&C (Telemetry, Tracking, Command):** Downlink signals are at $-150$ dBm → **Matched filtering** and **Viterbi decoding** are mandatory.
- **LIGO Gravitational Wave Detection:** Strain noise is $10^{-21}$ — **cross-correlation** between the LIGO-Hanford and LIGO-Livingston detectors over years of data is required.
- **Industrial Process Telemetry:** Strain-gauge bridges on rotating shafts use **Lock-in amplifiers** to detect sub-microvolt signals at known modulation frequencies.
- **Radar and Lidar:** Range gating is precisely a **Box-Car integration** problem in the time domain.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — SNR Improvement by Signal Averaging

**Problem Statement:** A repetitive signal $s(t)$ of amplitude $A$ is corrupted by zero-mean white Gaussian noise of RMS amplitude $\sigma_n$. After averaging $N$ independent sweeps, find (a) the signal amplitude, (b) the noise RMS, and (c) the SNR improvement factor.

**Step 1 — Write the $i$-th sweep:**
The $i$-th noisy sweep is
$$
x_i(t) = s(t) + n_i(t), \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, N
$$

**Step 2 — Form the averaged signal:**
$$
\bar{x}(t) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i(t) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} s(t) + \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} n_i(t)
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the signal term:**
Since $s(t)$ is identical in every sweep (deterministic), it factors out:
$$
\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} s(t) = \frac{N \cdot s(t)}{N} = s(t)
$$
So the **signal is preserved exactly** in the average.

**Step 4 — Analyze the noise term's mean:**
Taking the expectation over the noise ensemble:
$$
E\!\left\{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} n_i(t)\right\} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} E\{n_i(t)\} = \frac{1}{N} \cdot N \cdot 0 = 0
$$
Mean of the averaged noise is zero.

**Step 5 — Compute the variance of the noise term:**
For independent noise samples with identical variance $\sigma_n^{2}$:
$$
\text{Var}\!\left(\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} n_i(t)\right) = \frac{1}{N^{2}}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\text{Var}(n_i) = \frac{1}{N^{2}} \cdot N \cdot \sigma_n^{2} = \frac{\sigma_n^{2}}{N}
$$

**Step 6 — RMS of the averaged noise:**
$$
\sigma_{n,\text{avg}} = \sqrt{\frac{\sigma_n^{2}}{N}} = \frac{\sigma_n}{\sqrt{N}}
$$

**Step 7 — Compute output SNR (voltage form):**
$$
\left(\frac{S}{N}\right)_{\text{out}} = \frac{A^{2}}{\sigma_{n,\text{avg}}^{2}} = \frac{A^{2}}{\sigma_n^{2}/N} = N \cdot \frac{A^{2}}{\sigma_n^{2}} = N \cdot \left(\frac{S}{N}\right)_{\text{in}}
$$

In voltage (amplitude) form, using RMS values $V_s = A/\sqrt{2}$ and $V_n = \sigma_n/\sqrt{2}$:

$$
\left(\frac{V_s}{V_n}\right)_{\text{out}} = \frac{V_s}{\sigma_n/\sqrt{N}} = \sqrt{N} \cdot \left(\frac{V_s}{V_n}\right)_{\text{in}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Result:**
> $$\boxed{\;I_{\text{avg}} = \frac{(S/N)_{\text{out}}}{(S/N)_{\text{in}}} = N \quad \text{(power)}, \qquad \sqrt{N} \quad \text{(voltage/RMS)}\;}$$

**In decibels:**
$$
\text{SNR Improvement (dB)} = 10 \log_{10}(N) \quad \text{(power basis)}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Box-Car Integrator Transfer Function

**Problem Statement:** Derive the frequency-domain transfer function $H(f)$ of an ideal rectangular gate (box-car) of width $T_g$ centered at $t = 0$, and hence find its equivalent noise bandwidth.

**Step 1 — Define the gate function in time:**
$$
g(t) = \begin{cases} 1, & -T_g/2 \leq t \leq +T_g/2 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

**Step 2 — Take the Fourier Transform:**
$$
H(f) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} g(t)\, e^{-j 2\pi f t}\, dt = \int_{-T_g/2}^{+T_g/2} e^{-j 2\pi f t}\, dt
$$

**Step 3 — Integrate:**
$$
H(f) = \left[\frac{e^{-j 2\pi f t}}{-j 2\pi f}\right]_{-T_g/2}^{+T_g/2} = \frac{e^{-j\pi f T_g} - e^{+j\pi f T_g}}{-j 2\pi f}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Euler's identity:**
Using $e^{j\theta} - e^{-j\theta} = 2j\sin\theta$:
$$
H(f) = \frac{-2j\sin(\pi f T_g)}{-j 2\pi f} = \frac{\sin(\pi f T_g)}{\pi f}
$$

**Step 5 — Normalize to the sinc form:**
$$
H(f) = T_g \cdot \frac{\sin(\pi f T_g)}{\pi f T_g} = T_g \cdot \text{sinc}(f T_g)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Result:**
> $$\boxed{\;H(f) = T_g \cdot \text{sinc}(f T_g)\;}$$

**Step 6 — Compute the noise bandwidth:**
The equivalent noise bandwidth is
$$
B_n = \frac{1}{\vert H(0)\vert^{2}} \int_{0}^{\infty} \vert H(f)\vert^{2}\, df = \frac{1}{T_g^{2}} \int_{0}^{\infty} T_g^{2} \,\text{sinc}^{2}(f T_g)\, df
$$

Using the standard integral $\displaystyle\int_{0}^{\infty} \text{sinc}^{2}(x)\, dx = \dfrac{1}{2}$ and substituting $u = f T_g$, $du = T_g\, df$:
$$
B_n = \int_{0}^{\infty} \text{sinc}^{2}(f T_g)\, df = \frac{1}{T_g} \int_{0}^{\infty} \text{sinc}^{2}(u)\, du = \frac{1}{T_g} \cdot \frac{1}{2} = \frac{1}{2 T_g}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Result:**
> $$\boxed{\;B_n^{\text{boxcar}} = \frac{1}{2 T_g}\;}$$

This shows **directly** that a narrow gate → small noise bandwidth → large SNR improvement. ✅

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — SNR Improvement by Cross-Correlation (Lock-In)

**Problem Statement:** A signal $s(t) = A \sin(2\pi f_0 t)$ is corrupted by wideband noise $n(t)$ of PSD $N_0/2$. The signal is multiplied by a reference $r(t) = \sin(2\pi f_0 t)$ and low-pass filtered with cutoff $f_c$. Find the output SNR.

**Step 1 — Form the multiplier output:**
$$
v(t) = [s(t) + n(t)]\cdot r(t) = A \sin^{2}(2\pi f_0 t) + n(t)\sin(2\pi f_0 t)
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the trigonometric identity $\sin^{2}\theta = \dfrac{1-\cos 2\theta}{2}$:**
$$
v(t) = \frac{A}{2}[1 - \cos(4\pi f_0 t)] + n(t)\sin(2\pi f_0 t)
$$

**Step 3 — Pass through the LPF:**
The LPF removes the $2 f_0$ term and the high-frequency components of the noise-multiplication product. After filtering:
$$
v_{\text{out}}(t) \approx \frac{A}{2} + \text{low-frequency noise component}
$$

**Step 4 — Signal power at output:**
$$
S_{\text{out}} = \left(\frac{A}{2}\right)^{2} = \frac{A^{2}}{4}
$$

**Step 5 — Noise power at output:**
The noise is shifted in spectrum by multiplication, but its total power in the LPF passband $B_n = f_c$ is:
$$
N_{\text{out}} = N_0 \cdot B_n = N_0 f_c
$$

**Step 6 — Compute output SNR:**
$$
\left(\frac{S}{N}\right)_{\text{out}} = \frac{A^{2}/4}{N_0 f_c}
$$

**Step 7 — Compare with input SNR:**
Input noise bandwidth $B_{\text{in}}$ is much larger than $f_c$ (pre-filter):
$$
\left(\frac{S}{N}\right)_{\text{in}} = \frac{A^{2}/2}{N_0 B_{\text{in}}}
$$

**Step 8 — Form the improvement factor:**
$$
I_{\text{LI}} = \frac{(S/N)_{\text{out}}}{(S/N)_{\text{in}}} = \frac{A^{2}/4}{N_0 f_c} \cdot \frac{N_0 B_{\text{in}}}{A^{2}/2} = \frac{B_{\text{in}}}{2 f_c} = 2 B_{\text{in}} T_c
$$
using $f_c = 1/(2 T_c)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Result:**
> $$\boxed{\;I_{\text{LI}} = \frac{B_{\text{in}}}{2 f_c} = 2 B_{\text{in}} T_c\;}$$

For example, with $B_{\text{in}} = 1$ MHz and $T_c = 1$ s, the lock-in gives $I = 2 \times 10^{6}$ → **63 dB SNR improvement**. 🎯

---

## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Which Method When?

| Criterion | Filtering | Averaging | Box-Car | Correlation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Requires repetitive signal? | No | **Yes** | **Yes** | No (cross) / Yes (auto) |
| Best for narrowband signals | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓✓ |
| Best for broadband signals | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ |
| Real-time capable? | ✓✓ | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ |
| SNR Gain (typical) | $B_{\text{in}}/B_{\text{out}}$ | $\sqrt{N}$ | $\sqrt{m}$ | $2 B_{\text{in}} T_c$ |
| Computationally cheap? | ✓✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ (needs reference) |
| Synchronization required? | No | **Yes** | **Yes** | **Yes** |
| KTU weightage (typical) | High | **Very High** | Medium | High |

---

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** A telemetry receiver has input noise bandwidth $B_{\text{in}} = 100$ kHz. The signal is repetitive and is averaged over $N = 400$ sweeps, then passed through a matched filter of bandwidth $B_{\text{out}} = 1$ kHz. If the input SNR is $-10$ dB, find the output SNR.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — SNR in linear scale:
$$
(S/N)_{\text{in}} = 10^{-10/10} = 0.1
$$

Step 2 — SNR improvement by averaging (voltage form):
$$
I_{\text{avg}} = \sqrt{N} = \sqrt{400} = 20 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 26 \text{ dB}
$$

Step 3 — SNR improvement by matched filter:
$$
I_{\text{MF}} = \frac{B_{\text{in}}}{B_{\text{out}}} = \frac{100\,\text{kHz}}{1\,\text{kHz}} = 100 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 20 \text{ dB}
$$

Step 4 — Total SNR improvement (cascaded in voltage form, multiply linear):
$$
I_{\text{total}} = 20 \times 100 = 2000
$$

Step 5 — Output SNR in linear:
$$
(S/N)_{\text{out}} = 0.1 \times 2000 = 200
$$

Step 6 — Output SNR in dB:
$$
\text{SNR}_{\text{out,dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(200) = 23.01 \text{ dB}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Net gain ≈ 33 dB.** This is the engineering reality: combining multiple SNR-enhancement techniques yields a powerful cumulative effect.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Architecture — SNR Enhancement Subsystem

This block diagram shows how the four SNR-improvement methods are typically arranged in a modern digital telemetry receiver.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Antenna or Sensor Input] --> B[Analog Front End LNA and Pre Filter]
    B --> C[Analog to Digital Converter ADC]
    C --> D{Signal Repetitive?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Trigger and Sync Circuit]
    D -- No --> F[Matched Filter]
    E --> G[Signal Averager Memory Stack]
    G --> H[Box Car Integrator Optional Gate]
    H --> I[Digital Cross Correlator with Reference]
    I --> J[Low Pass Filter Output Stage]
    F --> J
    J --> K[Output SNR Enhanced Signal]
    K --> L[Demodulator and Decoder]

    classDef inputNode fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000
    classDef processNode fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#fd7e14,color:#000
    classDef outputNode fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000
    classDef decisionNode fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000

    class A,B,C inputNode
    class D decisionNode
    class E,F,G,H,I,J processNode
    class K,L outputNode
```

**Block-Level Functional Architecture Explanation:**

- The **Analog Front-End** (LNA + Pre-Filter) provides the first-stage analog SNR enhancement by limiting the noise bandwidth early — this prevents noise from being amplified by subsequent stages.
- The **ADC** digitizes the conditioned signal.
- A **decision branch** separates the processing path: repetitive signals go to the **Averager–Box-Car–Correlator** chain; non-repetitive signals go directly to the **Matched Filter**.
- The **Averager** is the workhorse for repetitive biomedical/industrial signals.
- The **Box-Car** provides additional gated integration for pulsed waveforms.
- The **Cross-Correlator** is the final SNR maximizer when a reference is available.

---

## 4.2 Signal Averaging Mechanism — Visual Trace

This Mermaid sequence diagram illustrates the noise cancellation principle of signal averaging over 4 sweeps.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SWEEP_1
        S1[Signal plus Noise] --> A1[Add to Memory Bank]
    end
    subgraph SWEEP_2
        S2[Signal plus Noise] --> A2[Add to Memory Bank]
    end
    subgraph SWEEP_3
        S3[Signal plus Noise] --> A3[Add to Memory Bank]
    end
    subgraph SWEEP_4
        S4[Signal plus Noise] --> A4[Add to Memory Bank]
    end
    A1 --> SUM[Coherent Summation Node]
    A2 --> SUM
    A3 --> SUM
    A4 --> SUM
    SUM --> DIV[Divide by N equals 4]
    DIV --> OUT[Clean Recovered Signal]

    classDef sweepNode fill:#e7f1ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000
    classDef procNode fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#fd7e14,color:#000
    classDef outNode fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000

    class OUT outNode
```

## 4.3 Lock-In Amplifier Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Noisy Input Signal x of t equals s of t plus n of t] --> M[Multiplier Mixer]
    R[Local Reference y of t equals sin 2 pi f0 t] --> M
    M --> P[Product x of t times y of t]
    P --> L[Low Pass Filter with Time Constant Tc]
    L --> O[DC Output Proportional to Signal Amplitude at f0]

    classDef inNode fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000
    classDef procNode fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#fd7e14,color:#000
    classDef outNode fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000

    class A,R inNode
    class M,P,L procNode
    class O outNode
```

**Explanation of the Lock-In flow:**

- The noisy input $x(t) = s(t) + n(t)$ enters the **multiplier**.
- A **reference** $y(t)$ is generated locally — usually by chopping the original signal at $f_0$ at the source.
- The **product** $x(t)\cdot y(t)$ contains a DC term proportional to the signal amplitude and AC terms at $2 f_0$ that the LPF rejects.
- The output is a clean DC value representing the signal amplitude at the reference frequency.

---

## 4.4 Frequency-Domain View: Filtering vs. Box-Car Equivalence

| Filter Type | Frequency Response | Equivalent Time-Domain Tool |
|---|---|---|
| Narrow LPF (cutoff $f_c$) | Rectangle in $f$ | Long integration time $T = 1/(2 f_c)$ |
| Box-Car (gate $T_g$) | Sinc in $f$ | Narrow time gate |

> This duality is a **key KTU exam point**: time-domain gating ↔ frequency-domain filtering, related by the Fourier transform.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Define Signal-to-Noise Ratio. Mention any two methods to improve SNR in a communication receiver.**

**Model Answer:**

The Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) is the ratio of signal power to noise power measured at a given point in a system:

$$
\text{SNR} = \frac{P_s}{P_n} = \frac{V_s^{2}/R}{V_n^{2}/R} = \left(\frac{V_s}{V_n}\right)^{2}
$$

It is commonly expressed in decibels as $\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(P_s/P_n)$.

**Two methods to improve SNR:**

1. **Signal Filtering:** Using band-pass filters to restrict the receiver bandwidth to the signal's spectral occupancy, thereby rejecting out-of-band noise.
2. **Signal Averaging:** For repetitive signals, adding $N$ successive sweeps coherently so that the signal grows as $N$ while the random noise grows only as $\sqrt{N}$, giving an SNR improvement factor of $\sqrt{N}$.

*(Alternative valid answers: Box-Car Integration, Matched Filtering, Cross-Correlation / Lock-in Amplifier.)* ✅

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**What is a Box-Car Integrator? Write its SNR improvement expression.**

**Model Answer:**

A Box-Car Integrator is a sample-and-hold signal processing circuit that opens a narrow time gate of width $T_g$ at a fixed delay after a trigger pulse, samples the input signal during this gate, and averages the sample values over multiple trigger cycles. It is used to recover repetitive pulsed signals buried in noise.

**Block Diagram (verbal):** Trigger → Delay Generator → Gate Pulse → Sample-and-Hold → Integrator (RC or digital) → Output.

**SNR Improvement Factor:**

$$
I_{\text{boxcar}} = \sqrt{m} = \sqrt{\frac{T_g}{\Delta t}}
$$

where $m$ is the number of independent samples within the gate window and $\Delta t$ is the sampling interval.

The equivalent noise bandwidth is $B_n = 1/(2 T_g)$. ✅

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Part B questions carry **14 marks** with a typical internal choice. We provide TWO independent 14-mark questions, each split into two 7-mark sub-parts.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**A.** (a) Explain the principle of signal averaging for improving SNR. Derive the expression for SNR improvement factor after averaging $N$ sweeps. (7 marks)

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the operation of a Box-Car Integrator. Derive its transfer function. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution to A(a)

**Principle:**
Signal averaging is a time-domain technique used when a deterministic signal $s(t)$ is repetitive and is buried in random noise $n(t)$. By recording $N$ identical sweeps and adding them point-by-point (after alignment), the periodic signal component adds **coherently** (linearly), while the random noise component adds **incoherently** (in RMS). The net result is an SNR enhancement.

**Block diagram (textual):** Input → Trigger Sync → ADC → Memory Stack → Accumulator → Divider-by-N → Averaged Output.

**Derivation:**

Let $x_i(t) = s(t) + n_i(t)$ be the $i$-th sweep.

$$
\bar{x}(t) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i(t) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} s(t) + \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} n_i(t)
$$

The signal term simplifies as:
$$
\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} s(t) = s(t)
$$

The averaged noise has:
- Mean: $E\!\left\{\dfrac{1}{N}\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{N} n_i(t)\right\} = 0$
- Variance: $\text{Var} = \dfrac{\sigma_n^{2}}{N}$, so RMS $= \dfrac{\sigma_n}{\sqrt{N}}$

Therefore:

$$
\left(\frac{S}{N}\right)_{\text{out}} = \frac{A^{2}}{\sigma_n^{2}/N} = N \cdot \left(\frac{S}{N}\right)_{\text{in}}
$$

In voltage terms:

$$
\boxed{\;I_{\text{avg}} = \sqrt{N} \quad \text{(amplitude SNR gain)}\;}
$$

In dB: $\text{Gain}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(N)$ (power) or $20 \log_{10}(\sqrt{N})$ (voltage). ✅

---

#### Model Solution to A(b)

**Box-Car Integrator Operation:**

A Box-Car Integrator consists of:
1. A **trigger input** synchronized to the repetitive signal.
2. A **delay generator** producing a precisely controllable delay $t_d$.
3. A **gating pulse** of adjustable width $T_g$ produced after the delay.
4. A **fast sample-and-hold** (S/H) circuit that captures the signal amplitude only during the gate.
5. An **integrator (RC or digital averager)** that averages the sample values over multiple cycles.

The output is a smooth DC voltage proportional to the average signal amplitude within the chosen gate window.

**Derivation of Transfer Function:**

Gate function in time domain:
$$
g(t) = \begin{cases} 1, & -T_g/2 \leq t \leq +T_g/2 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

Fourier transform:
$$
H(f) = \int_{-T_g/2}^{+T_g/2} e^{-j 2\pi f t}\, dt = T_g \cdot \text{sinc}(f T_g)
$$

Equivalent noise bandwidth:
$$
B_n = \frac{1}{2 T_g}
$$

SNR improvement with $m$ samples per gate:
$$
\boxed{\;I_{\text{boxcar}} = \sqrt{m} = \sqrt{\frac{T_g}{\Delta t}}\;}
$$

A narrow gate yields small $B_n$ and hence large SNR improvement — at the cost of reduced time resolution. ✅

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]**

**B.** (a) Define Autocorrelation and Cross-correlation functions. Explain how cross-correlation is used to extract a signal from noise. (7 marks)

**(b)** With a block diagram, explain the operation of a Lock-In Amplifier. A lock-in amplifier has an input noise bandwidth of 1 MHz and uses a time constant of 0.1 s. Calculate the SNR improvement factor and express it in dB. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution to B(a)

**Autocorrelation Function:**
The autocorrelation $R_{xx}(\tau)$ of a signal $x(t)$ measures the similarity between $x(t)$ and a time-shifted version $x(t+\tau)$:
$$
R_{xx}(\tau) = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} x(t)\, x(t+\tau)\, dt
$$

For a periodic signal, $R_{xx}(\tau)$ is also periodic with the same period. For random noise, $R_{xx}(\tau)$ decays rapidly to zero as $|\tau|$ increases (the noise is uncorrelated with its shifted self).

**Cross-Correlation Function:**
$$
R_{xy}(\tau) = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} x(t)\, y(t+\tau)\, dt
$$

**Use in Signal Extraction:**
Suppose $x(t) = s(t) + n(t)$ is the noisy received signal, and $y(t)$ is a **locally generated reference** that is a known copy of $s(t)$. Then:

$$
R_{xy}(\tau) = R_{sy}(\tau) + R_{ny}(\tau)
$$

- $R_{sy}(\tau)$ produces a sharp peak at the lag $\tau_0$ where $y(t)$ best aligns with $s(t)$.
- $R_{ny}(\tau) \approx 0$ since noise is uncorrelated with the deterministic reference.

Hence the **signal emerges as a clear peak** above a near-zero noise baseline, even when the original SNR was deeply negative. This is the basis of the lock-in amplifier. ✅

---

#### Model Solution to B(b)

**Lock-In Amplifier Block Diagram:**

```
Noisy Input → [Multiplier] ← Reference (sin 2π f₀ t)
                  ↓
           [Low-Pass Filter] (time constant T_c)
                  ↓
             DC Output (∝ signal amplitude)
```

**Working Principle:**
The noisy input is multiplied by a clean reference at the same frequency $f_0$ as the (modulated) signal of interest. Multiplication shifts the signal energy to DC, while noise spreads to higher frequencies. The LPF (with cutoff $\approx 1/T_c$) removes the high-frequency noise, leaving a clean DC proportional to the signal amplitude.

**Calculation:**

Given:
- Input noise bandwidth $B_{\text{in}} = 1$ MHz $= 10^{6}$ Hz
- Time constant $T_c = 0.1$ s
- Equivalent LPF bandwidth $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi T_c} \approx 1.59$ Hz

**SNR Improvement Factor:**

$$
I_{\text{LI}} = 2 B_{\text{in}} T_c = 2 \times 10^{6} \times 0.1 = 2 \times 10^{5}
$$

**In decibels:**

$$
I_{\text{LI,dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(2 \times 10^{5}) = 10\,[\log_{10}(2) + 5] = 10 \times 5.301 = 53.01 \text{ dB}
$$

> The lock-in amplifier provides a **53 dB SNR improvement**, allowing recovery of signals 53 dB below the input noise floor — equivalent to a factor of ~200,000. ✅

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Pitfall Callouts

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes (where KTU students lose marks):**
>
> 1. **Averaging Gain Confusion:** Writing SNR improvement = $N$ when the question asks for **voltage/amplitude** improvement. KTU expects **$\sqrt{N}$** unless explicitly stated "power SNR".
> 2. **Forgetting the Time Constant Relation:** Many students write $f_c = 1/T_c$. The correct relation for a single-pole RC LPF is $f_c = 1/(2\pi T_c)$. Missing the $2\pi$ factor costs 1 mark.
> 3. **Confusing Bandwidth vs. Noise Bandwidth:** $B$ and $B_n$ are not always equal. $B_n \geq B$ with equality only for the ideal brick-wall filter. Marks are lost when students blindly substitute signal bandwidth for noise bandwidth.
