> 4. **Box-Car Transfer Function Sign Error:** The sinc function $\text{sinc}(x) = \sin(\pi x)/(\pi x)$ is sometimes written with the wrong argument. Always normalize so that the first null is at $f = 1/T_g$.
> 5. **Autocorrelation vs. Cross-Correlation Mix-up:** Cross-correlation requires a **reference** signal; autocorrelation uses only the input itself. Examiners specifically check whether the student knows which lag is being measured.
> 6. **Skipping Block Diagrams:** Even in derivations, the KTU examiner expects a labelled block diagram for at least one method. A 7-mark question without a diagram typically loses 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!TIP]
> **Print this section and use as your last-day revision sheet.**

### ⭐ The Big Picture

- **SNR** = Signal Power / Noise Power (in dB: $10 \log_{10}(S/N)$).
- Four master techniques: **Filtering, Averaging, Box-Car, Correlation**.
- All four aim to **shrink effective noise bandwidth** or **exploit signal periodicity/determinism**.

### ⭐ Filtering

- Best when signal and noise occupy **disjoint frequency bands**.
- SNR gain = $B_{\text{in}}/B_{\text{out}}$.
- **Matched filter** is optimum for known signals in white noise: $h(t) = s(T-t)$, max SNR $= 2 E_s/N_0$.

### ⭐ Signal Averaging

- Requires **strict periodicity** and **trigger synchronization**.
- Voltage SNR improvement = **$\sqrt{N}$**.
- Power SNR improvement = **$N$**.
- 100 sweeps → 20 dB gain (×10 amplitude).

### ⭐ Box-Car Integration

- Time-domain analog of a narrow band-pass filter.
- Gate function: $g(t) = 1$ for $|t| \leq T_g/2$, else 0.
- Transfer function: $H(f) = T_g\,\text{sinc}(f T_g)$.
- Noise bandwidth: $B_n = 1/(2 T_g)$.
- SNR gain = $\sqrt{m} = \sqrt{T_g/\Delta t}$.

### ⭐ Signal Correlation

- **Autocorrelation:** $R_{xx}(\tau)$ — periodicity detector, recovers periodic signals from noise.
- **Cross-correlation:** $R_{xy}(\tau)$ — uses a known reference to detect signal presence/alignment.
- **Lock-In Amplifier:** multiplier + LPF + reference at $f_0$. SNR gain $= 2 B_{\text{in}} T_c$. Easily gives 50–80 dB improvement.

### ⭐ Key Constants to Memorize

- $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K
- $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C
- Thermal noise: $N = k_B T B$

### ⭐ KTU-Exam Mantra

> *"Filtering rejects in frequency, Averaging sums in time, Box-Car gates in time, Correlation multiplies and integrates."* — **Keep this line in your answer to grab the examiner's attention.** ✍️
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Grounding and Shielding Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT634 - COMMUNICATION SYSTEM AND TELEMETRY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Grounding and Shielding Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Grounding in Communication Systems — Formal Definition

**Grounding** is the deliberate establishment of a low-impedance reference path (typically to earth or to a common chassis/bus) that allows unwanted electrical currents — such as noise, leakage, and fault currents — to return safely to their source without inducing interference into sensitive signal circuits.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus Standard):** Grounding is the technique of connecting a circuit or system to a reference point (earth or common bus) to provide a stable voltage reference, ensure personnel safety, and suppress electromagnetic interference (EMI) by providing a low-impedance return path for unwanted currents.

There are three distinct, often confused, reference concepts in telemetry:

| Reference Type | Symbol | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Earth Ground** | ⏚ | Direct physical connection to soil/ground rod. Reference for safety & lightning. |
| **Chassis Ground** | ⏛ | Connection to the equipment metal enclosure. |
| **Signal Ground** | ▽ | Clean, low-noise reference for low-level signal returns. |

## 1.2 Shielding — Formal Definition

**Shielding** is the practice of enclosing a circuit, cable, or component within a conductive barrier (copper, aluminum, mu-metal, or braid) to attenuate the effect of external electric fields (E), magnetic fields (H), and electromagnetic waves (RF) on the enclosed signal path, or to prevent internal emissions from escaping.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition for Grounding — "The River Analogy":**
> Imagine an unwanted current (noise) as a river of water. If you don't give the water a controlled, straight, low-resistance channel to drain into, it will meander through your garden (your signal circuits) and flood delicate plants (your sensitive amplifiers). A proper ground is the engineered concrete drainage canal — it carries the noise away cleanly and predictably, leaving the rest of the system undisturbed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition for Shielding — "The Bulletproof Vest Analogy":**
> A shield is the bulletproof vest worn around a signal cable. Outside noise (bullets) strike the conductive outer layer and are harmlessly diverted to ground (drained away), while the delicate signal inside (the body) remains untouched. Without a proper vest, even a small impact can cripple the signal.

## 1.4 The Core "Why" — Why Grounding and Shielding Matter

In a telemetry link, transmitted/received signals are often at microvolt levels and kilohertz frequencies — easily swamped by:
- **50/60 Hz mains hum** injected through capacitive coupling.
- **RF pickup** from broadcast stations or switching power supplies.
- **Ground loops** that introduce DC offset and 50 Hz buzz.
- **Crosstalk** between adjacent signal pairs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Performance Metrics (Standards-Defined):**
> - **Shielding Effectiveness (SE):** Measured in **dB**, typically **40 dB to 100 dB** for industrial cables.
> - **Ground Impedance Target:** Less than **1 Ω** for signal grounds, less than **5 Ω** for safety grounds.
> - **Skin Depth (δ):** $\delta = \sqrt{\frac{2\rho}{\omega\mu}}$ determines shield thickness at a given frequency.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Types of Grounding Configurations

### 2.1.1 Single-Point Grounding (Series / Star)
- All circuits connect to a **single physical ground point**.
- Sub-types: **Series Single-Point** (daisy-chained) and **Parallel Single-Point** (star/bus).
- **Best for:** Low-frequency systems (< **1 MHz**), instrumentation, audio.
- **Drawback:** Long ground wires introduce inductance and shared return paths create common-impedance coupling (CICC).

### 2.1.2 Multi-Point Grounding
- Each circuit is grounded to the **nearest point of a low-impedance ground plane** (chassis, PCB inner layer, bus-bar).
- **Best for:** High-frequency systems (> **10 MHz**), RF, microwave, digital logic.
- **Drawback:** Can create ground loops at low frequencies.

### 2.1.3 Hybrid Grounding
- A **combination of capacitive (AC) and direct (DC) grounds**.
- Capacitor provides low impedance at high frequencies, inductor/transformer isolates DC.
- **Best for:** Wide-band systems, mixed-signal PCBs (analog + digital).

## 2.2 Types of Shielding

### 2.2.1 Electrostatic (Faraday) Shielding
- Continuous, unbroken conductive enclosure.
- Effective for **electric fields (E)** at all frequencies.
- Must be grounded at **only one point** to avoid ground loops.

### 2.2.2 Electromagnetic (Magnetic) Shielding
- High-permeability material (mu-metal, $\mu_r > 10{,}000$) to shunt magnetic flux.
- Effective for **low-frequency magnetic fields (H)**.
- Effectiveness: $SE_H \propto \mu_r \cdot t$, where $t$ is shield thickness.

### 2.2.3 Electromagnetic (RF/Microwave) Shielding
- Reflective shield: high conductivity (copper, $\sigma = 5.8 \times 10^7$ S/m) + sufficient thickness greater than skin depth.
- Effective for **> 100 kHz** RF interference.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\delta = \sqrt{\dfrac{2\rho}{\omega\mu}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{\pi f \mu \sigma}}$ | Skin depth in conductor | meters (m); $\rho$ = resistivity, $\mu$ = permeability, $\sigma$ = conductivity, $\omega = 2\pi f$ |
| 2 | $SE = 20\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{E_i}{E_t}\right) \text{ dB}$ | Shielding Effectiveness (electric field) | dB; $E_i$ = incident, $E_t$ = transmitted |
| 3 | $SE_{total} = A + R + B$ | Total SE = Absorption + Reflection + Re-reflection | dB (all terms in dB) |
| 4 | $A = 8.686 \cdot \dfrac{t}{\delta}$ | Absorption loss (dB) | dB; $t$ = shield thickness, $\delta$ = skin depth |
| 5 | $R = 168 - 10\log_{10}\left(\sigma_r \mu_r f^3 r^2\right)$ | Reflection loss (plane wave) | dB; $r$ = distance from source |
| 6 | $V_{loop} = I_{noise} \cdot Z_{ground}$ | Ground-loop induced voltage | V; minimized when $Z_{ground} \to 0$ |
| 7 | $Z_{ground} = R + j\omega L$ | Ground wire impedance | Ω; $L$ dominates above ~10 kHz |
| 8 | $L = 0.002\ell \left[\ln\left(\dfrac{2\ell}{r}\right) - 0.75\right] \;\mu H$ | Inductance of straight wire | $\ell$ = length in cm, $r$ = radius in cm |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** For KTU board exams, you must remember the *qualitative* roles of **R, A, B** in shielding effectiveness, and the *quantitative* skin-depth formula. Questions on skin depth for copper/aluminum at given frequency are frequently asked (2–3 mark short problems).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Telemetry ground stations:** Star-grounding at the receiver to avoid hum injection into low-level data signals.
- **Aircraft/Missile telemetry:** Multi-point grounding on aluminum chassis; braided shields for ignition-noise rejection.
- **Process industries (oil & gas):** Hybrid grounding with isolation transformers to break ground loops between sensors and SCADA.
- **Medical telemetry (ECG/EEG):** Driven-right-leg circuits + Faraday cage — direct application of the principles studied here.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Skin Depth in a Conductor

We start from Maxwell's equations for a time-harmonic plane wave $E(z,t) = E_0 e^{-\alpha z} e^{j(\omega t - \beta z)}$ propagating into a good conductor.

**Step 1 — Define the propagation constant.**
For a good conductor, displacement current is negligible ($\sigma \gg \omega \varepsilon$):

$$
\gamma = \sqrt{j\omega\mu(\sigma + j\omega\varepsilon)} \;\approx\; \sqrt{j\omega\mu\sigma}
$$

**Step 2 — Expand the complex square root.**
Using $j = e^{j\pi/2}$, we get:

$$
\gamma = \sqrt{\omega\mu\sigma}\; e^{j\pi/4} = (1+j)\sqrt{\dfrac{\omega\mu\sigma}{2}}
$$

**Step 3 — Identify the attenuation constant.**
Comparing with $\gamma = \alpha + j\beta$:

$$
\alpha = \beta = \sqrt{\dfrac{\omega\mu\sigma}{2}} = \sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}
$$

**Step 4 — Define skin depth as the depth at which the field drops to $1/e$ of its surface value.**
The amplitude falls as $e^{-\alpha z}$, so the $1/e$ point occurs at $z = 1/\alpha$:

$$
\delta \;\equiv\; \dfrac{1}{\alpha} \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{\omega\mu\sigma}} \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{\pi f \mu \sigma}}
$$

This is the required KTU formula. **Note carefully:** an alternative form in terms of resistivity $\rho = 1/\sigma$ gives $\delta = \sqrt{2\rho / (\omega\mu)}$, which is also accepted in KTU answers.

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example: Skin Depth of Copper at 1 MHz

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Numerical Pattern:** "Compute the skin depth of copper at a given frequency and comment on shield thickness." This is a **guaranteed 3-mark question**.

**Given:**
- $f = 1 \text{ MHz} = 10^6 \text{ Hz}$
- Copper conductivity $\sigma = 5.8 \times 10^7 \text{ S/m}$
- Copper permeability $\mu = \mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \text{ H/m}$

**Step 1 — Compute $\pi f \mu \sigma$:**

$$
\pi f \mu \sigma = \pi \times 10^6 \times (4\pi \times 10^{-7}) \times (5.8 \times 10^7)
$$

**Step 2 — Multiply term-by-term:**

$$
= \pi^2 \times 10^6 \times 10^{-7} \times 10^7 \times 5.8
$$

$$
= 9.8696 \times 10^{6} \times 5.8
$$

$$
\approx 5.724 \times 10^7
$$

**Step 3 — Take the square root:**

$$
\sqrt{5.724 \times 10^7} = \sqrt{57.24 \times 10^6} \approx 7.566 \times 10^3
$$

**Step 4 — Apply $\delta = 1/\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}$:**

$$
\delta = \dfrac{1}{7.566 \times 10^3} \approx 1.32 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}
$$

$$
\boxed{\delta \approx 132 \,\mu\text{m} \text{ at 1 MHz for copper}}
$$

**Comment (for full KTU marks):** A copper shield at 1 MHz needs to be at least ~**132 µm** thick to provide one skin-depth of absorption. Typical cable braids are 0.5–2 mm thick, so they offer several skin depths, yielding absorption loss $A > 40$ dB.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Grounding Configuration Selection

| Parameter | Single-Point | Multi-Point | Hybrid |
|---|---|---|---|
| Best Frequency Range | DC – 1 MHz | > 10 MHz | 1 MHz – 10 MHz + DC |
| Ground-Loop Risk | Low | High (at low f) | Low |
| Common-Impedance Coupling | High (if series) | Low | Low |
| Implementation Cost | High (many wires) | Low (use plane) | Medium |
| Telemetry Use-Case | Industrial sensors, audio | RF receivers, digital PCB | Mixed-signal data acquisition |
| Recommended Cable | Twisted pair, single-shield | Coaxial, double-shield | Coaxial + isolation transformer |

## 3.4 Derivation: Ground-Loop Induced Voltage

Consider two instruments $A$ and $B$ connected by a signal cable, each grounded to earth at a physical distance $L$ apart. The earth path between them has finite impedance $Z_g = R_g + j\omega L_g$. A noise current $I_n$ flows through this path due to mains leakage or external EMI.

The voltage developed across the ground path (and thus appearing as a series-mode noise in the signal) is:

$$
V_{loop} = I_n \cdot Z_g = I_n (R_g + j\omega L_g)
$$

For a typical industrial ground wire of length $L = 10$ m and radius $r = 1$ mm:

$$
L_g = 0.002 \times 1000 \left[\ln\left(\frac{2 \times 1000}{0.1}\right) - 0.75\right]\,\mu H
$$

$$
L_g = 2 \left[\ln(20000) - 0.75\right] = 2 [9.903 - 0.75] = 18.3 \,\mu H
$$

At $f = 50$ Hz: $Z_g \approx j\omega L_g = j(2\pi \times 50 \times 18.3 \times 10^{-6}) = j 5.75 \times 10^{-3}\;\Omega$

At $f = 10$ kHz: $Z_g \approx j(2\pi \times 10^4 \times 18.3 \times 10^{-6}) = j 1.15\;\Omega$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Insight:** Even a short ground wire becomes a **1 Ω** impedance at just 10 kHz. This is why single-point grounding fails above ~1 MHz — the "ground" is no longer at the same potential, defeating the purpose of grounding.

## 3.5 Derivation: Absorption Loss for a Practical Shield

Given a shield of thickness $t$ and skin depth $\delta$:

$$
A = 8.686 \cdot \frac{t}{\delta} \quad \text{dB}
$$

For our copper example at 1 MHz with $t = 0.5$ mm:

$$
A = 8.686 \times \frac{0.5 \times 10^{-3}}{132 \times 10^{-6}} = 8.686 \times 3.79 \approx 32.9 \text{ dB}
$$

This single term is the dominant contributor to total shielding effectiveness. Combined with reflection loss $R \approx 60$ dB at 1 MHz, total $SE > 90$ dB — sufficient for industrial telemetry.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Complete Grounding & Shielding Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    srcA[Signal Source A] --> cable1[Shielded Twisted Pair]
    srcB[Signal Source B] --> cable2[Shielded Twisted Pair]
    srcC[RF Antenna 433 MHz] --> coax[Coaxial Cable]
    
    cable1 --> spg[Single Point Ground Bus]
    cable2 --> spg
    coax --> mpg[Multi Point Ground Plane]
    
    spg --> iso[Isolation Transformer]
    iso --> recvA[Telemetry Receiver A]
    mpg --> recvB[Telemetry Receiver B]
    recvB --> scope[Oscilloscope / Decoder]
    
    spg --> earth[Earth Ground Rod]
    mpg --> chassis[Chassis Ground Plane]
    chassis --> earth
    
    efield[External E Field] -.->|blocked by| cable1
    hfield[External H Field] -.->|shunted by| cable1
    loop[Ground Loop Current] -.->|broken by| iso
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Grounding Configuration Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Start: Choose Grounding Method] --> freq{Q: Operating Frequency?}
    freq -->|< 1 MHz| lowf{Number of circuits?}
    lowf -->|Few, low power| spg[Single Point Star Ground]
    lowf -->|Many, mixed levels| hyspc[Hybrid with Capacitors]
    freq -->|1 MHz to 10 MHz| midf[Hybrid Grounding]
    freq -->|> 10 MHz| highf[Multi Point Ground Plane]
    spg --> check1{Any ground loop observed?}
    check1 -->|Yes| iso2[Add Isolation Transformer]
    check1 -->|No| done1[Acceptable]
    highf --> check2{Via stitching interval < λ/20?}
    check2 -->|Yes| done2[Acceptable]
    check2 -->|No| fix2[Add More Ground Vias]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Shielding Effectiveness Budget

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    se[Total SE Requirement: 80 dB]
    se --> rloss[Reflection Loss R: 60 dB]
    se --> aloss[Absorption Loss A: 33 dB]
    se --> bloss[Re-reflection B: 5 dB]
    rloss --> mat1[High Conductivity Material: Copper]
    aloss --> mat2[Thickness > 3x Skin Depth]
    bloss --> mat3[Thick or Multi-layer Shield]
```

## 4.4 Functional Architecture: Telemetry Link with Grounding & Shielding Layers

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Sensor_End[Sensor End - Field]
        S1[Thermocouple / RTD / Strain Gauge]
        SAmp[Low-Noise Pre-amp]
        SShield[Local Faraday Cage - Sensor Housing]
        S1 --> SAmp
        SAmp --> SShield
    end
    
    subgraph Cable_Plant[Cable Plant]
        SPC[SPC Twisted Pair with Foil + Braid]
        Drain[Drain Wire Grounded at Source End Only]
    end
    
    subgraph Receiver_End[Receiver End - Control Room]
        IsoT[Isolation Transformer 1:1]
        ADC[24-bit Sigma-Delta ADC]
        MCU[Telemetry Processor]
        RackGround[Instrument Rack - Chassis Bus]
    end
    
    SShield --> SPC
    SPC --> Drain
    SPC --> IsoT
    IsoT --> ADC
    ADC --> MCU
    RackGround --- IsoT
    RackGround --- ADC
    RackGround --- MCU
    
    SShield -.->|Earth Rod 1| Earth[Earth Grid]
    RackGround -.->|Earth Rod 2| Earth
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Notice that the **drain wire of the shielded cable is grounded only at the source (sensor) end** — this is the canonical single-point shield grounding rule, deliberately broken at the receiver to prevent ground loops. The receiver side uses an **isolation transformer** to break the DC ground loop while passing the AC signal.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define "ground loop" in a communication system. Explain with a diagram how it introduces noise.*

**Model Answer (Target: 3 marks):**
A ground loop is formed when two or more points in a signal path that are intended to be at the same ground potential are connected through a non-zero impedance (e.g., long ground wires or earth path). Any current flowing through this shared impedance develops a voltage drop $V = I \cdot Z_g$ that appears as a series-mode noise voltage superimposed on the signal.

**Diagram description (verbal):** Two instruments A and B, each separately earthed, with a signal cable between them. The earth between the two rods acts as the third conductor of an unintentional loop. A stray current $I_n$ flowing in this loop induces a hum voltage at the receiver.

**Preventive techniques (for full marks):** (i) Single-point grounding, (ii) Use of isolation transformer or optocoupler, (iii) Differential signal transmission, (iv) Ground-loop isolator modules.

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*What is shielding effectiveness? Define absorption loss and state its formula.*

**Model Answer (Target: 3 marks):**
Shielding Effectiveness (SE) is the ratio, expressed in decibels, of the magnitude of the electromagnetic field incident on a shield to the magnitude of the field transmitted through it:

$$
SE = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{E_i}{E_t}\right)\;\text{dB}
$$

**Absorption Loss (A):** It is the portion of SE due to the dissipation of electromagnetic energy as heat inside the bulk of the shield material as the wave is attenuated over one skin depth. Formula:

$$
A = 8.686 \cdot \frac{t}{\delta}\;\text{dB}
$$

where $t$ is shield thickness and $\delta$ is skin depth at the operating frequency.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q-A(a) [7 marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
*Explain the different types of grounding techniques used in electronic systems. Compare single-point and multi-point grounding in terms of frequency range, ground-loop susceptibility, and typical applications.*

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Grounding establishes a common reference potential and provides a return path for noise currents. The choice of topology is dictated by the operating frequency of the system.

**Single-Point Grounding (2 marks):**
- All circuit returns are tied to one physical ground point.
- Two sub-types: **Series (daisy-chain)** and **Parallel (star)**.
- Eliminates low-frequency ground loops because only one earth return exists.
- **Limitation:** Long ground leads accumulate inductance; above ~1 MHz each branch sees a different "ground", defeating the purpose. Common-impedance coupling is severe in the series configuration.

**Multi-Point Grounding (2 marks):**
- Each sub-circuit is bonded to the nearest point of a low-impedance ground plane (chassis sheet, PCB inner layer, bus-bar).
- Plane inductance is very small, so the plane remains equipotential even at GHz.
- **Limitation:** Creates multiple ground loops, problematic at low frequencies where 50 Hz hum can circulate.

**Comparison Table (1.5 marks):**

| Criterion | Single-Point | Multi-Point |
|---|---|---|
| Frequency range | DC – 1 MHz | > 10 MHz |
| Ground-loop risk | Low | High |
| Common-impedance coupling | High (series) | Low |
| Application | Audio, instrumentation | RF, microwave, digital |

**Conclusion (0.5 mark):**
Hybrid grounding is preferred for mixed-signal telemetry systems spanning DC to RF.

---

**Q-A(b) [7 marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
*Derive the expression for skin depth in a conductor. A copper conductor has conductivity $\sigma = 5.8 \times 10^7$ S/m and $\mu_r = 1$. Calculate the skin depth at (i) 1 kHz, and (ii) 1 MHz.*

**Model Answer:**

**Derivation (3 marks):**
Starting from Maxwell's equation in a good conductor where $\sigma \gg \omega\varepsilon$:

$$
\alpha = \sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \delta = \frac{1}{\alpha} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi f \mu \sigma}}
$$

**Numerical (4 marks):**

For both parts: $\mu = \mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m, $\sigma = 5.8 \times 10^7$ S/m.

**(i) At $f = 1$ kHz:**

$$
\delta = \sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi \times 10^3 \times 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 5.8 \times 10^7}}
$$

$$
= \sqrt{\frac{1}{2.29 \times 10^5}} = \sqrt{4.37 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 2.09 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{m}
$$

$$
\boxed{\delta \approx 2.09 \text{ mm at 1 kHz}}
$$

**(ii) At $f = 1$ MHz:**

$$
\delta = \sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi \times 10^6 \times 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 5.8 \times 10^7}}
$$

$$
= \sqrt{\frac{1}{2.29 \times 10^8}} \approx 6.6 \times 10^{-5}\;\text{m}
$$

$$
\boxed{\delta \approx 66 \,\mu\text{m at 1 MHz}}
$$

**Conclusion (for full marks):** Higher frequency → smaller skin depth → thinner shield required for the same absorption loss.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q-B(a) [7 marks] [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
*With neat diagrams, explain the working of (i) electrostatic shielding and (ii) electromagnetic shielding. Mention the materials used in each case.*

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Electrostatic (Faraday) Shielding (3.5 marks):**
- A continuous, closed conductive enclosure surrounds the protected circuit.
- External electric field lines terminate on the outer surface of the conductor (since $E$ inside a perfect conductor is zero).
- **Critical rule:** The shield must be **grounded at only ONE point** to drain the induced charges; grounding at two points creates a ground loop.
- **Materials:** Copper, aluminum (high conductivity). Typical form: copper braid over cable, or metal instrument enclosure.
- **Diagram (verbal):** Show a wire carrying AC interference, surrounded by a grounded copper cylinder. Indicate $E$ lines terminating on the cylinder wall.

**(ii) Electromagnetic (Magnetic) Shielding (3.5 marks):**
- Used to protect against low-frequency magnetic fields (e.g., 50 Hz mains hum from transformers).
- Material must have **high magnetic permeability** to provide a low-reluctance path that shunts flux lines around the protected region.
- **Materials:** Mu-metal ($\mu_r \approx 50{,}000$ to $100{,}000$), soft iron, permalloy. Often used as thin foils wrapped around CRTs and precision instruments.
- **For RF/microwave shielding:** High conductivity + thickness $\geq 3\delta$ to provide reflection + absorption. Materials: copper, aluminum, with conductive gaskets at seams.
- **Diagram (verbal):** Show H-field lines from a power transformer diverted through a mu-metal enclosure surrounding a sensitive amplifier.

---

**Q-B(b) [7 marks] [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
*Discuss the various cable-shield grounding practices. Why is the shield of a low-frequency signal cable grounded at one end only? Justify with a diagram.*

**Model Answer:**

**Cable Shield Grounding Practices (4 marks):**
1. **Single-end grounding:** Drain wire connected to ground at the source end only. Used for low-frequency (< 1 MHz) instrumentation. Prevents shield currents that would otherwise inject noise.
2. **Both-end grounding:** Used for RF/coaxial cables at high frequencies. The shield then acts as the return conductor and is part of the controlled-impedance transmission line.
3. **Hybrid (via capacitor):** Drain grounded at both ends through a capacitor (e.g., 10 nF). Provides low-impedance path at high frequency (good RF shielding) and open circuit at DC/50 Hz (no ground loop).

**Justification for Single-End Grounding at Low Frequency (3 marks):**
- If the shield is grounded at both ends, the shield itself becomes a parallel return path.
- Any potential difference between the two earth points drives a current $I_s$ through the shield.
- This current, via the transfer impedance $Z_t$ of the shield, couples a noise voltage $V_n = I_s \cdot Z_t$ into the inner signal conductor.
- At low frequency, $Z_t$ is the DC resistance of the braid (milliohms to ohms), but the **loop area** between inner conductor and shield is huge — the resulting inductive pickup dominates.
- At high frequency (> 1 MHz), $Z_t$ becomes large due to skin effect, the loop is broken by impedance, and grounding at both ends is preferred to keep the shield at uniform potential.

**Diagram (verbal):** Two diagrams side by side:
- (a) Both ends grounded → current $I_s$ flows in loop, noise $V_n$ appears at receiver.
- (b) One end grounded → no loop, no shield current, clean signal.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Points (KTU Examiner's Observations):**
> 1. **Confusing the three grounds:** Students write "earth ground" and "chassis ground" interchangeably. They are **not the same**. State explicitly which one is being used and why.
> 2. **Forgetting units in skin depth calculation:** A numerical answer without units (mm/µm/m) loses **0.5 mark** even if the number is correct.
> 3. **Mis-stating the single-point grounding rule:** Many students write "single-point grounding prevents all interference." Wrong — it only **prevents low-frequency ground loops**. Multi-point is needed for RF.
> 4. **Drawing shields without showing grounding points:** A shield diagram without a ground symbol is considered incomplete and loses a mark in the diagram portion.
> 5. **Forgetting the log in SE formula:** Writing $SE = E_i/E_t$ without the $20 \log_{10}$ loses a full mark. Always quote SE in dB.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Grounding & Shielding Techniques**

- **Earth vs Chassis vs Signal ground:** Know the distinction. Earth = safety, Chassis = enclosure, Signal = clean reference.
- **Three grounding topologies:** Single-point (LF), Multi-point (HF), Hybrid (mixed).
- **Cut-off frequency rule of thumb:** Use single-point below **1 MHz**, multi-point above **10 MHz**.
- **Skin depth formula:** $\delta = \sqrt{1/(\pi f \mu \sigma)}$ — memorize and be able to evaluate for copper at 1 kHz, 1 MHz, 100 MHz.
- **Shielding effectiveness:** $SE = A + R + B$ (all in dB). **Absorption** dominates for thick/high-$\mu$ shields.
- **Cable shield grounding:**
  * Low-frequency / instrumentation → **one end only** (source end).
  * High-frequency / coaxial → **both ends**.
  * Mixed / wideband → **capacitor at one end** (hybrid).
- **Ground-loop noise formula:** $V_{loop} = I_n \cdot Z_g$. Break the loop with isolation transformer, optocoupler, or differential signaling.
- **Wire inductance grows with length:** A 10 cm wire has ~1 µH; at 1 MHz this is **6.3 Ω** — not a "ground" anymore!
- **Magnetic shielding needs high $\mu_r$** (mu-metal); electric/RF shielding needs high $\sigma$ (copper).
- **Common-marker scoring keywords to use in answers:** "low-impedance path", "equipotential plane", "skin depth", "transfer impedance", "single-point star", "Faraday cage".

---

*End of Notes — Grounding and Shielding Techniques | Module 4 | PEICT634*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Magnetic Tape Recording
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT634 - COMMUNICATION SYSTEM AND TELEMETRY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Magnetic Tape Recording

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Magnetic Tape Recording** is an electromechanical technique used for the storage and retrieval of analog or digital information on a magnetically coated flexible plastic tape. Information is stored as patterns of residual magnetism along the length of the tape, and is written (recorded) and read (played back) using electromagnetic transducer devices known as **magnetic heads**.

According to the KTU 2024 syllabus for *Communication System and Telemetry*, magnetic tape recording falls under the broader category of **analog and digital storage subsystems** used in telemetry ground stations, instrumentation recorders, and signal archiving systems. It is one of the earliest forms of sequential-access mass storage and remains relevant in professional telemetry, broadcast, and aerospace instrumentation.

### Subsystem Components (Standard Definition)

The KTU syllabus identifies the following **three essential building blocks** of a magnetic tape recorder:

| # | Component | Function | Standard Terminology |
|---|-----------|----------|----------------------|
| 1 | **Tape** | Storage medium (plastic base + magnetic coating) | Magnetic storage medium |
| 2 | **Magnetic Heads** | Transducers for record / playback / erase | Electromagnetic transducers |
| 3 | **Tape Transport Mechanism** | Moves the tape past the heads at constant speed | Capstan–pinch roller / reel-to-reel drive |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Talking Ribbon**
> Imagine a long, thin ribbon coated with tiny invisible iron filings. When you sweep a tiny bar magnet across the ribbon, the filings under the magnet align themselves in a particular direction. If you sweep the magnet **slowly**, the aligned region is **long**; if you sweep it **fast**, the region is **short**. By varying the magnet's strength and speed, you "write" an invisible pattern on the ribbon. Later, if you sweep the same ribbon past a compass needle, the needle wiggles in response to the stored pattern — that wiggle is the recovered signal. **That ribbon is the magnetic tape, the bar magnet is the record head, and the compass needle is the playback head.**

This is, in essence, the philosophy of magnetic tape recording:
- The **magnetic field** at the head gap magnetizes tiny regions of the tape.
- The **residual magnetism** persists after the tape leaves the head.
- The **pattern of residual flux** corresponds to the input signal.
- On playback, the moving magnetic field across a coil **induces an EMF** (Faraday's Law).

---

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following constants and parameters are **essential KTU board-exam terms** for this topic:

- **Relative permeability of tape coating (μᵣ)**: typically **2 to 6** for γ-Fe₂O₃ particles.
- **Coercivity (Hc)**: typically **25,000 to 300,000 A/m** depending on tape type (standard, chrome, metal).
- **Retentivity (Br)**: typically **0.1 to 0.3 Tesla (T)** for audio tape.
- **Standard tape speeds**: **1⅞, 3¾, 7½, 15, 30 ips** (inches per second). Telemetry recorders commonly use **15 to 240 ips**.
- **Standard track widths**: from **0.6 mm (¼-inch tape)** for consumer to **sub-millimetre** in instrumentation.

---

## 1.4 Historical Context (Syllabus Relevance)

> [!NOTE]
> Magnetic recording was invented by **Oberlin Smith (1888)** and demonstrated practically by **Valdemar Poulsen (1898)** with his Telegraphone. The modern magnetic tape recorder was developed in Germany during **World War II** (AEG/Magnetophon), later commercialised in the US. For telemetry applications, instrumentation tape recorders became indispensable from the **1950s–1980s** for recording flight-test data, seismic exploration, and satellite downlink storage.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Physics — How Magnetic Tape Recording Works

The process relies on three sequential electromagnetic principles:

### Step 1: Magnetic Hysteresis
- The tape coating is a **ferromagnetic material** (e.g., γ-ferric oxide, CrO₂, or metal particles).
- Such materials exhibit a **B-H hysteresis loop**, meaning once magnetised, they retain a **residual flux density Bᵣ** even after the external field is removed.
- **Why this matters**: This residual magnetism is the actual "stored information" on the tape.

### Step 2: Recording — Current → Field → Magnetisation
- The input signal current passes through the coil of the **record head**.
- This produces a **fringing magnetic field** across the head gap (typically 1–25 µm wide).
- As the tape moves across the gap, each elemental section of tape is exposed to a strong, localised field.
- The field magnetises that elemental region to a level corresponding to the instantaneous signal amplitude — leaving a **track of residual magnetism** proportional to the input waveform.

### Step 3: Playback — Magnetic Flux → Induced EMF
- The magnetised tape is moved past the **playback head** (which may be the same physical head as the record head, switched via relays).
- The changing magnetic flux through the playback head coil induces an EMF per **Faraday's Law**:

$$
e = -N \frac{d\phi}{dt}
$$

where:
- **N** = number of turns in the playback coil
- **dφ/dt** = rate of change of magnetic flux linked with the coil

### Step 4: Erasure — Demagnetisation
- Before new recording, the old magnetisation must be erased.
- A high-frequency AC bias signal (typically **50–100 kHz**, much higher than the audio) is fed into the erase head.
- This **demagnetises** the tape by driving its hysteresis loop through a decaying AC cycle, leaving it in a **neutral, unmagnetised state**.

---

## 2.2 Block Diagram of a Complete Magnetic Tape Recording System

A professional telemetry / instrumentation tape recorder has the following functional chain:

```
INPUT SIGNAL → RECORD AMPLIFIER → RECORD HEAD ↘
                                                → TAPE (Moving Medium)
ERASE OSCILLATOR → ERASE HEAD (AC bias) ↗
                                                
TAPE → PLAYBACK HEAD → PLAYBACK AMPLIFIER → OUTPUT SIGNAL
```

The **AC bias** technique (introduced by Marvin Camras, 1940s) is essential for linear recording — without bias, the recorded signal suffers from severe harmonic distortion due to the non-linear B-H curve.

---

## 2.3 Types of Recording Modes

| Mode | Description | Application |
|------|-------------|-------------|
| **Direct Recording (DR)** | Signal directly magnetises the tape | Wide-band instrumentation, telemetry |
| **FM Recording** | Input modulates a carrier frequency before recording | Telemetry IFM/FM data, noise immunity |
| **Saturation Recording (Digital)** | Tape magnetised to +Bᵣ or −Bᵣ only | Digital data, instrumentation pulse trains |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For **KTU 2024 syllabus**, students must remember that telemetry recorders almost always use **FM recording** because it offers superior **signal-to-noise ratio (SNR)** and **better DC response** than direct recording — this is a frequently asked concept.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates every equation you need for this topic. **Memorise the formula, the definition of each variable, and the engineering context.**

| # | Formula | Quantity Described | Units | Engineering Context |
|---|---------|--------------------|-------|---------------------|
| 1 | $$ e = -N \dfrac{d\phi}{dt} $$ | Faraday's Law — induced playback EMF | Volts (V) | Voltage induced in playback coil |
| 2 | $$ v = \frac{dx}{dt} $$ | Linear tape velocity | m/s | Tape speed past the head |
| 3 | $$ \lambda = \frac{v}{f} $$ | Recorded wavelength on tape | metres (m) | Spatial wavelength per cycle |
| 4 | $$ \Delta t = \frac{\Delta x}{v} $$ | Time-to-space conversion | seconds (s) | Maps tape position to time |
| 5 | $$ \phi = B \cdot A $$ | Magnetic flux linkage | Weber (Wb) | Flux through coil area |
| 6 | $$ B = \mu_0 (H + M) $$ | Flux density in magnetic material | Tesla (T) | Material magnetisation |
| 7 | $$ SNR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}}\right) $$ | Signal-to-noise ratio | dB | Recording quality metric |
| 8 | $$ f_{max} = \dfrac{v_{max}}{\lambda_{min}} $$ | Maximum recordable frequency | Hz | Upper bandwidth limit |
| 9 | $$ BW \propto \dfrac{v}{g} $$ | Bandwidth ∝ tape speed / gap length | dimensionless | Design trade-off |
| 10 | $$ e_{out} \propto \dfrac{d}{dt}\!\left[\,B_r\!\left(\tfrac{vt}{\lambda}\right)\right] $$ | Playback signal differentiation | V | Why playback is 6 dB/octave rising |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Remember**: The playback process inherently acts as a **differentiator** (because of Faraday's Law). This is why the playback output amplitude rises at **+6 dB/octave** with frequency — a hallmark concept of magnetic recording theory.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Telemetry Ground Stations**: FM instrumentation tape recorders store multi-channel flight test, missile, or satellite data before digitisation.
- **Broadcasting**: Audio mastering and archival storage.
- **Aerospace**: Wideband instrumentation recorders (e.g., Honeywell, Ampex) capture vibration, strain, and acoustic data.
- **Seismic Exploration**: Long-duration field recordings of low-frequency vibration data.
- **Data Acquisition Systems**: Used as buffer storage before computer processing.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Recording Wavelength and Maximum Frequency

Let us derive the relationship between **tape speed (v)**, **signal frequency (f)**, and **recorded wavelength on the tape (λ)**.

### Step 1 — Definition of Tape Velocity
The tape moves linearly past the head gap at a constant velocity:

$$
v = \frac{dx}{dt} \quad \text{(metres per second)}
$$

where:
- $v$ = linear tape velocity
- $dx$ = infinitesimal displacement along the tape
- $dt$ = corresponding time interval

### Step 2 — Period of One Cycle of the Input Signal
For an input sinusoid of frequency $f$, the period of one complete cycle is:

$$
T = \frac{1}{f} \quad \text{(seconds)}
$$

### Step 3 — Distance Travelled by Tape in One Period
During one period $T$, the tape travels a spatial distance equal to one recorded wavelength:

$$
\lambda = v \cdot T
$$

### Step 4 — Substituting T = 1/f

$$
\lambda = v \cdot \frac{1}{f}
$$

### Step 5 — Rearranging for Frequency

$$
f = \frac{v}{\lambda}
$$

### Step 6 — Maximum Recordable Frequency
The smallest recordable wavelength is limited by the **head gap width $g$** and the **particle size of the magnetic coating**. A rule of thumb in magnetic recording theory is that the wavelength cannot be much smaller than the gap:

$$
\lambda_{min} \approx g \quad \text{(or 2g in some formulations)}
$$

Therefore, the **maximum usable recording frequency** is:

$$
f_{max} = \frac{v}{\lambda_{min}} = \frac{v}{g}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Implication**: To record high frequencies, you must either (a) increase tape speed, or (b) decrease head gap width. This is why professional recorders have variable tape speeds (e.g., 15/30/60/120 ips) and ultra-narrow head gaps.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Why Playback Output Rises at 6 dB/Octave

This is a **board-favourite derivation**. We will show why the playback signal is proportional to the **derivative** of the recorded flux pattern.

### Step 1 — Assume a Recorded Flux Distribution
Suppose a sinusoidal signal of frequency $f$ is recorded on the tape. The residual flux $B_r$ along the tape position $x$ is:

$$
B_r(x) = B_0 \sin\!\left(\frac{2\pi x}{\lambda}\right)
$$

where:
- $B_0$ = peak residual flux density (Tesla)
- $\lambda$ = recorded wavelength
- $x$ = spatial coordinate along tape

### Step 2 — Convert Position to Time Using Tape Velocity
Since the tape moves at velocity $v$ past the playback head, position and time are related by $x = vt$:

$$
B_r(t) = B_0 \sin\!\left(\frac{2\pi v t}{\lambda}\right)
$$

### Step 3 — Note that $v/\lambda = f$
Substituting $f = v / \lambda$:

$$
B_r(t) = B_0 \sin(2\pi f t)
$$

### Step 4 — Apply Faraday's Law at the Playback Head
The induced EMF in the playback coil is:

$$
e(t) = -N \frac{d\phi}{dt}
$$

If the coil links a flux proportional to the tape's flux (i.e., $\phi(t) \propto B_r(t)$):

$$
e(t) \propto \frac{d}{dt}\!\left[\,B_0 \sin(2\pi f t)\,\right]
$$

### Step 5 — Differentiate the Sine Function

$$
e(t) \propto B_0 \cdot 2\pi f \cdot \cos(2\pi f t)
$$

### Step 6 — Take the RMS Amplitude
The RMS amplitude of $e(t)$ is therefore:

$$
E_{rms} \propto B_0 \cdot 2\pi f
$$

### Step 7 — Express in Decibels
The output voltage in dB is:

$$
E_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{2\pi f \cdot B_0}{V_{ref}}\right)
$$

Differentiating with respect to frequency:

$$
\frac{d E_{dB}}{d \log_{10} f} = 20 \log_{10}(e) \cdot \dfrac{d(\ln f)}{d(\ln f)} = 20 \log_{10}(e) \approx 8.686 \text{ dB}
$$

Per doubling of frequency (one octave):

$$
\Delta E_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(2) \approx +6.02 \text{ dB}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion**: The playback voltage increases by approximately **+6 dB per octave** of frequency rise. This is the classic **6 dB/octave rising characteristic** of magnetic playback, which must be compensated by an **equalisation amplifier** in the playback chain.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Quantity | Symbol | Functional Dependence | Engineering Impact |
|----------|--------|-----------------------|---------------------|
| Recorded wavelength | $\lambda$ | $\lambda = v/f$ | Smaller $v$ → shorter wavelengths → head-gap-limited |
| Max frequency | $f_{max}$ | $f_{max} = v/g$ | Higher $v$ → higher bandwidth |
| Playback voltage | $E_{out}$ | $E_{out} \propto f$ | Rises +6 dB/octave → needs equalisation |
| Erasure frequency | $f_{erase}$ | $f_{erase} \gg f_{signal}$ | Typically 5–10× highest signal frequency |
| Tape speed | $v$ | Direct control | Higher speed = better SNR but more tape used |
| Track width | $w$ | $SNR \propto \sqrt{w}$ | Wider tracks = better SNR |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of a Magnetic Tape Recorder

The figure below shows the **complete functional architecture** of a professional magnetic tape recording system, including the erase, record, and playback signal paths.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sigIN[Input Signal] --> recPre[Record Preamplifier]
    recPre --> recAmp[Record Amplifier]
    recAmp --> recHead[Record Head]
    biasOsc[High Frequency Bias Oscillator] -->|AC bias| recHead
    biasOsc --> eraseHead[Erase Head]
    tape[Magnetic Tape Moving at Velocity v] -->|contact| eraseHead
    tape -->|contact| recHead
    tape -->|magnetised track| playHead[Playback Head]
    playHead --> playPre[Playback Preamplifier]
    playPre --> eqAmp[Equalisation Amplifier 6dB per octave]
    eqAmp --> playAmp[Playback Amplifier]
    playAmp --> sigOUT[Output Signal]
    motor[Tape Transport Motor] --> capstan[Capstan and Pinch Roller]
    capstan --> tape
```

---

## 4.2 Schematic — Record / Playback / Erase Head Configuration

This diagram illustrates the **spatial arrangement** of the three magnetic heads along the tape path (from left = supply reel to right = take-up reel).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    reelSupply[Supply Reel] --> tensionArm[Tension Arm]
    tensionArm --> eraseH[Erase Head]
    eraseH --> recH[Record Head]
    recH --> playH[Playback Head]
    playH --> capstan[Capstan Drive]
    capstan --> takeUpReel[Take Up Reel]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Note**: In consumer recorders, **erase, record, and playback functions** are often combined into a **single combined head** or a **2-head design** (erase + record/playback). Professional telemetry recorders use **separate heads** for each function for better isolation and head life.

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Direct vs FM Recording Comparison

This figure maps the two principal telemetry recording methods so you can compare their functional signal paths.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph DirectRecording[Direct Recording Path]
        dIn[Telemetry Input Signal] --> dAmp[Wideband Amplifier]
        dAmp --> dHead[Direct Record Head]
        dHead --> dTape[Tape]
        dTape --> dPlayHead[Playback Head]
        dPlayHead --> dEq[Equalisation]
        dEq --> dOut[Recovered Signal with HF Boost]
    end
    subgraph FMRecording[FM Recording Path]
        fIn[Telemetry Input Signal] --> fMod[FM Modulator Voltage Controlled Oscillator]
        fMod --> fHead[FM Record Head]
        fHead --> fTape[Tape]
        fTape --> fPlayHead[FM Playback Head]
        fPlayHead --> fLim[Limiter]
        fLim --> fDisc[FM Discriminator]
        fDisc --> fOut[Recovered Signal Flat Response]
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why FM wins for telemetry**: Direct recording suffers from 6 dB/octave roll-off and **DC cannot be recorded** because the head senses dφ/dt, so a constant flux (DC) gives zero output. **FM recording eliminates both problems** by modulating a carrier — making it the **KTU-preferred answer** for instrumentation recorders.

---

## 4.4 State Transition Diagram — Tape Hysteresis During AC Erasure

This flowchart represents the magnetic-state transitions during high-frequency AC erasure.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    s0[Tape in Random Magnetised State] --> s1[Enter Erase Head Gap Region]
    s1 --> s2[Strong AC Field Applied H greater than Hc]
    s2 --> s3[Saturation Cycles plus and minus Br Saturation]
    s3 --> s4[Tape Moves out of Erase Gap Region]
    s4 --> s5[AC Field Decays to Zero]
    s5 --> s6[Tape Reaches Neutral State Net Magnetisation Zero]
    s6 --> s7[Ready for New Recording]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**

### Q1. Define magnetic tape recording and list its three essential components.
**(3 Marks) — CO1 / Remember Level**

**Model Answer:**
Magnetic tape recording is a method of storing electrical signals as patterns of residual magnetism on a magnetically coated moving tape.

The **three essential components** are:
1. **Magnetic Tape** — the storage medium, consisting of a polyester base coated with ferromagnetic particles (e.g., γ-Fe₂O₃).
2. **Magnetic Heads** — electromagnetic transducers used for recording, playback, and erasing.
3. **Tape Transport Mechanism** — the mechanical subsystem (capstan, pinch roller, motors, reels) that moves the tape at a constant velocity past the heads.

---

### Q2. Why is a high-frequency AC bias used during recording?
**(3 Marks) — CO2 / Understand Level**

**Model Answer:**
A high-frequency AC bias (typically **50–100 kHz**, much higher than the audio signal) is superimposed on the record head current to:

1. **Linearise the recording process** — the B-H curve of the tape is highly non-linear near zero, which would cause severe harmonic distortion. The AC bias pushes the tape into its linear region of magnetisation.
2. **Improve signal-to-noise ratio (SNR)** — biasing increases the recorded flux level for a given input.
3. **Reduce distortion** — residual bias is filtered out by the playback equaliser since it lies far outside the signal band.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall**: Many students write only "to reduce noise" without explaining the **linearisation of the B-H curve**. Full credit requires mentioning **distortion reduction + linear magnetisation** as the primary reason.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Pattern)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]**

---

### ⭐ Question A — Choice 1 (14 Marks)

#### Q.A(a) Explain with a neat block diagram the working of a magnetic tape recording system. Describe the function of record, playback, and erase heads. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (2 Marks):**
A magnetic tape recording system stores electrical signals as variations of residual magnetism on a magnetically coated tape. The complete system consists of a tape transport, magnetic heads, and associated electronic circuitry.

**Block Diagram Description (3 Marks):**

```
Input → Record Amplifier → Record Head ↘
                                    ↘
High-Freq Bias Oscillator → Erase Head → Magnetic Tape → Playback Head → Playback Amp → Output
```

**Function of Heads (2 Marks):**
- **Erase Head**: Demagnetises the tape using high-frequency AC before new recording.
- **Record Head**: Converts input signal current into a varying magnetic field that magnetises the moving tape.
- **Playback Head**: Senses the residual flux on the tape and converts it back into an electrical signal via Faraday's Law.

**Conclusion:** The system achieves reversible electromechanical storage of analog/digital signals.

---

#### Q.A(b) Derive the relationship between tape speed, recorded wavelength, and signal frequency. Explain why the playback output rises at 6 dB per octave. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation (4 Marks):**

Given tape velocity $v$, signal frequency $f$, period $T = 1/f$:

$$
v = \frac{dx}{dt} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \lambda = v \cdot T = \frac{v}{f}
$$

$$
\therefore \quad f = \frac{v}{\lambda}
$$

Maximum recordable frequency is limited by the head gap $g$:
$$
f_{max} = \frac{v}{g}
$$

**6 dB/Octave Derivation (3 Marks):**

Residual flux on tape:
$$
B_r(t) = B_0 \sin(2\pi f t)
$$

Playback EMF by Faraday's Law:
$$
e(t) = -N \frac{d\phi}{dt} \propto \frac{dB_r}{dt} = B_0 \cdot 2\pi f \cos(2\pi f t)
$$

RMS amplitude:
$$
E_{rms} \propto B_0 \cdot 2\pi f
$$

In dB per octave:
$$
20 \log_{10}(2) \approx +6.02 \text{ dB}
$$

Hence playback output rises by **+6 dB per octave**, requiring equalisation.

---

### ⭐ Question B — Choice 2 (14 Marks)

#### Q.B(a) Compare direct recording and FM recording techniques used in instrumentation tape recorders. Which is preferred for telemetry applications and why? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | Direct Recording | FM Recording |
|-----------|------------------|--------------|
| Bandwidth | Wide (audio to MHz) | Limited by carrier deviation |
| DC response | Cannot record DC | Excellent DC response |
| SNR | Poor at low frequencies | Excellent |
| Flutter effect | Directly appears in output | Largely cancelled by limiter + discriminator |
| Complexity | Simple | More complex (modulator + demodulator) |
| Use case | Wideband vibration, acoustic | **Telemetry, slow-varying data, biomedical** |

**Preferred Technique (2 Marks):**
**FM Recording is preferred for telemetry** because:
1. DC and very low-frequency signals (e.g., temperature, pressure) can be recorded.
2. High-frequency noise and tape flutter are rejected by the discriminator.
3. Constant-amplitude playback (after limiting) gives stable output level.

---

#### Q.B(b) Explain the principle of AC erasure. With a hysteresis loop sketch, describe how the tape is demagnetised. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**AC Erasure Principle (3 Marks):**
A high-frequency alternating current (typically 50–100 kHz, amplitude much greater than coercivity) is passed through the erase head. As the tape moves past the erase head gap, it is exposed to a strong AC field whose amplitude gradually decays as the tape moves away from the gap.

**Hysteresis Loop Explanation (4 Marks):**

```
   B
   ↑      Br
   |     /\
   |    /  \
   |   /    \  ← Saturation cycles
   |  /      \
   | /        \
   |/__________\___→ H
   0           Hc
```

The tape undergoes many saturation cycles as it enters the gap. As it leaves, the field amplitude progressively decreases through the **tip of the hysteresis loop**, eventually reaching zero. The tape is left in a **magnetically neutral state** with zero net magnetisation — ready for fresh recording.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall** — Many students confuse **DC erasure** (which leaves the tape magnetised in one direction) with **AC erasure** (which leaves it neutral). Examiners deduct marks if you do not clearly state that AC erasure produces a **zero net residual flux**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick-Revision Checklist — Read this 5 minutes before the exam.**

- ☐ Magnetic tape recording = storage of signals as **residual magnetism** on coated tape.
- ☐ Three core components: **Tape, Heads, Transport**.
- ☐ Three head types: **Erase, Record, Playback**.
- ☐ Recording equation: $\lambda = v/f$.
- ☐ Maximum frequency: $f_{max} = v/g$ (gap-limited).
- ☐ Playback equation: $e = -N \dfrac{d\phi}{dt}$ (Faraday's Law).
- ☐ Playback response: **+6 dB per octave** rising — must be equalised.
- ☐ AC bias: **50–100 kHz**, used to **linearise** the B-H curve and reduce distortion.
- ☐ AC erasure: Strong decaying high-frequency AC → neutral state.
- ☐ **Direct recording**: Cannot record DC; suffers from 6 dB/octave issue.
- ☐ **FM recording**: Records DC; better SNR; **preferred for telemetry**.
- ☐ Key tape parameters: **Hc (coercivity)**, **Br (retentivity)**, **μᵣ (permeability)**.
- ☐ Higher tape speed → higher bandwidth but more tape consumption.
- ☐ Track width ↑ → SNR ↑ (proportional to √w).
- ☐ Instrumentation recorders use **separate heads** for each function.
- ☐ Standard tape speeds: 1⅞, 3¾, 7½, 15, 30 ips (consumer) and 15–240 ips (telemetry).

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Tip**: Always draw the **block diagram first** in any 7- or 14-mark question on this topic, then label each block with its function. Diagrams carry **at least 2–3 marks** by themselves. Pair them with the **6 dB/octave derivation** for a guaranteed high-score answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Modern Telemetry Techniques - Radio Telemetry - Multiplexing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT634 - COMMUNICATION SYSTEM AND TELEMETRY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Modern Telemetry Techniques - Radio Telemetry - Multiplexing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Modern Telemetry — Formal Definition
**Telemetry** is the science and technology of automated measurement and transmission of data from a remote, inaccessible, or hazardous source to a receiving station for monitoring, recording, and analysis. **Modern Telemetry Techniques** specifically refer to the suite of advanced, electronic, and digital methodologies that have replaced purely mechanical or wired systems, enabling high-speed, high-fidelity, and often wireless data acquisition across vast distances.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology**
> *Modern Telemetry* = the application of contemporary communication theory (digital modulation, microcontrollers, RF links, and computer-based processing) to traditional measurement systems.

### 1.2 Radio Telemetry — The Wireless Backbone
**Radio Telemetry** is a branch of telemetry in which the measured physical quantity (temperature, pressure, flow, biomedical signals, etc.) is converted into an electrical signal and transmitted through **free space using radio-frequency (RF) waves** as the propagation medium, eliminating the need for any physical conductor between the source and the destination.

### 1.3 Multiplexing — The Necessity Behind the Concept
**Multiplexing** is the process of simultaneously transmitting multiple independent measurement signals over a **single communication channel** without mutual interference, by allotting each signal a unique distinguishing parameter (time slot, frequency band, or code sequence).

### 1.4 Intuitive Analogy — The "Multi-Lane Highway"
Imagine a busy city with thousands of cars (data signals) trying to reach a distant hospital (the receiver/control room). Building a separate road for every single car is absurdly expensive and physically impossible. Instead, engineers build a **multi-lane superhighway (the multiplexed channel)**. There are three strategies:

* **Time-Division Multiplexing (TDM)** → A traffic police officer allows Car 1 to pass for 1 second, then Car 2 for 1 second, then Car 3, and so on, in rapid rotation. Each car gets the *whole road* but only for a *brief slice of time*.
* **Frequency-Division Multiplexing (FDM)** → The highway is divided into permanent side-by-side lanes. Car 1 always drives in Lane A (low frequency), Car 2 in Lane B (middle frequency), and so on. They travel *simultaneously* but in *separate frequency bands*.
* **Code-Division Multiplexing (CDM)** → Each car speaks a *different language* (unique pseudo-random code). All cars are on the road at the same time in the same lane, but the receiver, who understands the language, can filter out its intended car from the chatter.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Multiplexing is Critical in Telemetry:**
> A single rocket launch may carry **hundreds of sensors**. Wiring each sensor to ground via a separate cable is *physically impossible*. Multiplexing enables one RF link to carry all of them — saving weight, cost, and bandwidth.

### 1.5 Standard Physical & System Constants
* **Speed of Light (c)** = **3 × 10⁸ m/s**
* **Nyquist Sampling Rate** = **fs ≥ 2fm** (where fm is the highest frequency component of the message)
* **Standard Telemetry RF Bands (IRIG Standard):**
  * L-Band: **1.435 – 1.535 GHz**
  * S-Band: **2.2 – 2.3 GHz**
* **IRIG (Inter-Range Instrumentation Group)** = the global standard body for telemetry band allocation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Radio Telemetry — System Architecture (Step-by-Step)

A radio telemetry system is a *transmit–receive pair* designed for untethered data relay. Its operational chain is:

1. **Transducer Stage (Sensor)**: Converts the physical quantity (temperature, pressure, displacement) into a low-level electrical analog signal. *Example: Thermocouple, Strain gauge, LVDT.*
2. **Signal Conditioning**: Amplification, linearization, and noise filtering of the raw transducer output. *Why: Sensor outputs are often in mV range and incompatible with transmission.*
3. **Multiplexer (MUX)**: Combines multiple conditioned signals onto a single channel using TDM, FDM, or CDM.
4. **Encoder (ADC for digital systems)**: Converts the analog multiplexed signal into binary data. PCM (Pulse Code Modulation) is the industry standard.
5. **RF Transmitter**: Modulates a high-frequency carrier with the encoded baseband signal and feeds it to the antenna. Common modulations: **PCM/FM, PCM/PM, BPSK, QPSK**.
6. **Antenna (Tx)**: Radiates the modulated RF energy into free space.
7. **Channel (Free Space)**: The propagation medium — subject to path loss, fading, and noise.
8. **Antenna (Rx) & RF Receiver**: Captures and demodulates the incoming RF energy.
9. **Demultiplexer (DEMUX) & Decoder (DAC)**: Separates the individual channels and reconstructs the original analog signals.
10. **Display / Recorder / Computer**: Final data presentation and storage (oscilloscope, strip-chart, or PC-based DAQ).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners love the question *"Draw and explain the block diagram of a radio telemetry system."* Always draw in this exact order: Sensor → Conditioner → MUX → Encoder → Modulator → Tx Antenna → Channel → Rx Antenna → Demodulator → DEMUX → Decoder → Display.

### 2.2 Why Radio Telemetry? — Engineering Utility
* **Mobility**: Tracks missiles, satellites, race cars, and wildlife.
* **Hazardous Environments**: Monitors nuclear reactors, volcanic vents, and deep-sea pipelines.
* **Rotating Machinery**: Slip rings (wired) are unreliable; radio works on rotating turbine blades.
* **Aerospace**: Satellite-to-ground and rocket-to-ground links.

### 2.3 Multiplexing — The Three Pillars

#### 2.3.1 Time Division Multiplexing (TDM)
In **TDM**, the total transmission time (frame time $T_f$) is divided into $N$ sequential time slots, with each slot dedicated to one of the $N$ input channels. The switch rotates synchronously at the *frame rate*.

* **Sampling Theorem Pre-requisite**: Each input channel (bandwidth $W$ Hz) must be sampled at $f_s \geq 2W$.
* **Frame Rate** = Sampling Rate = $f_s$.
* **Frame Time** $T_f = \dfrac{1}{f_s}$.
* **Slot Duration** $T_{slot} = \dfrac{T_f}{N} = \dfrac{1}{N \cdot f_s}$.

#### 2.3.2 Frequency Division Multiplexing (FDM)
In **FDM**, each input signal is translated to a unique *subcarrier frequency band* using a modulator (typically DSB-SC). All modulated subcarriers are then summed and transmitted simultaneously.

* **Subcarrier Bandwidth** = Message Bandwidth $W$.
* **Guard Band** $\Delta f$: A small unused frequency gap inserted between adjacent subcarrier bands to prevent *inter-channel cross-talk* due to imperfect filters.
* **Total FDM Bandwidth**: $B_{FDM} = N \cdot W + (N-1) \cdot \Delta f$.

#### 2.3.3 Code Division Multiplexing (CDM/CDMA)
In **CDM**, every input signal is multiplied (XORed or spread) by a unique high-speed **Pseudo-Random Noise (PN) code sequence**. All spread signals occupy the *same frequency band simultaneously*. The receiver, knowing the intended code, correlates the incoming composite signal with its local replica of that code to extract the desired channel.

* **Processing Gain** = $\dfrac{\text{Chip Rate}}{\text{Data Rate}}$ (typically 100 to 1000).
* Provides excellent resistance to narrow-band interference and eavesdropping (used in GPS and military telemetry).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| # | Parameter | Formula | Variables / Units | Typical Use |
|---|-----------|---------|-------------------|-------------|
| 1 | Nyquist Sampling Rate | $f_s \geq 2 f_m$ | $f_s$ in Hz, $f_m$ = max message frequency | TDM sampling requirement |
| 2 | TDM Frame Time | $T_f = \dfrac{1}{f_s}$ | $T_f$ in seconds | TDM frame duration |
| 3 | TDM Time Slot | $T_{slot} = \dfrac{T_f}{N}$ | $N$ = number of channels | Per-channel allocation |
| 4 | TDM Bit Rate (PCM) | $R_b = N \cdot f_s \cdot n$ | $n$ = bits per sample | Output bit rate of MUX |
| 5 | TDM Min. Bandwidth | $B_{TDM} \geq \dfrac{R_b}{2}$ | $B_{TDM}$ in Hz (baseband) | Channel capacity |
| 6 | FDM Total Bandwidth | $B_{FDM} = N W + (N-1)\Delta f$ | $W$ = channel BW, $\Delta f$ = guard band | Spectrum allocation |
| 7 | Sub-carrier Spacing | $f_{sc} = W + \Delta f$ | Hz | FDM modulator design |
| 8 | CDM Processing Gain | $G_p = \dfrac{R_c}{R_b}$ | $R_c$ = chip rate, $R_b$ = data rate | CDM link budget |
| 9 | Free-space Path Loss | $L = \left(\dfrac{4 \pi d}{\lambda}\right)^2$ | $d$ = distance, $\lambda$ = wavelength | RF link budget |
| 10 | Antenna Gain (parabolic) | $G = \eta \left(\dfrac{\pi D}{\lambda}\right)^2$ | $D$ = dish diameter, $\eta$ = efficiency | Ground-station design |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Caution:** In all TDM numerical problems, students frequently forget that *sampling frequency must be strictly greater than* $2f_m$, not merely equal. If the message is band-limited to **$W$ Hz**, the minimum sampling rate is **$2W$ Hz** (Nyquist rate).

### 2.5 Comparative Analysis of Multiplexing Techniques

| Feature | TDM | FDM | CDM |
|---------|-----|-----|-----|
| Domain | Time | Frequency | Code |
| Synchronization | Strict (frame sync needed) | Loose (filter-based) | Strict (code sync needed) |
| Hardware Complexity | Low (digital switches) | High (analog filters) | Very high (DSP/ASIC) |
| Noise Immunity | Excellent (digital) | Poor (analog) | Outstanding (spread spectrum) |
| Bandwidth Efficiency | High | Low (guard bands waste) | Moderate |
| Security | Low | Very Low | Very High (pseudo-random) |
| Typical Telemetry Use | PCM telemetry (rockets) | Legacy analog telemetry | GPS, secure military |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Minimum TDM Bit Rate for *N* Multiplexed Channels

**Problem Statement:** A telemetry system multiplexes $N$ input signals, each band-limited to $W$ Hz, using PCM-TDM. Each sample is quantized to $n$ bits. Derive the minimum transmission bit rate.

**Step 1 — Apply the Sampling Theorem to one channel.**

The highest frequency in any input channel is $W$ Hz. To reconstruct the signal without aliasing, the sampling frequency must be at least twice the message bandwidth.

$$f_s = 2W$$

*Logic:* A band-limited signal of bandwidth $W$ contains $2W$ independent samples per second. Sampling slower than this causes *aliasing*, an irreversible distortion.

**Step 2 — Determine the TDM frame rate.**

The multiplexer visits each of the $N$ channels once per frame. Thus the frame rate equals the per-channel sampling rate.

$$R_{frame} = f_s = 2W$$

**Step 3 — Convert each analog sample to a digital word.**

If an ADC quantizes each sample using $n$ bits, the bit rate contributed by one channel is:

$$R_{b,1} = f_s \cdot n = 2Wn$$

**Step 4 — Aggregate the bit rate over all N channels.**

Since all $N$ channels are time-interleaved into a single serial bitstream, the bit rates add linearly.

$$R_b = N \cdot R_{b,1} = 2NWn \text{ bits/sec}$$

*Logic:* The MUX outputs $N$ samples per frame, and each sample is $n$ bits wide. The serial line must therefore shift out $N \cdot n$ bits per frame, and frames arrive at rate $f_s = 2W$. Hence the multiplication.

**Step 5 — Minimum Baseband Transmission Bandwidth.**

For a baseband binary transmission, the Nyquist bandwidth bound states that the minimum theoretical bandwidth is half the bit rate.

$$B_{min} = \frac{R_b}{2} = NWn \text{ Hz}$$

*Final Compact Formula:*

$$\boxed{\,R_b = 2NWn \quad \text{and} \quad B_{min} = NWn\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Numerical Example for KTU Practice:** $N = 8$ channels, $W = 4$ kHz, $n = 8$ bits/sample.
> Then $R_b = 2 \times 8 \times 4000 \times 8 = 512{,}000$ bps = **512 kbps**, and $B_{min} = 256$ kHz.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Total FDM Bandwidth with Guard Bands

**Problem Statement:** $N$ band-limited signals of bandwidth $W$ Hz each are multiplexed using FDM. A guard band of $\Delta f$ Hz is provided between adjacent channels. Derive the total transmission bandwidth.

**Step 1 — Visualize the spectrum.**

Channel 1 occupies the band $[0, W]$, Channel 2 occupies $[W + \Delta f, 2W + \Delta f]$, Channel 3 occupies $[2(W + \Delta f), 3W + 2\Delta f]$, and so on.

**Step 2 — Identify the pattern.**

* The bandwidth of one modulated channel is $W$ Hz.
* The number of *gaps* (guard bands) is $N - 1$ (guard bands exist *between* channels, not at the ends).
* The width of each gap is $\Delta f$.

**Step 3 — Sum the contributions.**

$$B_{FDM} = N \cdot W + (N-1) \cdot \Delta f$$

*Logic:* Think of the spectrum as $N$ blocks of width $W$ separated by $N-1$ slits of width $\Delta f$. The total width is the sum.

**Step 4 — Special case: no guard band ($\Delta f = 0$).**

$$B_{FDM} = NW$$

This is the theoretical minimum FDM bandwidth. In practice, $\Delta f > 0$ is always used to allow practical (non-ideal) bandpass filters to separate the channels.

$$\boxed{\,B_{FDM} = N \cdot W + (N-1) \cdot \Delta f\,}$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Frame Time and Slot Duration in a TDM System

**Given:** Sampling frequency $f_s$, number of channels $N$, synchronization bits $k$ per frame.

**Step 1 — Compute the frame time.**

By definition, a frame is the time interval between two consecutive samples of the *same* channel.

$$T_f = \frac{1}{f_s}$$

**Step 2 — Effective channel time slot (excluding sync overhead).**

If $k$ synchronization bits are added per frame for receiver alignment, the total frame has $N \cdot n + k$ bits. The bit duration is:

$$T_b = \frac{T_f}{N \cdot n + k}$$

**Step 3 — Per-channel pure data time slot.**

$$T_{slot} = n \cdot T_b = \frac{n \cdot T_f}{N \cdot n + k}$$

> [!NOTE]
> As $k \to 0$ (idealized case), $T_{slot} \to T_f / N$, recovering the basic formula.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Multiplexing on Channel Capacity

| Scenario | Channels (N) | Per-Channel BW (W) | Guard Band (Δf) | Total System BW | Spectral Efficiency (channels per kHz) |
|----------|--------------|--------------------|------------------|-----------------|----------------------------------------|
| TDM (PCM) | 8 | 4 kHz | N/A | 256 kHz | 0.0313 ch/kHz (temporal packing) |
| FDM (no guard) | 8 | 4 kHz | 0 | 32 kHz | 0.250 ch/kHz |
| FDM (with guard) | 8 | 4 kHz | 1 kHz | 39 kHz | 0.205 ch/kHz |
| CDM (processing gain = 100) | 8 | 4 kHz | N/A | 3200 kHz | 0.0025 ch/kHz (high overhead) |

*Insight:* TDM wins on **bandwidth efficiency** in digital systems; FDM wins on **simplicity** in analog systems; CDM wins on **security and robustness** in hostile RF environments.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Complete Radio Telemetry System (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

The following Mermaid diagram renders the **full block-level functional architecture** of a Radio Telemetry system, mapping every signal-flow interaction from sensor to display.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph source["DATA ACQUISITION SIDE"]
        A1[Sensor 1: Physical Quantity]
        A2[Sensor 2: Physical Quantity]
        A3[Sensor N: Physical Quantity]
        B1[Signal Conditioner 1]
        B2[Signal Conditioner 2]
        B3[Signal Conditioner N]
        C[Multiplexer MUX]
        D[Analog to Digital Converter ADC]
        E[PCM Encoder]
    end

    subgraph transmitter["RF TRANSMITTER SECTION"]
        F[Baseband Formatter]
        G[RF Modulator BPSK or PCM FM]
        H[Power Amplifier]
        I[TX Antenna]
    end

    subgraph channel["PROPAGATION CHANNEL"]
        J[Free Space RF Link with Fading and Noise]
    end

    subgraph receiver["RF RECEIVER SECTION"]
        K[RX Antenna]
        L[Low Noise Amplifier LNA]
        M[RF Demodulator]
        N[Bit Synchronizer]
        O[PCM Decoder and DAC]
    end

    subgraph output["DATA RECONSTRUCTION SIDE"]
        P[Demultiplexer DEMUX]
        Q1[Display 1]
        Q2[Display 2]
        Q3[Display N]
        R[Data Logger or PC]
    end

    A1 --> B1 --> C
    A2 --> B2 --> C
    A3 --> B3 --> C
    C --> D --> E --> F --> G --> H --> I
    I --> J --> K --> L --> M --> N --> O --> P
    P --> Q1
    P --> Q2
    P --> Q3
    P --> R
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Arrows indicate the unidirectional flow of information. The system is essentially a one-way data pipe; for two-way (command) telemetry, a return path (downlink/uplink) is added symmetrically.

### 4.2 Block Diagram — TDM Transmitter (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph inputs["ANALOG INPUT CHANNELS"]
        S1[Input 1 Bandwidth W]
        S2[Input 2 Bandwidth W]
        S3[Input N Bandwidth W]
    end

    subgraph sampling["SAMPLING AND QUANTIZATION"]
        SW[Rotary Electronic Switch at fs Hertz]
        Q[Quantizer n bits per sample]
    end

    subgraph framing["FRAME ASSEMBLY"]
        FR[Frame Builder N samples per frame]
        SY[Sync Bit Inserter k bits]
    end

    subgraph output["SERIAL TRANSMISSION"]
        CH[Single Digital Channel]
    end

    S1 --> SW
    S2 --> SW
    S3 --> SW
    SW --> Q --> FR --> SY --> CH
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — FDM Transmitter (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph sources["INPUT MESSAGES"]
        M1[Message 1 Bandwidth W]
        M2[Message 2 Bandwidth W]
        M3[Message N Bandwidth W]
    end

    subgraph modulation["SUBCARRIER MODULATION"]
        SC1[Subcarrier Oscillator f1]
        SC2[Subcarrier Oscillator f2]
        SC3[Subcarrier Oscillator fN]
        MOD1[Balanced Modulator DSB SC]
        MOD2[Balanced Modulator DSB SC]
        MOD3[Balanced Modulator DSB SC]
    end

    subgraph combining["SUMMING AND TRANSMISSION"]
        SUM[Summing Amplifier]
        BPF[Bandpass Filter]
        CH[Composite FDM Channel]
    end

    M1 --> MOD1
    M2 --> MOD2
    M3 --> MOD3
    SC1 --> MOD1
    SC2 --> MOD2
    SC3 --> MOD3
    MOD1 --> SUM
    MOD2 --> SUM
    MOD3 --> SUM
    SUM --> BPF --> CH
```

### 4.4 Comparative Block Diagram — TDM vs FDM vs CDM at a Glance

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph tdm["TDM DOMAIN TIME"]
        T1[All Channels share TIME]
        T2[Sequential Time Slots per Channel]
    end

    subgraph fdm["FDM DOMAIN FREQUENCY"]
        F1[All Channels share TIME but separate FREQUENCIES]
        F2[Adjacent Guard Bands Avoid Interference]
    end

    subgraph cdm["CDM DOMAIN CODE"]
        C1[All Channels share TIME and FREQUENCY]
        C2[Each Channel has Unique PN Code]
    end

    T1 --> T2
    F1 --> F2
    C1 --> C2
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mermaid Compilation Notes Used:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `A1`, `SC1`, `MOD2`). No reserved words (`end`, `graph`, `subgraph`, `style`) are used as standalone IDs. All labels are uppercase alphanumeric text without markdown formatting tags.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]** Define Radio Telemetry. List any four of its applications.
**Model Answer:**
Radio Telemetry is the wireless transmission of measurement data from a remote or inaccessible location to a receiving station using radio-frequency electromagnetic waves as the propagation medium. The system employs a transducer, signal conditioner, multiplexer, RF modulator, and antenna on the transmit side, with a corresponding demodulator and display on the receive side.
*Four Applications:*
1. Spacecraft and satellite health monitoring (ISRO, NASA missions).
2. Wildlife tracking and ecological research (radio collars on animals).
3. Missile and rocket flight testing.
4. Industrial process monitoring in hazardous environments (nuclear reactors, oil rigs).
5. Biomedical telemetry (ECG/EEG from ambulances).

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]** What is Multiplexing? Why is it essential in modern telemetry systems?
**Model Answer:**
Multiplexing is the technique of simultaneously transmitting multiple independent signals over a single communication channel by assigning each signal a unique distinguishing parameter — time slot (TDM), frequency band (FDM), or code sequence (CDM).
*Essentiality in Modern Telemetry:*
* A single modern platform (rocket, aircraft, factory) generates **hundreds of sensor outputs**. Separate channels for each would be prohibitively expensive, bulky, and bandwidth-consuming.
* Multiplexing allows a **single RF link** to carry all measurements, drastically reducing weight, cable cost, and spectrum usage.
* It also enables synchronization and centralized processing at the ground station.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> For 14-mark Part B questions, examiners allocate marks as follows: Block diagram = 4 marks, Working explanation = 4 marks, Mathematical analysis/formulas = 4 marks, Applications/advantages = 2 marks. Students who *omit the block diagram* or *skip derivation steps* routinely lose 5–6 marks. Always **draw the diagram first**, then explain step-by-step, and conclude with applications.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **Radio Telemetry System**. Discuss the function of each block in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A telemetry system uses **PCM-TDM** to transmit **8 channels** of voice-grade data. Each channel has a maximum message frequency of **3.4 kHz** and is sampled at **20% above the Nyquist rate**. Each sample is quantized to **8 bits**, and one **synchronization bit** is added per frame.
*Calculate:*
* (i) The sampling frequency.
* (ii) The frame time.
* (iii) The bit duration.
* (iv) The minimum transmission bandwidth.
**(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to A(a):**

The block diagram is as rendered in **Section 4.1** above.

**Working Explanation (Block-by-Block):**

1. **Sensors (Transducers)**: Convert physical quantities (e.g., temperature → voltage) into electrical signals. Common sensors include thermocouples (temperature), strain gauges (pressure), and LVDTs (displacement).
2. **Signal Conditioners**: Each transducer output is weak (a few millivolts) and noisy. The conditioner amplifies, filters, and linearizes the signal to a standard range (e.g., 0–5 V).
3. **Multiplexer (MUX)**: A high-speed electronic switch sequentially selects one channel at a time, combining $N$ signals into a single time-interleaved stream. (See Section 2.3 for TDM/FDM/CDM types.)
4. **ADC + PCM Encoder**: The analog multiplexed signal is sampled and quantized into binary words, typically using successive-approximation ADCs.
5. **Baseband Formatter**: Adds frame synchronization words, error-correction codes (e.g., Hamming, convolutional), and idle patterns.
6. **RF Modulator**: Impresses the binary data onto a high-frequency carrier. **PCM/FM** (binary FSK) and **PCM/PSK** (BPSK/QPSK) are the most common telemetry modulations.
7. **Power Amplifier + TX Antenna**: Boosts the modulated RF signal to a sufficient power level (Watts to kiloWatts) and radiates it via a directional or omnidirectional antenna.
8. **Channel (Free Space)**: The RF wave propagates. The received signal power falls off as $1/d^2$ in free space (Friis equation).
9. **RX Antenna + LNA**: Captures the weak incoming signal. The Low Noise Amplifier boosts it with minimal added noise.
10. **RF Demodulator**: Recovers the baseband binary stream from the carrier.
11. **Bit Synchronizer + PCM Decoder + DAC**: Aligns the bit clock, decodes the binary words, and converts them back to analog voltages.
